The Vigyan Bhairav Tantra · Osho's Book of Secrets

The Complete 112 Techniques

All 112 meditation methods Shiva gave to Devi — arranged in Osho's book order, with full commentary and practice notes.

39 chapters · 112 sutras · 15 thematic groups · single-file offline edition

Preface — First Access This

Critical Disclaimer — Read Before Practising

Practise at your own discretion and responsibility.

These are contemplative practices, not medical or psychological treatment. They are not a substitute for professional mental-health care, therapy, or medical advice. If you have any serious health condition, consult a qualified professional before beginning.

Some techniques carry specific risks. Read the safety section below carefully and respect the cautions listed in each file:

  • If you have severe mental-health conditions (psychosis, severe trauma, suicidal ideation, dissociative disorders), do not begin without consulting a qualified professional. Avoid identity-dissolution and void techniques.
  • If you have cardiovascular conditions, avoid breath retention, intense kundalini-rising practices, and prolonged loud chanting.
  • If you are pregnant, avoid intense breath techniques, deep pelvic-energy work, and forceful kundalini practices.
  • If you are recovering from recent surgery or acute injury, avoid pain-as-doorway and intense postural techniques.
  • If you have glaucoma or eye conditions, avoid pressure-on-eyeballs techniques.
  • If overwhelm arises during practice, stop immediately. Open your eyes. Ground through body and breath. Drink water. Walk in nature.

The 21-day commitment described in this collection is intended for committed seekers, not casual experimenters. Some techniques (especially Tantric Union and energy work) require maturity, discernment, and respect for yourself and any partner.

When in doubt, choose the gentler technique. When unsure, do less, not more.

Find your starting technique — an 8-question quiz

Answer all eight honestly. The quiz will recommend one of the 112 techniques that fits the way you already meet life. You can re-take it as often as you like.

Question 1 of 8 When you sit down to meditate, what comes most naturally?
Question 2 of 8 Which sense pulls you in most strongly?
Question 3 of 8 What's the deepest reason you came here?
Question 4 of 8 How do you feel about the body?
Question 5 of 8 When something painful arises inside, what happens?
Question 6 of 8 How do you feel about emptiness, silence, the unknown?
Question 7 of 8 What dissolves "you" most easily, even briefly?
Question 8 of 8 Tonight, before sleep, what would feel right?

About

The Vigyan Bhairav Tantra

An ancient Sanskrit text dated to roughly 5,000 years ago, presented as a dialogue between Shiva and his consort Devi. Devi asks: "How can I know the divine?" Shiva replies with 112 techniques — methods, devices, doorways — covering breath, sound, light, witnessing, centering, devotion, and more. Each technique is a path; one is enough.

Osho (1931–1990)

An Indian mystic and teacher who, between 1972 and 1973, gave 80 talks on this text in Mumbai. Those talks were transcribed and published as The Book of Secrets. His insistence: do not believe; experiment. The techniques are scientific in spirit — they either work for you or they do not. Try one for 21 days, observe, decide.

The Book of Secrets

Osho's commentary on each of the 112 techniques is the source for the distillations in this collection. Many techniques are accompanied by Osho's stories, contextual teaching, and questions from his audience — all of which informed the "Questions From The Book" sections in each file.

This Project

Fifteen self-contained HTML files — one per category — plus this index. Each file is a digital companion to the original book: the sutra, plain-English explanation, step-by-step practice, signs of progress, common pitfalls, and modern commentary. Each works fully offline. There are no servers, no tracking, no analytics — only the techniques.

How to use this collection

Read this page first. Take the disclaimer and safety sections seriously. Use the situation matcher above, browse the category grid, or simply press the random button if you trust your intuition. When you find a technique that resonates, commit to 21 days. Don't shop. Don't switch. The discipline is the work.

The 112 Techniques · In Osho's Book Order

Chapter 3 · Sutras 1–4 · 4 techniques

Breath – A Bridge to the Universe

Osho's framing

Before practising any breath technique, Osho asks you to drop the seeker's mistake. Truth is not a future goal - it is already the case, and you already are it. The mind, however, only operates in past or future; the present has no room for it to move. So as long as you are seeking - even seeking liberation or no-desire - you keep missing what is already here. Shiva therefore gives techniques without any philosophical preamble, because the mind would only turn the philosophy into another goal. The four breath sutras are simple turnings of attention back into this moment.

You cannot seek truth. You can find it, but you cannot seek it. The very seeking is the hindrance.

— Osho
Sutra 1 of 112
Category: Breath · Tags: gap · awareness · pause · beginner
Radiant one, this experience may dawn between two breaths. After breath comes in — down — and just before turning up — out — the beneficence.

In Plain English

Become aware of the small, unforced pause that happens after the in-breath ends, before the out-breath begins. In that pause, thought has not yet started; awareness is naked.

How to Practice

  1. Sit comfortably. Spine upright, shoulders soft, eyes closed.
  2. Don't change anything about your breath — let it be exactly as it is.
  3. Bring attention to the moment the in-breath finishes.
  4. Stay with the small natural pause that follows. Do not try to lengthen it.
  5. The out-breath will start by itself. Let it.
  6. On the next breath, do the same. Continue for 20 minutes.
  7. If you forget, simply return — the next gap is one breath away.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner
Duration
20 min (start with 10)
Best time
Early morning, or last thing before sleep
Posture
Seated, upright, comfortable
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Anxious or restless minds; first-time meditators
Avoid if
You have severe respiratory illness or a panic disorder around breath-watching

Signs It's Working

  • A subtle "drop" feeling, like settling onto a deeper floor
  • Thoughts space out — not stopped, just less continuous
  • The gap feels longer than the second or two it actually is
  • A quiet warmth or aliveness in the chest or belly

Common Pitfalls

  • Holding the breath on purpose — the gap must come by itself, not be manufactured
  • Counting breaths or measuring gaps — turns meditation into arithmetic
  • Trying to make the gap "bigger" — extension is the mind's reflex; let it stay tiny
  • Treating boredom as failure — the boredom IS the doorway thinning

Questions From The Book

How can simply being aware of a tiny gap in the breath lead to enlightenment?

Asked by a seeker · Chapter 4, "Overcoming the Deceptions of the Mind"

Osho's answer, in essence: spirituality is not an attainment but a recognition. You are already that. So even the smallest doorway is enough — the size of the door has nothing to do with the size of what lies beyond it. A door six inches wide opens onto the same sky as a door six feet wide.

The reason such a small gap can do so much: in that gap, the mind is not. The mind is a movement, a flow forward; the gap is the only place in your day where forward motion stops without sleep, without unconsciousness. There you are awake AND mindless. Even one second of that, fully tasted, is enlightenment in miniature — and that taste, repeated, becomes the standing flavour of life.

It feels improbable only because we have been taught that big results need big effort. Tantra's discovery is the opposite: the deepest things are reached not by force but by noticing what was always already happening.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: The mind is a continuous machine of language and prediction. The breath is the only thing that interrupts itself naturally, twice per cycle. The sutra says: in that interruption, the machine briefly halts — and what remains is what you actually are.

A modern analogy: Like the silence between two notes in music. You don't hear the silence, but it's what holds the music up. The gap between two breaths is the silence that holds your aliveness up.

Why neuroscience cares: The end-inhale moment correlates with a brief uptick in vagal tone and a transient quieting of the default mode network in fMRI studies. You are not imagining the stillness — there is a measurable neural pause that lines up with the breath's natural pause.

EssenceThe pause is not the absence of breath. It is presence without thought. Stop looking for a door — you have been walking through it 20,000 times a day.

Sutra 2 of 112
Category: Breath · Tags: turning point · in-out · out-in · beginner
As breath turns from down to up, and again as breath curves from up to down — through both these turns, realize.

In Plain English

The first technique uses one pause per breath. This one uses both: the moment the in-breath becomes the out-breath, AND the moment the out-breath becomes the in-breath. Two doorways every cycle.

How to Practice

  1. Same setup as Technique 1: comfortable seat, eyes closed, breath untouched.
  2. Notice the moment the in-breath stops becoming "in" and starts becoming "out". That is the upper turn.
  3. Notice the moment the out-breath stops becoming "out" and starts becoming "in". That is the lower turn.
  4. You are not staying in one or the other — you are alert to whichever is arriving next.
  5. Do this for 15 to 20 minutes.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner+
Duration
15–20 min
Best time
Any time you feel scattered — doubles the points of return
Posture
Seated
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Those who tried Technique 1 and found one gap not enough to hold attention
Avoid if
You feel dizzy when you watch breath closely

Signs It's Working

  • You start to feel the breath as a wave, not as two events
  • The two turns begin to feel like the same point repeated
  • A subtle internal "click" at each turn

Common Pitfalls

  • Trying to catch both turns equally well from day one — pick whichever is easier to feel and start there
  • Mentally labeling "now! now!" — that is thinking about the turn, not noticing it

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Every breath is a small pendulum. At each extreme, the pendulum is momentarily still before reversing. The sutra says: that stillness is not nothing — it is the place from which the whole movement is being seen.

A modern analogy: Like the apex of a thrown ball. Mid-air, before it falls, there is a frame of perfect stillness. The breath has two such frames, every few seconds, free of charge.

EssenceThere are two doors per breath, not one. Which one will you walk through?

Sutra 3 of 112
Category: Breath · Tags: navel · fusion · centre · intermediate
Or, whenever in-breath and out-breath fuse, at this instant touch the energy-less, energy-filled centre.

In Plain English

There is a point near the navel where the energy of the in-breath and the energy of the out-breath meet and cancel. The sutra says: rest attention there. Osho calls it "energy-less" because your fuel-energy doesn't reach it, and "energy-filled" because cosmic energy does.

How to Practice

  1. Lie on your back or sit upright. Body soft.
  2. Breathe deeply but naturally — let the breath go all the way to the belly. (Most adults breathe shallowly; let the belly rise and fall.)
  3. Place attention near the navel.
  4. Feel the in-breath travelling down to meet the out-breath travelling up. Find the point where they meet.
  5. That meeting point is what to settle into. Stay there.
  6. Begin with 15 minutes. Build to 30 over time.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
15–30 min
Best time
Morning, or after a meal has settled (not on a full stomach)
Posture
Lying or seated
Eyes
Closed
Best for
People who feel "stuck in their head" — this drops attention into the body
Avoid if
You haven't yet got comfortable with deep belly breathing — do that first

Signs It's Working

  • The belly becomes warm or tingles softly
  • A sense of being "centred" — a felt geographical pull toward the navel area
  • Thinking weakens; the body becomes the foreground

Common Pitfalls

  • Forcing belly breathing in a way that strains the diaphragm — let it deepen on its own
  • Looking for the "meeting point" as a precise anatomical spot — it is felt, not located
  • Skipping straight to this technique without trying Techniques 1 and 2 first

Questions From The Book

Why has my breathing become shallow as I grew up? What blocks belly breathing?

Implied throughout Chapter 3 · "Breath — A Bridge to the Universe"

Osho's answer, in essence: a child breathes with the whole belly, naturally. Adults breathe shallowly because deep breathing reaches the navel — and the navel is near the sex centre. Civilisation's discomfort with sex has, generation after generation, conditioned the breath to stay above the chest. To recover this technique, you also recover a body that is not afraid of itself.

Watch a sleeping baby: the whole abdomen rises and falls. Watch a stressed adult at a desk: the breath barely moves the collarbones. The sutra is asking you to become, again, the way you began.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Where two opposite movements meet, neither exists. The sutra is using breath as a way to point you to a place in the body where the dualities you live in (in/out, give/take, expand/contract) cancel — and what remains is undifferentiated awareness.

A modern analogy: Where two opposing waves cross in a tank, the water at the crossing point becomes momentarily still. That stillness is not absence of energy — it is two energies in perfect balance.

Why neuroscience cares: Slow diaphragmatic breathing measurably increases vagal tone, lowers heart rate, and shifts the autonomic balance toward parasympathetic dominance. The "warm centred feeling" practitioners describe lines up with measurable physiology, not just metaphor.

EssenceThe deepest stillness in the body is not where nothing is happening — it is where two opposites are perfectly cancelling.

Sutra 4 of 112
Category: Breath · Tags: stoppage · universal pause · surrender · advanced
Or, when breath is all out (up) and stopped of itself, or all in (down) and stopped — in such universal pause, one's small self vanishes. This is difficult only for the impure.

In Plain English

Sometimes, naturally, the breath stops at full out or full in — not because you held it, but because it just stops. The sutra says: in that moment, the sense of being a separate "you" can drop. The phrase "difficult only for the impure" means: the resistance is not technical, it is psychological. Anything you cling to will refuse this pause.

How to Practice

Note from the source: Osho is unusually direct about this technique — he says, "It is difficult for you; you cannot practice it. But you can feel it sometimes suddenly." The pause it speaks of cannot be manufactured by sitting down to do it; it arrives in shock-moments (a near-accident, a sudden stunning sight) when breath naturally stops. So this is less a routine and more an alertness.

  1. Build a foundation in Techniques 1–3 first. Those train you to recognise breath-gaps when they appear.
  2. Through the day, watch for moments when something stops you — beauty, shock, surprise, sudden silence. In those moments breath often pauses by itself.
  3. When you notice it has paused, don't grab. Don't extend it by holding. Don't rush back to inhale. Just remain in whatever the pause is.
  4. If, while sitting in Technique 1, the breath stops longer than usual on its own — that is this technique arriving. Stay alert; do nothing.
  5. "Difficult only for the impure" — Osho explains this is not a moral judgement. Whatever you cling to (a thought, an identity, a plan) refuses to let go in the pause. Notice what refuses.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Advanced — arrives, not chosen
Duration
Whenever it happens; not scheduled
Best time
Any moment that interrupts you
Posture
Whatever you happen to be in
Eyes
Open or closed — whichever they are
Best for
Practitioners with at least a few months in Techniques 1–3
Avoid if
You have anxiety around breath-holding, or sleep apnoea

Signs It's Working

Osho doesn't give a checklist for this technique. The one criterion he names is below.

  • The pause you didn't take begins to deepen on its own — the body holds you in it longer than usual
  • In the pause, the sense of being a separate "you" briefly thins or vanishes

Common Pitfalls

  • Trying to do this technique. Osho is explicit: it cannot be practised deliberately. Treating it as a routine produces nothing.
  • Holding the breath. This kills the whole technique. The pause is given, not taken.
  • Panicking when the next breath is slow to return — the body breathes itself; trust it
  • Trying this advanced technique before Techniques 1–3 feel natural

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: When breath stops, "you" stop. Identity is not a stable thing — it is a process refreshed every breath. Catch the gap and the process is briefly suspended. What remains is the simplest thing there is.

If this feels too advanced — the simpler version: Try Technique 1 (gap after in-breath) for a few weeks first. The natural stoppage of Technique 4 will start arriving on its own, without you having to do anything special.

About "difficult only for the impure": Not a moral judgement. It means: whatever you are still gripping — a resentment, a plan, an identity — will refuse to let go in the pause. Nothing to fix, just notice what refuses, and let it refuse.

EssenceWhen breath stops, the small self stops. That is the door. It opens by itself for those who do not push.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 5 · Sutras 5–9 · 5 techniques

Five Techniques of Attentiveness

Osho's framing

Osho opens with Pythagoras, who was refused entry to an Egyptian mystery school until he completed forty days of fasting and conscious breathing. Afterwards he said his centre had moved from intellect to feeling - from concept to actual experience. The same Indian method now appears in Shiva's first sutra: focus attention between the eyebrows, on the dormant third eye (the pineal gland). Osho says this point is magnetic - it pulls attention into itself effortlessly. Once attention settles there, you stop being identified with anger or desire and start watching thoughts pass like clouds in the sky. As he puts it

For the third eye, attention is food. It has been hungry for lives and lives.

— Osho
Sutra 5 of 112
Category: Breath · Tags: third eye · visualisation · light · advanced
Attention between eyebrows, let mind be before thought. Let form fill with breath essence to the top of the head and there shower as light.

In Plain English

Focus attention at the point between the eyebrows (the third eye). Become aware of the subtle essence of the breath — not the air, but the aliveness it carries. Imagine that essence rising up the head, reaching the crown, and pouring back over you as light. This is the technique that, according to Osho, was given to Pythagoras in Egypt; he carried it back and seeded all Western mysticism with it.

How to Practice

  1. Sit upright. Eyes gently closed.
  2. Bring both eyes to look softly toward the middle of the forehead — the spot between the eyebrows. Don't strain. The eyes will lock there if you've found the spot.
  3. Stay focused there until thoughts begin to feel like clouds passing in front of you, rather than things you are inside of.
  4. Now feel the breath as more than air — feel its aliveness. (Tantra calls this prana, modern terms might be vitality or charge.)
  5. Imagine that aliveness rising from the breath up to the crown of the head.
  6. At the crown, let it shower back down over you as soft light.
  7. 20–30 minutes. Best done with a stable foundation in Techniques 1–4.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Advanced
Duration
20–30 min
Best time
Pre-dawn or dusk — lower ambient light helps the inner light experience
Posture
Seated, spine erect
Eyes
Closed, looking softly upward
Best for
Those drawn to visualisation, devotion, or "energy-body" work
Avoid if
You have a history of psychosis, dissociation, or migraines triggered by upward eye gaze

Signs It's Working

  • Eyes lock at the third-eye point and feel reluctant to move
  • Thoughts appear as separate from you, like film on a screen — the witness arises
  • A sensation of warmth, pressure, or light at the forehead or crown
  • Quietude that feels textured, not empty

Common Pitfalls

  • Forcing the eyes to point upward — strain replaces focus
  • Practising with an unsettled mind — the sutra's old commentary insists on purity first because at the third eye, imagination becomes unusually potent
  • Confusing visualised light with attainment — light is a sign of energy moving, not the goal

Questions From The Book

What is the third eye, scientifically? Is the pineal gland really involved?

Discussed in Chapter 5, "Five Techniques of Attentiveness"

Osho identifies the third eye with the pineal gland — a small endocrine gland between the two eyes. He calls it not blind, just closed; latent. He claims the gland actively absorbs attention: focus on it and your eyes lock in spite of you. Modern science has since confirmed the pineal regulates melatonin and circadian rhythm; whether it has the deeper functions Tantra assigns to it remains outside the scope of physiology.

Either way, the practice is the same: it is the act of focusing attention there that matters, not the metaphysics of why.

Why do practitioners of psychic techniques often have tense, fearful eyes?

Chapter 22, "Unblocking the Third Eye"

Osho's answer, in essence: hypnotists, mesmerists and forcing-the-third-eye practitioners use the eyes as a tool of will. The eye becomes hardened. In genuine practice the eye softens, the gaze becomes inward, and tension gives way to a kind of liquid attention. If your eyes feel tense or scared after this technique, you're forcing it. Stop. Return to Technique 1 for a week. Come back gentler.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Where attention goes, energy follows. The technique uses three layers stacked: a precise location (third eye), a quality of awareness (witnessing thought rather than being inside it), and a guided imagination (essence rising and showering as light). Each amplifies the next.

A modern analogy: Like sunlight through a magnifying glass — focused attention concentrates a diffuse field. Add visualisation and you direct that concentration. The "light shower" is your energy field reorganising under directed attention.

If this feels too advanced — the simpler version: Just Step 1 alone. Sit, close eyes, look softly at the spot between the eyebrows. Stay there for 10 minutes. Skip the visualisation. Build the focus first; the rest comes later.

Caveat: The book treats this as the technique that needs the most groundwork. Pythagoras did 40 days of fasting and breath training before he was allowed to practice it. Don't start here. End up here.

EssenceWhere attention goes, energy follows. Where energy gathers, light is felt. The third eye is not a place — it is what you see with when the two eyes stop fighting for the picture.

Sutra 6 of 112
Category: Breath · Tags: daily life · integration · awareness · beginner
When in worldly activity, keep attention between two breaths, and so practising, in a few days be born anew.

In Plain English

Take Technique 1 off the cushion. While doing ordinary things — washing dishes, walking, waiting in line — keep a small thread of attention on the gap between your breaths. The sutra promises that a few days of this is enough to start a kind of inner rebirth.

How to Practice

  1. Pick one routine activity to start with: brushing teeth, walking to work, washing dishes.
  2. While doing it, keep a soft, background awareness on the gap after the in-breath.
  3. Don't let it interrupt the activity. The activity continues; the awareness just rides alongside.
  4. Each time you forget, return — no self-blame, no restart. The next gap is one breath away.
  5. Over a few weeks, expand to more activities.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner
Duration
Continuous, woven through daily life
Best time
Always — that is the point
Posture
Whatever you are already in
Eyes
Open
Best for
Householders, busy people who can't add a long sitting practice
Avoid if
You are operating heavy machinery in a way that requires full external focus — in those moments just do the task

Signs It's Working

  • Routine tasks start feeling oddly fresh, even pleasant
  • You catch yourself less often in autopilot
  • Reactions to small irritations soften — there is now a tiny gap between trigger and response
  • The sense of "rushing through" the day weakens

Common Pitfalls

  • Trying to maintain it as background only on the cushion — Osho says this technique "has to be done continuously"; the whole point is that it goes with you everywhere
  • Using it to escape unpleasant tasks — this is integration, not retreat
  • Treating forgetting as failure — remembering is the practice; forgetting just makes remembering possible
  • Reducing the "drama/play" framing to plain mindfulness — in Osho's reading, this technique turns life itself into an acting, a play in which you remain centred in being while the body-and-personality performs the part

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Most "spiritual practice" stays on the cushion and dies the moment life resumes. This technique closes that gap. It says: the gap between two breaths is portable. Take it with you.

A modern analogy: Like keeping a song playing softly in your head while you talk. The song doesn't stop the talking; the talking doesn't stop the song. Two layers, simultaneously.

Why this might be the most important breath technique for modern people: Daily life is now denser than at any point in history. Most of us cannot find a real 30-minute silence. This sutra says: you don't need to. You need 30 micro-pauses spread through the day. The arithmetic still works.

EssenceMeditation isn't where you sit. It's what stays awake while you live.

Sutra 7 of 112
Category: Breath · Tags: sleep · dream · death · advanced
With intangible breath in centre of forehead, as this reaches heart at the moment of sleep, have direction over dreams and over death itself.

In Plain English

A pre-sleep practice. Lying in bed, place attention at the forehead. Imagine a subtle thread of breath descending from there to the heart. If you can stay aware as you fall asleep, the sutra promises lucid dreaming and, eventually, conscious passage through death.

How to Practice

  1. Lie in bed at the actual moment of going to sleep — not some other time.
  2. Eyes closed. Body settled.
  3. Place attention at the centre of the forehead.
  4. Imagine an "intangible breath" — a thread of fine awareness — descending slowly from forehead to heart.
  5. Stay with the thread. Let it deepen, slow, and reach the heart at the very moment sleep takes you.
  6. If you wake, that's fine. Notice. Try again the next night.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Advanced
Duration
Until sleep arrives (5–20 min)
Best time
The actual moment of falling asleep
Posture
Lying in your usual sleeping position
Eyes
Closed
Best for
People who have an existing waking practice; anyone interested in lucid dreaming
Avoid if
You have insomnia — this technique can paradoxically delay sleep at first

Signs It's Working

  • Vivid hypnagogic imagery (the swirl of pictures right before sleep)
  • A few seconds of recognising "I am falling asleep" without losing the recognition
  • Increasingly clear, often lucid, dreams

Common Pitfalls

  • Becoming too alert — the practice is gentle, not active. If it wakes you up, soften.
  • Being attached to having lucid dreams — that ambition itself blocks the technique
  • Practising occasionally — this one rewards consistency; nightly is the dose

Questions From The Book

What other factors help one stay conscious while dreaming?

Chapter 6, "Devices to Transcend Dreaming"

Osho's answer, in essence: three things support lucid dreaming. (1) Falling asleep with awareness — this technique. (2) Eating light at dinner — a heavy stomach pulls awareness into digestion, not consciousness. (3) Sleeping in a particular way: facing east, head supported, body relaxed. Most importantly: drop the ambition to "achieve" lucidity. Awareness can ride into sleep; ambition cannot.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: The moment of falling asleep is a real event with a real signature in the brain — a transition through hypnagogia, the gateway between waking and dream. The sutra is asking you to keep one thread of awareness alive across that transition.

The death claim: Tantra views the falling-asleep moment and the dying moment as structurally identical — both are an unwinding of the mind from the body. Practise the small one nightly, the sutra implies, and the big one will be familiar.

Why neuroscience cares: Modern lucid dreaming research (Stephen LaBerge, Voss et al.) confirms that pre-sleep attention to a chosen object substantially raises the probability of dream lucidity. The forehead-to-heart visualisation here is essentially a focused MILD-style induction with an added somatic element.

EssenceThe moment between waking and sleep is identical to the moment of death. Catch one nightly — you have rehearsed for the other.

Sutra 8 of 112
Category: Breath · Tags: devotion · junctions · bhakti · intermediate
With utmost devotion, centre on the two junctions of breath, and know the knower.

In Plain English

Same two turning points as Technique 2 — but with one ingredient added: devotion. Whatever you most love or revere — the divine, your teacher, the universe, life itself — let that love be the colour of your attention. Devotion makes attention effortless. Then, in those two turning points, look for the one who is looking.

How to Practice

  1. Sit. Settle. Breath natural.
  2. Bring to mind whatever moves you most — a beloved figure, the open sky, the felt sense of life itself. Let the heart soften.
  3. Now, with that softness, watch the two turns of breath (in→out and out→in).
  4. At each turn, gently ask: who is noticing this turn?
  5. Don't answer in words. Let the question pull attention back to its source.
  6. 20–30 minutes.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
20–30 min
Best time
Whenever the heart is open — after music, after a good conversation, in nature
Posture
Seated
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Heart-centred or devotional types — bhaktas, lovers, artists
Avoid if
You are emotionally raw or in acute grief — let the rawness settle first

Signs It's Working

  • Attention requires no effort — the heart holds it
  • The "knower" question stops feeling intellectual and starts feeling near
  • Tears, sometimes, with no sadness behind them

Common Pitfalls

  • Performing devotion. Real devotion is found, not generated.
  • Turning "who is the knower?" into a riddle to solve — it is not a riddle, it is a direction

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Pure attention is hard. Loving attention is easy — you've experienced it whenever you've truly fallen for someone or something. The sutra hijacks that mechanism: take the natural focus that love already produces, and aim it at the breath's turning points, with the question "who is noticing?" riding on top.

A modern analogy: Falling in love makes you naturally attentive without trying. This technique uses that same engine, deliberately.

Why this is paired with Technique 2 in the book: Technique 2 is the same anatomy without the fuel. For some practitioners, dry attention is enough. For others, devotion is the carrier wave. The book offers both because people are different.

EssenceDevotion is concentrated love. Concentrated love is attention without effort.

Sutra 9 of 112
Category: Sleep & Dream · Tags: death-posture · stillness · shavasana
Lie down as dead. Enraged in wrath, stay so. Or stare without moving an eyelash. Or suck something and become the sucking.

In Plain English

Lie like a corpse — no movement. Feel as if dead. When the body is totally still and “dead,” consciousness separates from it. You discover you are not the body. The sutra offers four variations — death posture, frozen rage, unblinking stare, or total absorption in sucking — all creating such intense stillness or single-pointed focus that the self separates from the body and witnesses it from beyond.

How to Practice

  1. Prepare for bed as normal: Complete your evening routine. Brush teeth, dim lights, put away screens. When you lie down in bed, this is your practice. No special setup required — the technique IS the transition from waking to sleep.
  2. Lie flat on your back: Arms at your sides, palms up or down — whichever is natural. Legs slightly apart. This is shavasana, the corpse pose. Feel the bed supporting your full weight. Let gravity take everything.
  3. Decide to be dead: This is the crucial inner shift. You are not “relaxing” — you are DEAD. A dead body cannot move a finger, cannot scratch an itch, cannot adjust position. Make a firm inner commitment: “I will not move. I am dead.” This commitment must be total.
  4. Surrender all muscular control: Starting from the feet, consciously release every muscle group. Feet — dead. Calves — dead. Thighs — dead. Pelvis, belly, chest — dead. Arms, hands, fingers — dead. Neck, jaw, face, eyes — dead. Even the tongue — let it fall back, lifeless. Feel the entire body as a corpse lying on the bed.
  5. Do not respond to the body: The body will test you. An itch will arise. An urge to swallow. A desire to adjust position. A restless leg. DO NOT RESPOND. A dead body does not scratch. Let every sensation arise, peak, and pass WITHOUT ANY RESPONSE. This is the death posture — total non-responsiveness.
  6. Feel the separation: After 10–15 minutes of absolute stillness, something remarkable happens: consciousness begins to SEPARATE from the body. You feel aware, alert, present — but the body feels distant, foreign, not-you. You are witnessing the body rather than being it. This is the discovery: you are not the body.
  7. Maintain awareness through the sleep transition: As the body sinks deeper into its death-stillness, sleep will approach. The hypnagogic imagery will begin — flashes of light, random images, a feeling of floating. STAY AWARE through this transition. Don’t engage with the imagery, don’t follow it — just witness.
  8. Enter sleep consciously: If you can maintain awareness through the hypnagogic transition, you will enter sleep KNOWING you are entering sleep. The body sleeps; you remain. This is yoga nidra — conscious sleep. Even if you only achieve this for moments before unconsciousness takes over, those moments are transformative.
  9. The morning insight: Upon waking, notice: something was aware even during sleep. You may not remember it clearly, but there is a sense of continuity — of something that was present through the night even when “you” were gone. That something is the witness — what you truly are.
  10. Practise every night: Make this your nightly ritual. Over 21 days, the periods of conscious sleep will extend. Some nights you will succeed beautifully; other nights sleep will take you immediately. Both are fine. The practice accumulates. Eventually, conscious sleep becomes your natural state.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner-friendly
Duration
20–45 min (until sleep comes)
Posture
Lying flat on back, corpse pose
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Everyone; especially those who want to use bedtime as practice time; people interested in lucid dreaming; those who fear death

Signs It’s Working

  • The body feels increasingly distant, heavy, or numb after 10–15 minutes of stillness
  • A clear sense of being separate from the body — witnessing it rather than being it
  • Hypnagogic imagery arising with you AWARE of it rather than lost in it
  • Moments of knowing you are falling asleep while remaining conscious
  • Waking with a sense that awareness persisted through the night
  • Reduced fear of death — death feels like a familiar transition, not an unknown terror

Common Pitfalls

  • Moving even slightly — any movement resets the process; the commitment to stillness must be absolute
  • Trying too hard to stay awake — the goal is not insomnia but CONSCIOUS sleep; let sleep come, just maintain witness
  • Getting excited when the separation begins and thereby losing it — stay neutral, observing
  • Giving up after 5 minutes because “nothing is happening” — 15–20 minutes of total stillness is the minimum
  • Confusing physical relaxation with the death posture — relaxation still implies a living body; this technique requires feeling genuinely DEAD

Questions From The Book

Why does the sutra combine death posture with rage, staring, and sucking?

Chapter 66, "Lie Down As Dead"

Osho explains: all four variations share one principle — TOTALITY. Lie as dead TOTALLY. Be in rage TOTALLY (without acting on it). Stare TOTALLY without blinking. Suck TOTALLY until you become the sucking. The common thread is total absorption that leaves no energy for the ego. When absorption is total, the self drops. The death posture is the most accessible version for sleep practice — but any total absorption creates the same gap.

Is this the same as yoga nidra?

Chapter 66

Osho says: yoga nidra as taught today is a systematized version of this ancient sutra. The principle is identical — maintain awareness while the body enters sleep. Traditional yoga nidra uses body scanning and rotation of consciousness; this sutra uses the more direct method of simply being dead. Both arrive at the same place: conscious sleep, where the witness remains while the body-mind complex rests.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Sleep paralysis research shows that the body has a natural mechanism for disconnecting motor control during sleep (atonia). This technique deliberately invokes that mechanism through voluntary stillness. By refusing to move, you trigger the body’s sleep-entry protocols while maintaining cortical alertness. The result is a dissociation between body-state (sleeping) and awareness-state (alert) — proving experientially that consciousness is not dependent on bodily activity.

Lucid dreaming research: The Wake-Initiated Lucid Dream (WILD) technique in lucid dreaming research uses exactly this method: maintain awareness while the body falls asleep. Studies show that practitioners can reliably enter REM sleep with metacognitive awareness intact. This 5,000-year-old sutra is the original WILD technique.

One-line essenceLie as dead. Do not move. When the body is truly still, consciousness separates — and you discover you were never the body at all.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 7 · Sutras 10–12 · 3 techniques

Techniques to Put You at Ease

Osho's framing

Osho frames these techniques by saying we all have a centre but live off it, and this off-centre living is the root of every tension. He sketches four types: the "normal" person with a fixed identity, who is really dead inside; the artist, liquid and swinging between madness and ecstasy; the permanently mad, gone outside completely; and the Buddha, who has returned home. The Buddha still eats, sleeps and feels heat, but always in this moment, with no projection into tomorrow. Wherever there is becoming, there is tension; wherever there is pure being, there is ease.

The normal man is really a dead man, living between these two points.

— Osho
Sutra 10 of 112
Category: Tantric Union · Tags: while · caressed · sweet · princess
While being caressed, sweet princess, enter the caress as everlasting life.

In Plain English

Love is the easiest natural doorway out of the busy mind, because in a real moment of love the past and the future both disappear. Osho’s instruction is simple: when you are loving — caressing, kissing, embracing, holding — do not stand back as the lover. Disappear into the loving itself. Don’t be the one who is kissing; be the kiss.

How to Practice

  1. Choose a moment of real, unhurried physical or emotional closeness with someone you love — a long hug, a caress, a kiss, holding hands. (You can also use any moment of pure tenderness: stroking a child’s hair, a beloved pet, even resting your forehead against a tree.)
  2. Before the moment begins, drop every expectation. Don’t announce to yourself, “Now I am going to meditate.” Don’t bring purpose. Osho is clear: love only happens when there is no purpose, no “in order to.”
  3. As the touch begins, let your attention drop into the touching itself, not into the one who is giving or the one who is receiving. The contact is bigger than both of you. Move your awareness into it.
  4. Forget who is touching whom. The hand and the skin both dissolve into the caress. Osho: “While caressing your lover or beloved, become the caress. While kissing, do not be the kisser or the kissed — be the kiss.”
  5. Don’t watch the experience from inside your head. The eyes are not seeing — love is seeing. The hands are not moving — love is moving. The heart is not beating — love is beating.
  6. As you go deeper, you may notice past and future have quietly fallen away. There is no thought of “what next,” no memory tugging you back. Only this. Only now. This is what Shiva calls everlasting life — not endless time, but the eternity that exists only in the present.
  7. Stay in this state for as long as it lasts on its own. Even a few seconds is the whole technique. Don’t try to stretch it — the moment you try, the mind has come back and the love has become an effort again.
  8. When the mind returns (it will, with thoughts like “is this working?”), don’t fight it. Notice it without struggle, and the next time a moment of love arises, drop in again.
  9. Practise this in any honest moment of love — with a partner, a friend, a parent, a child, a pet, music that moves you, a meal eaten with full attention, the sky at sunset. The instruction is always the same: become the loving, not the lover.
  10. Don’t turn this into a marriage with the technique. Osho specifically warns: the moment you say “I will repeat this every day at 7 p.m.,” you have killed it. Stay open. Each moment of love is new.

Osho’s Key Insight

Man has a center, but he lives off of it – off the center. That creates an inner tension, a constant turmoil, anguish. You are not where you should be; you are not at your right balance. You are off balance, and this being off balance, off center, is the base of all mental tensions. If it becomes too much, you go mad. A madman is one who has gone out of himself completely. The enlightened man i...

Source: 7. Techniques to Put You at Ease, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 11 of 112
Category: The Senses · Tags: stop · doors · senses · feeling
Stop the doors of the senses when feeling the creeping of an ant. Then.

In Plain English

Use any small body sensation — an itch, a pain, the cold of a sheet, a mosquito’s hum, a creeping ant — as your trigger. The moment you notice it, slam shut all five sense-doors at once and become like a stone, like a statue, like a corpse. With the doors closed the sensation cannot reach you, and you are thrown back into your own centre. Osho says “the creeping of an ant” is only an example — anything will do.

How to Practice

  1. Sit or lie down. The technique can be done in bed, on a chair, on a meditation cushion — anywhere. You don’t need a special posture.
  2. Wait for any small body sensation to appear on its own — an itch, a slight ache, the press of fabric, the temperature of the air, a heartbeat you suddenly hear, an insect on your skin. Don’t manufacture one. Use whatever is already there.
  3. The instant you notice the sensation, close your eyes and treat yourself as if you were blind. There is no “seeing” function in you any more.
  4. At the same time, treat your ears as if you were deaf. There is no hearing.
  5. Do the same with smell, taste and touch. With each sense, simply assume the door is shut. You have five locked doors and nothing can come in.
  6. Stop your breath for a single moment. Osho is direct here: “Stop breathing for a single moment: all your senses will be closed.” Let the suspension of breath suspend the senses.
  7. Now imagine your whole body has turned to stone — a statue, dead weight. You cannot move a finger. Even if you wanted to open your eyes you couldn’t. Even if you wanted to lift your hand, it is granite.
  8. Hold this stone-state. Don’t try to “feel” anything. Actively refuse to receive anything from outside. The world is gone. The body itself is gone — because the body, says Osho, is part of the world, not part of you.
  9. Now watch the original sensation. Osho describes what happens: first it begins to feel distant, as if it’s happening to someone else; then it recedes further; then it disappears altogether. Pain, itch, cold — whatever it was — melts away.
  10. When all sensations have dropped, you are alone, far inside, at your own centre. Stay there as long as the state lasts on its own.
  11. Come back gently when the state ends. Open one sense at a time — first hearing, then touch, then sight.
  12. You don’t need an ant. Use this anywhere — in pain, in illness, lying in bed unable to sleep, in physical discomfort, on a long flight. Osho gives the example of a friend with a bedridden injury who used this for thirty minutes and the severe pain disappeared completely.

Osho’s Key Insight

Man has a center, but he lives off of it – off the center. That creates an inner tension, a constant turmoil, anguish. You are not where you should be; you are not at your right balance. You are off balance, and this being off balance, off center, is the base of all mental tensions. If it becomes too much, you go mad. A madman is one who has gone out of himself completely. The enlightened man i...

Source: 7. Techniques to Put You at Ease, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 12 of 112
Category: The Senses · Tags: seat · yourself · weightless · beyond
When on a bed or a seat, let yourself become weightless, beyond mind.

In Plain English

Weight belongs to the body, not to you. Sit in a way that is least pulled by gravity, then go on feeling “I am weightless” until the body’s heaviness drops away. When the weight is gone, you discover you are not the body — and the mind, which also has weight, falls away with it. What remains is pure consciousness, weightless.

How to Practice

  1. Sit on the floor, not on a chair or sofa. If possible, on natural ground — earth, grass, wood — rather than concrete or tile. Naked is even better, says Osho, because clothes add weight and barrier; a thin natural cloth is fine if naked is impractical.
  2. Sit in siddhasana or any cross-legged posture (the “Buddha posture”). Hands rest one on top of the other in your lap. Legs locked into the body’s base. If full lotus isn’t possible, simple cross-legged is fine. What matters is a closed circuit (legs and hands locked) and a straight spine.
  3. Keep the spine straight. This is the position with the smallest body-area in contact with gravity — the less surface, the less pull.
  4. Close your eyes.
  5. Find the centre. Lean a little to the right and feel the pull of gravity on that side. Come back. Lean left, feel it. Forward, feel it. Backward, feel it.
  6. Settle at the exact balance-point where you feel the least weight. The body should feel as though it is suspended in its own posture, not pressing in any direction.
  7. Now begin to feel: “I am weightless.” Don’t analyse. Don’t argue with the part of the mind that says “but I can feel my body.” Just keep feeling weightlessness, again and again.
  8. If you don’t feel it at first, that is normal. Osho says: “Go on feeling, feeling, feeling, a moment comes when suddenly you realize you are weightless.” The body is a hypnosis you have been living in. You are de-hypnotising yourself.
  9. As the weight begins to drop, the body starts to disappear from your awareness. Then the mind itself begins to fall away — Osho says the mind also has weight; only consciousness is weightless.
  10. Stay in this weightless state. There is nothing to do here. You have stepped out of the body and out of the mind for as long as the feeling lasts.
  11. Helpful hint from Osho: you naturally feel light when happy and heavy when sad. So practise this on a day you feel good — happiness is itself a doorway into weightlessness. Don’t attempt it when grief or fatigue is pressing you down.
  12. Stick with it for several days before judging. Osho’s general rule for all 112: try one technique for at least a few days, don’t flit from one to the next.

Osho’s Key Insight

Man has a center, but he lives off of it – off the center. That creates an inner tension, a constant turmoil, anguish. You are not where you should be; you are not at your right balance. You are off balance, and this being off balance, off center, is the base of all mental tensions. If it becomes too much, you go mad. A madman is one who has gone out of himself completely. The enlightened man i...

Source: 7. Techniques to Put You at Ease, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 9 · Sutras 13–14 · 2 techniques

Techniques for Centering

Osho's framing

Osho says we are all born with a centre but completely forget it. We can live without knowing it, but not without it. Without that knowing, life feels rootless - what Sartre called "thrownness," the alien sense that one has been hurled into a foreign world. If you live as thrown, anguish follows; if you sense yourself as grown organically, like a tree, bliss follows. A child first lives rooted in the navel (the hara), then drifts to the heart, then to the head, losing the original ground. These centering sutras are devices to take consciousness back.

Bliss is the result of an organic unity with the universe, and anguish the result of an enmity.

— Osho
Sutra 13 of 112
Category: Centering · Tags: concentration · single-point · body · piercing
Or, imagine the five-coloured circles of the peacock tail to be your five senses in illimitable space. Now let their beauty melt within. Similarly, at any point in space or on a wall — until the point dissolves. Then your wish for another comes true.

In Plain English

Choose any small point — a dot on the wall, a candle flame, or even an imagined point inside your body — and pour your total attention into it until the point dissolves and only awareness remains.

How to Practice

  1. Five-colour version (Osho's first instruction): Imagine the five colours of the peacock tail filling all of space, then drawing inward and meeting at a single point inside — preferably the navel/hara, the body's natural centre.
  2. Or, dot version: Sit facing a blank wall. Place a small dot at eye-level (or use an existing mark).
  3. Do not blink. Osho is explicit on this — "do not blink your eyes, because blinking gives a space for the mind to move again." If your eyes water, let them water. If they begin to close, let them close softly while attention stays on the point.
  4. Let all peripheral vision blur. Only the point exists.
  5. Eventually the point may seem to disappear, expand, or merge with you.
  6. When the point dissolves, rest in the spacious awareness that remains.
  7. The five-colour version suits people sensitive to colour and inner imagery; the dot version suits extroverts who can't yet visualise within.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
15–30 min
Posture
Seated, upright
Eyes
Open (fixed gaze) or closed (inner point)
Best for
Strong-willed minds; those who like clear tasks

Signs It's Working

  • The point seems to shimmer or disappear
  • Peripheral vision fades naturally
  • A deep quiet descends, thought-free
  • Time distortion — 20 minutes feel like 5

Common Pitfalls

  • Blinking — Osho says do not. Bodhidharma is said to have torn off his own eyelids over this point. The pitfall is not the watering eye; the pitfall is the blink, which gives the mind a gap to slip back in.
  • Choosing too complex an object — the simpler the point, the better
  • Picking just any internal point — Osho specifically recommends the navel/hara if going inside
  • Expecting visions — the goal is dissolution of the point, not fireworks

Questions From The Book

Why does concentrating on a point lead to freedom rather than more narrowness?

Chapter 8, "Centering & Concentration"

Osho says: narrowing attention to a single point is like narrowing a river into a gorge — the energy intensifies until it explodes through. The point is a device; once concentration is total, the device falls away and what remains is unlimited awareness, because the mind that limits has been exhausted into silence.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Attention is normally scattered across thousands of micro-objects per second. Giving it one single object starves the default mode network of fuel. When the DMN goes quiet, you experience what meditators call "emptiness" — not blankness, but awareness without content.

Trataka parallel: This is essentially the yogic practice of trataka (steady gazing). Research shows it improves attention span, reduces anxiety, and can induce mild altered states even in beginners.

One-line essenceGive attention one point, and when the point dissolves, what remains is you — boundless.
Sutra 14 of 112
Category: Centering · Tags: place · whole · attention · nerve
Place your whole attention in the nerve, delicate as the lotus thread, in the center of your spinal column. In such be transformed.

In Plain English

Inside the spine, says Osho, runs a non-physical “silver cord” — a current of life-energy as fine as a lotus thread. You cannot find it in a dissection; you can only see it from the inside, in deep concentration. The technique is to sit with a perfectly straight spine and use visualisation to find this inner thread. When you do, the body fills with light from within.

How to Practice

  1. Before you start, look at a picture of the human spine — from a textbook, online, anywhere. Osho recommends this in plain words: study the structure of the body so you know what you are looking for. (Skip this if you already have a clear image of the spine in your mind.)
  2. Sit with a fully straight, erect spine. This is non-negotiable for this technique. If the spine bends, the thread cannot be seen. Cross-legged on the floor is best; a firm chair with a straight back is acceptable.
  3. Close your eyes. Take a few quiet breaths to settle. Don’t do any preliminary practice — this technique is not breath-based.
  4. Visualise your spine from the inside, as if you were looking at it from within your own body. Start at the base, move up, all the way to the top of the neck. See the bones, the vertebrae, the column standing straight inside you.
  5. It will feel like imagination at first. That’s expected. Don’t fight that. Osho is explicit: keep going, and over time the imagined spine is replaced by the real one as it appears to inner sight. (You may even discover your inner spine looks different from the textbook picture — if so, drop the picture and follow what you actually see.)
  6. Now move your attention into the very middle of the spinal column. Right in the centre, running top to bottom, visualise a single fine thread — as delicate as a lotus filament, like a silver cord. This thread is not physical; it is an energy current.
  7. Place your whole attention on this thread. Not on the bones around it — on the thread itself. Don’t pull, don’t push, don’t expand it. Just see it and stay with it.
  8. Keep returning. The mind will drift — bring it back to the thread. The mind will say “there’s nothing there” — ignore it, keep concentrating. The mind will dissolve only when you refuse to obey it.
  9. As concentration deepens, the thread begins to feel real, not imagined. You may sense a current, a vibration, a quiet luminosity along the spine. This is normal and is what Osho calls the thread becoming visible.
  10. Stay with the thread as long as you can. Sessions of 20–40 minutes are useful. The transformation Shiva mentions is not sudden — it builds with continued practice.
  11. Caution from Osho: this technique is body-oriented and well suited to people who feel themselves to be primarily their body. If you are very mind-oriented, start with one of the simpler concentration techniques first — this one is less direct for you.
  12. One sign that the technique is working over weeks of practice: a quality of inner light begins to be felt along the spine and through the body. Auras become luminous from inside — this, says Osho, is why enlightened beings are traditionally painted with halos. They are not decoration; they are seen.

Osho’s Key Insight

Man is born with a center, but he remains completely oblivious of it. Man can live without knowing his center, but man cannot be without a center. The center is the link between man and existence; it is the root. You may not know it, knowledge is not essential for the center to be, but if you do not know it you will lead a life that is rootless – as if rootless. You will not feel any ground, yo...

Source: 9. Techniques for Centering, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 11 · Sutras 15–17 · 3 techniques

Techniques to Penetrate the Inner

Osho's framing

Osho diagnoses the basic human condition as living on the circumference of a circle that has no centre. We have a "without" but no real "within" - even when physically alone, we are inwardly crowded with images of other people. So all our talk of going inside is hollow: the words have meaning only intellectually, because we have never actually been there. These three techniques - closing the senses with the hands, dropping into the heart, hovering in the middle of opposites - are devices to pierce that crowded circumference and finally enter.

Man is as if he is a circle without a centre.

— Osho
Sutra 15 of 112
Category: Centering · Tags: head · seal · sound · inner listening
Closing the seven openings of the head with your hands, a space between your eyes becomes all-inclusive.

In Plain English

Block the ears, eyes, nostrils, and mouth with your fingers. With all outer input sealed, attention naturally falls inward to the space between the eyes — the third eye area — and a vast inner world opens.

How to Practice

Note from the source: Osho is unusually emphatic that this technique "is not to be practiced; only suddenly is it helpful." Repeating it daily as a routine, he says, turns it into a mechanical habit and produces nothing. The instruction below is therefore for the rare, sudden use of the gesture — not a daily breath-practice.

  1. Reserve this technique for a moment of inner urgency — when you feel cornered, when the mind is unbearably noisy, when "ordinary practice" cannot reach. Then suddenly close all seven openings.
  2. Thumbs into the ears. Index fingers lightly over closed eyelids. Middle fingers closing the nostrils. Ring fingers above the upper lip and little fingers below the lower lip, sealing the mouth.
  3. The breath stops. All seven openings are closed; you cannot breathe. This is the technique. Don't fight the suffocation immediately.
  4. Osho's exact instruction: "When you feel suffocated, go on — unless it becomes absolutely unbearable." Push to your edge. Built-in safety: if you ever go unconscious, the hands fall loose by themselves and breath resumes.
  5. In that suffocation moment the breath/thought association breaks. Attention is thrown to the space between the eyebrows, which becomes "all-inclusive."
  6. Release when the moment is genuinely unbearable. Then sit. Don't repeat tomorrow.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Advanced — sudden use only
Duration
Seconds, not minutes — until suffocation is genuine
Posture
Seated, upright
Eyes
Closed (covered)
Best for
Moments of inner desperation, not regular practice
Caution
If you have heart, lung, blood-pressure or panic conditions, do not do this. Osho's "go on" assumes an otherwise healthy practitioner.

Signs It's Working

  • The mind genuinely stops — not slows, stops — in the moment of suffocation
  • The "space between the eyes" suddenly feels open and inclusive of everything

Common Pitfalls

  • Treating it as a daily pranayama — this is the most common modern error. The yogic Shanmukhi Mudra (which keeps the breath flowing softly through the nostrils) is a related but different practice. Osho's technique is not Shanmukhi Mudra.
  • Pressing too hard on the eyes — feather-light touch only
  • Releasing at the first hint of discomfort — the breakthrough is on the other side of suffocation, not before it
  • Practising it on a schedule — "if you practice it, then nothing will happen," says Osho

Questions From The Book

What is the inner sound one hears when the ears are sealed?

Chapter 9, "The Seven Openings"

Osho explains that the body is never silent — blood circulation, neural activity, the heartbeat all create sound. Normally drowned out by external noise, sealing the ears reveals this primal hum. Tantra calls it "anahad nad" — the unstruck sound. It is not mystical; it is the sound of your own aliveness.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Sensory deprivation, even partial, shifts attention from exteroception to interoception. The brain, starved of input, amplifies internal signals. The "third eye" sensation is the prefrontal cortex becoming aware of its own activity without the usual outward-facing data stream.

Float-tank parallel: Modern flotation therapy achieves similar states — reduced external input, amplified inner awareness, deep relaxation. This 5,000-year-old technique does it with just your ten fingers.

One-line essenceSeal the doors and the inner sky reveals itself — it was always there, just drowned out.
Sutra 16 of 112
Category: Centering · Tags: heart · senses · absorption · devotion
Blessed one, as senses are absorbed in the heart, reach the centre of the lotus.

In Plain English

Instead of attention flowing outward through the senses, reverse it: let seeing, hearing, touching, tasting and smelling all flow inward toward the heart. The heart is the lotus; the five senses are its petals. When the petals fold back into the centre, you reach the centre of the lotus — your own being.

How to Practice

  1. Sit quietly. Eyes closed.
  2. Pick one sense at a time and "fold it back" toward the heart. Touch a tree or another person from the heart, not the hand — Osho's example: "close your eyes, feel the touch coming from the heart."
  3. For hearing: listen to music or a voice while imagining you have no head — the sound enters directly through the chest. Osho is specific about this headless-listening image.
  4. For sight: open the eyes briefly and let what is seen "drop down" into the chest rather than be processed in the head.
  5. Run the same exercise for all five senses, one at a time, then together.
  6. Rest at the centre of the lotus — the heart receiving everything. Stay 20 minutes.
  7. Osho notes this technique is for people who are heart-oriented (feeling type, devotional). Once heart-centring is stable, attention naturally falls further to the navel/hara — the next centre below.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
20 min
Posture
Seated or lying down
Eyes
Closed or half-open
Best for
Feeling types; those with active senses; people who feel scattered

Signs It's Working

  • The chest feels warm, alive, or expanding
  • Sensory input feels less intrusive — you're receiving, not grasping
  • A sense of integration — everything belongs
  • Unexpected tenderness or gratitude

Common Pitfalls

  • Trying to suppress senses — you're not blocking them, you're redirecting them
  • Confusion between physical heart and heart centre — use the middle of the chest
  • Getting emotional and stopping — emotion is a sign of opening; let it pass

Questions From The Book

How does the heart "absorb" senses? Isn't awareness in the brain?

Chapter 9, "The Heart as Centre"

Osho explains that in the Eastern understanding, the heart is the true centre of being — not anatomically, but energetically. The brain processes, but the heart receives. When senses flow toward the heart, you shift from processing-mode to being-mode. The technique doesn't require physiological accuracy — it requires a felt shift in the direction of attention.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: The heart has its own neural network (~40,000 neurons). HeartMath research shows that "heart coherence" — a state where heart rhythms become smooth and ordered — correlates with reduced cortisol, increased DHEA, and improved emotional regulation. Directing attention to the heart promotes this coherence.

Polyvagal parallel: The vagus nerve connects the heart to the brain. When attention rests at the heart, vagal tone increases, triggering the parasympathetic "rest and connect" response.

One-line essenceThe senses scatter you outward. Gather them at the heart, and you come home.
Sutra 17 of 112
Category: Centering · Tags: middle · non-choosing · equanimity · balance
Unminding mind, keep in the middle — until.

In Plain English

Whenever you notice the mind leaning toward one side of a pair of opposites — like/dislike, success/failure, love/hate — don't choose either. Stay in the exact middle. Don't lean. Stay there "until" — until something opens.

How to Practice

  1. In daily life, watch for any moment you are choosing or preferring.
  2. Notice both poles: "I like this" and "I dislike that."
  3. Deliberately stay in neither. Don't suppress the preference — just don't follow it.
  4. Stay at the zero point, the fulcrum, between yes and no.
  5. In formal sitting: notice thoughts dividing reality into pairs. For each pair, rest in the middle.
  6. The word "until" means: don't set a time. Stay in the middle until something happens by itself.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Advanced
Duration
All day (or 20-min formal sitting)
Posture
Any — this is primarily a life-technique
Eyes
Open (daily life) or closed (sitting)
Best for
Over-thinkers; indecisive minds; those caught in judgment loops

Signs It's Working

  • A surprising spaciousness between thoughts
  • Situations that normally trigger reactivity feel neutral
  • A sense of freedom — "I don't have to choose"
  • Equanimity even amid chaos

Common Pitfalls

  • Confusing the middle with indifference — this is alert balance, not numbness
  • Using it to avoid decisions in practical life — this is an inner practice, not outer paralysis
  • Turning "not choosing" into a rule you rigidly follow — even that is a choice

Questions From The Book

Isn't the middle just fence-sitting? How is it different from cowardice?

Chapter 10, "The Middle Way"

Osho says: fence-sitting is confusion — the middle is clarity. The fence-sitter wants to choose but cannot. The one in the middle has seen that both sides are the same energy with different labels, and no longer needs to choose. It is not avoidance — it is transcendence. Buddha called it the "middle way" for this reason.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: The brain's evaluation system (anterior cingulate cortex) constantly categorises experience as approach/avoid. This sutra short-circuits that binary by refusing to activate either circuit. The result: the ACC quiets, and open monitoring awareness (associated with the PFC) takes over.

ACT parallel: In Acceptance and Commitment Therapy, "defusion" is the practice of noticing a thought without buying into it or pushing it away. This is the modern clinical version of "keep in the middle."

One-line essenceThe mind divides. You, the witness, don't need to take sides. Stay in the middle, and the mind falls silent.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 13 · Sutras 18–21 · 4 techniques

Inner Centering

Osho's framing

Osho explains that the body has two dimensions: outward through the senses, where consciousness meets matter, and inward through centering, where the same reality appears as non-matter. The seer is always behind the eyes; close the senses and the seer remains. But the inner centre can never be approached head-on. Like happiness in play or music, it is always a by-product - appearing only when you forget the goal and absorb yourself in some other act. These techniques therefore never tell you to seek the centre; they ask you to look lovingly at an object, sit on the buttocks, sway gently - and let the centre arrive on its own.

Whatsoever is beautiful, whatsoever is eternal is so delicate that if you try to grab it directly it is destroyed.

— Osho
Sutra 18 of 112
Category: Centering · Tags: look · lovingly · object · another
Look lovingly at some object. Do not go to another object. Here in the middle of the object – the blessing.

In Plain English

Pick one thing — a flower, a face, a candle, a stone, anything. Look at it not as a viewer but as a lover. Forget yourself completely. Want only its happiness, its flowering. Do not move on to a second thing. Stay with this one. When the looking has become love and the self has dissolved into concern for the other, the blessing comes — you are suddenly centred without ever having tried to be.

How to Practice

  1. Choose one object you will stay with for the whole session. A rose-flower is Osho’s favourite example, but anything will work — a candle flame, a tree, a beloved’s face, a small stone, even a car you love. The object doesn’t matter; the love does.
  2. Sit before the object at a comfortable distance. Eyes open. No special posture is required.
  3. First instruction (Osho’s exact words): “Forget yourself completely.” The “I” that is looking has to step back. Let the object fill your awareness so completely that you, the looker, become absent.
  4. Look at it not to study it but to love it. Let a feeling of warmth begin to flow from your chest toward the object. Don’t fake the feeling — let it arise naturally as you keep looking.
  5. Now hold a single thought (this is Osho’s specific instruction): “What can I do to help this flower flower more, to be more beautiful, more alive, more blissful?” Let this question be the only thought in you.
  6. Don’t actually try to do anything. The point is the feeling, the ache of caring, not the action. Whether or not you can “do” anything is irrelevant — what matters is that every fibre of you is leaning toward the object’s well-being.
  7. Do not let your eyes wander to other objects. Osho is firm on this: “Do not go to another object. Remain with one.” The whole world should fall away — only this one thing remains in your consciousness.
  8. You will reach a strange point: the wish to make this thing more beautiful, more happy, will collide with the realisation that there is nothing you can actually do. This helplessness is the doorway. Don’t resist it. Stay in it.
  9. In that helplessness the mind has nothing to chew on. Self-centred thought stops. There is no “I” thinking, only the silent looking and the silent caring. You are empty.
  10. This is the moment Shiva calls “the blessing.” Don’t try to grab it. Just stay where you are. The bliss will come on its own, as a by-product of the self’s absence.
  11. Stay as long as you can. When the session ends, don’t break it sharply. Look down, sit quietly, then go on with the day.
  12. Practise with the same object, or with the same kind of object (always a flower, always a candle, always a face), so that mood-shifts of attention don’t pull you. Osho says: love itself is the greatest method — if you can do this, no other technique is needed.

Osho’s Key Insight

The human body is a mysterious mechanism. Its working is two-dimensional. In order to go to the outside, your consciousness goes through the senses to meet the world, to meet matter. But this is only one dimension of your body’s functions. Your body has another dimension also: it leads you in. If consciousness goes out, then whatsoever you know is matter; if consciousness goes in, then whatsoev...

Source: 13. Inner Centering, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 19 of 112
Category: Centering · Tags: balance · body · buttocks · unsupported
Without support for feet or hands, sit only on the buttocks. Suddenly, the centering.

In Plain English

Sit on the floor with only your buttocks touching the ground — legs lifted, arms lifted or free. The moment you find physical balance, the inner centre reveals itself. The body's search for equilibrium becomes awareness's search for stillness.

How to Practice

Note from the source: Osho describes a different physical setup than the boat-pose this page originally suggested. The sutra word "buttocks" refers to weight-bearing — sit in a normal seated meditation posture (lotus, half-lotus, siddhasana, or simply cross-legged) where your weight rests on the buttocks alone, with the legs folded and the arms not bracing. The technique is then a balance of weight between the two buttocks, not a v-sit.

  1. Prerequisite: Osho says the buttocks must first be made sensitive. Spend a week or two simply noticing them — against the bed, against a chair, while sitting. Most adults have lost feeling in this area. Without that sensitivity the technique cannot land.
  2. Sit on a firm, flat surface in your normal cross-legged posture. Spine upright. Hands resting on knees or in lap — not bearing weight.
  3. Close the eyes. Feel which buttock currently bears more of your weight. It is almost never even.
  4. Without moving externally, shift weight slightly to the lighter side. Then back. Find the exact balance where weight rests evenly between the two buttocks.
  5. The instant the balance is true — "suddenly, the centering" — awareness drops to the navel/hara. The mind stops because the body has nothing to hold against.
  6. Stay there. If you wobble out, find balance again. Each finding is a fresh centering.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner — once buttocks are sensitive
Duration
20–30 min
Posture
Cross-legged / lotus / siddhasana — not boat-pose
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Anyone who can sit cross-legged; especially good after some weeks of buttock-sensitisation

Signs It's Working

  • The moment of true balance is unmistakable — the mind goes quiet without effort
  • Awareness drops to the navel/hara on its own
  • The body feels light because it has stopped fighting itself

Common Pitfalls

  • Skipping the buttock-sensitivity prerequisite — without it, you have nothing to balance with
  • Treating "leaving legs and arms unsupported" as lift them off the floor (boat-pose) — Osho means: weight not borne by hands or feet, not lifted into the air
  • Holding the breath to stabilise — keep breathing normally
  • Looking for the centring outside the moment of balance — the centring is the moment of balance, nothing more

Questions From The Book

This seems like just a yoga pose. What's the spiritual dimension?

Chapter 10, "Balance & Centering"

Osho says: the difference between a yoga pose and this technique is intention. In yoga, you hold the pose for the body. Here, you use the body's search for balance as a mirror for consciousness. The moment the body finds centre, the mind involuntarily stops — because balance demands total presence. That total presence IS meditation.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Balancing requires real-time proprioceptive feedback that monopolises attention — leaving nothing for discursive thought. This is why balancing poses in yoga are inherently meditative. The sutra makes this explicit: use the physical demand of balance as a shortcut to mental stillness.

Flow-state parallel: Csikszentmihalyi's flow research shows that challenge-skill balance silences the inner critic. This pose creates that balance instantly — challenging enough to demand full attention, simple enough to not frustrate.

One-line essenceWhen the body must balance, the mind must be silent. Use the body's need as the mind's liberation.
Sutra 20 of 112
Category: Centering · Tags: movement · vehicle · rhythm · swaying
In a moving vehicle, by rhythmically swaying, experience. Or in a still vehicle, by letting yourself swing in slowing invisible circles.

In Plain English

Use the natural movement of a bus, train, or swing to let the body rock gently. Instead of bracing against the motion, surrender to it — let it carry awareness inward to the still axis around which the swaying happens.

How to Practice

  1. Sit in a moving bus, train, or on a swing. Close your eyes if safe.
  2. Let the body sway naturally with the motion — don't resist.
  3. Feel for the still point inside around which the swaying happens — the axis.
  4. Rest attention at that centre. Let the body keep moving, but you stay at the unmoving point.
  5. If in a still room, gently initiate small circular swaying yourself, then let the circles slow down until they stop — remain at the zero-point.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner
Duration
10–20 min (or any commute)
Posture
Seated in vehicle, or on a swing
Eyes
Closed (if safe) or half-closed
Best for
Commuters; restless bodies that can't sit still

Signs It's Working

  • The swaying feels effortless, almost pleasurable
  • A sense of a still "core" emerges distinct from the moving body
  • Time seems to slip — the commute feels shorter
  • The mind quiets without effort

Common Pitfalls

  • Forcing large movements — subtle rocking is more effective
  • Getting motion-sick by closing eyes on a winding road — choose smooth transit
  • Intellectualising the "centre" instead of feeling it physically

Questions From The Book

Can movement really lead to stillness?

Chapter 9, "Techniques for Centering"

Osho explains: stillness is not the opposite of movement — it is the axis of movement. A wheel moves, but the hub remains still. The sutra uses physical motion to reveal the hub — your own centre that never moves, no matter what the body does.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Your vestibular system constantly computes a still reference frame even while the body is in motion. This sutra makes that computation conscious — you feel the still frame directly, not just compute it unconsciously.

Modern parallel: Rocking has been shown to synchronise brain oscillations and promote sleep (Current Biology, 2019). The centering aspect works similarly — rhythmic motion entrains attention inward.

One-line essenceMovement is the wheel. You are the hub. Find the hub, and motion becomes meditation.
Sutra 21 of 112
Category: Centering · Tags: pain · body · awareness · doorway
Pierce some part of your nectar-filled form with a pin, and gently enter the piercing — and attain to the inner purity.

In Plain English
