When you feel a physical pain or sensation — a headache, a cramp, an itch — don't avoid it. Instead, pour your total awareness directly into its centre. Enter it. In the centre of pain there is no pain — only energy.

How to Practice

  1. Whenever a pain or strong sensation arises in the body, pause.
  2. Don't resist, don't try to fix it. Just turn full attention toward it.
  3. Enter the sensation as if you were diving into its exact centre.
  4. Notice: at the very centre, the quality changes — from "pain" to pure intensity or vibration.
  5. Rest there. Let the energy be — without labelling it as pain.
  6. Alternatively (not recommended for beginners): lightly pinch the skin and meditate on the sensation's centre.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Advanced
Duration
As long as the sensation lasts
Posture
Any
Eyes
Closed
Best for
People with chronic pain; stoic temperaments; those who learn through intensity
Caution
Not for acute injuries or medical emergencies

Signs It's Working

  • The pain transforms into vibration, heat, or neutral intensity
  • The boundary between "me" and "pain" dissolves
  • Fear of the pain vanishes — replaced by curiosity
  • A profound sense of aliveness or purity at the centre

Common Pitfalls

  • Using this to bypass necessary medical attention — this is meditation, not treatment
  • About the pin: in the original sutra and Osho's commentary, the pin is literal — a friend pierces a sensitive spot, and you witness from inside. We do not recommend that on this page; the safer modern equivalent is to use a strong already-existing pain (a headache, a cramp) as your "pin." Do not deliberately injure yourself; the technique works equally well with sensations the body produces on its own.
  • Forcing attention into unbearable pain — start with mild discomfort and build tolerance
  • Tensing around the pain instead of softening into it

Questions From The Book

Isn't it masochistic to meditate on pain?

Chapter 10, "Pain & Awareness"

Osho responds: a masochist enjoys pain. This technique has nothing to do with enjoyment — it uses pain as a concentration device. Pain demands attention; the technique rides that demand inward. The "nectar-filled form" in the sutra means: even pain, when fully entered, reveals bliss at its core — because at the centre of any sensation is pure awareness, which is inherently blissful.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Pain neuroscience distinguishes between nociception (the signal) and suffering (the emotional interpretation). When you bring non-reactive attention to pain's centre, you decouple signal from suffering. The signal remains, but the "self" that suffers steps back. This is the basis of mindfulness-based pain management (MBSR).

Clinical parallel: Jon Kabat-Zinn's MBSR programme teaches exactly this — approach pain with curiosity rather than aversion. Studies show it reduces subjective pain intensity by 30–50% in chronic pain patients.

One-line essenceAt the centre of pain, there is no pain — only the energy you were calling pain. Enter the centre, and you are free.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 15 · Sutras 22–24 · 3 techniques

Seeing the Past as a Dream

Osho's framing

Osho borrows from Gurdjieff that identification is the only real sin - and these sutras attack it directly. We were children once and are no longer, yet we still merge with the memory rather than watching it like a film. The move, Osho says, is to begin with the past: see your childhood as a dream and you suddenly notice that what is present today will also become a dream tomorrow. The witness itself is never inside time. He cites Rousseau's Confessions as a man who admitted every sin except being identified with them.

Anything that you can look at as a witness is a dream.

— Osho
Sutra 22 of 112
Category: Witnessing · Tags: attention · place · where · seeing
Let attention be at a place where you are seeing some past happening, and even your form, having lost its present characteristics, is transformed.

In Plain English

Replay an event from your past, but watch it as if it happened to someone else. The figure inside the memory is your old form, not you — you are the witness. Osho calls this an unwinding of the mind: by re-running past happenings backwards, without identifying with them, the past loses its grip and the river of forms becomes visible to the one who is not a form.

How to Practice

  1. Lie in bed at night, just before sleep. Close your eyes. This is the easiest entry point Osho recommends, and it doubles as a cure for insomnia.
  2. Do not start at the morning. Start at the very last thing — you, lying here on the bed right now — and move backwards step by step through the day. Osho is emphatic: backwards only. Going forwards from the morning re-emphasises the trap; going backwards unwinds it.
  3. See each event in slow motion, like a film being run in reverse. Don’t hurry. The slower you go, the more detail you recover.
  4. Watch the figure inside the memory — the one being insulted at 3 p.m., the one praised at noon — and remember Osho’s key reminder: that is your form, not you. “You were never there. Always your form is involved; you are never involved.”
  5. If anger rises again as you watch the insult, you are identified and you have missed the point. Don’t fight the anger; just notice the identification, step back, and continue watching as a witness. If praise re-elates you, the same: step back.
  6. Continue all the way back to the first thing you remember on waking that morning. By the time you arrive there, sleep usually takes you on its own. Osho says the sleep that follows is meditative, almost dream-less, “like a very small child.”
  7. In the morning, before opening your eyes, do the same thing in reverse for the night: try to move back into the last fragment of dream, then deeper. This part takes weeks of practice; expect very little at first.
  8. To train the mind for backward movement, Osho suggests counting from 100 down to 1, slowly, once a day. The mind resists, because it is grooved to count up — that resistance is exactly what the technique is loosening.
  9. Once you are at home with one day, take a free day, go somewhere quiet, fast and stay silent. Lie on the ground or under a tree and run the same backwards process across your whole life — year by year, back into childhood, back to the earliest thing you can remember. Osho says you may eventually break through the four-or-five-year-old barrier, even reach the womb.
  10. While doing this, don’t analyse and don’t try to “heal” anything. Awareness alone is the work. Osho: “You are not to do anything, you are just to be aware.” Many psychological complexes simply dissolve as their hidden moment of origin is witnessed.
  11. The transformation the sutra promises happens slowly: you begin to feel that you are like a river of forms, and that the form changes while something behind it does not. Old grievances, old longings, old self-images quietly stop hooking you.
  12. Persist. Osho says the method is “not very difficult, only persistent effort is needed.” Even doing only the night-rewind, every night, is enough to begin.

Osho’s Key Insight

One of the great tantrikas of this age, George Gurdjieff, says that identification is the only sin. The next sutra, the tenth sutra on centering – which we are going to penetrate tonight – is concerned with identification. So first be crystal clear on what identification means.

Source: 15. Seeing the Past as a Dream, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 23 of 112
Category: Emptiness & Void · Tags: single-object · empty-place · filling · knower · void-behind
Feel an object before you. Feel the absence of all other objects but this one. Then leaving aside the object-feeling and the absence-feeling, realize.

In Plain English

Three steps. (1) Feel one object — a flower, a stone, a flame — so completely that it fills consciousness. Don’t just look at it; feel it through every sense. (2) Feel the absence of every other object — the whole world has dropped away; only this one thing remains. (3) Now drop both: drop the feeling-of-the-object, and drop the feeling-of-everything-else-being-absent. What remains is pure consciousness without content — the void, your own nature. Osho calls this an “abyss”: when there is no object and no absence-of-other-objects to hold, only awareness is left, like a flame burning without anything to illuminate.

How to Practice

  1. Choose a quiet, sparse place: The sutra itself does not specify, but Osho recommends an environment with as little visual clutter as possible. A simple room, a quiet garden, an empty corner. The fewer competing objects in the field, the easier it is for the chosen object to fully fill consciousness.
  2. Place one object: In this empty space, place (or find) a single object. A stone. A candle flame. A dot on the wall. A flower. It should be simple, singular, and visually distinct from the empty background. The contrast between the ONE object and the EMPTY space is essential.
  3. Fix attention totally: Sit comfortably facing the object. Now fix your mind on it with total concentration. See nothing else. Think of nothing else. Let the object fill your entire field of awareness. Not forcefully — lovingly. Let the object become the whole world.
  4. Let it fill you: As concentration deepens, the object begins to “enter” you. The boundary between you (the seer) and it (the seen) thins. You become FULL of the object — saturated with its presence, its form, its reality. There is nothing else in consciousness except this stone, this flame, this dot.
  5. Feel the absence of all other objects: Now register that the rest of the world has fallen away. Just this object exists; everything else is absent. This absence-feeling is itself a felt experience — not just intellectual knowledge but the lived sense of “only this is here.” Stay with both feelings simultaneously: this object is here AND everything else is absent.
  6. Now leave both aside: This is the pivot Osho calls the most difficult part. Having reached the state where you feel one object fully and feel the absence of all else, drop BOTH feelings — the object-feeling and the absence-feeling. Don’t replace them with anything. Don’t ask “who is filled?” (that would be a different technique). Simply let go of the object-feeling, let go of the absence-feeling, and what remains is what the sutra calls realisation — pure consciousness with nothing in it.
  7. Remove the object: Now close your eyes (or turn away from the object). The object is gone from awareness. What remains? The void — the empty container. But now you KNOW it is there because the object revealed it. Without the object, the void might seem like “nothing.” After the object, the void is clearly felt as the SPACE in which objects appear.
  8. Rest in the void: Stay in this awareness of the void — the empty, open, knowing space that contains all experience but is itself empty. It is serene, peaceful, without boundary. It is what you actually ARE — the space, not the objects that pass through it.
  9. Alternate: For deeper practice, alternate: open eyes, fill with object, feel fullness. Close eyes, feel the void that was filled. Open eyes again, fill again. Close, void again. Each alternation makes the void more clearly felt. The object is the finger pointing at the moon — the void is the moon itself.
  10. Integration: Once this is understood experientially, you can do it anywhere. Any object can reveal the void behind it. Look at a tree and ask: “Who sees?” The seeing is happening in void-space. The tree reveals the void. Every perception becomes a doorway to emptiness.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
30 min
Posture
Seated, facing the object
Eyes
Open (on object) then closed (for void)
Best for
Visual meditators; those who enjoy concentration practices; people in environments with access to empty spaces

Signs It’s Working

  • The object begins to feel “alive” or extraordinarily vivid as concentration deepens
  • The boundary between you and the object begins to thin or dissolve
  • The whole world genuinely drops away — only the chosen object remains in consciousness
  • The moment of letting both feelings go produces a felt “abyss” or sudden spaciousness
  • After removing the object: the void feels luminous, present, alive — not dead or blank
  • Growing ability to feel the same emptiness behind ANY object in daily life

Common Pitfalls

  • Choosing an environment with too many distractions — the contrast between one-thing-here and nothing-else is essential
  • Not concentrating deeply enough on the object — it must fully fill consciousness before the dropping is meaningful
  • Trying to do the whole technique in one sitting — Osho explicitly says practise the filling phase for days or weeks first, then add the dropping
  • Expecting to find SOMETHING when both feelings are dropped — the realisation is the absence of any thing, not the presence of a hidden one
  • Skipping the filling phase and jumping straight to the void — the contrast of fullness is necessary; the object is the vehicle

Questions From The Book

Why does Osho recommend an empty environment?

Chapter 50 — Moving to the Roots

The sutra itself does not specify location, but Osho’s commentary stresses: if the environment is full of objects, the mind bounces between them and cannot achieve the singular concentration needed for the “feel one object” phase. More subtly: when the outer field has only one thing in it, the inner field can mirror that condition more easily. Practical (fewer distractions) and supportive (one object in an empty visual field is itself a small enactment of the technique — one thought in empty awareness).

What is the difference between this and normal concentration meditation?

Chapter 61

Osho clarifies: in normal concentration, the goal IS the object — you want to become one with it (samadhi with seed). Here, the object is merely a TOOL. The real target is what remains when even the object-feeling and the absence-of-other-objects-feeling are dropped — the awareness that received them. Normal concentration ends with fullness; this technique uses fullness as a stepping-stone to the void. The dropping of both feelings is what transforms concentration into self-knowledge.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This technique uses the figure-ground relationship in perception. Normally we focus on figures (objects) and ignore the ground (space/awareness). By deliberately concentrating on ONE figure until it saturates attention, and then shifting to ask “what is the ground?” — you discover that the ground of all experience is empty awareness. Every object rests in the void; the void holds all objects without being any of them.

Perceptual psychology: Gestalt psychology shows that figure and ground are complementary — you cannot have one without the other. By saturating attention with figure (the object), you make ground (the void) maximally apparent by contrast. This is an elegant use of perceptual mechanics for spiritual investigation.

One-line essenceFeel one object completely; feel the absence of all else; then drop both. What remains is the realisation.
Sutra 24 of 112
Category: Witnessing · Tags: mood · against · someone · arises
When a mood against someone or for someone arises, do not place it on the person in question, but remain centered.

In Plain English

When love, hate, anger or any strong mood arises towards someone, the ordinary mind throws it onto that person and calls them the cause. Tantra says the opposite: you are the source, the other is only a screen. Refuse to project. Use the heat of the mood as a wire that runs back inward to the place it came from, and stay there.

How to Practice

  1. This is a technique for the middle of life, not the cushion. Wait for an actual mood — sudden anger at a colleague, a wave of love for someone, a flash of hate, jealousy, irritation. The hotter the mood, the better the doorway.
  2. The instant you notice the mood, do not let it rush out toward the person. Don’t speak. Don’t act on it. Don’t even rehearse what you will say. The whole technique depends on cutting the projection at its root.
  3. Inwardly thank the person who triggered you. Osho is direct on this: “Someone has given you a chance to be aware of your own anger — thank him immediately and forget him.” They are not the source; they are only the bucket dropped into your well. What came up was already there.
  4. Close your eyes if you can (in a meeting, lower the gaze). Turn your attention 180 degrees: away from the other and back along the line of the mood — toward the place inside you it is coming from.
  5. Don’t suppress the mood and don’t express it. Feel it, hot and alive, but follow it inward. Osho calls the emotion “a hot wire” — while it is hot, it is conductive; you can ride it back to the source far more easily than you can find that source in calm.
  6. As you move inward, you reach a cooler point: the centre from which the anger or love is being thrown out. Stop there. Don’t analyse why — just rest at the source.
  7. Stay centred for as long as the mood lasts. The mood may continue to burn for a while; let it. You are no longer pouring it onto the person. It will exhaust itself in your awareness instead of in their face.
  8. If a thought tries to drag you back outward (“but they really did this…”), recognise it as the projection trying to restart, and bring attention back to the inner source.
  9. Remember Lin Chi’s empty boat. A drifting boat hit his at night and he was furious — until he opened his eyes and saw the boat was empty. There was nowhere to send the anger. He let it float backwards into himself and reached his centre. After that, even when a person came and insulted him he would say, “This boat is also empty,” close his eyes, and go within. Treat every triggering person as an empty boat.
  10. You can also do this with positive moods. When sudden affection or admiration arises for someone, don’t spill it onto them either; trace it back to the source. The same well that throws hate also throws love. Knowing the well is the point.
  11. Practise on small irritations first — a queue, a slow driver, a careless word. The big ones (close relationships) are harder because the urgency leaves no space. Build the muscle on minor moods, then it is available for the major ones.
  12. Over weeks, you will notice a shift: moods still arise, but they no longer reach the screen. They illuminate the projector instead. That is the centring the sutra promises.

Osho’s Key Insight

One of the great tantrikas of this age, George Gurdjieff, says that identification is the only sin. The next sutra, the tenth sutra on centering – which we are going to penetrate tonight – is concerned with identification. So first be crystal clear on what identification means.

Source: 15. Seeing the Past as a Dream, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 17 · Sutras 25–27 · 3 techniques

Several Stop Techniques

Osho's framing

Osho splits life into two balances - being and doing. Being is already the case; doing and having are achievements piled around it like a circumference. Most people live entirely on that circumference and never touch the centre. These "stop" sutras attack it directly. Gurdjieff borrowed the same method from Tibetan lamas (who got it from Vigyan Bhairav): in the middle of dance, freeze. The body still has momentum to continue, but you don't cooperate. In that sudden gap, the body and the watcher inside it split apart, and for an instant you actually see yourself. The smallest adjustment ruins it.

Whatsoever you have is not your being, and whatsoever you do is not your being.

— Osho
Sutra 25 of 112
Category: Death Contemplation · Tags: intuition · stopping · death-of-expectation
Just as you have the impulse to do something, stop.

Source check: this exact wording was not located in Osho’s Book of Secrets. The closest Osho sutras are the “stop techniques” cluster (Chapter 17, e.g. “Whenever an action begins, stop. Then no longer doing nor having a thought, leap into being.” and the sneeze-stop sutra). The technique below is a synthesised practice in that family but is not Osho’s commentary on a single matched sutra.

In Plain English

When you sense something is about to happen — a thought about to form, a desire about to arise, an event about to unfold — STOP right at that edge. Don’t let it happen. Stay at the precipice of happening without falling in. That edge is where life meets death, where creation meets void. Stay there.

How to Practice

  1. Understand the edge: There is a moment BEFORE every happening — a split second of intuition, of knowing that something is about to occur. Before a thought completes itself, you sense its direction. Before a sneeze, there is a pre-sneeze moment. Before anger erupts, there is a micro-second of gathering. The technique lives in that pre-moment — the edge between not-yet and happening.
  2. Start with gross examples: Begin with physical events you can anticipate. When you sense a yawn coming — STOP at the edge. Feel the impulse without completing it. When you sense a sneeze building — catch the moment just before (don’t suppress the sneeze; observe the edge-moment). When anger is rising — catch it at the very first sensation, before it becomes full anger.
  3. Move to thoughts: As mindfulness sharpens, notice thoughts BEFORE they fully form. There is a moment when a thought is just a stirring, a directionality, an intention without content. Catch it there. Don’t let it complete into a full thought. Stay at the pre-thought moment — the impulse without the expression. This is the edge.
  4. The death connection: Every happening is a small birth. Every non-happening is a small death. When you stop at the edge — preventing the birth of a thought, an action, an emotion — you experience a micro-death. The impulse dies unborn. Something that was about to live doesn’t live. YOU experience being at the edge between existence and non-existence. This is death’s edge, experienced moment-to-moment.
  5. Stay at the precipice: Don’t fall forward (into the happening) or retreat backward (into ordinary unconsciousness). Stay EXACTLY at the edge. It feels like standing at the edge of a cliff — leaning forward enough to see the void but not falling. The edge is alive, vibrating, full of potential. It is the bardo — the gap between one state and the next.
  6. Notice what the edge reveals: When you successfully stay at the edge — preventing the happening without retreating from the edge — something remarkable appears: pure potential. The moment before a thought is richer than the thought itself. The moment before action is more alive than the action. The moment before birth (or death) is the most potent moment. You are touching the creative void.
  7. Apply to desire: When a desire arises (hunger, lust, ambition, any wanting), catch it at the FIRST moment of its arising. Stay there. Don’t let it develop into a full desire with plans and fantasies. Don’t suppress it either. Just stay at the first flicker. The desire is at the edge of life and death — alive enough to be felt, but stopped before it can take over. This is mastery.
  8. The intuition sharpens: With practice, your capacity for sensing “what is about to happen” becomes extremely refined. You begin to sense thoughts before they form, emotions before they arise, even external events before they manifest. This heightened intuition is a natural byproduct of living at the edge rather than in the already-happened.
  9. Formal sitting practice: Sit quietly for 20 minutes. Simply watch the mind. Each time a thought begins to form — and you catch the very BEGINNING of its formation — stop there. Don’t complete the thought and don’t push it away. Just hover at its birth-point. Thought after thought, catch the edge. The mind gradually becomes a field of edges without completions — pure potential without manifestation. This is the void.
  10. Living at the edge: Eventually this becomes a way of life: living at the edge of each moment, where what-was meets what-will-be. You are always at the point of creation/destruction — the point where life and death are ONE. This is the most alive place to be. It is where masters live: at the edge of each instant, fully present, never carried away by what has already happened or what might happen next.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Advanced
Duration
20 min (formal) or continuous (life practice)
Posture
Any — seated for formal practice, any position for life practice
Eyes
Open or closed
Best for
Experienced meditators; those with refined attention; people interested in the nature of time and causation; zen-oriented practitioners

Signs It’s Working

  • Increasing ability to catch thoughts at their very inception — before they form fully
  • A sense of “living at the edge” — always at the point of creation, never in the already-created
  • Heightened intuition — sensing what will happen before it happens
  • Thoughts becoming less compulsive — you can stop them before they take over
  • A quality of freshness in each moment — nothing is stale because you are always at the beginning
  • Deep familiarity with the void — the creative emptiness from which all things emerge

Common Pitfalls

  • Suppressing thoughts/impulses rather than stopping AT THE EDGE — suppression is violence; this is pure attention
  • Not catching the moment early enough — once the thought is half-formed, you’re too late for the edge
  • Creating tension through over-vigilance — the watching should be relaxed, effortless, like a cat at a mousehole
  • Becoming frustrated when thoughts keep completing before you catch them — this takes time; even occasional catches are valuable
  • Confusing mental dullness with the void — the edge is ALIVE and vibrant; dullness is unconsciousness

Questions From The Book

How is this related to death?

Chapter 57, "The Edge of Happening"

Osho explains: death is the ultimate “edge” — the moment when life is about to become non-life. If you can be present at THAT edge (rather than unconscious, as most people are at death), you achieve liberation. But you cannot suddenly be present at death’s edge if you haven’t practised. This technique practises edge-awareness moment-to-moment with small happenings so that when the great edge (death) comes, you are ready. Every micro-stopping at the edge of a thought is rehearsal for the great stopping at the edge of life itself.

What is the connection to Zen’s “beginner’s mind”?

Chapter 57

Osho notes: “beginner’s mind” means always being at the beginning — never arriving at conclusions, never settling into the already-known. This sutra provides the METHOD for maintaining beginner’s mind: stop at the edge of each happening. When you don’t let thoughts complete, you are always at the beginning. When you don’t let expectations manifest, everything remains fresh. The edge IS beginner’s mind — always new, always before-the-known, always at the first instant of creation.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This technique develops what neuroscience calls “response inhibition” — the capacity to catch an impulse before it executes. But it goes beyond mere impulse control: it uses the pre-response moment as a doorway to awareness of awareness itself. The gap between stimulus and response (which Viktor Frankl identified as the seat of human freedom) is here explored as a doorway to the deathless.

Quantum mechanics metaphor: Before observation, quantum systems exist in superposition — multiple possibilities coexisting. The moment of “observation” (happening) collapses possibilities into one actuality. This technique asks you to stay in the pre-collapse state — where all possibilities coexist, where nothing has yet been determined. That state of pure potential is the creative void, the bardo, the edge where life and death meet.

One-line essenceBetween not-yet and already-happened is an edge — alive with pure potential. Stop there. Don’t let the next moment be born. At that precipice where creation meets void, you find the deathless present.
Sutra 26 of 112
Category: Senses · Tags: desire · quitting · sudden-stop · gap · freedom
When some desire comes, consider it. Then suddenly, quit it.

In Plain English

A desire arises — to eat, to speak, to move, to check your phone. Consider it fully — feel it, acknowledge it, let it build. Then SUDDENLY quit it. Drop it completely. In the instant of dropping, a gap opens. The desire was momentum; the sudden stop creates a void-moment. In that void: freedom. The technique is not suppression (which is gradual) but sudden abandonment (which is total).

How to Practice

  1. Understand the difference from suppression: Suppression says “don’t have this desire.” This technique says “HAVE the desire fully, then drop it suddenly.” The first creates conflict; the second creates freedom. You need the desire present and felt — then the sudden dropping creates the gap.
  2. Start with small desires: Don’t begin with your biggest addiction. Start tiny: the desire to scratch an itch, to shift position, to check your phone, to take another bite. These micro-desires are perfect training ground.
  3. Phase 1 — CONSIDER: When a desire arises, turn your full attention to it. Feel it in the body. Where does it live? The belly? The throat? The hands? What sensation does it create? Urgency? Pulling? Heat? Don’t resist it. Let it build to its peak intensity. CONSIDER it — feel it completely.
  4. Phase 2 — SUDDENLY QUIT: At the peak of the desire — when it is strongest and most demanding — drop it INSTANTANEOUSLY. Not gradually. Not by talking yourself out of it. Not by replacing it with something else. Simply DROP. Like releasing a held breath. Like opening a clenched fist. One moment desire; next moment nothing.
  5. Phase 3 — NOTICE THE GAP: In the instant of dropping, something extraordinary happens: a vacuum, a void, a moment of absolute freedom. The energy that was going toward the desire has nowhere to go. It pools in pure awareness. THAT moment is the meditation. That gap is the door.
  6. Practice with movement: Start walking across the room with intention. Build momentum. Then STOP. Freeze mid-stride. The body stops, and in the sudden stop, the mind stops too. Feel the gap between the momentum and the stillness.
  7. Practice with speech: You want to say something. Feel the words forming, the desire to speak building. Then — close your mouth. Don’t say it. Not because it’s wrong to say it, but for the experiment. Feel the gap when the impulse to speak is suddenly abandoned.
  8. Practice with reaching: Reach for your phone, your coffee, a book. Let the hand extend. Then STOP before contact. Pull the hand back. Feel the sudden emptiness where the grasping was. That emptiness is awareness.
  9. Evening practice (10 desires): In the evening, sit quietly and recall 10 desires that arose during the day. With each one, mentally re-create the desire, feel it build, then suddenly drop it. Feel the gap each time. Ten gaps per evening, consciously created.
  10. The mastery: Over weeks, you develop the ability to drop ANY desire at ANY moment. Not from suppression but from the understanding that desire is just energy — and when it is dropped, that energy becomes available for awareness. This is not anti-life; it is profoundly pro-freedom.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
Throughout the day
Posture
Any
Eyes
Open or closed
Best for
Those working with addictive patterns; impulsive personalities; people who feel enslaved by desire; anyone who wants more freedom and less compulsion

Signs It’s Working

  • The gap after dropping becomes palpable — a clear, distinct moment of freedom
  • You begin to feel choice where previously there was only compulsion
  • Desires lose their hypnotic power — they arise but no longer dictate action
  • A sense of inner sovereignty: “I can desire, AND I can drop the desire”
  • The energy that was locked in habitual desires becomes available for creativity and awareness
  • Others notice you seem less reactive, less driven, more centred

Common Pitfalls

  • Confusing this with suppression — suppression denies the desire; this technique ALLOWS it fully first, then drops it
  • Dropping gradually instead of suddenly — the power is in the ABRUPTNESS; gradual dropping is just losing interest
  • Not letting the desire build to full intensity first — a weak desire dropped produces a weak gap; a strong desire dropped produces a powerful gap
  • Applying this to survival needs inappropriately — don’t drop the desire for water when dehydrated; use non-essential desires
  • Making it a moral exercise (“I should not desire”) — this is consciousness work, not ethics

Questions From The Book

How is suddenly quitting different from will-power?

Chapter 55, "Consider and Quit"

Osho is emphatic: this is NOT will-power. Will-power fights desire with a counter-force — creating tension, conflict, and eventual explosion. Sudden quitting is more like relaxing a clenched fist. You don’t fight the fist open; you simply stop clenching. The desire was being held by attention; when attention is suddenly withdrawn, the desire simply collapses — not because of force but because of the absence of fuel. No fight, no conflict — just sudden cessation.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This maps to the neuroscience of “response inhibition” — the prefrontal cortex’s ability to halt a motor or cognitive programme already in progress. But the technique adds a contemplative dimension: the gap created by inhibition is not just a pause but a window into non-compulsive awareness. It trains the brain that desire is not destiny.

Addiction research parallel: “Urge surfing” in addiction therapy teaches people to feel cravings without acting on them. This technique is similar but more radical: it says feel the craving fully, then DROP it suddenly. The resulting neurological reset — the sudden release of dopaminergic anticipation — creates a distinct moment of clarity that weakens the habit loop.

One-line essenceDesire is momentum. A sudden stop reveals the freedom that was always behind the wanting.
Sutra 27 of 112
Category: Emptiness & Void · Tags: exhaustion · dropping · surrender · wholeness · effort-to-effortlessness
Roam about until exhausted and then, dropping to the ground, in this dropping be whole.

In Plain English

Move the body vigorously — dance wildly, run, spin, do Osho’s Dynamic Meditation. Continue until the body is utterly exhausted and DROPS of its own accord. In that dropping — when effort is impossible — the ego also drops. You become whole because the doer has been exhausted into silence. The void reveals itself when there is no energy left to maintain the ego.

How to Practice

  1. Choose your method of exhaustion: Wild dancing, running, vigorous shaking, spinning (Sufi-style), Osho’s Dynamic Meditation, or simply roaming/walking intensely. The specific movement doesn’t matter — what matters is that it exhausts the body TOTALLY.
  2. Create a safe space: You need a space where you can move wildly without self-consciousness or danger. A private room with padded floor, a secluded garden, a beach at dawn. Remove obstacles you could trip over. Wear minimal, comfortable clothing.
  3. Begin with intensity: Start moving with TOTAL intensity. Not half-hearted — give 100%. If dancing, dance as if possessed. If running, run full-speed. If shaking, shake every cell. The key is: hold nothing back. The ego maintains itself through reserves of energy; exhaust ALL reserves.
  4. Continue past comfort: When the body says “enough,” continue. When the mind says “I can’t,” continue. The first “I can’t” is a lie — the body has reserves. Push through the first barrier. And the second. Continue until the body GENUINELY cannot continue — not out of choice but out of true physical impossibility.
  5. The dropping: When exhaustion is total, LET yourself drop. Don’t lower yourself carefully — DROP. Fall to the ground. Collapse. The dropping must be total, sudden, without control. Like a puppet whose strings are cut. This is the critical moment.
  6. In the dropping, be whole: The moment you drop — in that instant of total surrender to gravity — FEEL the wholeness. The ego, which requires energy to maintain its divisions and tensions, simply cannot function when all energy is spent. In that powerless state, you are whole because there is no energy to maintain the fragments.
  7. Lie still on the ground: After dropping, lie completely still. Face down or face up — however you fell. Don’t adjust, don’t make yourself comfortable. The body will feel like it’s melting into the earth. Let it. Breathe heavily — that’s natural. But don’t move. Stay 15–20 minutes in this absolute stillness.
  8. Observe the void: In the stillness after exhaustion, notice: the mind is empty. Not because you forced it to be empty, but because there is simply no energy left to think. Thoughts require energy. When energy is spent, thoughts stop. This is the most effortless meditation — emptiness achieved through total effort.
  9. Feel the wholeness: Without the ego’s constant dividing activity, you feel whole — undivided, unfragmented, simply and purely present. No inner conflict, no “I should” or “I shouldn’t,” no past or future. Just this: the ground, the body, the breath, the void. Total.
  10. Rise slowly: When energy begins to return, rise very slowly. Notice: as energy returns, the ego also returns. Watch it reassembling itself — first the body-sense, then preferences, then memories, then the “I.” Seeing this reassembly AFTER having been without it is deeply instructive. The ego is clearly seen as a construction, not a given.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner-friendly
Duration
45–60 min (20–30 active + 15–20 stillness)
Posture
Moving then lying flat
Eyes
Open during movement, closed after dropping
Best for
Active types; people who can’t sit still; those with excess energy; young practitioners; anyone frustrated with “quiet” meditation

Signs It’s Working

  • The body drops truly involuntarily — you didn’t choose to fall, you simply couldn’t stand
  • Profound silence in the mind after dropping — no thoughts, no effort, just presence
  • A feeling of melting into the earth — the boundary between body and ground dissolves
  • Complete absence of inner conflict or division — pure wholeness
  • Upon rising: a fresh, childlike clarity — the world looks new
  • Deep sleep that night — the body and mind are thoroughly cleansed

Common Pitfalls

  • Not committing fully to the movement — half-hearted effort creates half-hearted exhaustion and NO void
  • Lowering yourself carefully instead of truly dropping — the ego is still in control if you “sit down gently”
  • Stopping too early because of mental fatigue (boredom) rather than true physical exhaustion
  • Moving again too soon after dropping — the stillness phase is where the technique completes
  • Doing this with physical injuries or health conditions that make extreme exertion dangerous — use common sense

Questions From The Book

Why does exhaustion lead to wholeness?

Chapter 59, "Dropping Into Wholeness"

Osho explains: the ego is an activity, not a thing. It requires constant energy to maintain — dividing experience into “me” and “not-me,” judging, controlling, resisting. When all energy is exhausted, the ego simply cannot perform its activity. It stops. And when the ego stops, what remains is wholeness — because the only thing preventing wholeness was the ego’s constant dividing. You don’t ACHIEVE wholeness; you simply exhaust the obstacle to it.

Is this why Osho created Dynamic Meditation?

Chapter 59

Osho confirms: Dynamic Meditation is a modern systematisation of this ancient sutra. The first three stages (chaotic breathing, catharsis, jumping with “Hoo!”) exhaust the body and release emotional blocks. The fourth stage (freeze/stop) IS the dropping. The fifth stage (celebration) is the wholeness expressed. The sutra gives the principle; Dynamic Meditation gives the method. For modern people with sedentary lives and repressed energy, the structured approach of Dynamic Meditation may be more effective than simply “roaming about.”

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: The ego/self-model requires metabolic energy to maintain. Research on ego-depletion shows that self-control, decision-making, and identity-maintenance all consume glucose. When physical exhaustion depletes these resources, the self-model temporarily simplifies or dissolves. This technique uses that biological reality deliberately — exhaust the fuel that powers the ego, and the ego stops.

Parallel practices: Sufi whirling, ecstatic dance traditions, marathon running (“runner’s high” as ego-dissolution), and various indigenous dance-until-you-drop ceremonies all use the same principle. The body becomes the vehicle for transcendence when pushed beyond its normal operating limits.

One-line essenceThe ego needs energy to maintain its illusion of separateness. Exhaust all energy, drop to the ground, and discover: without the effort of being someone, you are whole.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 19 · Sutras 28–29 · 2 techniques

A Technique for the Intellectual and a Technique for the Feeling Type

Osho's framing

Tantra's diagnosis here is radical: the mind is not sick - the mind is the sickness. Western psychology assumes the average mind is healthy and only treats deviations from the norm; it tries to readjust the genius back to average. Osho says this misses the point - the average mind is itself "normally ill," just commonly enough that no one notices. The body, since it is always dying, can never be perfectly healthy; only relatively. The mind is the same: structurally a bridge between matter and spirit, dying and deathless, so it is bound to be tense. Curing it from inside is impossible. The only move is transcendence.

It is not that you are tense within, but rather, you are the tension.

— Osho
Sutra 28 of 112
Category: Emptiness & Void · Tags: deprivation · transcend · letting-go · weakness · surrender
Suppose you are gradually being deprived of strength or of knowledge. At the instant of deprivation, transcend.

In Plain English

Imagine strength leaving the body — muscles dissolving, power fading. OR imagine knowledge leaving — certainties dissolving, opinions evaporating, all you “know” disappearing. At the MOMENT nothing remains — no strength, no knowledge — something transcends. The void is reached not by gaining but by losing everything.

How to Practice

  1. Choose your path — strength or knowledge: This sutra offers two approaches. Try both over different sessions, then commit to whichever resonates more deeply. Strength-deprivation works better for body-identified people; knowledge-deprivation for intellectuals.
  2. PATH A — Deprivation of strength: Lie down comfortably. Close your eyes. Feel your body as it is — its solidity, its musculature, its capacity for action. Now begin to FEEL that strength draining away. Start with the feet — feel them becoming empty, powerless, weightless.
  3. Let weakness rise: The emptying of strength rises slowly through the body — calves dissolving, thighs becoming hollow, pelvis emptying of power. Feel the belly losing its strength, the chest becoming light and insubstantial. Arms losing their grip, hands becoming feather-light, unable to hold anything.
  4. Complete deprivation: Continue until the entire body feels utterly powerless — as if you couldn’t lift a finger if your life depended on it. Not tense weakness but RELEASED weakness — like a rag doll, like a deflated balloon. No strength anywhere. Completely helpless.
  5. PATH B — Deprivation of knowledge: Sit comfortably, eyes closed. Begin by feeling all your certainties. Your name, your age, your beliefs, your opinions, your skills, your memories. FEEL how much you “know.” Now begin to let it dissolve. Start with opinions — feel them evaporating. Then beliefs. Then facts. Then memories.
  6. Feel knowledge dissolving: Let your name dissolve — you no longer know what you are called. Let language dissolve — words lose meaning. Let all concepts dissolve — categories become meaningless. Let even the knowledge of WHERE you are dissolve. Who you are. WHAT you are. All dissolving into emptiness.
  7. The instant of total deprivation: In BOTH paths, arrive at a point where NOTHING remains. No strength to act. Or no knowledge to orient. In that instant — that precise moment of complete deprivation — something transcends. Not “you” transcend — the “you” has been deprived of everything. SOMETHING ELSE transcends. The void itself becomes alive.
  8. Don’t try to transcend: The sutra says “at the instant of deprivation, transcend” — but this is a description, not a command. You cannot DO transcendence. You can only create the conditions for it. The conditions are: total deprivation. When nothing remains, transcendence happens on its own.
  9. Rest in whatever opens: After the moment of transcendence (which may be subtle or dramatic), rest in whatever space has opened. Don’t analyse it. Don’t grab at it. Let it be what it is. It may feel like floating in space, like being nothing, like infinite peace, like silent aliveness.
  10. Return gradually: Allow strength or knowledge to return naturally. Don’t rush back. Notice: as they return, the ego reassembles around them. Strength and knowledge are the building blocks of the ego. Without them, there is only the void. With them, there is a “someone.” You now know the choice is available.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
25 min
Posture
Lying (Path A) or seated (Path B)
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Those who identify strongly with physical strength or intellectual knowledge; control-oriented personalities ready to let go

Signs It’s Working

  • A genuine felt sense of the body becoming lighter, weaker, more transparent (Path A)
  • A dizzying but exhilarating sense of not knowing anything (Path B)
  • At the moment of total deprivation: a “click” or shift — something opens that was not open before
  • Profound peace that comes from having nothing to protect or maintain
  • After returning: a lighter relationship with strength or knowledge — having them without BEING them

Common Pitfalls

  • Imagining deprivation without FEELING it — the body must actually feel weak/empty for Path A to work
  • Fear of powerlessness (Path A) or ignorance (Path B) causing the mind to resist the process
  • Trying to “transcend” as an action — transcendence is what happens naturally at total deprivation; you can’t do it
  • Remaining partially identified — keeping a small reserve of strength or knowledge “just in case”
  • Conflating this with depression or learned helplessness — this is CHOSEN, temporary, and leads to expansion, not contraction

Questions From The Book

Why does Shiva say “suppose” — is this just imagination?

Chapter 60, "Deprivation and Transcendence"

Osho explains: the word “suppose” is a skillful device. If Shiva said “be deprived,” the ego would resist violently. But “suppose” gives the mind permission — “it’s only hypothetical, only imagination.” The ego relaxes. And then the supposing becomes real. Because when you FEEL yourself truly without strength, even in imagination, the body responds as if it were real. The nervous system doesn’t distinguish between vividly imagined and actual deprivation. So “suppose” is the Trojan horse that gets past the ego’s defences.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: The ego maintains itself through two pillars: the capacity to act (strength) and the capacity to know (knowledge). Remove both, and the ego structure cannot sustain itself. This is a guided approach to what naturally happens in deep surrender states, near-death experiences, and moments of total overwhelm — but here it is done deliberately, safely, and reversibly.

Neuroscience parallel: Research on “flow states” shows that when the sense of agency (strength) and the internal narrative (knowledge/stories) both quiet down, people report timelessness, egolessness, and transcendent states. This technique achieves the same result through imaginative deprivation rather than optimal performance.

One-line essenceThe void is not reached by gaining but by losing. Lose all strength. Lose all knowing. At the moment nothing is left, something beyond both is revealed.
Sutra 29 of 112
Category: Heart & Devotion · Tags: devotion · love · surrender · freedom
Devotion frees.

In Plain English

Pour total love toward any beloved — a person, God, existence. The quality of total self-giving empties the heart of ego. What remains is pure love without an object — divine love. Devotion is the heart’s way of dissolving the self: you cannot THINK yourself into egolessness, but you can LOVE yourself into it. When giving is total, the giver disappears.

How to Practice

  1. Choose your beloved: Who or what can your heart pour itself toward without reservation? A teacher, God in any form, the universe, a loved one, nature, truth, beauty, existence itself. The object matters less than the QUALITY of your devotion. Choose whatever makes your heart overflow most naturally.
  2. Create a devotional space: Light a candle, place a photo or symbol of your beloved, play soft devotional music if it helps. The space should say to your heart: “Here you are safe to love without limit.” Or go to a natural cathedral — a forest, a hilltop, a riverbank — wherever your heart opens spontaneously.
  3. Open the heart centre: Sit or kneel. Bring awareness to the centre of the chest. Feel it softening, warming, expanding. You may place a hand over the heart to connect more deeply. Breathe into the heart space. Let it become the centre of your world.
  4. Begin to pour: Now direct the heart’s energy toward your chosen beloved. Not as thought (“I love you”) but as FEELING — a warmth, a flow, a stream of devotional energy moving from your heart toward the beloved. Let it be continuous, like a river that never stops flowing.
  5. Give yourself totally: Don’t just give love — give YOURSELF. Give your thoughts, your memories, your identity, your fears, your hopes. Pour everything into the stream. The beloved is a vast ocean; you are a river emptying into it. Hold nothing back. Keep nothing in reserve.
  6. Feel the emptying: As you give and give, notice: you are becoming empty. Lighter. More spacious. The more you give, the less remains of “you.” This is not loss — it is liberation. Every ounce of self given away is an ounce of freedom gained. The ego dissolves in the acid of total love.
  7. When nothing is left: Continue pouring until there is no one left to pour. The giver has been given. The river has reached the ocean and is ocean now. In this moment, there is no “you” and no “beloved” — only love itself, without subject or object. THIS is divine love. Not love FROM someone TO someone, but love as the fabric of existence.
  8. Rest in freedom: The sutra says “devotion frees.” Feel the freedom. Not freedom FROM something (that is merely escape) but freedom AS something — freedom as your natural state when the ego is dissolved. You are free because there is no one left to be imprisoned. The cage is empty. The bird is the sky.
  9. Use devotional practices: Singing bhajans, chanting mantras, writing love letters to God, prostrating before your symbol, serving others selflessly — all are forms of pouring. Choose whichever form helps your heart pour most naturally. The form is the vehicle; the pouring is the practice.
  10. Make daily life devotional: Once you taste the freedom, bring devotion into everything. Cook as an offering. Walk as a prayer. Work as service. Each action done with devotional quality empties the ego a little more. Eventually, life itself becomes continuous devotion — a constant pouring of love toward existence.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate (easy for devotional types, hard for intellectuals)
Duration
20–40 min
Posture
Any — sitting, kneeling, dancing, prostrating
Eyes
Open or closed
Best for
Heart-oriented people; lovers; those with natural reverence; anyone who has experienced the ego-dissolution of falling in love; bhakti temperaments

Signs It’s Working

  • Tears that come without sadness — the heart overflowing in gratitude
  • A feeling of emptying out, becoming lighter, more spacious
  • Moments of ecstasy where the boundary between self and beloved dissolves
  • The distinction between “I” and “the beloved” thinning until it disappears
  • A background hum of love in daily life — devotion spreading beyond formal practice to colour everything
  • Freedom felt as lightness, spaciousness, and the absence of self-concern

Common Pitfalls

  • Confusing devotion with dependence — devotion empties the self; dependence fills it with need and clinging
  • Becoming proud of one’s devotion (“I am so devoted”) — this creates a new, subtler ego
  • Devotion to a person becoming possessive attachment — true devotion does not need response or reciprocation
  • Holding back a “watcher” who observes the devotion — total devotion has no witness; give the watcher too
  • Intellectualising instead of feeling — the head cannot do devotion; only the heart can

Questions From The Book

Can devotion be practised without believing in God?

Chapter 60, "Devotion Frees"

Osho says absolutely: devotion to truth, to beauty, to nature, to existence itself — all work. Even devotion to one’s own inquiry. What matters is the quality of TOTAL self-giving, not the theological framework. A scientist lost in wonder at the cosmos is more devoted than a believer mechanically repeating prayers. The intensity of self-giving is everything; the belief system is nothing.

Why only two words — the shortest sutra?

Chapter 60

Osho explains: because devotion cannot be taught through long explanation. Either you know the taste of total self-giving or you do not. Words cannot convey it — only experience can. Two words are sufficient: devotion frees. Try it. The experience will teach you everything that words cannot. The brevity of the sutra IS the teaching — devotion is simple, direct, and needs no elaboration. It is the heart speaking to the heart in the fewest possible words.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Devotion reverses the direction of attention. Normally all attention flows INWARD (self-referential processing, the default mode network). Devotion redirects ALL attention OUTWARD toward the beloved. When the stream of self-attention is completely redirected, the self-structure loses its fuel and temporarily dissolves. Freedom is the experiential result of this dissolution — no self means no prison.

Research on self-transcendent emotions: Studies on awe, gratitude, and love show that these emotions reliably reduce self-referential thinking and increase prosocial behaviour. The mechanism is identical to what this technique produces: when the heart opens fully toward something greater, the small self becomes transparent. This is measurable, replicable, and universal across cultures.

One-line essenceTwo words contain the entire path: Devotion frees. Pour yourself out so totally that no one remains to be imprisoned. The empty vessel IS freedom.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 21 · Sutras 30–32 · 3 techniques

Three Looking Techniques

Osho's framing

Before these three sutras Osho lingers on the eyes themselves. Of all the body, the eyes are the least bodily - a meeting point where matter comes closest to the consciousness behind it. The hand, the heart, no other organ comes that near. Because of that proximity, a single attentive jump from the eyes can land you at the source. These techniques use that bridge: closing the eyes to see your inner being in detail, looking at a bowl without registering the sides or material, or seeing an ordinary face as if for the very first time. The point is not what you look at but the quality of the looking.

A single jump from the eyes can lead you to the source.

— Osho
Sutra 30 of 112
Category: The Senses · Tags: eyes · closed · inner · detail
Eyes closed, see your inner being in detail. Thus see your true nature.

In Plain English

Closing the eyelids is not enough. The eyes must stop moving, like stones. Behind closed eyes most people still “watch” images, memories, dreams — that is still seeing the outside. When the eyes are totally still and there is nothing to be seen, the look itself turns inward, and you see your own body and mind from within for the first time. Osho calls that turn the doorway to your true nature.

How to Practice

  1. Sit in a quiet place where you will not be disturbed. Any comfortable upright posture is fine; spine straight.
  2. Close your eyes. Then check: are images, memories, fantasies still moving on the inner screen? If yes, the eyes are not yet closed in the way the sutra means. Osho: total closing means “nothing to see — not even an inside image of an outside object.”
  3. Stop the movement of the eyeballs themselves. Behind the lids, let the eyes become “just like stones.” No flickering, no shifting, no following of inner pictures. If you don’t know what this feels like, simply feel the eyes as solid and refuse to let them move.
  4. Stay in that stony stillness. Don’t do anything else. Don’t chant, don’t breathe in any special way. Just hold the eyes still in a blank darkness, as if you had suddenly gone blind not only to the world but also to dreams.
  5. Expect this to take time. Osho is honest: a long training is needed; it cannot happen the first day. Repeat at any free moment in the day — whenever you have a few minutes, close, still the eyes, hold.
  6. One day, suddenly, the look will turn around. The eyes that were always looking outwards will look inwards. There is nothing dramatic to wait for — only a clear inner glimpse, like seeing the inside walls of a room you had only ever seen from outside.
  7. Once that glimpse comes, begin to move attention through the body in detail. Osho says: forget the whole body and go to the toe. Stay there. Look. Then move slowly up — through the legs, the belly, the chest, each arm, the throat, the head. Visit each part from inside.
  8. This is the “in detail” the sutra asks for. Do not hurry. The body is being seen from a new direction; treat it as exploration, not inspection.
  9. When the body has been entered, do the same with the mind: now watch the layers of thought, mood, memory rising and falling. The mind too becomes an object; what is watching it is something else.
  10. Notice the simple shift: the looker is never the looked-at. The body can be seen, the mind can be seen — therefore you are neither. Whatever cannot itself be turned into an object is what you really are. Osho calls this the witness, and the technique calls it your true nature.
  11. Don’t try to witness the witness. Osho warns this is the trap: “You cannot witness the witnessing source — that is absurd.” Just rest as it.
  12. Sit for as long as the state lasts. When you open the eyes, do so gently, and carry the inward orientation into the next activity. With practice the closing-and-stilling becomes a knack you can do in seconds.

Osho’s Key Insight

Tonight’s techniques are concerned with the practice of looking. Before we enter these techniques, something has to be understood about the eyes, because all these seven techniques depend on that. The first thing: eyes are the most non-bodily part in the human body, the least bodily. If matter can become non-matter, then such is the case with eyes. Eyes are material, but simultaneously they are...

Source: 21. Three Looking Techniques, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 31 of 112
Category: Imagination · Tags: bowl · container · space · aware
Look upon a bowl without seeing the sides or the material. In a few moments become aware.

In Plain English

Look at an empty bowl. Don’t see the clay, the sides, the form — see the SPACE the bowl contains. The usefulness of a bowl is its emptiness, not its walls. Similarly: your body is a bowl. Its value is the awareness it contains, not the form. See through form to space.

How to Practice

  1. Find a bowl: Choose a simple, empty bowl. Ceramic, wooden, or metal — material doesn’t matter. What matters is that it is empty and that you can see the interior space. Place it at comfortable viewing distance — arm’s length, slightly below eye level. A plain, undecorated bowl works best.
  2. Look at the bowl normally first: Spend one minute looking at the bowl as you usually would. Notice the clay, the glaze, the rim, the curve of the sides, the weight, the colour. This is how the mind normally perceives — it sees MATERIAL, FORM, OBJECT. The mind sees the walls, not the space.
  3. Shift attention to the space: Now deliberately shift: stop seeing the WALLS and start seeing the SPACE inside. The empty volume that the walls contain. The space has no colour, no texture, no weight. It is simply there — open, receptive, empty. Let the walls become background and the space become foreground.
  4. Let the material disappear: As you focus on the interior space, allow the material (clay, wood, metal) to fade from attention. It’s still there but you are no longer SEEING it. You are seeing THROUGH it to the emptiness it holds. The bowl becomes transparent to your awareness — only the space remains.
  5. Feel the space as primary: Lao Tzu said: “We shape clay into a pot, but it is the emptiness inside that holds whatever we want.” FEEL this truth. The space is what makes the bowl useful. Without the space, the bowl is just a lump of clay. The emptiness is the essence; the form is the servant of the emptiness.
  6. Apply to your body: Now close your eyes. Feel your body. Normally you feel its FORM — skin, bones, muscles, weight. Now shift: feel the SPACE inside. Your body is a bowl. What does it contain? Awareness. Consciousness. The interior space of the body is not blood and organs — experienced from within, it is an empty space filled with awareness.
  7. See through your own form: Just as you saw through the bowl’s walls to its space, now see through your body’s form to the awareness it contains. The body is useful not because it is flesh but because it houses consciousness. You are not the bowl — you are the space. You are not the body — you are the awareness.
  8. Expand the space: Once you feel the inner space clearly, notice: does it actually have boundaries? The bowl’s space seems bounded by the walls — but is your awareness actually bounded by the body? Feel for the edges of awareness. You may find: there are no edges. The “bowl” of the body may be more like a lens than a container — awareness flows through it infinitely in all directions.
  9. Rest as space: For the final minutes of practice, simply BE the space rather than the container. Identity shifts from form to emptiness, from walls to openness, from bowl to space. This is a profound reorientation — you are not a solid thing but an open space in which experience happens.
  10. Daily practice with objects: Throughout the day, look at containers — cups, rooms, buildings, the sky — and see the SPACE rather than the boundaries. Each container reminds you: the value is the emptiness, not the walls. And you are the most important container — a body-bowl full of infinite awareness.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner-friendly
Duration
20 min
Posture
Seated, eyes open (then closed)
Eyes
Open initially, then closed
Best for
Visual thinkers; those who learn through analogy and metaphor; people who appreciate Taoism and Lao Tzu; artists and designers

Signs It’s Working

  • A distinct perceptual shift when looking at the bowl — the space “pops forward” and the walls recede
  • A growing sense of the interior of your body as SPACE rather than as solid matter
  • Feeling lighter, more open, less dense as identity shifts from form to space
  • Spontaneously seeing the space in rooms, buildings, landscapes before seeing the structures
  • The body beginning to feel like a window rather than a wall — transparent rather than opaque
  • A quiet awe at the simplicity: you are space, not stuff

Common Pitfalls

  • Intellectualising the teaching without actually LOOKING at a bowl — the perceptual shift requires real visual practice
  • Choosing a decorated or full bowl — it must be empty and simple for the space to become visible
  • Not spending enough time on the first perceptual shift (seeing space in the bowl) before moving to the body
  • Confusing “imagining space” with actually PERCEIVING space — this is a perceptual technique, not a visualisation
  • Getting metaphysically abstract too quickly — start simple, start physical, start with an actual bowl

Questions From The Book

Why a bowl specifically and not any other object?

Chapter 73, "The Space Within"

Osho explains: a bowl is the perfect teaching object because it makes the relationship between form and space visible. A bowl is OBVIOUSLY about its space — without the hollow interior, it cannot hold anything. This makes the perceptual shift easier than with a solid object. Once you can see space in a bowl (easy), you learn to see space everywhere (harder). The bowl is the beginner’s aid — it teaches the eyes and the mind a new way of seeing before that seeing is applied to the body and the self.

What does Osho mean by “become aware”?

Chapter 73

Osho says: the sutra says “in a few moments become aware” — meaning that when you see past the material to the space, awareness ITSELF becomes visible. You become aware of awareness. The space in the bowl is not just empty air — it is a pointer to the space of consciousness. When form drops away, what remains is awareness knowing itself. “Become aware” means: the space you see in the bowl IS your own awareness, looking at itself through a physical metaphor.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This is a figure-ground reversal technique. Normally perception privileges solid objects (figure) over empty space (ground). By deliberately inverting this — seeing space as primary and form as secondary — you train the mind to recognise that consciousness (space) is fundamental and objects (forms) arise within it. This is the perceptual equivalent of the metaphysical insight: awareness is primary; contents are secondary.

Design thinking parallel: Architects and designers know that negative space (emptiness) is what makes a design work. A room is useful because of its space, not its walls. This technique applies that design principle to the self: you are useful (alive, aware, capable) because of the inner space of consciousness, not because of the body’s form.

One-line essenceA bowl’s value is its emptiness, not its walls. Your value is your awareness, not your body. Look through form to space, and discover what you truly are.
Sutra 32 of 112
Category: Senses · Tags: fresh-seeing · beginner-mind · wonder · ordinary · beauty
See as if for the first time a beauteous person or an ordinary object.

In Plain English

Look at anything — your partner, a cup, a tree, your own hand — as if you have NEVER seen it before. Drop all familiarity, all memory, all labels. See it with the freshness of a child’s first encounter. When you look at your spouse as if for the first time, love rushes back. When you look at a leaf as if for the first time, wonder arises. Familiarity is the death of perception. This technique kills familiarity and resurrects wonder.

How to Practice

  1. Choose something familiar: The more familiar the better. Your own hand. Your coffee cup. The view from your window. Your partner’s face. The wall of your room. Choose something so familiar that you have stopped SEEING it entirely — it has become invisible through repetition.
  2. Forget its name: The first step is to drop the label. Don’t call it a “hand” or a “cup” or a “tree.” Names create the illusion of knowing. You say “tree” and think you know what you’re looking at. You don’t. You know a word. Drop the word and see the THING.
  3. Forget its function: Don’t think about what it’s FOR. A cup is not “something to drink from” — that’s function, not reality. A hand is not “something to grasp with.” Drop all utility. See it as a being, not a tool.
  4. Forget your history with it: Drop all memories associated with this object or person. Your partner is not “the person I married in 2015” or “the person who annoyed me yesterday.” All history creates a screen between you and the reality. See them fresh, NOW, without the weight of the past.
  5. See with a child’s eyes: Imagine you are a baby seeing this for the very first time. You have no concepts, no language, no categories. Just pure visual (and tactile, and olfactory) perception. Raw sensation without interpretation. What do you ACTUALLY see? Colours. Shapes. Textures. Light and shadow. Movement or stillness. That’s all. And it is miraculous.
  6. Stay with the seeing for 5 minutes: Don’t glance and move on. STAY with the object. Keep seeing it fresh. The mind will try to reassert labels (“it’s just a cup”). Each time, drop the label again and return to raw seeing. Five minutes of truly fresh seeing can transform your relationship with reality.
  7. Apply to your partner or loved ones: This is where the technique becomes transformative for relationships. Look at your partner — right now — as if you have never seen them. See their face as a landscape you are exploring for the first time. The curve of an eyebrow. The texture of skin. The mystery of eyes looking back at you. Love returns instantly when familiarity dies.
  8. Apply to your own body: Look at your hand as if seeing a hand for the first time ever. Notice: five fingers, each different, each articulated, each capable of independent movement. Skin that stretches and folds. Nails growing from within. Veins carrying life beneath the surface. Your hand is an absolute miracle — you just stopped noticing.
  9. Walk through your home as a stranger: Walk through your living space as if you have never been here before. A visitor from another planet seeing human habitation for the first time. Every object becomes fascinating. The familiar becomes extraordinary. THIS is how a child experiences the world — and why children are so full of wonder.
  10. Make it daily: Choose one thing each day to see freshly. Rotate: Monday, your hand. Tuesday, the sky. Wednesday, your partner’s face. Thursday, your food. Friday, a tree. Over time, the fresh-seeing becomes a permanent quality — the eyes lose their deadness and reality regains its magic.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner
Duration
Throughout the day (5 min per object)
Posture
Any
Eyes
Open — wide, soft, receptive
Best for
Those in long-term relationships needing renewal; depressed or bored people; anyone who feels life has lost its magic; artists seeking fresh perception; children of all ages

Signs It’s Working

  • A sudden rush of wonder at something completely ordinary
  • The familiar becomes strange and beautiful — your own hand amazes you
  • Love or tenderness arises when seeing a partner or friend freshly
  • Colours appear more vivid, textures more distinct, the world more three-dimensional
  • A feeling of being newly alive — as if you just arrived on Earth this morning
  • Boredom disappears — how can you be bored when everything is seen for the first time?

Common Pitfalls

  • Trying to THINK freshness rather than SEE freshly — the technique is perceptual, not intellectual
  • Saying “I know this trick, it’s just reframing” — the mind will try to domesticate the technique; let it remain wild
  • Only applying it to “beautiful” things — apply it equally to ordinary, ugly, and mundane things; everything becomes extraordinary when seen fresh
  • Expecting permanent transformation from one attempt — like any muscle, fresh-seeing needs daily exercise
  • Forgetting to include PEOPLE in the practice — seeing people freshly is where the technique becomes most powerful and most healing

Questions From The Book

Why does Osho say familiarity destroys love?

Chapter 57, "Seeing Fresh"

Osho is passionate on this point: love does not die because of time or incompatibility. Love dies because of familiarity — because we stop SEEING the other person. After a year of marriage, we see not the person but our accumulated image of them. We respond not to THEM (who is always changing, always new) but to our MEMORY of them (which is fixed, dead, past). The moment you see your partner fresh — without the image — love floods back. It never left; it was just buried under familiarity.

How is this related to beginner’s mind in Zen?

Chapter 57

Osho acknowledges the connection: Shunryu Suzuki’s “beginner’s mind” (shoshin) is the same quality — approaching each moment without preconceptions. But Osho says the Vigyan Bhairav Tantra is more specific: it gives you a METHOD for recovering beginner’s mind rather than just describing it as a goal. The method: actively forget the name, the function, and the history. Three subtractions, and freshness returns automatically.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Habituation is the brain’s most fundamental mechanism — repeated stimuli produce decreasing neural responses. We literally stop perceiving what is familiar. This technique is a deliberate de-habituation practice: by stripping away labels, functions, and memories, you bypass the brain’s efficiency filters and return to raw perception. The result is what scientists call “enhanced perceptual processing” — seeing more detail, more colour, more depth.

Relationship research: Gottman’s research on long-term couples confirms that “turning toward” (attending freshly to your partner) is the single strongest predictor of relationship longevity. Couples who continue to see each other with curiosity and interest stay together; those who operate on autopilot drift apart. This technique is a systematic method for staying awake within intimacy.

One-line essenceFamiliarity is a veil. Remove it, and every cup is a miracle, every face is a universe, every moment is the first moment of creation.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 23 · Sutras 33–36 · 4 techniques

Several More Looking Methods

Osho's framing

Osho sets up a clean diagram before these sutras: consciousness sits at the centre, the senses sit on the boundary, and objects lie beyond it. The senses are a doorway that opens both ways - outward to the world, or inward to the centre - and the distance is the same. Quoting Zen master Bokuju, he says nirvana is no farther away than the marketplace. We turn outward only because bodily needs pull us out; nothing yet pulls us in. The need to move inward must first be created, and Osho says it is born from one thing - the awareness of one's own death. Animals see others die; only a human can see himself dying. As he puts it

If you are not aware of death, you have not yet become man.

— Osho
Sutra 33 of 112
Category: Emptiness & Void · Tags: sky · blue · serenity · beyond-clouds · simplicity
Simply by looking into the blue sky beyond clouds, the serenity.

In Plain English

Look at the sky — but specifically BEYOND the clouds, into the pure blue. The clouds are thoughts; the blue is the void. Don’t focus on clouds (thoughts) — see PAST them to the infinite blue emptiness. Applied internally: look past your thoughts to the space they move in. That space is the inner void. It is always serene because nothing disturbs it. Thoughts disturb themselves; the space is untouched.

How to Practice

  1. Find a sky with clouds: Unlike the pure-blue-sky technique (which requires cloudless sky), this one NEEDS clouds. The clouds are essential — they are the analogy for thoughts. You need both clouds AND blue to practise this. A partly cloudy day is ideal.
  2. Lie down or sit comfortably: Position yourself where you can see a large expanse of sky without straining your neck. Lying on your back is ideal. Let the body be completely relaxed and supported.
  3. Notice the clouds: First, see the clouds. Watch them move, change shape, dissolve, and reform. They are constantly shifting — never stable, never permanent. This is exactly like your thoughts: constantly shifting, reforming, dissolving.
  4. Now look BEYOND them: Shift your gaze — and your attention — from the clouds to the blue sky BEHIND them. The blue is always there, whether clouds cover it or not. It doesn’t move, doesn’t change, doesn’t react to the clouds. It simply IS. The clouds pass through it without affecting it.
  5. Feel the analogy: The blue sky is your awareness — the void. The clouds are your thoughts. Thoughts pass through awareness like clouds pass through sky. The sky is never disturbed by clouds, no matter how dark or turbulent. Similarly, the void of awareness is never disturbed by thoughts, no matter how intense.
  6. Apply it inwardly: Close your eyes. Now see your THOUGHTS as clouds. Don’t engage with them — notice them moving across the inner sky. And now look PAST them — look at the space they move in. The inner blue sky. The void. It is always there, always serene, always untouched. Thoughts cannot damage it any more than clouds can damage the sky.
  7. Rest in the blue: Both externally (staring past clouds) and internally (seeing past thoughts), REST your attention in the blue. Let the clouds/thoughts do whatever they do — they are not your concern. Your home is the sky, not the clouds. The void, not the thoughts.
  8. The serenity: The sutra says “the serenity” — note the article “the.” Not “a” serenity (one among many) but THE serenity — the absolute, the only, the ultimate. The void is by nature serene. Nothing can disturb it because it has no form to be disturbed. When you rest there, you share its nature. You BECOME serene.
  9. Duration: Start with 20 minutes and extend naturally. Many practitioners find this so enjoyable that they naturally stay 40 minutes or more. There is no upper limit — the sky doesn’t run out of blue, and the void doesn’t run out of serenity.
  10. Daily reminder: Throughout the day, whenever you notice thoughts becoming turbulent (anxiety, anger, worry), remember: you are the sky, not the clouds. Look past the thoughts to the space they move in. Even a 3-second recognition of the inner sky brings instant relief. The void is always accessible — it is closer than your next thought.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner-friendly
Duration
20–40 min
Posture
Lying on back or seated with sky view
Eyes
Open (outer sky) then closed (inner sky)
Best for
Everyone; especially anxious minds; overthinkers; those who find the void concept frightening (the sky analogy makes it gentle); nature lovers

Signs It’s Working

  • A felt shift from “watching clouds” to “resting in blue” — attention moves from foreground to background
  • Thoughts become less personal, less urgent — they are just clouds passing
  • A spontaneous serenity that requires no effort to maintain
  • The inner sky feels vast, spacious, utterly peaceful
  • Emotions (even strong ones) begin to feel like weather — passing through, not defining you
  • A growing unshakeability in daily life — disturbances still come but don’t penetrate to the core

Common Pitfalls
