  • Engaging with the clouds/thoughts instead of seeing past them — getting caught in content rather than resting in context
  • Trying to push thoughts away or make the sky cloudless — the technique does NOT require thought-free states; it requires seeing PAST thoughts
  • Intellectualising the analogy without actually FEELING the spaciousness — the understanding must be bodily, not merely conceptual
  • Choosing a completely cloudless day (then there is no contrast, no “beyond”) or a completely overcast day (then the blue is not visible)
  • Making it effortful — this is the most effortless technique in the entire tantra; it works by relaxation, not by trying

Questions From The Book

Why does Osho say this is one of the most important techniques?

Chapter 61, "The Serenity of Blue"

Osho explains: because it is completely safe, completely natural, and directly experiential. There is no energy to mishandle, no complex visualisation, no danger of any kind. AND it gives the mind a perfect, liveable analogy for the relationship between awareness and thought. Once you truly understand “I am the sky, not the clouds,” 90% of psychological suffering ends instantly. Most suffering comes from identification WITH thoughts (clouds). This technique breaks that identification permanently.

What if I live in a place without blue sky?

Chapter 61

Osho says: the outer sky is only the beginning. Once the analogy is understood, you can practise entirely with the INNER sky. Close your eyes and look past thoughts to the space they inhabit. That inner space is always blue (metaphorically) — always clear, always serene, always available. The outer sky is training wheels; the inner sky is the real practice. You can do this on a bus, in a meeting, in bed at night. The void is not weather-dependent.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This is a perfect metaphor for metacognition — the awareness OF awareness. Normally we are lost IN our thoughts (staring at clouds). This technique shifts attention to the SPACE that contains thoughts (the sky). In psychology, this shift is called “cognitive defusion” — unhooking from thought content to recognise thoughts as passing mental events rather than reality.

ACT therapy parallel: Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) uses exactly this metaphor: “You are the sky; thoughts and feelings are the weather.” Research shows this single shift — from identification with mental content to identification with awareness itself — dramatically reduces anxiety, depression, and psychological rigidity. This 5,000-year-old technique anticipated modern psychotherapy by millennia.

One-line essenceClouds cannot damage the sky. Thoughts cannot damage the void. Look past your thoughts to the blue serenity that was always there — and rest there. You ARE there.
Sutra 34 of 112
Category: Light & Gazing · Tags: listen · while · ultimate · mystical
Listen while the ultimate mystical teaching is imparted. Eyes still, without blinking, at once become absolutely free.

In Plain English

This is the classical Tantric transmission technique — the moment a master whispers his secret into the disciple’s ear. The disciple’s job is total receptivity: eyes open and unblinking, the body still, the mind a vacant ear. Osho is firm that this cannot just be “done” from a book; it is essentially a long waiting. The technique has two halves: the patient ripening, and the instant of total stillness when the words land in a no-mind.

How to Practice

  1. Be honest about what this sutra is. Osho calls it “a secret method.” It is meant for the moment a living master imparts his secret — sometimes a single word whispered in the ear — to a disciple who is ready. If you do not have such a master, this is preparation only: you are building the receptivity in advance.
  2. The first half is the preparation, and that is what most people actually have to do. Become someone who can wait without asking. Osho: “He is not to ask, because asking means desire. He is not to expect, because expectation means conditions. He is just to wait.”
  3. Practise the receptive posture daily. Sit before something you trust as teaching — a passage of a master’s words, a recording, even a photograph — or simply sit, as Lin Chi did on his master’s verandah, with no agenda at all.
  4. Keep the eyes open and as still as possible. No winking. Osho is specific: even a slight movement of the eyes shows inner disturbance. Whenever the eyes flicker, the inside has shifted; let them be stones again.
  5. At the same time, become the ear. Osho’s exact image: “Just become an empty ear with no movement within.” Don’t listen for something — that becomes thinking. Just be open.
  6. Drop expectation altogether. If you are waiting for a particular word, a particular insight, that is the mind. Osho insists: “just waiting — not waiting for something, because then it will become thinking.” Pure waiting, without object.
  7. Don’t strain. The eyes can blink when they have to; tension defeats the technique. The stillness is from the inside — when the mind stops, the eyes stop on their own.
  8. This may take a long time. Lin Chi waited six years on the verandah, in silence, no reading, no talking. The old nun waited until the bamboo of her shoulder-pole broke and the water-pots fell. The technique is not the sitting; the technique is the inner ripening that the sitting allows.
  9. Notice that anything can become the trigger when you are ready — a master’s laughter, the sound of a gong, a pail of water breaking on a moonlit path. Don’t fix on what the moment must look like. The Tantric point: readiness is everything; the trigger is incidental.
  10. If you do receive teaching from a teacher you trust — even reading a sentence that strikes deep — bring this technique to it: stop the eyes, stop the mind, become the ear, let the words fall into vacancy. Don’t analyse afterwards. The seed is sown into emptiness.
  11. If no master is in your life, do not invent one and do not despair. Practise the inner posture daily. Osho says even Shiva’s own words bounce off if the soil is wrong; reverse the rule — if you become the soil, even a small thing will work.
  12. Conclude each sitting without judgement. The success of this technique can never be measured by the sitting itself; it shows in what you become.

Osho’s Key Insight

We live on the surface of ourselves – just at the fringe, the boundary. The senses are just on the boundary and your consciousness is way deep down at the center. We live in the senses; that is natural. But that is not the ultimate flowering, it is just the beginning. And when we are living in the senses, we are basically concerned with objects, because senses are irrelevant unless there is con...

Source: 23. Several More Looking Methods, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 35 of 112
Category: Light & Gazing · Tags: edge · deep · well · look
At the edge of a deep well look steadily into its depths until – the wondrousness.

In Plain English

Find a real deep well. Stand at the edge, lean over, and look down into its depth without thinking. Osho takes this sutra literally. The well’s depth is reflected in you; if you keep looking long enough, with no thought, you discover the same depth inside. The instruction ends with a single word — the wondrousness. You go on looking until wonder fills you.

How to Practice

  1. Find an actual deep well. Osho is not metaphorical here. A village well, an old stone well, anything with real darkness at the bottom. Failing that, anything with a similar quality of depth — a deep gorge, a quarry, a still dark pond, a chasm — can be used, but a well is best.
  2. Make sure the place is safe. Stand or kneel at the edge so you can lean over and look straight down. Don’t lean further than is steady. The technique requires settled attention, not vertigo.
  3. Look down into the depth. Eyes open. Don’t scan, don’t analyse, don’t identify the shapes you see. Osho: “Look! Do not think. I need not repeat it. Always remember that look means: look, do not think.”
  4. Stay there. Feel the depth pulling your sight further and further down. The well becomes a kind of mirror — the outer depth begins to suggest an inner depth.
  5. Don’t hurry the result. Osho: “Go on looking, go on looking, go on looking, day after day, month after month.” Make it a daily practice if you can. A long, patient gaze is the whole instruction.
  6. Whenever a thought slips in, drop it; return the gaze to the depth. Don’t fight the thought; just don’t feed it. The depth is the stronger object.
  7. Notice the relationship reversing. Like Chuang Tzu watching the river from the bridge until the river stopped and the bridge flowed, with long enough looking the fixed division between “outside well” and “inside me” loosens. The depth is the same depth.
  8. The sutra promises one thing only: the wondrousness. Osho explains this is not a vision, an experience, or a sensation. It is wonder — the simple sense of mystery suddenly all around and within you. Mind disappears; mystery descends.
  9. Don’t stop before that wonder comes. Most people give up after a few minutes because nothing seems to be happening. The technique is built for sustained gazing — if you only do it for ten minutes once, almost certainly nothing happens.
  10. If a real well is not accessible at all, Osho permits a substitute: any object with the quality of unfathomable depth that you can return to daily — a deep pool of clear water, a star-filled night sky used the same way (gazing into rather than at). But the well is preferred because it is dark, vertical, and silent.
  11. Keep silence around the practice. No conversation just before or just after. Walk away gently when you finish.
  12. The day the wondrousness arrives, you will not have to ask whether it has arrived. Until then: look, only look.

Osho’s Key Insight

We live on the surface of ourselves – just at the fringe, the boundary. The senses are just on the boundary and your consciousness is way deep down at the center. We live in the senses; that is natural. But that is not the ultimate flowering, it is just the beginning. And when we are living in the senses, we are basically concerned with objects, because senses are irrelevant unless there is con...

Source: 23. Several More Looking Methods, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 36 of 112
Category: Light & Gazing · Tags: look · object · slowly · withdraw
Look upon some object, then slowly withdraw your sight from it, then slowly withdraw your thought from it. Then.

In Plain English

Look at any object — a flower, a candle, a temple statue — until thinking has stopped and only the object remains. Then slowly turn your eyes away. The object disappears outwardly but the mental image of it stays. Now drop the inner image too. When the object has gone and the picture of the object has gone, you are left in the bare aloneness Shiva calls Then. That aloneness is the centre.

How to Practice

  1. Choose a single object and stay with it for the whole training. Osho’s example is a roseflower; a candle flame, a temple statue (the original purpose of the image, he says), or any single still object will work. Use the same object every day so the inner image is built up cleanly.
  2. Sit comfortably. Place the object at a comfortable distance, eye-level. Light it well enough to see clearly.
  3. Begin with looking, not thinking. Osho is firm: “Look means: look, do not think. If you think, it is not a look; then you have contaminated everything.” Just take in the object visually. Don’t name it, classify it, comment on it.
  4. Continue looking until thoughts subside on their own and the field of awareness has just one thing in it: this object. There is no hurry — this part may take many minutes the first few times.
  5. When you can feel that “simply the flower is there in your mind, nothing else,” begin the first withdrawal. Slowly slide your gaze a little away from the object — off centre, then further, until the object is no longer in focus. Don’t close the eyes yet.
  6. The outer object is now out of focus, but the mental image will be vivid in your inner mirror. Stay with that image, with the eyes still open and the gaze loose.
  7. Now close the eyes. The image of the object is the only thing in awareness. Don’t try to make it sharper; don’t cling to it. Just let it be there.
  8. Begin the second withdrawal. Don’t fight the image, don’t push it away. Osho’s precise instruction: become indifferent to it. Feel as though you have moved away from the image, as you moved your eyes away from the object — but inwardly. The image fades on its own when you stop facing it.
  9. Wait. The image will dim. Then it will dissolve. Don’t reach for it again when it goes.
  10. What remains is what Shiva calls simply Then. No object, no image, only you, alone. Don’t add anything. Don’t look for an experience. This bare aloneness is the realisation.
  11. If thoughts re-enter, repeat the technique. The skill is in the careful sequence: outer first, inner second. Trying to drop both at once normally fails — that is why the sutra splits the work in two.
  12. Practise daily with the same object. Osho says the second withdrawal becomes much easier once the first is stable. After some weeks the whole sequence can be done in a few minutes, on demand.

Osho’s Key Insight

We live on the surface of ourselves – just at the fringe, the boundary. The senses are just on the boundary and your consciousness is way deep down at the center. We live in the senses; that is natural. But that is not the ultimate flowering, it is just the beginning. And when we are living in the senses, we are basically concerned with objects, because senses are irrelevant unless there is con...

Source: 23. Several More Looking Methods, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 25 · Sutras 37–38 · 2 techniques

From Words to Pure Words to Being

Osho's framing

Osho opens by noting that Sartre titled his autobiography simply "Words" - and that, he says, is the autobiography of every human being. We are stuffed with words from waking to sleeping. The self, however, lives below, behind, around the mind - never inside it. The mistake is identification: we believe we are the mind, and that single error is the root of every misery. Why do we identify so deeply? Because thinking has been the human survival weapon, against animals, nature, and other people. The proof is simple - call someone's body ill and they shrug; call their mind ill and they take offense. These sound techniques begin to unhook us from words.

You are filled with words, and this process of words continues the whole day, even in the mind.

— Osho
Sutra 37 of 112
Category: Sound & Mantra · Tags: letters · feeling · dissolution · freedom
Devi, imagine the Sanskrit letters in these honey-filled foci of awareness, first as letters, then more subtly as sounds, then as most subtle feeling. Then, leaving them aside, be free.

In Plain English

Take any word or syllable. First see it as written form. Then hear it as pure sound, dropping the meaning. Then feel it as vibration in the body. Then drop even that — and what remains is freedom, the awareness prior to all form.

How to Practice

  1. Choose a word — a Sanskrit syllable like AUM, or any word that resonates.
  2. Visualise it as written letters. See the shape in your mind's eye.
  3. Now drop the visual — hear only the sound. Repeat it mentally or aloud.
  4. Drop the meaning of the sound. Let it become pure vibration, a humming in the body.
  5. Drop even the vibration. Leave everything aside.
  6. Rest in what remains — the silence after all layers have been removed.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
20–30 min
Posture
Seated, relaxed
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Intellectual types; those who live in words; writers and thinkers

Signs It's Working

  • Each layer genuinely dissolves — you can feel the shift from form to sound to vibration to silence
  • A moment of pure emptiness after the final "leaving aside"
  • The word loses all meaning, becomes absurd, then vanishes
  • A sense of spacious freedom behind all words

Common Pitfalls

  • Rushing through the layers — spend real time at each stage
  • Clinging to the vibration stage because it feels pleasant — the instruction is to leave it aside too
  • Choosing a word with too much emotional charge — start with a neutral syllable

Questions From The Book

Why Sanskrit specifically? Can any language work?

Chapter 25, "From Words to Pure Sounds to Being"

Osho clarifies: the sutra mentions Sanskrit because it was the original language of the text. But any language works — because the technique moves away from language entirely. The point is to go from the gross (written form) through the subtle (sound) to the most subtle (feeling) and then beyond. The language is the starting material, not the destination.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Language is layered: symbol (visual) → phoneme (auditory) → meaning (cognitive) → felt-sense (somatic). This technique deconstructs that stack, peeling each layer off. What remains is awareness itself — prior to representation.

Linguistic parallel: Semantic satiation (repeating a word until it loses meaning) is the accidental, trivial version of this. The sutra makes it intentional and takes it further — past meaning-loss into body-vibration, then past even that into formlessness.

One-line essenceWords veil the silence. Peel them back — form, sound, feeling, nothing — and the silence was always already here.
Sutra 38 of 112
Category: Sound & Mantra · Tags: listening · waterfall · omnipresence · immersion
Bathe in the centre of sound, as in the continuous sound of a waterfall. Or, by putting the fingers in the ears, hear the sound of sounds.

In Plain English

Immerse yourself in ambient sound — traffic, rain, a fan, a waterfall — until you are surrounded by it from all directions. Then find the centre of that sound-sphere: that centre is you, the listener. Or plug your ears and listen to the inner sound of your own body.

How to Practice

  1. Sit anywhere sound is continuous — near traffic, running water, a fan, rain.
  2. Close your eyes. Let the sound arrive from all directions simultaneously.
  3. Don't label individual sounds. Let them merge into one ocean of vibration.
  4. Feel yourself at the centre of this sphere of sound. You are the hub; sound is the wheel.
  5. Rest at the centre. The sounds come to you; you don't go to them.
  6. Alternative: press thumbs gently into ears. Listen to the rushing inner sound (blood, neural activity). Stay with it for 10–20 minutes.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner
Duration
15–20 min
Posture
Any
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Auditory learners; city-dwellers; those who find silence intimidating

Signs It's Working

  • Individual sounds merge into one continuous field
  • You feel centred, as if sound is arriving at you from all directions equally
  • A sense of spaciousness or vastness opens
  • The distinction between "inside" and "outside" blurs

Common Pitfalls

  • Labelling sounds — "that's a car, that's a bird" keeps the mind active
  • Choosing harsh or jarring environments — start with smooth, continuous sounds
  • Pressing too hard on the ears (alternative method) — gentle pressure only

Questions From The Book

How does listening to noise lead to silence?

Chapter 25, "Bathe in Sound"

Osho explains: the technique doesn't fight noise — it uses it. When you merge with the totality of sound without preference, the labelling mind stops. The mind needs to distinguish, categorise, and name. When sound is allowed as one mass, the mind has nothing to do — and falls silent naturally. The noise continues; you find silence at its centre.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This is open-monitoring meditation applied specifically to sound. Instead of focusing on one sound (concentrative), you receive all sounds equally. The brain's auditory cortex, overwhelmed by non-discriminative input, eventually quiets the categorisation circuits. What remains is pure listening — awareness without object.

Modern parallel: Sound baths (singing bowls, gongs) work on this principle — saturate the auditory field so the mind gives up trying to track individual sounds. This ancient sutra teaches you to do it with whatever sounds already exist around you.

One-line essenceYou cannot find silence by fighting noise. Drown in the noise — and at its very centre, silence waits.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 27 · Sutras 39–41 · 3 techniques

Soundlessness, Soundfulness and Total Awareness

Osho's framing

Osho threads modern physics into Tantra here. Just as physicists now postulate anti-matter - a hole of nothingness behind every dense object - Tantra has always said anti-sound, silence, sits just behind every sound. They are two faces of one coin. Sound can therefore be used as a doorway into soundlessness, mind into no-mind. He notes the two schools: Sankhya (and J. Krishnamurti) insists mind cannot be used at all, only seen through; Yoga and Tantra insist mind must be used - but negatively, as a springboard that weakens itself. These chants of "Aum" work on the second principle.

Wherever there is sound, just behind it there is silence.

— Osho
Sutra 39 of 112
Category: Sound & Mantra · Tags: AUM · chanting · merging · vibration
Intone a sound, as AUM, slowly. As sound enters soundfulness, so do you.

In Plain English

Chant AUM (or any single tone) slowly and with total attention. As the sound fills you completely — until there is no gap between you and the sound — you enter the state Osho calls "soundfulness": sound without a separate listener.

How to Practice

  1. Sit upright. Take three slow breaths to settle.
  2. Inhale fully. On the exhale, chant "AUM" slowly — let each syllable (A-U-M) take several seconds.
  3. Feel the vibration in the belly (A), chest (U), and skull (M).
  4. Don't just hear the sound — become it. Let there be no listener separate from the chanting.
  5. As the echo fades at the end of each AUM, ride the silence for a moment before the next.
  6. Continue for 20–30 minutes. Let the chant become slower and slower over time.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner
Duration
20–30 min
Posture
Seated, upright (for full resonance)
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Everyone; especially those who feel disconnected from body; singers; vocal people

Signs It's Working

  • The boundary between "I" and "sound" dissolves
  • The body vibrates as one instrument
  • Time disappears; you don't know how long you've been chanting
  • After stopping, the silence is extraordinarily deep

Common Pitfalls

  • Chanting too fast — the instruction is slowly; slower than you think
  • Performing the sound rather than becoming it — drop aesthetics
  • Stopping too soon because the mind says "this is boring" — boredom is the edge; stay past it

Questions From The Book

What is "soundfulness" and how is it different from just hearing?

Chapter 27, "Soundlessness, Soundfulness"

Osho says: hearing implies a hearer and a heard — two things. Soundfulness is non-dual: the sound has filled you so completely that the listener has dissolved. It's like a sponge soaked in water — you can no longer find the sponge separate from the water. You become the vibration. That is the door — because if the listener has dissolved, what remains is pure consciousness.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Chanting AUM stimulates the vagus nerve through slow exhalation and laryngeal vibration. fMRI studies show AUM chanting deactivates the amygdala and default mode network more effectively than quiet rest. The "merging" experience correlates with reduced self-referential processing.

Overtone science: The three syllables A-U-M create distinct resonance chambers in the body. The transition between them produces overtones that the inner ear registers even when the conscious mind doesn't. These overtones may explain the "filling" quality.

One-line essenceChant slowly enough that you dissolve into the chant. When only sound remains, that is the doorway.
Sutra 40 of 112
Category: Sound & Mantra · Tags: beginning · awakening · subtle · alertness
In the beginning and gradual refinement of the sound of any letter, awake.

In Plain English

When you speak or chant any letter or syllable, bring total awareness to the very first instant the sound is born — the spark before it becomes full voice. That initial impulse, caught with alertness, is a moment of pure awakening.

How to Practice

  1. Choose any simple sound — "Ah", "Oh", "Mm", or a single letter.
  2. Before you voice it, notice the impulse — the micro-moment when intention becomes vibration.
  3. Slowly begin the sound. Catch the very first edge where silence becomes audible.
  4. As the sound develops, notice how it refines — becomes clearer, fuller.
  5. Stay alert through the entire arc: birth, growth, peak, decay, silence.
  6. Repeat. Each time, try to catch the beginning even earlier — the pre-sound impulse.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
15–20 min
Posture
Seated
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Detail-oriented minds; musicians; those who enjoy subtlety

Signs It's Working

  • You begin to perceive a micro-gap between intention and sound
  • The beginning of the sound feels electric, vivid
  • Alertness sharpens without tension
  • You start noticing "beginnings" in all experience, not just sound

Common Pitfalls

  • Analysing instead of feeling — this is about alertness, not thinking about alertness
  • Making the sound too quickly to catch the beginning — go extremely slowly
  • Getting frustrated when you "miss" it — the missing itself is part of sharpening awareness

Questions From The Book

Why focus on the beginning of a sound rather than the sound itself?

Chapter 27, "The Beginning of Sound"

Osho explains: the beginning is where creation happens. At the beginning of any phenomenon, energy is fresh, undivided, pure. Once the sound is established, the mind relaxes and goes unconscious again. But at the beginning, you must be totally present — because beginnings demand alertness. The technique exploits this natural demand.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Neuroscience calls this "event-related potential" — the brain's response to the onset of a stimulus is always stronger than to its continuation. This sutra aligns with that: catch the onset, because that's when attention naturally peaks. By making onset-catching deliberate, you train sustained alertness.

Mindfulness parallel: Noting practice in Vipassana ("arising, arising") is similar — catching phenomena at their birth point. This sutra focuses it specifically on sound, making it more tangible.

One-line essenceEvery sound has a birthday — a spark from silence into vibration. Catch that spark, and you are awake.
Sutra 41 of 112
Category: Sound & Mantra · Tags: music · strings · composite · omnipresence
While listening to stringed instruments, hear their composite central sound; thus omnipresence.

In Plain English

When listening to a sitar, guitar, or any stringed instrument, don't follow individual notes. Instead, listen for the one unified sound beneath all the notes — the "hum" that holds the music together. That central sound is omnipresent, and finding it makes you omnipresent too.

How to Practice

  1. Play or listen to any stringed instrument — sitar, tanpura, guitar, cello, harp.
  2. Close your eyes. Don't follow the melody or individual notes.
  3. Listen for the one continuous sound underneath — the drone, the resonance, the overtone that persists even as notes change.
  4. Let individual notes fade into background. Only the central hum matters.
  5. Merge with that hum. Feel it as the sound of existence itself.
  6. Stay in this listening for the duration of the music, and then in the silence after.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner
Duration
15–30 min (length of the music)
Posture
Any relaxed position
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Music lovers; those with good ears; anyone who finds silence difficult
Best instrument
Tanpura (Indian drone) or any sustained-resonance instrument

Signs It's Working

  • The music stops being "entertainment" and becomes a field
  • You hear a continuous hum or tone underneath the melody
  • A sense of being everywhere the sound is — not localised in the body
  • Deep emotional opening without sentimentality

Common Pitfalls

  • Following the melody — the melody is the surface; you want the depth
  • Choosing music with lyrics — words engage the language brain and pull you out
  • Intellectualising "omnipresence" — just feel the sound everywhere; the concept will follow

Questions From The Book

How can a stringed instrument create omnipresence?

Chapter 27, "The Composite Central Sound"

Osho explains: every stringed instrument produces overtones — sounds above and below the played note. When you stop tracking individual notes and listen to the totality, you hear something that has no single source. It seems to come from everywhere. That "everywhere" quality is what the sutra means by omnipresence. You merge with a sound that has no boundary — and therefore you, the listener, become boundless.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Psychoacoustics shows that our brain localises sounds by their transients (attacks). Sustained, resonant tones with rich overtones lose localisation — they seem to come from "everywhere." The sutra exploits this: by attending to the sustained resonance rather than the note-attacks, you shift from focused to diffuse auditory processing — and with it, your sense of self diffuses.

Nada yoga: This is the basis of the Indian tradition of Nada Yoga — using sound (particularly the tanpura drone) as a meditation vehicle. The drone has no melody, no rhythm — only pure resonance. It is this technique in instrument form.

One-line essenceBeneath every melody is one sound. Find it, and you find the sound of existence itself — which is everywhere, and so are you.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 29 · Sutras 42–44 · 3 techniques

Methods for the Dropping of Mind

Osho's framing

Tantra, Osho says, refuses the religious split between this world (sansara) and liberation (moksha). The two are not contradictory; the ultimate is hidden inside the immediate, here and now. The mind, however, is a prism: it divides whatever passes through it, and it cannot do otherwise - that is its nature. So a no-mind approach is needed to perceive wholeness. Because Tantra denies division, total acceptance becomes possible, and acceptance alone produces real relaxation. Religions that brand the world or the body as sin only generate guilt, neurosis and seriousness. Osho says every religion has an outer life-denying face and an inner esoteric core that, when healthy, is always tantric.

Unless you accept the world totally, you cannot be at ease within.

— Osho
Sutra 42 of 112
Category: Sound & Mantra · Tags: fading · feeling · heart · passage
Intone a sound audibly, then less and less audibly as feeling deepens into this silent harmony.

In Plain English

Chant any sound aloud. Gradually make it quieter — softer and softer — until only the feeling of the sound remains, without any audible vibration. The sound was the vehicle; the feeling is the destination.

How to Practice

  1. Choose a sound — AUM, a loved one's name, a favourite mantra, or any word.
  2. Begin chanting it at normal volume. Full voice.
  3. After a few minutes, reduce the volume — still audible, but softer.
  4. Continue reducing. Whisper. Then move to lip-only movement with no sound.
  5. Then drop the lips. Only the mental sound remains.
  6. Then drop the mental sound. Only the feeling — the vibration, the emotion, the quality — remains.
  7. Rest in that feeling. It is the silent harmony the sutra speaks of.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
25–30 min
Posture
Seated or lying
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Devotional types; people who feel more than think; heart-oriented seekers

Signs It's Working

  • The transition from sound to feeling is seamless — no gap, no jolt
  • The feeling lives on long after the sound has stopped
  • Tears, warmth, or tenderness may arise as the heart opens
  • The body feels harmonised — like a bell that has just stopped ringing but still vibrates

Common Pitfalls

  • Dropping the volume too fast — take at least 15 minutes to move from full voice to silence
  • Thinking "feeling" means emotion — it can be; but it can also be a bodily vibration, a quality of space
  • Getting stuck at the whisper level and never entering silence

Questions From The Book

What is the "feeling" that remains when sound disappears?

Chapter 29, "Sound as Passage toward Feeling"

Osho says: the sound always carries a feeling — like a boat carries a passenger. When the boat reaches the shore (silence), the passenger (feeling) steps off. The feeling is not an emotion specifically — it is the vibratory quality of the sound experienced in the heart rather than the ears. A-U-M has a feeling-tone of vastness and completion. Your name has a feeling-tone of identity. A lover's name has a feeling-tone of devotion. The technique uses sound to reach that tone, then discards the carrier.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Cognitive science distinguishes between "cold" processing (conceptual) and "hot" processing (felt-sense). Sound starts as auditory cortex activity (cold) but gradually recruits limbic areas (hot) through repetition and emotional association. By progressively fading the auditory input while maintaining attention, you shift entirely into felt-sense mode — embodied, non-verbal awareness.

Japa tradition: This is the classical progression of japa (mantra repetition): vaikhari (spoken) → upamshu (whispered) → manasik (mental) → ajapa (happening by itself, only feeling). This sutra makes that entire journey explicit in one sitting.

One-line essenceSound is the boat. Feeling is the shore. Let the boat dissolve, and you arrive where you always were.
Sutra 43 of 112
Category: Sound & Mantra · Tags: mouth · slightly · open · keep
With mouth slightly open, keep mind in the middle of the tongue. Or, as breath comes silently in, feel the sound “hh.”

In Plain English

Move the focus of mind out of the head and into the centre of the tongue. Open the mouth slightly — not for speaking, but as if you are about to speak — and place attention exactly in the middle of the tongue. Thinking, says Osho, is talking-within; the silent vibration of thought rises from a centre in the middle of the tongue. Hold awareness there, and the inner talking stops.

How to Practice

  1. Sit upright in any comfortable posture. Eyes closed.
  2. Open the mouth very slightly — just enough that the lips part. Osho is precise: “as if you are going to speak, not like when you are speaking, but like when you are just going to speak.”
  3. Don’t move the tongue. Let it rest naturally inside the open mouth.
  4. Now bring the whole of your attention — what Osho calls “the focus of the mind” — out of the head and down to the exact middle of the tongue. Feel as if you are not thinking from the head any longer; you are located in the centre of the tongue.
  5. This will feel strange. That is the point. Osho says you will discover a control-centre for thought right there in the middle of the tongue. Stay on it.
  6. Notice that thought is talking-within. Watch the tongue while you think a sentence: it actually vibrates very slightly. The vibrations come from the middle of the tongue outward. Confirm this for yourself; it is not a metaphor.
  7. When attention is fully placed at that centre, the inner talking begins to stop on its own. Don’t try to stop it — just be at the middle of the tongue. Thinking falls because its source is being held still by attention.
  8. Stay with this until you feel a quality Osho describes as solidity — an inner stillness, almost like becoming immovable. Thought is the inner motion; without it, you are momentarily out of motion.
  9. Now move on to the second half of the sutra (the “Or” option, which Osho says combines naturally with the first). Keep the mouth slightly open. As the breath comes in, listen for the soft sound “hh” — the natural hush of inhalation passing across the open mouth and tongue.
  10. Do not create the sound. Osho is firm: “If you create it, you have missed the point.” Listen only. The sound is barely audible — you must become very alert to hear it at all.
  11. Listen to “hh” only on the in-breath, not the out-breath. The out-breath carries you outward; you want the inward direction. Let each in-breath sound the soft “hh.”
  12. As you continue, you will notice the sound is not only on the tongue. It begins to be heard in the throat, very faintly. Then deeper still, in the heart. Osho: “When it reaches the heart, you have gone beyond mind.” Stay with whichever level of the “hh” you can hear, and the next level will open on its own.

Osho’s Key Insight

Tantra divides life into two dimensions: one is the sansara – that which is, the world; and the other is moksha – that which can be, the ultimate, the hidden which can become manifest. But there is no contradiction between these two. The hidden is just here and now, in the world – of course, unknown to you, but not non-existent. It is there. The ultimate and the immediate are not two things, bu...

Source: 29. Methods for the Dropping of Mind, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 44 of 112
Category: Sound & Mantra · Tags: center · sound
Center on the sound “aum” without any “a” or “m.”

In Plain English

Aum is not one sound; it is three sounds — a, u, m — fused together. Intone Aum and learn to hear the three separately inside it. Then drop the “a” and the “m,” leaving only the middle “u.” The technique is not really about Aum — it is a way of forcing the ear to become so sensitive that the mind cannot keep working alongside it. Osho calls it a method for those with a sensitive ear: musicians, poets, anyone whose hearing is alive.

How to Practice

  1. Sit comfortably in a quiet place where you can hear small sounds. The technique depends on auditory delicacy.
  2. Begin to chant Aum aloud, slowly. Pronounce it fully: a-u-m. Don’t rush. Each chant should last as long as a comfortable out-breath.
  3. While chanting, listen. Try to hear the three sounds inside the single Aum. They are infused together but, Osho says, distinct — very close, but separate.
  4. Don’t pronounce them as three: don’t say “a-u-m” in pieces. The chant remains one continuous sound. The work is in the hearing, not in the speaking.
  5. Continue chanting day after day. This may take time. Osho is honest: if your ear has not been trained, this is difficult. Train it — sit by a river or in the wind and listen for the changing nuances of natural sound. Sensitivity grows by use.
  6. One day the three sounds will separate clearly inside the chant. You will hear: a… u… m, woven into one breath.
  7. Now begin to soften the “a” at the start. Let it become smaller, less voiced, until it drops away.
  8. Then soften the “m” at the end the same way. The closing hum becomes less and less, until it too drops.
  9. What remains is only the middle “u” — pure, sustained, with no beginning “a” and no closing “m.” This sound has no name in ordinary speech; you can no longer call it Aum because the boundary-sounds are gone.
  10. Centre on this sound. Osho explains the secret: by now your ear is so finely engaged and your attention so absorbed that the mind has nothing left to do. The mantra is incidental — the real work was the sensitivity it forced.
  11. Don’t expect a particular experience. Osho recounts the boy who was given the Zen koan “hear the sound of one hand,” and after long sincere effort returned and said: “I have heard, but it is soundlessness.” The same may happen to you. The pure middle sound dissolves into a silent, attentive, alive emptiness.
  12. If you find this technique too subtle, Osho gives an honest verdict: it is not for everyone. Try the tongue-centre technique above (#43) or the “hh” instead. The 112 sutras are a buffet; pick what your ear can actually do.

Osho’s Key Insight

Tantra divides life into two dimensions: one is the sansara – that which is, the world; and the other is moksha – that which can be, the ultimate, the hidden which can become manifest. But there is no contradiction between these two. The hidden is just here and now, in the world – of course, unknown to you, but not non-existent. It is there. The ultimate and the immediate are not two things, bu...

Source: 29. Methods for the Dropping of Mind, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 31 · Sutras 45–47 · 3 techniques

From Sound to Inner Silence

Osho's framing

Osho sharpens the difference between philosophy, science and Tantra here. Philosophy thinks about ultimate truth; science discovers what is. Tantra is also a science, but its territory is the inner - a subjective experiment rather than speculation. The very word tantra, he points out, means technique, and so this book contains no doctrine, only one hundred and twelve experiments. He warns against chasing the ultimate: it is hidden inside the immediate, and by reaching past the immediate you lose both. He tells the joke of a priest asking the dying Mulla Nasruddin about the Trinity - and concludes

All philosophy is like this: it is asking puzzles while you are dying.

— Osho
Sutra 45 of 112
Category: Sound & Mantra · Tags: AH · exhale · release · death · spontaneity
Silently intone a word ending in "AH." Then in the "HH," effortlessly, the spontaneity.

In Plain English

Choose any word that ends with the sound "AH" (like "Allah," "Buddha," "mama," or simply "ahhh"). Intone it silently, emphasising the final "AH." That "AH" is an exhale, a release, a letting-go. In the effortless silence after the "H" sound — spontaneity, the uncaused, arises.

How to Practice

  1. Sit comfortably. Eyes closed.
  2. Choose a word ending in "AH" — or simply use the sound "Ahhh" alone.
  3. Silently (mentally) intone the word. Feel the "AH" as a complete exhale — everything leaving.
  4. At the end of the "AH," notice the trailing breath: "HH..." — a whisper of silence.
  5. In that "HH" — don't do anything. Don't intend, don't seek. Let whatever arises arise by itself.
  6. That is spontaneity — action without a doer, experience without an experiencer.
  7. Repeat for 20 minutes.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
20 min
Posture
Seated, relaxed
Eyes
Closed
Best for
People who hold tension; control-freaks; those who struggle to let go

Signs It's Working

  • The exhale feels like a complete release — physical and emotional
  • After the "AH," a moment of total emptiness — no doer
  • Something happens by itself: a smile, a sensation, a movement — without "you" doing it
  • A sense of dying and being reborn with each cycle

Common Pitfalls

  • Waiting for something spectacular in the "HH" — spontaneity is often subtle and quiet
  • Making the "AH" too aggressive — it should be a gentle, total exhale, not a shout
  • Trying to control what arises in the gap — control is the opposite of spontaneity

Questions From The Book

Why specifically "AH"? What is special about this sound?

Chapter 31, "From Sound to Inner Silence"

Osho explains: "AH" is the sound of complete exhalation — you cannot say "AH" while inhaling. It is the sound of letting go, the sound of death (the last breath is always an out-breath). By emphasising "AH," you practise a miniature death — a total release of holding. And in that release, life springs back fresh, unconditioned, spontaneous. It is renewal through surrender.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: The "AH" sound lengthens the exhale, which directly stimulates the vagus nerve and shifts the nervous system from sympathetic (fight-or-flight) to parasympathetic (rest-and-release). The "spontaneity" that follows is the body's natural response when the grip of conscious control loosens — what somatic therapists call "the unwind."

Somatic parallel: Peter Levine's Somatic Experiencing uses spontaneous micro-movements after release as signs of trauma discharge. This sutra does the same thing: create a total exhale (release), then let whatever needs to happen, happen — without directing it.

One-line essence"AH" is the sound of letting go. In the silence after, life moves by itself — and that movement is grace.
Sutra 46 of 112
Category: Sound & Mantra · Tags: stopping · ears · pressing · rectum
Stopping ears by pressing and the rectum by contracting, enter the sound.

In Plain English

Press your fingers into your ears so all outer sound is shut out, and at the same time pull the muscles of the anus upward and contract them. Two doors close at once. With both shut, an inner sound — a kind of pillar of soundlessness — appears inside the body. Enter it. Without the rectum contracted, Osho says, this very subtle sound leaks out and you cannot find it.

How to Practice

  1. Sit upright in a comfortable posture — cross-legged on the floor, or on a firm chair with feet flat on the ground. Spine straight.
  2. Use your thumbs or index fingers to press firmly into both ears, sealing the ear canals. Press hard enough that outer sound is genuinely shut out. The other fingers can rest on the head or face.
  3. At the same time, pull the rectum (anal sphincter and pelvic-floor muscles) upward and contract. This is the same muscle action used to stop urination mid-flow, but with the anal area too — a firm, lifting squeeze. Hold the contraction.
  4. So you have closed two openings simultaneously: the ears above, the rectum below. The body is now sealed at both ends along its central channel.
  5. Close your eyes. Don’t do anything else. Just be inside the closed body.
  6. Listen. With outer sound shut out, an inner sound is audible — usually first as a low hum or hiss, sometimes as a high tone. Osho calls it “a pillar of sound” rising in you. Many people normally don’t hear it because, he says, the rectum is the opening through which this very subtle sound escapes; once you contract, the sound is held inside and becomes vivid.
  7. Don’t name what you hear, don’t classify it. Just let it be loud in you. The instruction is “enter the sound” — not analyse it, not chase it, but move into it as into a room.
  8. Let the sound become bigger than you. The body becomes a vessel filled with this inner pillar. Osho says it produces a deep feeling of fulfilment, as if the whole world has stopped and time has stopped.
  9. If the rectum contraction tires, release briefly and re-contract. Don’t strain. The contraction must be firm but not painful. The same with the ear-pressure: firm seal, not crushing.
  10. Stay 10–20 minutes the first times. As the inner sound becomes available you can sit longer.
  11. Release slowly. First release the rectum. Then take the fingers out of the ears one at a time. Sit a moment with eyes closed before opening them. Outer sound will seem unusually crude for a few minutes — this is normal.
  12. This is a body technique. Osho is clear that most modern people are insensitive to the body and so miss it; if at first nothing happens, treat your body as a sensitive instrument that needs a few sessions to wake up. Practise daily.

Osho’s Key Insight

Tantra is not a philosophy. Rather, it is a science with one difference: science is objective, Tantra is subjective. But still, it is a science and not a philosophy. Philosophy thinks about the truth, the unknown, the ultimate; science tries to discover what is. Science enters the immediate; philosophy thinks of the ultimate. Philosophy is always looking toward the sky; science is more down to ...

Source: 31. From Sound to Inner Silence, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 47 of 112
Category: Sound & Mantra · Tags: name · identity · mantra · dissolution
Enter the sound of your name and, through this sound, all sounds.

In Plain English

Repeat your own name as a mantra. Not to reinforce identity, but to dissolve it — because when a name is repeated enough, it loses meaning and becomes pure sound. Through your name, enter sound itself. Through sound itself, enter silence.

How to Practice

  1. Sit quietly. Close your eyes.
  2. Begin repeating your own name — aloud at first, then softer, then mentally.
  3. Don't think about who the name belongs to. Let it be just a sound.
  4. As meaning drops away, the name becomes a vibration — like any other mantra.
  5. Follow that vibration inward. It leads to the same silence as any other sound.
  6. From that silence, all sounds arise — including your name. Rest in the source.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner
Duration
20 min
Posture
Any
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Those with a strong sense of identity; ego-oriented types; people who don't resonate with Sanskrit mantras

Signs It's Working

  • Your name starts to sound strange, foreign, meaningless
  • A gap opens between "you" and "your name" — you realise you are not the name
  • The name becomes as arbitrary and beautiful as birdsong
  • A taste of what you are beyond all names

Common Pitfalls

  • Reinforcing identity — if you feel more "me" rather than less, you're holding the meaning too tight
  • Using a nickname that triggers emotions — use your full given name for neutrality
  • Comparing this to affirmations — this is the opposite; you're emptying the name, not filling it

Questions From The Book

Won't repeating my name make me more egotistic?

Chapter 31, "Your Name as Mantra"

Osho says: the ego uses the name as an identification. But repetition destroys identification. Say any word five hundred times and it becomes nonsense — it loses all connection to meaning. That is exactly the point. You are using the ego's own tool (the name) to dissolve the ego. It is homeopathic: the poison becomes the medicine when given in the right way. After enough repetition, you stand behind the name — and for the first time, you know you are not it.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Semantic satiation — the well-documented phenomenon where repeating any word causes it to temporarily lose meaning — is the mechanism here. But the sutra goes beyond the linguistic trick: it uses your most identity-loaded word (your name) to create a visceral experience of "I am not my name." That disidentification is liberating.

IFS parallel: Internal Family Systems therapy distinguishes "Self" from "parts." Your name belongs to a part — the social identity. When the name dissolves, you contact Self — the awareness beneath all roles. This sutra achieves that unburdening through sound alone.

One-line essenceYour name is the first sound that bound you. Repeat it until it unbinds you — and discover who was here before the name.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 33 · Sutras 48–52 · 5 techniques

The Spirituality of the Tantric Sex Act

Osho's framing

Freud said man is born neurotic; Osho calls this only half true. The newborn is born real, with feeling intact - society then drives the split. To be loved by his parents the child must suppress what he feels and fit a code, and slowly the feeling self is buried under the thinking self. Once split, even our love becomes head-talk. Real needs (love, contact) get replaced by symbolic ones (food, fame, attention from crowds). These tantric sutras - including the famous one on sex - work as instruments of reunion, returning a person to the feeling centre. Sex, properly approached, can throw you back into wholeness, which is why it opens this set.

Even when you say that you feel, you only think that you feel.

— Osho
Sutra 48 of 112
Category: Tantric Union · Tags: sex · fire · beginning · awareness · energy
At the start of sexual union, keep attentive on the fire in the beginning, and so continuing, avoid the embers in the end.

In Plain English

During lovemaking, bring total awareness to the BEGINNING — the initial arousal, the first touch, the fire igniting. Stay at the beginning. Don’t rush toward climax (the “embers”). By keeping attention at the fire’s start, sexual energy rises upward rather than discharging downward. The act becomes meditation.

How to Practice

  1. Set the intention beforehand: Before lovemaking begins, both partners (or yourself, if practising with self-generated arousal) should agree: this is meditation, not performance. There is no goal, no climax to reach, no timeline. The BEGINNING is the destination. Discuss this openly — shared intention is crucial.
  2. Slow everything down: Begin physical contact extremely slowly. The first touch, the first kiss, the first caress — let each last ten times longer than usual. Bring total attention to the SENSATION of beginning. Feel the fire igniting — the first warmth, the first stirring of energy. This is where the technique lives.
  3. Keep attention on the fire, not the goal: As arousal builds, the mind will want to rush forward toward climax. This is the habit pattern. Instead, bring attention BACK to the beginning quality — the freshness, the first spark, the igniting. Stay at the point of ignition. Feel the energy at its SOURCE, not its destination.
  4. Notice where energy moves: Sexual energy naturally moves downward toward discharge. With awareness at the beginning (the source), the energy begins to move UPWARD — through the belly, the heart, the throat. You may feel warmth rising through the spine. Don’t force this — simply keep awareness at the fire’s start and notice where energy naturally wants to flow.
  5. Avoid the embers: “Embers” means the end — climax, discharge, conclusion. The sutra says: avoid this. Not through suppression or tension, but through ATTENTION. When attention remains at the beginning, the end simply doesn’t arrive. The fire keeps burning without becoming embers. Energy circulates rather than discharges.
  6. Ride the plateau: With practice, you discover a “plateau” state — highly aroused, deeply present, energy circulating upward, but without moving toward climax. This plateau can be sustained for extended periods (30 minutes, an hour, longer). The longer you stay, the more the energy transforms — from sexual heat to spiritual luminosity.
  7. Let the body be totally relaxed: Tension drives energy toward discharge. Relaxation allows energy to rise. Keep the body soft, especially the pelvis, belly, and jaw. Breathe deeply and slowly. A relaxed body is the vessel through which energy rises; a tense body is a closed system that can only discharge.
  8. The transformation: When sexual energy rises upward through awareness rather than discharging downward through tension, something remarkable happens: the energy reaches the heart (you feel overwhelming love), the throat (you may want to sing or cry), and the crown (states of meditation, bliss, ego-dissolution). The same energy that creates children can create consciousness.
  9. After practice: Lie together in stillness for at least 15 minutes. Don’t talk immediately. Let the energy settle. You may feel vibrating, expanded, deeply peaceful. This afterglow is the residue of transformed energy — savour it. It continues working even after the practice ends.
  10. Solo practice: This technique can be practised alone. During self-generated arousal, apply the same principle: stay at the beginning, keep attention on the fire of initial arousal, avoid rushing toward conclusion. The energy rises the same way. A partner is helpful but not essential.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Advanced
Duration
30–60 min
Posture
Any comfortable position for lovemaking
Eyes
Soft gaze or closed
Best for
Couples practising together; individuals comfortable with sexual energy; those seeking to transform desire rather than suppress it

Signs It’s Working

  • Sexual energy feels like it is rising upward through the spine rather than building toward discharge
  • A plateau state emerges — highly aroused yet deeply peaceful, without urgency
  • The heart opens during lovemaking — feelings of overwhelming love, tenderness, gratitude
  • Time seems to stop — the experience becomes timeless, meditative
  • After practice: deep peace, luminosity, expanded awareness that lasts hours
  • Gradually, the need for climax diminishes — the journey becomes more satisfying than the destination

Common Pitfalls

  • Using tension or suppression to “avoid the embers” — this creates frustration, not transformation; the key is AWARENESS, not control
  • Making it performance-oriented — trying to “achieve” something defeats the purpose; this is surrender, not accomplishment
  • Disconnecting from the partner by becoming too internally focused — stay present WITH the other while being aware of energy
  • Guilt or shame about using sexuality as meditation — tantra views sexual energy as sacred; release religious conditioning
  • Forcing the energy upward rather than allowing it — relaxation and awareness guide energy naturally; force creates blockage

Questions From The Book

Why does Shiva use “fire” and “embers” as metaphors?

Chapter 41, "The Fire in the Beginning"

Osho explains: fire is alive, dynamic, rising upward. Embers are dead, spent, sinking downward. The beginning of sexual energy is like fire — alive, upward-moving, transformative. The climax is like embers — the fire has spent itself, the energy collapses. By staying with the fire (the living, rising energy) and avoiding the embers (the spent, collapsed energy), you ride the upward flame indefinitely. The metaphor teaches the direction: UP with the fire, not DOWN with the embers.

Is this technique about suppressing orgasm?

Chapter 41

Osho is emphatic: NO. Suppression creates tension and neurosis. This technique is about TRANSCENDING orgasm through awareness, not suppressing it through will. When awareness is total at the beginning, the energy naturally transforms rather than discharges. It is not that you STOP the orgasm; rather, the orgasm is replaced by something higher — a full-body, continuous state of bliss that makes genital orgasm seem small by comparison. You don’t lose anything; you gain everything.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Neurologically, orgasm is a discharge event — a rapid release of accumulated tension. But the arousal phase activates the parasympathetic nervous system, producing states of deep relaxation and expanded awareness. By extending the arousal phase indefinitely (staying in “the fire”) while avoiding the sympathetic discharge event (“the embers”), you maintain a state of expanded consciousness sustained by sexual energy.

Research parallel: Studies on non-orgasmic sexual meditation practices (karezza, Daoist sexual cultivation) show that prolonged arousal without discharge correlates with elevated oxytocin, sustained dopamine (rather than the post-orgasmic dopamine crash), and subjective reports of expanded awareness, deep bonding, and spiritual experiences.

One-line essenceStay with the fire of beginning. Don’t rush to the embers of ending. When awareness illuminates desire at its source, the energy rises — and sex becomes samadhi.
Sutra 49 of 112
Category: Tantric Union · Tags: embrace · shaking · energy · surrender
When in such embrace your senses are shaken as leaves, enter this shaking.

In Plain English

During embrace (sexual or deep holding), the body may tremble or shake with energy. Don’t suppress the shaking — ENTER it. Let the body vibrate freely. The shaking is energy releasing old patterns. Enter it totally and it becomes a doorway to ecstatic dissolution.

How to Practice

  1. Understand the shaking: When two bodies meet in deep embrace — especially with sexual energy present — a trembling often arises. Most people suppress it (embarrassment, control, fear). This sutra says: do the opposite. The shaking is not weakness or nervousness — it is ENERGY moving through the body, clearing pathways, dissolving blocks.
  2. Create the conditions: Hold your partner (or be held) in a deep, full-body embrace. Not a brief hug but a sustained holding — chest to chest, belly to belly, as much skin contact as possible. Breathe together. Let the embrace last many minutes without agenda. Eventually, energy will build between the bodies.
  3. Wait for the trembling: As energy builds, a subtle vibration begins. It may start in the legs, the arms, the belly, or the spine. It may be barely perceptible at first — a fine tremor, a quivering. Don’t look for it with the mind; FEEL for it with the body. When it comes, it comes on its own.
  4. Don’t suppress it: The habitual response is to tighten muscles and stop the trembling. Every social conditioning says: stay in control, don’t shake, don’t lose composure. Drop all of this. Allow the trembling completely. Give it permission. Let the body do whatever it wants to do.
  5. ENTER the shaking: This is the key instruction. Not just “allow” but ENTER. Become the shaking. Don’t stand apart and watch yourself shake — dissolve INTO the vibration. Let your identity merge with the trembling. You are not someone who is shaking; you ARE the shaking itself. There is no “you” separate from the vibration.
  6. Amplify naturally: Once you enter the shaking without resistance, it often amplifies on its own. The trembling may become stronger, spread through the whole body, become rhythmic or chaotic. Let it. The body is releasing stored tension, emotion, energy blocks. It knows what to do. Your job is only to not interfere.
  7. The senses dissolve: The sutra says “your senses are shaken as leaves.” When the trembling is total, the usual sensory boundaries dissolve. You no longer know where your body ends and your partner’s begins. Sight, hearing, touch all merge into one vibrating field of energy. The separate self shakes apart like leaves in a storm.
  8. Ecstatic dissolution: When the shaking is total and the senses have merged, the ego dissolves. There is no longer “you” and “partner” — there is only vibrating energy, ecstatic and boundless. This is the tantric dissolution — two becoming one, and the one dissolving into the whole. Stay here as long as it lasts.
  9. Solo practice: You can practise this alone. Stand with knees slightly bent and allow the body to shake. Start mechanically if needed — shake the hands, arms, legs — then let the shaking take over. Enter it completely. Osho’s Kundalini Meditation uses this principle: 15 minutes of shaking followed by 15 minutes of stillness.
  10. After the shaking: When the shaking subsides naturally, lie or sit in complete stillness. Don’t move for 10–15 minutes. The body will feel deeply relaxed, cleared, vibrating with subtle aliveness. The mind will be profoundly silent. This post-shaking stillness is where the deepest meditation happens — the body has cleared the way.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner-friendly
Duration
20–40 min
Posture
Embracing a partner, or standing alone
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Body-oriented people; those who hold tension; couples wanting energetic connection; people who suppress emotion; those familiar with shaking meditation

Signs It’s Working

  • Spontaneous trembling that feels alive and pleasurable, not nervous
  • Emotional releases during shaking — laughter, tears, sighs
  • The boundary between self and partner dissolving during embrace
  • After shaking: profound physical relaxation and mental silence
  • Old tensions or held emotions surfacing and releasing over days/weeks of practice
  • Growing comfort with surrender and loss of control

Common Pitfalls

  • Suppressing the trembling out of embarrassment or desire to appear “together”
  • Faking or forcing the shaking — mechanical shaking can START the process but you must let the body take over
  • Staying separate from the shaking (watching it) rather than entering it (becoming it)
  • Stopping too early when emotional content surfaces — the emotions are releasing; let them flow
  • Partner being unsupportive or judgemental of the shaking — this requires mutual trust and permission

Questions From The Book

What is this energy that causes the shaking?

Chapter 42, "Enter the Shaking"

Osho explains: it is kundalini — the coiled energy at the base of the spine that, when awakened through deep contact or arousal, begins to move upward. As it moves, it encounters blocks — muscular tensions, emotional armoring, energy stagnation. The shaking IS the energy clearing these blocks. Each tremor is a block dissolving. When all blocks are cleared, the energy flows freely and the shaking transforms into stillness — the stillness of total flow, like a river moving so smoothly it appears still.

Why does the sutra use the image of leaves?

Chapter 42

Osho notes: leaves shake because they have no resistance. The wind blows and the leaf responds totally — it doesn’t try to stay still, doesn’t fight the wind, doesn’t hold itself rigid. It simply moves with whatever force moves through it. This is the quality the sutra asks of you: be like a leaf. When energy-wind blows through your body, don’t resist, don’t control — respond as totally and as innocently as a leaf in the breeze.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Neurogenic tremoring (TRE — Tension and Trauma Release Exercises) is a modern therapeutic approach that deliberately activates the body’s natural tremor mechanism to release stored stress and trauma. The principle is identical: the body knows how to shake off held tension if we let it. Peter Levine’s Somatic Experiencing similarly works with the body’s trembling response as a healing mechanism.

Polyvagal perspective: The shaking represents the body completing arrested fight/flight responses. When the nervous system is held in a state of safety (the embrace), stored survival energy can discharge through tremoring. The social engagement system (activated by the embrace) creates the safety needed for the body to release what it has held.

One-line essenceWhen energy shakes you like a leaf in the wind, don’t resist — enter the shaking completely. Let the trembling dissolve the boundary between you and existence.
Sutra 50 of 112
Category: Tantric Union · Tags: memory · sexual-energy · transformation · alone
Even remembering union, without the embrace, transformation.

In Plain English

You don’t need a partner. Simply REMEMBER a moment of deep union — the energy, the feeling, the dissolution. Memory can recreate the same energetic state. Feel the fire of union arising from memory alone. This transforms sexual energy into spiritual energy without physical contact.

How to Practice

  1. Choose your memory: Recall a moment of deep union — sexual, romantic, or even a moment of profound connection (holding a newborn, merging with nature, deep friendship). The key quality you are looking for is: a moment when the boundary between you and another dissolved. When two became one, however briefly.
  2. Create the conditions: Sit or lie down in a quiet, private space. Close your eyes. Breathe slowly and deeply. Let the body relax completely. Give yourself at least 20 minutes. This technique works best in the evening or early morning when the mind is naturally more receptive.
  3. Don’t just think — FEEL the memory: The mind’s tendency is to remember visually or narratively (“we were in that place, we did that thing”). Drop the story. Instead, feel the ENERGY of the moment. What did your body feel like? Where was the warmth? Where was the openness? What was the quality of consciousness in that moment of union?
  4. Rebuild the feeling in the body: Let the memory recreate its physical sensations. If there was warmth in the chest, feel it NOW. If there was tingling in the spine, feel it NOW. If there was a sense of boundarylessness, feel it NOW. The body does not distinguish between present experience and vividly felt memory. The same neural circuits fire.
  5. Let the energy build: As you dwell in the felt memory, notice: energy begins to stir. The same sexual/life energy that was present during the actual union begins to awaken through memory alone. This is the discovery: the energy is YOURS, not dependent on another person. The other person was a catalyst, but the energy belongs to you.
  6. Stay with the fire, not the embers: As with Technique 1, keep attention on the BEGINNING quality of the energy — the igniting, the freshness, the rising flame. Don’t let the memory move toward its conclusion. Stay at the peak of connection — the moment of maximum dissolution — and let the energy circulate without discharge.
  7. Feel the transformation happening: With sustained attention on the energetic memory of union, something shifts. The energy that began as sexual (located in the pelvis) begins to rise — through belly, heart, throat, crown. It changes quality as it rises: from hot desire to warm love to cool bliss to vast spaciousness. This is sublimation — not by suppression but by awareness.
  8. Discover your own completeness: The deepest teaching of this technique: you don’t need another person to access the state of union. The union was always happening WITHIN you — the other person simply gave you permission to feel it. Once you can access it through memory alone, you know: this energy, this dissolution, this bliss is your own nature.
  9. Rest in the transformed state: When the energy has risen and transformed, rest in whatever state has opened. It may feel like meditation, like love without object, like spacious awareness, like inner light. Don’t analyse it — let it be. This is the “transformation” the sutra promises: sexual energy has become spiritual awareness.
  10. Practice regularly: Do this daily for 21 days. Each session deepens the capacity to access the energetic state of union independently. Over time, you may find you can access the state without even needing the memory — the energy is always available; you simply learn to turn toward it.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
20–30 min
Posture
Seated or lying, relaxed
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Those without a partner; individuals seeking to transform sexual energy solo; anyone wanting to discover that bliss is self-sourced, not other-dependent

Signs It’s Working

  • The memory recreates genuine physical sensations — warmth, tingling, energy movement
  • Sexual energy awakens through memory alone, without physical stimulation
  • The energy rises upward of its own accord — from pelvis to heart to crown
  • A shift from desire-for-other to contentment-in-self
  • The realisation: “This energy was always mine; the other was merely a mirror”
  • Growing independence from needing external stimulation to feel alive and connected

Common Pitfalls

  • Getting lost in the narrative/story of the memory rather than the ENERGY of it — drop the plot, keep the feeling
  • Creating dependency on one particular memory — use it as a gateway but eventually access the energy directly
  • Attachment or longing for the person in the memory — the technique aims to show that the energy is YOURS, not theirs
  • Using this to fantasize rather than meditate — fantasy engages the mind; this technique drops below mind to pure energy
  • Frustration if energy doesn’t rise immediately — this takes practice; the body must learn that memory can activate energy

Questions From The Book

Why does Shiva say “even remembering” — what makes memory so powerful?

Chapter 42, "Memory as Alchemy"

Osho explains: the body cannot tell the difference between a vividly relived memory and present experience. The same hormones release, the same energy moves, the same neural pathways activate. This is why nightmares produce real sweat and sexual dreams produce real arousal. If you can remember deeply enough — not just mentally but with the whole body — the energy state of union recreates itself completely. Memory is not about the past; it is a present-moment activation of stored energy patterns.

Does this technique work for people who have never experienced sexual union?

Chapter 42

Osho says: yes, because “union” is not limited to sexual union. Any moment of deep connection — with nature, with music, with a moment of beauty, with a loved one (even non-sexually) — contains the same quality of dissolution. Remember ANY moment when the boundary of self dissolved even slightly. The energy is the same. Tantra uses sexuality as the most obvious example because it is the most common experience of ego-dissolution, but it is not the only one.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Neuroscience confirms that vividly recalled experiences activate largely the same brain regions as the original experience (the “reactivation principle”). fMRI studies show that remembering sexual arousal activates the same hypothalamic and reward circuits as actual arousal. This technique leverages neuroplasticity: by repeatedly activating the energy pattern of union through memory, you strengthen the neural pathway until it becomes accessible at will.

Therapeutic application: This principle is used in somatic therapy — recalling a moment of safety and connection to activate the corresponding physiological state. The body is a storehouse of experience-patterns that can be re-activated through intentional remembering. This is not mere imagination; it is re-embodiment of a lived state.

One-line essenceThe fire of union lives in your memory. Recall it — not as thought but as energy — and discover that transformation needs no partner. The alchemy is always available within.
Sutra 51 of 112
Category: Tantric Union · Tags: joy · reunion · permeation · love
On joyously seeing a long-absent friend, permeate this joy.

In Plain English

When meeting a beloved friend after long absence, CATCH the joy. Don’t let it pass unconsciously. Feel it fully — let it permeate every cell. That joy of reunion is the same quality as divine love. Permeate it, become it, and you taste the eternal.

How to Practice

  1. Understand “permeate”: Normally when joy arises — at seeing a loved one, receiving good news, experiencing beauty — we feel it briefly and then it passes. We are too distracted, too much in our heads, to let it fully FILL us. “Permeate” means: let the joy soak into every cell, every corner of consciousness. Like a sponge dropped in water — saturate completely.
  2. Watch for the moment: The technique begins BEFORE the reunion. Whenever you know you will meet someone you love after long absence (arriving at an airport, opening a door, receiving a call), prepare your awareness. Tell yourself: “When the joy comes, I will CATCH it. I will not let it pass unconsciously.”
  3. The first instant: When you see the friend — in that FIRST instant of recognition, that flash of joy — CATCH IT. Don’t let the mind immediately start talking, planning, asking questions. Stay with the pure flash of joy for even 3–5 seconds before engaging. That first flash is the purest form of the energy.
  4. Let it permeate: Instead of the joy staying in the head (as an idea: “I’m happy to see you”), let it sink downward into the body. Feel joy in the chest, the belly, the legs, the arms. Let it fill the WHOLE body. Not just “I feel happy” but the entire organism vibrating with joy. Head to toes. Every cell celebrating.
  5. Become the joy: Take it further: not “I FEEL joy” but “I AM joy.” There is no separate “you” who experiences joy — there is only joy, permeating everything. The boundary between feeler and feeling dissolves. You don’t HAVE joy; you ARE joy. This is permeation at its deepest.
  6. Don’t rush into activity: The social habit is to immediately embrace, talk, catch up, make plans. These are all fine LATER. But first, take 30 seconds to simply BE with the joy. Let it permeate. Then engage. Those 30 seconds of pure permeation teach you more about the nature of consciousness than years of philosophy.
  7. Extend to smaller joys: Once you practise with the intense joy of reunion, apply the same technique to smaller joys: seeing a flower bloom, tasting good food, hearing a favourite song, watching a sunset. CATCH the joy. Let it permeate. Become it. Every joy — however small — is a doorway to the divine when fully permeated.
  8. The teaching: What this technique reveals is that joy does not come FROM the friend, the flower, or the sunset. Joy is your natural state — these external triggers simply give you PERMISSION to feel what is always there. By practising permeation, you learn to access joy directly, without needing a trigger. The friend was the excuse; the joy is your nature.
  9. Practice without trigger: After weeks of permeating joy whenever it naturally arises, try this: sit quietly and REMEMBER the joy of reunion (similar to Technique 3). Let the memory permeate you. Then let go of even the memory — can you feel joy with NO cause? This is the ultimate goal: causeless joy, your natural state rediscovered.
  10. Daily awareness: Throughout the day, notice every moment of joy — however fleeting — and practise permeation. The barista smiles at you: permeate. A child laughs nearby: permeate. You complete a task: permeate. Each micro-permeation teaches the body that joy is always available. Over weeks, the background state shifts from neutral to joyful.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Very easy
Duration
Moments (in life) or 15 min (formal practice with memory)
Posture
Any — this is a life technique
Eyes
Open (during reunion) or closed (during memory practice)
Best for
Everyone; especially those who feel joy but let it pass too quickly; those seeking to expand their capacity for happiness; heart-oriented people

Signs It’s Working

  • Joy lasts longer — instead of a brief flash, it saturates the body for minutes
  • Physical sensations of joy become noticeable — warmth in the chest, lightness, vibration
  • You begin catching joy in smaller and smaller moments throughout the day
  • The distinction between “I” and “joy” begins to blur — you ARE the joy rather than experiencing it
  • Others notice: “You seem different. Lighter. Happier.”
  • Moments of causeless joy begin to arise — joy with no trigger, simply your natural state surfacing

Common Pitfalls

  • Intellectualising the joy instead of feeling it bodily — “I’m happy” is a thought; let the joy fill the BODY
  • Waiting only for big joys (reunion after years) and missing the small ones (a kind word, a warm meal)
  • Clinging to joy after it naturally fades — permeate fully WHILE it is here, but let it go when it passes
  • Performing joy for others rather than genuinely feeling it — this is internal work, not external display
  • Judging yourself for not feeling joy when “you should” — sometimes sadness is present; honour that too

Questions From The Book

Why does Shiva place this technique among the tantric sutras?

Chapter 42, "Permeate This Joy"

Osho explains: because tantra is the path of using positive emotions as vehicles for transcendence. Just as sexual energy can become a vehicle (Techniques 1-3), so can JOY. Joy is love-energy in its purest, most accessible form. When joy is fully permeated, the same dissolution happens as in sexual union — the separate self dissolves into the feeling. The difference is only intensity: sex is the strongest trigger, but joy is the most available. This technique brings tantra into ordinary daily life.
