  2. Choose a moment when a sense is naturally vivid: music coming through good speakers, a long kiss, sunlight on water, the first bite of fruit, a child laughing. Don’t manufacture vividness; wait for it.
  3. The instant the sense lights up, do this small inner shift: instead of being lost in the experience (the song, the taste, the colour), feel that you are standing behind the sense. Osho’s exact image: as if your spectacles have slipped down your nose and you are looking from above the lenses.
  4. Notice the difference: looking by the eyes, you forget yourself in what you see. Looking through the eyes, you remain present at your own centre and the eyes become just an opening.
  5. Keep the awareness. Don’t lose yourself in the music; let the music come to you. Don’t lose yourself in the kiss; remain the one through whom the kiss is happening. The sutra’s exact instruction: keep in the awareness.
  6. If the experience pulls you back into forgetting, the moment you notice, return to standing-behind-the-sense. This is the whole technique — remembering, again and again, that you are not the eye, the ear, the hand.
  7. Practise first with objects, not with people. Osho is specific about this. A flower, a tree, the night sky — they don’t mind being looked through. They “feel appreciated.” You can sit before a flower for ten minutes looking through the eyes, not by them.
  8. Then try with someone you love — a partner, a child. With a child it works best because, Osho says, the child is “still uncrippled by society.” The child will feel your presence; the touch and the look will become alive.
  9. Test the difference of touch. Place a hand on someone first while “withdrawn,” mind elsewhere. Then again, fully present in the hand — awareness coming all the way down into the palm. The other person feels the difference. Osho says women and children pick this up immediately.
  10. Don’t practise the deep look on strangers. Osho warns: a deep look feels like trespass to someone unloved. Save the technique for objects, for nature, and for those who are open to you. With the rest, do it gently and without staring.
  11. As all five senses begin to be used “through” rather than “by,” you discover the inner centre where they all report. Osho gives this as the proof: it is the same person you are seeing, hearing, smelling, touching — how do you know? Because somewhere inside, all five senses fall onto a single point. That point is what the technique reveals.
  12. With persistence, the world begins to feel slightly different — less solid, more like an appearance arising in the senses, while you remain steady at the centre that is not any of them.

Osho’s Key Insight

Sri Aurobindo says somewhere that the whole life is yoga – and it is so. Everything can become a meditation. And unless everything becomes a meditation, meditation has not happened to you. Meditation cannot be a part, a fragment. Either it is – and when it is you are wholly in it – or it is not. You cannot make a part of your life meditative. That is impossible, and that is what is being tried ...

Source: 39. From the Wave to the Cosmic Ocean, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 41 · Sutras 64–65 · 2 techniques

Tantric Methods for Awareness and NonJudgment

Osho's framing

Osho frames these techniques with a paradox: you have to attain what you already are. Sansara and nirvana, the world and liberation, are not two places but two attitudes toward the same reality. The methods can therefore be very simple. The catch is the ego: it craves difficulty, because only difficulty offers it the trophy of conquest. Anything easy feels insulting, so the mind dismisses simple techniques even when they work. Spiritual awakening is not a causal event needing time; it is more like waking from sleep - first disorientation, then sudden recognition. These eight sutras cultivate that growing alertness.

You are already the person you long to be, you are already where you want to reach.

— Osho
Sutra 64 of 112
Category: Senses · Tags: sneeze · fright · gaps · shock · continuous-awareness
At the start of sneezing, during fright, in anxiety, above a chasm, flying in battle, in extreme curiosity, at the beginning of hunger, at the end of hunger, be uninterruptedly aware.

In Plain English

Nature gives you free meditation moments every day — instants when the mind stops involuntarily. The moment before a sneeze. A sudden fright. Looking over a cliff edge. Extreme curiosity. The first pang of hunger and the satisfaction when it ends. In all these moments, thought stops naturally. The technique: instead of missing these gaps, USE them. Stay alert THROUGH them. They are doorways that nature opens for you — walk through.

How to Practice

  1. Understand the principle first: Between any two thoughts there is a gap. Normally this gap is so tiny you miss it. But certain situations FORCE the gap to widen: sneezing, shock, fright, vertigo, extreme curiosity, hunger pangs. In these moments, the thinking machinery halts involuntarily. The technique is to be AWARE during these natural halts.
  2. Make a list of YOUR gaps: Osho suggests cataloguing the moments in your life when the mind naturally stops. For some it’s sneezing. For others it’s the moment of jumping into cold water. For others it’s the instant of unexpected news. Identify YOUR natural gap-moments.
  3. The sneeze practice: When you feel a sneeze building, become extremely alert. Don’t suppress the sneeze — let it come — but stay AWARE through the entire arc: the building, the explosion, the aftermath. In the instant of the sneeze itself, thinking stops completely. Catch that gap. What is there when thought is absent? Pure awareness.
  4. The fright practice: You cannot manufacture real fright, but you can use surprises. Ask a friend to startle you at random moments. Or notice natural frights: a car horn, a door slamming, someone appearing unexpectedly. In that first instant of fright — BEFORE the reaction — there is a gap of pure awareness. Catch it.
  5. The vertigo practice: Stand at the edge of a safe height (balcony with railing, cliff edge with barrier). Look down. The mind reels and stops. In that moment of vertigo, thinking ceases. Stay aware. Don’t grab the railing out of panic — you are safe. Simply notice the gap.
  6. The curiosity practice: When something creates extreme curiosity — an unopened letter, a mystery about to be revealed, a door about to open — notice: in the instant BEFORE the revelation, the mind is completely still, completely attentive. That stillness IS the meditation.
  7. The hunger practice: When hunger first arises as a pang, catch that instant. The body sends a signal; the mind has not yet reacted. In that first flash of hunger there is pure sensation without interpretation. Similarly: the first bite of food when very hungry — the satisfaction is so total that the mind stops. Be aware in THAT moment.
  8. Make it continuous: The sutra says “uninterruptedly aware.” This means: don’t just catch one gap per day. Make it a 24-hour practice. Whenever ANY gap naturally occurs — be there. Over time, you develop a background awareness that is always watching for these openings.
  9. Evening review: Each night before sleep, review: how many gaps did I catch today? What happened in them? The review itself sharpens the sensitivity for the next day. Within a week, you will begin catching gaps you never noticed before.
  10. The integration: After weeks of practice, something extraordinary happens: you realise the gaps are not separate from the flow of life. The awareness that catches the gaps begins to pervade the non-gap moments too. The background awareness becomes foreground. This is the flowering of the technique.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
All day (catching moments)
Posture
Any — life as it happens
Eyes
Open
Best for
Active people; those who dislike sitting meditation; adrenaline seekers; busy professionals who think they have no time to meditate

Signs It’s Working

  • You begin “catching” 2–3 natural gaps per day that you previously missed entirely
  • The gaps feel like tiny windows of crystal clarity — thought-free and vivid
  • Fright no longer produces only panic; it also produces a flash of awareness
  • You develop a “watcher” that operates in the background, always slightly alert
  • Sneezes become mini-meditations rather than unconscious convulsions
  • The gaps begin to lengthen — what was a split-second becomes a moment, then several seconds

Common Pitfalls

  • Trying to MANUFACTURE gaps artificially — don’t scare yourself on purpose; use naturally occurring moments
  • Suppressing the sneeze or fright to “extend” the gap — let it happen naturally; just be aware through it
  • Becoming frustrated that you keep missing gaps — this IS the practice; each “I missed it!” sharpens awareness for next time
  • Confusing the gap with thinking ABOUT the gap — the gap is pre-thought; if you’re narrating it, you’ve already left it
  • Practising only one type of gap and ignoring others — expand your repertoire

Questions From The Book

Why does Osho call these “free meditations” that nature provides?

Chapter 53, "Gaps in the Natural Flow"

Osho explains: most meditation techniques require effort — you must DO something. But nature already provides moments when the mind stops on its own. A sneeze, a shock, a moment of vertigo — these are gifts. The mind stops WITHOUT your effort. All you need to do is be alert enough to notice. It’s like finding a door already open — you don’t need to push. Just walk through. The tragedy is that millions of gaps occur in your life and you sleep through every one.

How is the gap during fright different from unconscious panic?

Chapter 53

Osho distinguishes: in fright there are two moments. The FIRST is the gap — pure awareness, no-thought, just alertness. The SECOND is the reaction — fear, panic, running. Most people immediately jump to the second and never notice the first. The technique is to catch that first instant — BEFORE the fear reaction kicks in. That first instant is pure consciousness without content. It is the same consciousness a Buddha lives in permanently.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: The startle reflex creates a measurable 200–500ms window where the default mode network (self-referential thinking) goes offline. This technique uses that neurological fact as a meditation doorway. The “gap” is not mystical — it’s the brief period when the brain’s narrative machinery shuts down in response to novel input.

Neuroscience parallel: Orienting response research shows that novel or startling stimuli produce a momentary suspension of ongoing cognitive activity. The brain enters a state of heightened receptivity with minimal conceptual processing. This is exactly what meditators spend years cultivating — and the sneeze gives it to you for free, if you can catch it.

One-line essenceNature gives you free meditation moments every day — the sneeze, the shock, the hunger pang. Stop sleeping through them.
Sutra 65 of 112
Category: Senses · Tags: beyond-purity · non-judgement · tantra · acceptance · freedom
The purity of other teachings is an impurity to us. In reality, know nothing as pure or impure.

In Plain English

Drop ALL categories of pure/impure, good/bad, sacred/profane. Tantra says: these categories are the chains. Nothing is impure and nothing is pure — these are mental overlays on reality. When you drop judging (this food is “bad,” this act is “impure”), a tremendous freedom arises. The energy that was consumed by constant moral categorisation becomes available for awareness.

How to Practice

  1. Understand the context: This is the most radical sutra in the tantra. Every other spiritual tradition divides the world into sacred/profane, pure/impure, allowed/forbidden. Tantra says: THAT division is the real impurity. It splits reality and splits you. This technique heals that split.
  2. One-day experiment: Choose one full day. From morning to night, make ZERO moral judgments. Not about food (“this is healthy/unhealthy”). Not about people (“she is good/he is bad”). Not about yourself (“I was wrong/right”). Not about actions (“this is proper/improper”). Simply observe without the overlays of judgement.
  3. Notice the judge: You will be astonished how CONSTANT the judging is. The mind categorises everything: this is acceptable, that is not. This is clean, that is dirty. This person is worthy, that one is not. The judge runs 24/7 and you have never noticed because it feels like “reality.”
  4. Catch each judgement as it arises: When the mind says “impure” or “wrong” or “bad” — catch it. Don’t fight it. Just notice: “Ah, the judge is speaking again.” And let the judgement go. Return to raw perception without the overlay.
  5. See what remains without judgement: When you look at something “dirty” without the label “dirty” — what do you actually see? Colour, texture, shape. That’s all. The “dirtiness” was added by the mind. Remove it, and reality is simply what it is — neither pure nor impure.
  6. Notice the energy freed: Judging consumes enormous mental energy. Cataloguing, comparing, condemning, approving — it never stops. When it stops (even for an hour), you will feel a surge of free energy, lightness, spaciousness. This is the freedom tantra promises.
  7. Apply to the body: Notice how you judge your own body — this part is beautiful, that part is ugly. This function is “dignified,” that function is “shameful.” Drop all of it. The body is simply the body. No part is impure.
  8. Apply to emotions: Stop labelling emotions as positive or negative. Anger arises — it is not “bad.” Sadness arises — it is not “wrong.” Joy arises — it is not “better.” They are all simply energies moving through you. Without judgement, they flow freely and resolve naturally.
  9. The paradox of morality: This technique does NOT make you immoral. Osho is clear: when judgement drops, a natural morality arises that is far more authentic than the imposed kind. Without the judge, compassion flows naturally because you are no longer separated from others by categories of better/worse.
  10. Extend to one week: After your one-day experiment, extend to a full week. Notice how profoundly your relationship to reality changes when you stop dividing it into acceptable and unacceptable. Reality becomes seamless, whole, sacred in its totality.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
One full day (experiment), then ongoing
Posture
Any — life as lived
Eyes
Open
Best for
Those burdened by guilt; perfectionists; people raised in rigid religious frameworks; anyone exhausted by the inner critic; moralizers seeking freedom

Signs It’s Working

  • A sudden awareness of how CONSTANT the judging is — you had no idea it was this pervasive
  • Moments of seeing reality without overlay — raw, vivid, neither good nor bad, just IS
  • A flood of energy that was previously consumed by the judging machinery
  • Reduced guilt and self-condemnation — not from rationalizing but from seeing that the categories were invented
  • Greater compassion for others — without pure/impure, everyone is simply human
  • A lightness and playfulness that comes when the inner moralist retires

Common Pitfalls

  • Using this to justify harmful behaviour — the point is not “anything goes” but “the mind’s categories are not reality”
  • Judging yourself for judging (“I shouldn’t be judging!”) — that’s just the judge judging itself; notice and let it pass
  • Intellectualizing non-judgement without actually practising it — this must be lived, not believed
  • Abandoning discernment along with judgement — you can still perceive what is helpful vs. harmful without the moralistic overlay
  • Provoking others with this philosophy — practise silently; don’t preach non-judgement

Questions From The Book

Doesn’t this lead to immorality?

Chapter 56, "Beyond Purity"

Osho addresses this fear directly: dropping mental categories of pure/impure does not make you a criminal. In fact, the opposite happens. When the imposed morality drops, a natural compassion and sensitivity arise. The judge was never truly moral — it was just trained. A person without the judge responds to reality directly, from the heart, with natural kindness. They don’t need rules because they can feel what is appropriate in each moment. Morality imposed from outside creates hypocrites; morality arising from awareness creates authentic beings.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This parallels the Buddhist concept of “non-dual awareness” and the Zen teaching of “before thinking.” Cognitive science confirms that value judgements are learned overlays on raw perception — the brain adds “good/bad” labels after the fact. This technique strips perception back to its pre-conceptual freshness.

Psychological parallel: The inner critic (in psychotherapy terms, the “superego”) is the source of much human suffering. Techniques like Internal Family Systems (IFS) therapy work to soften the inner critic’s grip. This 5,000-year-old technique does the same: by noticing that purity/impurity are mental constructs, the critic loses its power and the psyche heals.

One-line essenceThe judge in your head is not God — it’s conditioning. When it retires, reality reveals itself as neither pure nor impure but simply, luminously, what it is.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 43 · Sutras 66–67 · 2 techniques

Finding the Changeless through the Changing

Osho's framing

Osho draws on Northrope to contrast two minds. The Western mind hunts the "theoretical component" - the causal levers by which nature can be predicted and controlled. The Eastern mind hunts the "aesthetic component" - how to participate in nature, how to dissolve into it. He calls science a relationship of hatred and religion a relationship of love. Tantra is purely Eastern: a love effort. He gives a vivid example - when Hillary climbed Everest, Western papers said "conquered"; a Zen monastery's wall paper said "Everest has been befriended." Approached as conquest, existence shows only dead matter; approached as participation, the same existence reveals life.

If you approach it through analysis, it appears material; if you approach it through participation, it appears as life.

— Osho
Sutra 66 of 112
Category: Witnessing · Tags: unsame · friend · stranger · honor
Be the unsame same to friend as to stranger, in honor and dishonor.

In Plain English

Two things are happening in you at once: a periphery that is always changing, and a centre that has been the same since childhood. With a friend you behave one way, with a stranger another — that is the periphery, and that is fine. But underneath, something must remain the same. Praise and insult, friend and stranger, honour and dishonour all touch only the periphery. The technique is to act differently on the surface (“unsame”) while resting in the same centre underneath (“same”).

How to Practice

  1. This is a life-technique, practised through real encounters, not on a cushion. Decide that for one week you will use every interaction as the test ground.
  2. Begin by noticing the changing in yourself. Through the day, watch the body sensations, the moods, the thoughts — all of it shifting moment by moment. Osho: every cell of the body has changed in seven years; the mind shifts even more often than the body.
  3. At the same time, notice that something in you knows all this changing — the same one who knew your childhood, and your last love, and yesterday’s grief. That knower has not aged. Get a feel for it as the unchanging witness.
  4. Now bring the technique into encounters. When a friend approaches, behave as a friend behaves. When a stranger approaches, behave as one would with a stranger. Don’t fake; the periphery should differ. Osho: “You cannot meet an unknown person as if you know him already.”
  5. But underneath, do not let the centre shift. Whether the person greeting you is a beloved or a stranger, your inner stance — the awareness, the warmth-towards-existence — is the same. Practise feeling that.
  6. Reverse the usual rule. Look at your friend as if a stranger. Osho insists this comes first: only then can you look at the stranger as a friend. With your wife, your husband, your closest people, look afresh and notice how much of them you don’t actually know — they are river-like. Don’t treat them as fixed.
  7. Look at a stranger and find the friend. Same humanity, same existence, same fact of being alive in this moment. Osho: even a tree, even a stone, shares this. The meeting is in the essence; the strangeness is on the surface.
  8. Use praise and insult as a clear test. When someone praises you, watch: the periphery wants to swell — refuse to identify. They are praising a manifestation; you are not the manifestation. When someone insults you, watch the same way: they are dishonouring a manifestation; you are not it.
  9. Don’t fake equanimity. Osho is firm: a forced detachment means you are still on the periphery. Don’t suppress the surface reaction; just keep also feeling the centre. The detachment is then natural, not performed.
  10. Practise on small opposite pairs across one day: comfort and discomfort, hunger and satisfaction, being early and being late. In each pair, notice the periphery moves; the centre does not.
  11. Use the verbal change Osho recommends. Don’t say “I am hungry”; say “the body is hungry, and I know.” Don’t say “I am angry”; say “anger is on the periphery, and I know.” This is not a trick — it is the precise truth.
  12. Carry the Zen monk’s response in mind: when honoured he said only “Is it so?” When dishonoured the next day, also “Is it so?” Same centre under both verdicts. Make this your inner reply when the world’s opinion of you flips.

Osho’s Key Insight

Northrope says somewhere that the Western mind has been continuously searching for the theoretical component of existence – the causal link for how things happen, what is the cause, how the effect can be controlled, how man can manipulate nature. And the Eastern mind, says Northrope, has been on a different adventure. The search has been to find the aesthetic component of reality – not the theo...

Source: 43. Finding the Changeless through the Changing, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 67 of 112
Category: Witnessing · Tags: sphere · change · consume
Here is the sphere of change, change, change. Through change consume change.

In Plain English

Buddha’s answer to change is to leave it. Tantra’s answer is the opposite: don’t escape change — move into it. Live whatever is changing; flow with the river instead of swimming against it. When you stop fighting, you suddenly notice that the river is all around but you are not the river. Change consumes itself, and what remains is the unchanging.

How to Practice

  1. Begin with a simple recognition through the day: everything you can see is changing. Body, mind, room, weather, relationships, the so-called solid Himalaya. The fan that looks still is moving very fast. The wall that looks fixed is electrons spinning. Confirm this for yourself before practising.
  2. Now choose any wave of change happening to you right now — a mood, a desire, a sensation, a relationship phase, an ageing in the body. Anything in motion.
  3. Don’t fight it. Tantra’s instruction is sharp: don’t cling to it either, but don’t escape. Both clinging and escape are reactions to the change; both lock you to it. Stop both.
  4. Move with it. Osho’s exact image: get into the river, relax, allow it to take you. “Don’t even swim; allow the river to take you.” This isn’t passivity — it is letting the change be itself, fully.
  5. If you are sad, be sad. Don’t add the second arrow of “I shouldn’t be sad” or “how do I get out of sadness.” Just be the sad one, fully, without fight. Same with desire, with anger, with longing, with joy.
  6. Watch what happens when there is no resistance. The wave does its thing — it crests and falls. You haven’t spent any energy holding it or fleeing it.
  7. Now notice the strange byproduct. When you flow with the river all the way, a moment comes when you suddenly know: the river is all around me, but I am not the river. Osho: “Some day try it in a real river. Allow it to take you. You will feel that the river is all around, but you are not the river.”
  8. This is what the sutra calls “through change consume change.” Change itself shows you what is not change, by contrast, when you stop opposing it. Fighting hides the contrast.
  9. Use this with strong emotions especially. When fear comes, don’t shut it out and don’t panic into it — flow with it. When pleasure comes, don’t cling and don’t reject — flow with it. Each wave is a chance to discover the riverbed.
  10. Use it with relationships. People you love change every day. Don’t hold them to yesterday’s shape. Allow the change. Osho says only a dead person is predictable; if your husband or wife is alive, they will be different tomorrow. Move with that.
  11. Use it with the body. As ageing happens, illness arrives, energy waxes and wanes, don’t resist. The body is the river; you are not. Each change in the body is one more piece of evidence for the technique.
  12. Don’t expect a permanent state at the end. The river keeps flowing — that is its nature. What changes is your relation to it: from a swimmer thrashing against the current you become someone floating, who has discovered they are not the water but something behind it that the water cannot wet.

Osho’s Key Insight

Northrope says somewhere that the Western mind has been continuously searching for the theoretical component of existence – the causal link for how things happen, what is the cause, how the effect can be controlled, how man can manipulate nature. And the Eastern mind, says Northrope, has been on a different adventure. The search has been to find the aesthetic component of reality – not the theo...

Source: 43. Finding the Changeless through the Changing, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 45 · Sutras 68–69 · 2 techniques

Remaining with the Real

Osho's framing

Osho opens with a Hui-neng question: you are alive, you are conscious - why don't you know who you are? You must have built a barrier. The good news, he says, is that nothing positive needs to be done; the barrier just has to be seen. And the barrier is the dreaming mind - the past constantly interpreting whatever happens now. When you listen, you don't really hear; you hear your own commentary. Look at a flower and the word "beautiful" steps between you and it. These two sutras - the hen mothering her chicks, the many suns reflected in water - are about settling back into reality without interpretation.

You are not hearing me; you are hearing yourself, because simultaneously you are interpreting.

— Osho
Sutra 68 of 112
Category: Self-Inquiry · Tags: mothers · chicks · mother · particular
As a hen mothers her chicks, mother particular knowings, particular doings, in reality.

In Plain English

A hen sits on her chicks, warms them, protects them, broods them into life. Do the same with your own, real, first-hand knowings and doings — and let go of everything borrowed. Don’t mother theories, scriptures and other people’s opinions. Mother only what you have actually tasted yourself, however small. The key phrase is “in reality”: live in the present, drop hope-based dreaming, and protect the few experiences that are genuinely yours.

How to Practice

  1. Begin with an honest audit. Sit quietly with paper. List things you “know” — about love, God, meditation, what is right and wrong. Mark which were tasted by you and which were given to you (parents, teachers, books, internet). Be ruthless. Most will be borrowed.
  2. Mark also things you do daily — ways of greeting, of being angry, of working, of expressing love. For each, ask Osho’s exact question: “Is this me, or is my father doing it through me? Is my mother angry, or am I?”
  3. Make a small set of things that survived — a real knowing, a genuine like or dislike, a feeling that is unmistakably your own. They may be tiny. That is fine. Tiny is enough.
  4. Now begin to mother them, like a hen with chicks. Spend time with them. Trust them. Build outward from them. Whatever is borrowed, let drop, even if it leaves a hole.
  5. Live in the present. Osho says hope is the door through which dreaming enters. So for one week, refuse to live for tomorrow. Do not pull yourself forward with “something is going to happen.” If today is hopeless, accept it as hopeless — but stay here.
  6. Watch for the small false acts that fill the day. The painted smile when family arrives. The polite agreement you don’t mean. The respect shown to someone you don’t respect. Stop them, one by one. Don’t replace them with rude opposites — just don’t do the false version.
  7. Save your real smile for when something real happens. Osho is specific: “Don’t smile, preserve the energy. The smile will come, and then it will change you completely. Then every cell of your body will smile.” Same with tears, same with affection.
  8. For seven days, make a single rule: do nothing false. Whatever is lost from your life because you refused to fake it, let it be lost. Osho promises: in seven days a new living current will be felt; the dead layers begin to break.
  9. Expect a gap. The false things will drop before the real ones come. There will be a dull, empty stretch — this is normal, not failure. Don’t panic and don’t fill it with new borrowed things.
  10. Distinguish trust from belief. Don’t set up a new master to follow; that is the same trap. Osho quotes Buddha to Sariputta: “If you don’t believe in yourself, how can you believe in me?” The first trust is always inward.
  11. Don’t imitate. Love a Buddha or a Jesus, but don’t become a carbon copy — carbon copies are not accepted by existence. Whatever route you take, it must be authentically your own.
  12. Continue mothering. Day after day return to your small set of real knowings, warm them, protect them. They grow. The hen does not analyse her chicks; she simply broods them. Do the same with your truth.

Osho’s Key Insight

Hui-neng had asked a person, “What is the problem? What are the roots so that man can be solved and man can make some efforts to know who he is?”

Source: 45. Remaining with the Real, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 69 of 112
Category: Self-Inquiry · Tags: since · truth · bondage · freedom
Since, in truth, bondage and freedom are relative, these words are only for those terrified with the universe. This universe is a reflection of minds. As you see many suns in water from one sun, so see bondage and liberation.

In Plain English

Bondage and liberation are not opposites — they are two ends of the same stick. The world is a mirror; whatever you see in it — chains or freedom — is your own reflection. The man who runs after money and the man who runs from money are equally bound to money. Tantra says: don’t move to the opposite pole. See that both poles are reflections of one mind, and the seeking itself drops.

How to Practice

  1. This is a contemplation more than a sit-down practice. The sutra warns up front that bondage/freedom talk is “only for those terrified with the universe” — that is, for people who are running. The practice is to see through that running.
  2. Sit and review your spiritual life so far. List what you have been running from (sex, money, anger, the world, the body) and what you have been running toward (brahmacharya, simplicity, peace, liberation, the divine). Lay them in two columns.
  3. Now look at each pair carefully. Osho is sharp here: the saint who shudders at money is still obsessed with money — just in reverse. The man who cannot look at a woman is still obsessed with women — in reverse. Check whether your “spiritual” column is just the negative of your worldly column. In most cases it is.
  4. This is the recognition the sutra wants: bondage and freedom are relative, like hot and cold — degrees of one phenomenon, not different things. If hot water feels cold to a hot hand, the “cold” is in the hand, not the water. Apply the same logic to bondage and liberation.
  5. Stop creating an opposite pole. Don’t resolve to be non-greedy if greed troubles you, don’t resolve to be celibate if sex troubles you, don’t resolve to leave the world if the world feels heavy. Each resolution is just a reversed version of the same obsession.
  6. Instead, look at the one who is desiring. Osho: “Today you desire A, tomorrow B, day after C, and you think you are transformed. But you are the same. The desiring is you.” The objects change; the desire-er does not. Watch the desire-er.
  7. Practise the mirror image. Through the day notice that what disturbs you outside is a reflection of what is inside. The thief sees thieves; the lustful sees lust; the angry sees anger everywhere. When you next find yourself condemning a quality in someone, turn the condemnation around like a lamp shone at yourself: “What in me is reflected here?”
  8. Sit with the example Shiva uses. One sun reflects in many ponds. Counting reflections will give you a thousand suns. Looking up at the source gives you one. Whenever you find yourself listing “the bondages of my life” or “the freedoms I will reach,” remember — reflections, not realities.
  9. Drop the search for liberation. This is the hardest step and Osho is direct about it: desire for liberation is itself bondage, because desire is bondage. You cannot desire your way out of desiring. Stop desiring liberation. The moment desiring drops, what remains is what was always there.
  10. Don’t panic at this. Stopping the search doesn’t mean drifting into worldliness; it means standing still in the middle, neither pulled by sansara nor by moksha. Osho says: when desiring stops, the future stops, and there is nowhere to move. That stopping is the freedom — and you can no longer call it freedom, because there is no longer a bondage to contrast it with.
  11. Use Naropa’s answer when you wonder whether you have “made progress.” Asked if he was now liberated, Naropa said: “Yes and no both. Yes, I am not in bondage; and no, because that liberation was also a reflection of bondage.” Carry this answer; let it dissolve the question.
  12. This technique is for the rare mind, the sutra says. Don’t force it. If your obsession is still strong, simpler techniques will serve you better. Come to this one when seeking has worn you out and you are ready to drop the seeking itself.

Osho’s Key Insight

Hui-neng had asked a person, “What is the problem? What are the roots so that man can be solved and man can make some efforts to know who he is?”

Source: 45. Remaining with the Real, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 47 · Sutras 70–72 · 3 techniques

Tantric Meditation Using Light

Osho's framing

Before these light techniques Osho redraws the map of human possibility. There are three kinds of people, he says: the normal, the abnormal, and the supernormal. Western psychology, born in the clinic, has only studied the abnormal - the person who has fallen below the norm - and so the whole of Freud's framework rests on sick patients. Eastern psychology watches a different population: the supernormal, the person who has gone beyond the norm. Both deviate from average, but one downward and one upward. Tantra exists to map that upward direction, and these three sutras - light rising the spine, lightning between centres, the cosmos as translucent presence - belong to that ascent.

One who is supernormal is abnormal because he is more healthy than any normal human being.

— Osho
Sutra 70 of 112
Category: Light & Gazing · Tags: spine · chakras · light · energy · visualization
Consider your essence as light rays rising from centre to centre up the vertebrae, and so rises livingness in you.

In Plain English

Visualise — no, feel — light rising through your spine from the sex centre at the base to the crown at the top. As the light ascends through each energy centre, feel aliveness intensifying. You are not imagining something false; physics confirms you are made of light particles. This technique asks you to experience that truth directly.

How to Practice

  1. Preparation: Choose a time when you will not be disturbed for at least 45 minutes. Lock the door. Turn off your phone. This is non-negotiable — interruption mid-practice can leave energy stranded in uncomfortable places.
  2. Settle the body: Sit comfortably with spine erect, or lie down flat. Take 5–10 slow breaths. Let the body become heavy, grounded, still. Don’t begin the main practice until the surface mind has quieted.
  3. Develop feeling, not thinking: This is the critical distinction Osho emphasises. Do NOT think “light is rising.” Instead, FEEL warmth, tingling, aliveness. Start by feeling the warmth of your own body — the heat at your sex centre. That heat IS light slowed down. Let yourself feel it as luminous.
  4. First centre (Muladhara/sex centre): Feel a pool of warm golden light at the base of your spine. Don’t rush. Spend several minutes here until the warmth feels real. Let the warmth intensify naturally. It is not imagination — blood flows here, heat is real.
  5. Rising to the navel: When the base feels alive, allow the light to rise — like a slow river of warm honey — up through the belly to the navel centre. Feel the navel glow. Warmth spreads through the abdomen. The belly softens. Stay until it feels alive.
  6. Rising to the heart: Let the river of light continue upward to the heart. Here, something changes: breathing deepens spontaneously, the heartbeat may slow or become more noticeable. A feeling of openness, vulnerability, love may arise. Stay as long as the heart needs. Don’t push feelings away.
  7. Rising to the throat: The light ascends to the throat. You may feel a lump, a desire to speak or sing or cry. Let the throat open. The light purifies whatever is held here. Stay until it feels clear.
  8. Rising to the third eye: The light moves to the space between the eyebrows. Pressure may build here — this is normal. The inner visual field may shift: colours, patterns, or a sense of vast space. Stay until the energy feels ready to move further.
  9. Rising to the crown (sahasrar): Finally, feel the light push through the top of the head. Imagine — feel — the crown opening like a thousand-petalled lotus. Light streams upward into infinite space. You are not contained by the body any longer. Stay here as long as feels natural.
  10. Release into cosmos: Let the light dissolve upward into the sky. You are empty, transparent, clean. The “livingness” the sutra mentions is this: you feel more alive than you have ever felt, because the life-force is no longer blocked at any point.
  11. CRITICAL — Never leave incomplete: If you must stop early, ALWAYS bring the energy to the crown and release it upward before opening your eyes. Energy left at the sex centre or navel creates agitation; at the heart, emotional overwhelm; at the third eye, headaches. Complete the circuit.
  12. Return: When ready, feel the body again. Wiggle toes and fingers. Open eyes slowly. Sit for 2–3 minutes before standing. Drink water.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Advanced
Duration
40–60 min
Posture
Seated (spine erect) or lying flat
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Those comfortable with energy work; patient practitioners; people who feel “stuck” or low-energy

Signs It’s Working

  • Distinct warmth or tingling at each centre as the light arrives
  • Spontaneous changes in breathing and heartbeat at the heart centre
  • Colours appearing in the inner visual field at the third eye
  • A sense of the crown “opening” — like a flower blooming or a fountain releasing
  • Feeling extraordinarily alive, vibrant, and clear after the session
  • Over weeks: increased energy, emotional clarity, and a deepening sense of inner aliveness

Common Pitfalls

  • Thinking instead of feeling — if you’re narrating “now the light is at the navel,” you’re in the head, not the body
  • Rushing through centres to “get to the top” — each centre needs real time; this is not a race
  • Stopping mid-practice due to disturbance — always complete to crown before stopping
  • Forcing the energy with willpower — the light rises by invitation, by feeling, not by force
  • Practising in a public or shared space where interruption is possible

Questions From The Book

Why does Osho say this is not imagination but reality?

Chapter 46, "From the Beginning of Light"

Osho explains: modern physics confirms that all matter is condensed energy, and energy is light vibrating at different frequencies. Your body is literally made of light — photons, electrons, quanta of energy. When you “imagine” yourself as light, you are not adding something false; you are removing the false solidity that the mind projects. You are seeing what is actually there. The feeling confirms this — heat, tingling, aliveness — these are not hallucinations but direct perceptions of your own energy field.

What happens if energy gets stuck at a centre?

Chapter 46

Osho warns strongly: never leave this practice incomplete. If energy accumulates at the sex centre, you will feel extreme restlessness and sexual agitation. At the navel, digestive disturbance and anxiety. At the heart, uncontrollable emotions. At the throat, inability to sleep. At the third eye, splitting headaches. ALWAYS take the energy to the crown and release it. If you are interrupted, immediately visualise the light shooting up and out through the top of the head. This takes only seconds and prevents all problems.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This maps directly to the yogic model of kundalini rising through the sushumna channel. Whether you frame it as literal energy or as progressive relaxation of neuromuscular holding patterns along the spine, the experiential result is the same: a wave of aliveness ascending the body, culminating in a sense of boundless openness at the crown.

Neuroscience parallel: The spine contains the central nervous system. “Light rising through the vertebrae” may correspond to sequential activation of spinal nerve plexuses, which map roughly to the traditional chakra locations. The warmth practitioners report is consistent with increased blood flow to these regions during focused interoceptive attention.

One-line essenceYou are made of light pretending to be solid. Let the light remember itself — rising, centre by centre, until the pretence dissolves at the crown.
Sutra 71 of 112
Category: Light & Gazing · Tags: lightning · jump · chakras · energy · spark
Or in the spaces between, feel this as lightning.

In Plain English

This is a variation of the previous technique. Instead of light rising gradually like a river, feel it JUMPING between centres like lightning bolts — instantaneous, electric, sharp. The spaces between the chakras are bridged in a flash. For minds that are naturally jumpy, restless, or male-energy-dominant, this abrupt approach may work better than the slow, gradual river of the previous sutra.

How to Practice

  1. Same preparation as Technique 1: Total privacy. 40–60 minutes of uninterrupted time. Spine erect or lying flat. 5–10 breaths to settle.
  2. Feel the base centre: Become aware of warmth and energy at the base of the spine. Let it gather intensity for 2–3 minutes — like a storm cloud building charge.
  3. FLASH to navel: In one instant — not gradually — feel the energy JUMP from base to navel. Like a lightning bolt. Like a spark leaping a gap. The sensation should be sudden, electric, vivid.
  4. Let the navel absorb: Pause at the navel for 1–2 minutes. Let the energy settle and intensify again. Build the charge.
  5. FLASH to heart: Another instantaneous jump. Base-to-navel was the first bolt; navel-to-heart is the second. Feel the electric shock of arrival at the heart.
  6. Continue upward: Heart to throat — flash. Throat to third eye — flash. Third eye to crown — the final bolt.
  7. At the crown: Feel all the lightning energy collecting and then bursting upward like a thunderbolt into the sky. Complete release.
  8. Rest in the aftermath: After the final release, sit in the profound silence that follows a storm. The body may feel electric, buzzing, incredibly alive.
  9. If too much heat arises: Osho warns that this technique can generate intense heat. If you feel burning, agitation, or overwhelm, SWITCH immediately to the gradual method (Technique 1). Move the energy slowly and gently to the crown. Not everyone is suited to the lightning approach.
  10. Experiment with both: Try the gradual method for a week. Then try the lightning method for a week. Your body will tell you which one is yours. Some people are rivers; some people are lightning. Neither is superior.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Advanced
Duration
40–60 min
Posture
Seated or lying flat
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Restless minds; male-energy-dominant types; those who find gradual methods boring; impatient practitioners

Signs It’s Working

  • Distinct electric “jolts” as the energy jumps between centres
  • The body may physically jerk or twitch at the moment of each jump
  • A sense of speed, excitement, intensity that the gradual method lacks
  • After the final release at the crown: profound stillness, like the calm after a thunderstorm
  • Heat in the spine that is intense but not painful

Common Pitfalls

  • Generating too much heat and continuing anyway — if it burns, switch to the gradual method immediately
  • Making it mental/imaginary rather than felt — the “lightning” must be a body sensation, not a thought
  • Skipping the pause between jumps — each centre needs a moment to absorb the charge before the next bolt
  • Same as Technique 1: NEVER leave incomplete. Always release at the crown.

Questions From The Book

Why does Osho say this suits the male mind better?

Chapter 46, "Lightning or River"

Osho explains: by “male mind” he does not mean biological men only. He means the yang, active, restless quality of consciousness that jumps rather than flows. Some women have this quality; some men are more river-like. The point is energetic temperament, not gender. If you find gradual approaches tedious, if your mind naturally jumps from thing to thing, then this lightning method uses your own nature rather than fighting it. The restlessness becomes the vehicle.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Where Technique 1 is the parasympathetic approach (slow, gentle, river-like), this is the sympathetic burst — using intensity and surprise to bypass the thinking mind. The sudden “jump” doesn’t give the mind time to interfere. It’s the difference between easing into cold water and diving in.

Somatic parallel: In trauma therapy, some clients respond better to titration (gradual) and others to pendulation (rapid oscillation between states). This mirrors that distinction perfectly. Know your nervous system.

One-line essenceNot all rivers reach the ocean first. Sometimes lightning is faster — one bolt, one flash, and the sky is split open.
Sutra 72 of 112
Category: Light & Gazing · Tags: translucence · cosmos · presence · sensitivity · perception
Feel the cosmos as a translucent ever-living presence.

In Plain English

Develop such sensitivity that you begin to perceive everything around you — trees, rocks, sky, other people — as luminous, translucent, alive with inner light. Not as dead matter but as living presence. This is not a belief to adopt but a perception to develop through months of patient practice.

How to Practice

  1. Phase 1 — Develop sensitivity (Week 1–2): Before attempting the main technique, spend two weeks developing raw sensitivity. Follow a child for an hour and notice how they touch everything. Touch your own body with awareness — feel the texture of your skin, the warmth. In the shower, feel every drop of water. Eat slowly, tasting fully. This phase is essential preparation.
  2. Phase 2 — Candle gazing (Week 3–4): Sit in a dark room with a single candle. Adopt a prayerful attitude — not religious prayer but an attitude of reverence and openness. Gaze at the flame for 5 minutes without blinking if possible. Notice: the flame is not one colour. As your eyes relax, you’ll see the full rainbow within it — blue at the base, white at the centre, gold and red at the edges. The flame is alive, dancing, ever-changing.
  3. Phase 3 — Seeing luminosity in objects (Week 5–8): After candle-gazing, look at other objects in the room. Carry the quality of the flame-gazing — that soft, receptive, reverent seeing — to everything. See the table as glowing from within. See the wall as translucent. Don’t THINK “this is translucent” — FEEL it. Let it happen.
  4. Phase 4 — Walking outside (Week 9–12): Take this perception outdoors. Walk slowly. See trees not as solid wood but as columns of living light. See the sky not as empty space but as luminous presence. See rocks not as dead matter but as condensed light vibrating slowly. See other people as flames walking around in body-shaped lanterns.
  5. The key instruction: Don’t think ABOUT translucence. Don’t philosophise. Don’t try to convince yourself. Just soften your gaze, open your feeling, and ALLOW the world to reveal its luminous nature. It will, if you are patient and sensitive enough.
  6. Duration: Practise 1 hour daily. The full transformation typically takes 3 months of consistent practice. Some moments of “seeing through” may come earlier as glimpses.
  7. Attitude: Approach with childlike wonder, not adult analysis. The child sees the world as magical because the child has not yet learned to see it as dead matter. You are unlearning deadness.
  8. Integration: Once this perception stabilises, it becomes your default way of seeing. The world never looks dead again. This is what mystics mean by “seeing God everywhere” — not a belief but a direct perception of aliveness.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
60 min daily for 3 months
Posture
Various — seated for candle, walking for outdoors
Eyes
Open (soft gaze)
Best for
Visual people; artists; nature lovers; those who feel the world is “dead” or meaningless

Signs It’s Working

  • Moments of spontaneous beauty that stop you in your tracks
  • The candle flame reveals unexpected colours and depth
  • Objects begin to look “more real” — edges softer, colours richer, presence palpable
  • A sense of everything being alive, breathing, aware
  • Gratitude arises without reason
  • Other people notice something different about your eyes — a softness, a depth

Common Pitfalls

  • Trying to see translucence with the thinking mind — this blocks the perception
  • Skipping the sensitivity-building phase and jumping straight to “seeing light”
  • Getting discouraged in the first weeks when nothing seems to change
  • Forcing it — translucence reveals itself when you are receptive, not when you demand it
  • Confusing philosophical belief with direct perception — believing “everything is light” is not the same as SEEING it

Questions From The Book

How is this different from just pretending the world is beautiful?

Chapter 47, "Translucent Presence"

Osho is clear: this is not positive thinking, not affirmation, not pretending. It is a change in perception that happens naturally when sensitivity is sufficiently developed. When your eyes become soft and receptive enough, reality reveals a dimension that was always there but that the hardened, categorising mind cannot see. You are not adding beauty to the world — you are removing the barrier that prevented you from seeing what was always present.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Our default perceptual mode is utilitarian — we see objects in terms of use, category, and threat. This technique shifts perception from “what is it?” to “that it IS.” This mirrors the phenomenological “reduction” — bracketing interpretation to encounter raw presence. When interpretation drops, everything looks luminous because you’re seeing the aliveness that categories obscure.

Research parallel: Studies on awe show that experiences of beauty and vastness reduce default mode network activity and self-referential thinking. This technique systematically cultivates the perceptual conditions that produce awe — making the extraordinary perception of aliveness a stable trait rather than a fleeting state.

One-line essenceThe world is not dead. Your seeing has become dead. Resurrect your seeing, and the cosmos reveals itself as a translucent, ever-living presence it always was.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 49 · Sutras 73–75 · 3 techniques

Conscious Doing

Osho's framing

Osho opens with a piercing observation: when he looks into people's eyes, no one is really there - they exist absently. This absence, he says, is the root of all human suffering. Whatever you do, if done with full presence, becomes ecstasy; if done in absence, becomes hell. So there are two kinds of seeker - one obsessed with what to do, one focused on how to be. Only the second is on the right path. He retells Buddha turning back a man who wanted to serve the world: "You cannot do anything because you are not. First be." These three sutras (the clear summer sky, space absorbed in your head, knowing yourself as light) bring you to presence rather than to action.

Hell means your absence.

— Osho
Sutra 73 of 112
Category: Light & Gazing · Tags: sky · clarity · emptiness · open · staring
In summer when you see the entire sky endlessly clear, enter such clarity.

In Plain English

On a cloudless day, lie on your back and stare into the clear blue sky. Don’t think about the sky — enter it. Lose yourself in its boundlessness. When the outer sky fills you so completely that your inner space becomes sky-like, close your eyes — and discover the same infinite clarity within. The outer sky and the inner sky are one.

How to Practice

  1. Find your spot: A lonely beach, a rooftop, an open field, a garden — anywhere you can lie flat on your back with the full sky above you. The sky must be clear — cloudless, blue, boundless. Summer days are ideal.
  2. Lie down comfortably: Flat on your back, arms slightly away from the body, palms up. Let the body be completely relaxed. The ground holds you; you don’t need to hold yourself.
  3. Rule 1 — Don’t blink: Osho’s specific instruction. Keep your eyes open without blinking. Tears will come — this is good. The tears cleanse the eyes, and after the tears pass, vision becomes extraordinarily clear. The effort of not blinking keeps the mind from wandering.
  4. Rule 2 — Don’t think ABOUT the sky: Don’t say “how beautiful.” Don’t say “how vast.” Don’t analyse, don’t poeticise. Just stare. Let the sky pour into your eyes without commentary.
  5. Rule 3 — Stay at least 40 minutes: The mind will resist. At 5 minutes it will say “this is boring.” At 15 minutes it will create urgent thoughts. Stay. At 30–40 minutes, something shifts — the boundary between you and sky begins to dissolve.
  6. Enter the sky: Move deeper and deeper into the blue. Try to find its boundary — there is none. Try to find where it ends — it doesn’t. The boundlessness of the sky begins to infect your consciousness. Your mind, which is normally full of boundaries, starts to become boundless.
  7. The merging: At some point, “you” looking at “sky” becomes simply... sky. The watcher and the watched merge. This may come as a sudden shift or a gradual dissolution. Either way, it is unmistakable.
  8. Close your eyes: When the merging happens (or after 40 minutes), gently close your eyes. Now look within. The same sky is here — vast, clear, boundless, empty. The inner sky meets the outer sky. They were never separate.
  9. Alternative for cloudy days: If no clear sky is available, close your eyes and focus on the SPACE between thoughts. Not the thoughts themselves — the gaps. Those gaps are the inner sky. They are just as vast, just as clear, just as boundless. Rest in the gaps.
  10. Return: When you feel ready, bring attention back to the body. Feel the ground beneath you. Sit up slowly. The clarity of the sky often remains for hours afterward.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner
Duration
40+ min
Posture
Lying flat on back
Eyes
Open (without blinking) then closed
Best for
Everyone; especially anxious minds; overthinkers; those who feel trapped or confined

Signs It’s Working

  • Tears come and pass; vision becomes crystal-clear afterward
  • The sense of boundary between self and sky dissolves
  • A feeling of vast spaciousness, both outside and inside
  • When you close your eyes, the inner space feels as limitless as the outer
  • Thoughts slow dramatically or stop entirely
  • A deep, effortless peace — not imposed but natural, like the sky itself

Common Pitfalls

  • Thinking about the sky instead of entering it — every “how beautiful” is a barrier
  • Giving up at 10–15 minutes because “nothing is happening” — the shift needs 30–40 minutes
  • Blinking constantly — the non-blinking creates a specific quality of absorption; commit to it
  • Choosing a day with clouds — clouds give the mind objects to track; you need formless, clear blue
  • Looking at the sun (obviously!) — look at the sky AWAY from the sun

Questions From The Book

Why does Shiva say “in summer” specifically?

Chapter 47, "The Sky Technique"

Osho explains: in India, summer skies are the clearest — no clouds, no haze, pure blue vastness. But the deeper reason is that summer is when the body is most alive, most warm, most expanded. In winter we contract; in summer we naturally open. The combination of a clear sky and an open, warm body creates the ideal conditions for this dissolution of boundaries. However, any clear sky will work regardless of season.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: The clear sky is a perfect mirror for awareness itself — formless, boundless, unchanging, containing everything without being affected by anything. By staring into it, you are staring into a physical representation of your own deepest nature. The technique uses the outer analogy to reveal the inner reality.

Neuroscience parallel: Soft, unfocused gazing at a uniform visual field (Ganzfeld effect) is known to produce altered states of consciousness, perceptual dissolution, and reduced self-referential thinking. The brain, deprived of edges and objects to categorise, enters a state similar to deep meditation. This 5,000-year-old technique stumbled on what researchers confirmed only in the 20th century.

One-line essenceThe sky has no boundaries. Stare long enough, and you remember: neither do you.
Sutra 74 of 112
Category: Light & Gazing · Tags: space · head · absorption · vastness · expansion
Shakti, see all space as if already absorbed in your own head in the brilliance.

In Plain English

Expand your sense of self until all space — the room, the city, the sky, the cosmos — is contained within your own head. Not that you are in space, but that space is in you. This is the most powerful and potentially overwhelming technique in this category. The mind, which needs narrowness to exist, cannot survive when all space enters it. The mind dissolves; only brilliance remains.

How to Practice

  1. CAUTION: This technique should be done with a companion present (in the next room or nearby). It can produce dizziness, disorientation, or loss of consciousness in some practitioners. Do NOT attempt this alone until you have done it successfully with support at least 5–10 times.
  2. Week 1 — Small expansion: Lie in bed before sleep. Feel your body as normal. Then imagine — FEEL — your body extending 6 inches beyond its physical boundary in every direction. Feel your feet 6 inches longer, your head 6 inches taller, your body 6 inches wider. Just 6 inches. Get comfortable with this small expansion. Practice nightly for a week.
  3. Week 2 — Room-sized: Same practice, but now expand until you FEEL 12 feet tall, filling the entire room. The walls are inside you. The ceiling is inside your head. The floor is inside your feet. Don’t think it — FEEL it. Your body-sense expands to fill the room.
  4. Week 3 — House-sized: Expand further. Feel the whole house within you. Other rooms, other people — all inside your expanded body-sense. The roof is inside your head. The garden is inside your belly.
  5. Week 4 — City-sized and beyond: Continue expanding. The city is inside you. The countryside. The continent. The earth. The sky. Eventually: ALL SPACE is absorbed in your own head. You are not inside space — space is inside you.
  6. The critical moment: When space truly enters your head, the mind disappears. This is because the mind needs narrowness, confinement, boundaries to function. When all boundaries are gone — when infinity is inside you — the mind has no walls to bounce between. It collapses. What remains is “brilliance” — pure awareness without form.
  7. The impact is tremendous: Osho warns that this can be overwhelming. Some people faint. Some feel terror. Some experience ecstasy. This is why a companion is necessary — someone to bring you water, to ground you, to simply be present so you are not alone if the experience becomes too intense.
  8. Grounding afterward: After each session, deliberately contract back to body-size. Feel your physical boundaries. Stamp your feet. Drink cold water. Touch something solid. Don’t drive or operate machinery for at least 30 minutes after deep practice.
  9. Build over 3 months: This is NOT a technique to rush. Each week’s expansion must be stable before moving to the next. If any stage produces too much discomfort, stay at that stage until it becomes comfortable. There is no prize for speed.
  10. Group practice: Osho recommends doing this in a group of 3–5 people, all practising simultaneously. The shared field supports the expansion and provides safety.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Very Advanced
Duration
Build over 3 months (20–40 min per session)
Posture
Lying down (especially for advanced stages)
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Experienced meditators only; those with a stable sense of self who can afford to lose it temporarily

Signs It’s Working

  • Genuine physical sensation of the body expanding beyond its boundaries
  • A feeling of the room (or larger space) being “inside” rather than “around”
  • At advanced stages: the mind goes completely silent because it has no reference points
  • A sense of brilliance or luminosity filling the expanded space
  • Afterward: profound peace, spaciousness, and a lightness that lasts hours or days

Common Pitfalls

  • Attempting advanced stages without building gradually — this is dangerous
  • Doing it alone without support, especially in early attempts
  • Confusing intellectual understanding with felt experience — THINKING “space is in me” is worthless; you must FEEL it
  • Not grounding adequately afterward — this can leave you spacey and disoriented for hours
  • Pushing through fear or dizziness rather than backing off — respect your limits

Questions From The Book

Why does Osho say the mind needs narrowness to exist?

Chapter 47, "Space Absorbed in the Head"

Osho explains: the mind functions by creating boundaries — between this and that, here and there, self and other. It is a boundary-making mechanism. When you remove all boundaries by absorbing all space into yourself, the mind has nothing to do. It’s like a fish taken out of water — it cannot function in infinity. It needs the narrowness of “I am here, the world is there” to operate. Remove that division, and the mind simply stops. What remains is consciousness without mind — what the sutra calls “brilliance.”

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: The default sense of self has spatial boundaries: “I end at my skin.” This technique systematically expands that spatial self-model until it includes everything. When the boundary includes everything, it is no longer a boundary — and the self-model collapses. What’s left is unbounded awareness. This maps to what neuroscience calls “ego dissolution” or “oceanic boundlessness.”

Research parallel: Rubber hand illusion and full-body ownership transfer experiments show that the brain’s body schema is remarkably flexible — it can be extended to virtual avatars, tools, and even entire rooms. This technique leverages that neural plasticity deliberately, pushing the body schema to its absolute limit — and past it.

One-line essenceYou think you are inside space. Reverse it: space is inside you. And in that reversal, the walls of the mind come down forever.
Sutra 75 of 112
Category: Light & Gazing · Tags: light · flame · waking · sleeping · dreaming · continuity
Waking, sleeping, dreaming, know you as light.

In Plain English

Carry a single perception — that you are a flame of light — through all three states of consciousness: waking, dreaming, and deep sleep. Start with waking only. When the flame becomes stable enough that it persists into dreams, carry it deeper into dreamless sleep. When you remain aware through all three states, you become the fourth — turiya — the witness that never sleeps.

How to Practice

  1. Phase 1 — Waking state (Months 1–3): Throughout your day — while walking, eating, working, talking — maintain the feeling that a small, bright flame is burning in your heart centre. The body is merely the aura around this flame — a transparent glass chimney. You are not a solid physical body with a soul somewhere inside; you are a flame with a body-shaped glow around it.
  2. Make it felt, not thought: Don’t keep repeating “I am light.” Instead, FEEL the warmth at the heart. Feel the luminosity. Feel the lightness (both less-heavy and more-luminous). Let it become your background body-sense.
  3. Persist for 3 months: This is not a 20-minute meditation — it is a 24/7 remembrance during waking hours. At first you’ll forget constantly. That’s fine. Each time you remember, gently return to the flame. Over weeks, the gaps between forgetting shorten.
  4. The sign that Phase 1 is complete: You feel a continuous warmth in the heart area throughout the day without needing to remind yourself. The flame has become a background presence. When the remembrance has become effortless and nearly constant during waking hours, you are ready to proceed.
  5. Phase 2 — Dreaming state: Only after Phase 1 is stable. As you fall asleep each night, feel the flame at the heart. Let it be the last thing in awareness as you drift off. The flame will begin to appear in your dreams — sometimes as a literal light, sometimes as an unusual clarity within the dream. Eventually, the flame’s presence in dreams gives you lucidity — you know you are dreaming.
  6. When dreams disappear: As the flame becomes constant in dreams, something remarkable happens: dreams stop. Not because you are suppressing them, but because the flame’s light dissolves the dream-material. Dreams are made of unconsciousness; when light enters, they evaporate.
  7. Phase 3 — Deep sleep: Only when dreams have largely disappeared. Now the flame persists even in deep sleep. You are no longer unconscious during sleep — a witness remains, aware of the darkness, aware of the body resting, aware of the gap. This is turiya — the fourth state.
  8. Timeline: This is a long-term practice. Phase 1 alone takes 3 months minimum. The full practice may take a year or more. This is not a quick technique — it is a life-practice.
  9. Don’t skip phases: Each phase must be stable before moving to the next. If the waking-state flame is not yet noticed by others, the dreaming phase will not work. If dreams have not dissolved, deep-sleep awareness will not arise. Trust the sequence.
  10. The result: When turiya is established, you never truly sleep again — the body sleeps but you remain aware. This is not insomnia; the body gets full rest. But consciousness never switches off. You become continuous awareness — 24/7, unbroken, the same light in waking, dreaming, and deep sleep.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Advanced (long-term)
Duration
3+ months continuous (24/7 remembrance)
Posture
All postures — this is done in daily life
Eyes
Open and closed (all states)
Best for
Committed practitioners; those seeking continuity of awareness; lucid dreamers; people with strong devotional capacity

Signs It’s Working

  • Phase 1: You feel warmth at the heart centre consistently throughout the day
  • Phase 1: You feel lighter, less solid, more transparent throughout the day
  • Phase 2: The flame appears in dreams; lucid dreams begin
  • Phase 2: Dreams gradually become less frequent, then disappear
  • Phase 3: Moments of awareness during deep sleep — you know the body is asleep but “you” are awake
  • Full: Unbroken continuity — no gap in awareness between waking, dreaming, and sleep

Common Pitfalls

  • Making it a strain — the remembrance should be gentle, not a grim self-imposed duty
  • Expecting results in days or weeks — this is a months-to-years practice
  • Jumping to Phase 2 without the inner confirmation that the flame is continuous in your waking awareness
  • Confusing lucid dreaming techniques (reality checks, etc.) with this — this works through the flame, not through cognitive tricks
  • Sleep disturbance early on — if you lose sleep quality, you are trying too hard at bedtime; let it be soft

Questions From The Book

What is turiya and why is it called “the fourth”?

Chapter 48, "Know You As Light"

Osho explains: we normally know three states — waking, dreaming, and deep sleep. We cycle through them daily. But there is a fourth that is not a state at all — it is the awareness that witnesses all three states without being any of them. It is called “the fourth” (turiya) simply because it has no name of its own — it is beyond naming, beyond states. It is what you are when you stop identifying with any particular state. This technique creates continuity through all states, and that continuity IS turiya.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This technique creates what sleep researchers call “witnessing sleep” — a condition documented in advanced meditators where gamma brainwave activity persists during NREM sleep. The meditator reports continuous awareness even in deep sleep. This is not sleep deprivation; the body’s restoration continues normally. It is consciousness becoming independent of its usual state-dependent switching.

Modern research: Studies on long-term TM practitioners (20+ years) show increased EEG coherence during sleep and self-reports of continuous witnessing awareness. This maps precisely to what this 5,000-year-old sutra describes as the goal.

One-line essenceA flame that burns through waking, dreams, and sleep without flickering — that flame is you. Not the body, not the mind, not any state — the light that witnesses them all.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 51 · Sutras 76–78 · 3 techniques

Coming Back to Existence

Osho's framing

Osho opens with a folk joke: a village postmaster meets a "Doctor of Philosophy" and replies, "I have never heard of any case of this disease here." The joke, he says, is nearer to the truth than we think - philosophy is a kind of disease, abstract thinking that moves in circles and never lands. Science and religion both reach answers because both rest on experiment; only philosophy speculates without experimenting and concludes nothing. Religion is the deeper science because the experimenter himself is the experiment. The warning is sharp: the mind will gladly turn even meditation into another topic to think "about." Don't read about meditation - do it.

Life's problems can be solved only when you become deeply rooted in existence.

— Osho
Sutra 76 of 112
Category: Light & Gazing · Tags: darkness · night · form · formlessness · surrender
In rain during a black night, enter that blackness as the form of forms.

In Plain English

On a moonless, rainy night — when darkness is absolute — go outside with open eyes and stare into that blackness. Not the blackness behind closed eyelids (that is merely the negative of light) but real, external, total darkness. Let the darkness enter your eyes. Let yourself dissolve into it. The darkness is not empty — it is the “form of forms,” the womb from which all creation arises. Enter it as you would enter a mother’s embrace.

How to Practice

  1. Phase 1 — Overcome fear of darkness (Weeks 1–2): Most people have a deep, unconscious fear of darkness. Before attempting the main technique, befriend the dark. Sit in your room with all lights off for 30 minutes nightly. Don’t meditate — just BE in the dark. Let your eyes adjust. Let the fear surface, be felt, and pass. Do this until darkness feels comfortable, even inviting.
  2. Phase 2 — Develop love for darkness: Begin to notice what darkness gives you that light cannot: rest for the eyes, cool relaxation, mystery, womb-like safety, the dissolution of visual boundaries. Let yourself LOVE the dark — as a friend, as a mother, as the original state from which you came.
  3. Phase 3 — The main practice: Wait for a moonless, rainy night. Rain is specified because rain clouds block all starlight, making the darkness truly total. Also, the sound of rain relaxes the mind and provides a gentle background for surrender.
  4. Go outside: Stand or sit in a safe, open area. Keep your eyes OPEN — this is crucial. You are not looking at closed-eyelid darkness (which is just retinal noise) but at actual, external, absolute darkness. There is nothing to see — and that “nothing” becomes everything.
  5. Let darkness enter your eyes: Normally you project outward through the eyes — looking AT things. Now reverse it. Let the darkness come IN through the eyes. Be receptive. Your eyes are doors — normally you go out through them; now let the dark come in. Tears may come; the eyes may hurt slightly. Persist.
  6. Enter the darkness: When real darkness has entered you, a shift happens: YOU enter IT. The boundary between inside and outside dissolves. You are no longer a person surrounded by darkness. You ARE the darkness. There is no separation.
  7. Feel darkness as the cosmic womb: Lie down if you can (dry shelter nearby if raining heavily). Feel yourself held by infinite blackness as a baby is held in the womb. There is nothing to do, nothing to be, no identity needed in total darkness. The ego cannot exist without light to reflect off of. In absolute dark, you become formless — and that formlessness is peace.
  8. Carry a patch of darkness: After the session, as you return to light, try to carry a patch of that inner darkness with you. Throughout the next day, remember the black velvet quality behind your solar plexus. Osho says: those who carry darkness within become deeply relaxed, cool, passionless, free of anger and agitation.
  9. Historical note: The Essenes — an ancient mystery school — meditated on God as absolute darkness, not light. Their reasoning: light is created; darkness is uncreated. Darkness was before “let there be light.” Darkness IS the ultimate formlessness from which all forms emerge.
  10. Duration: 1 hour sessions, practised for 3 months for full transformation. Weekly sessions on dark nights as available.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
1 hour daily for 3 months
Posture
Standing, sitting, or lying — outside in darkness
Eyes
Open (into real darkness)
Best for
Those afraid of the dark; insomniacs; people with anger issues; those exhausted by too much stimulation

Signs It’s Working

  • Fear of darkness transforms into love of darkness
  • A sense of being held, cradled, embraced by the dark
  • The boundary between inner and outer dissolves — you become the darkness
  • Deep physical relaxation that lasts into the next day
  • Reduction in anger, agitation, and restlessness over weeks
  • Sleep quality improves dramatically (darkness becomes associated with deep rest)

Common Pitfalls

  • Skipping Phase 1 — doing the outdoor practice while still afraid of the dark makes it a horror exercise, not meditation
  • Keeping eyes closed — closed-eye darkness is NOT the same; it’s just retinal patterns. You need real external darkness entering open eyes
  • Choosing a night that isn’t dark enough — streetlights, starlight, or moonlight defeat the purpose
  • Fighting the darkness rather than surrendering into it
  • Safety: don’t do this in unsafe locations; choose a known, safe outdoor space

Questions From The Book

Why does Osho call darkness “the form of forms”?

Chapter 48, "Darkness: The Womb"

Osho explains: light reveals forms but is not itself the source of form. Darkness is the original state — the uncreated backdrop from which all creation emerges. Before the Big Bang, there was darkness. Before conception, the womb is dark. Before a seed sprouts, it is underground in darkness. Darkness is the matrix, the mould, the womb of all forms. It is called “the form of forms” because every form arises from it and returns to it. To enter darkness consciously is to return to the source of all creation.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Complete darkness eliminates all visual input — the brain’s most active sensory channel goes quiet. The visual cortex, deprived of input, enters a state of reduced metabolic activity. The result is a profound quieting of the entire nervous system. Combined with the symbolic/archetypal associations of darkness (womb, rest, dissolution of identity), this creates conditions for deep surrender.

Therapeutic parallel: Darkness retreats (practiced in some traditions for 1–14 days) have been shown to increase melatonin, DMT precursors, and endogenous opioids. Even one hour of total darkness produces measurable relaxation responses. The ancient technique predates the modern discovery of these mechanisms by millennia.

One-line essenceLight reveals the world. Darkness reveals you — the formless awareness that was here before any world arose.
Sutra 77 of 112
Category: Light & Gazing · Tags: darkness · inner · faults · purification · eyes
When a moonless rainy night is not present, close eyes and find blackness before you. Opening eyes, see blackness. So faults disappear forever.

In Plain English

This is the indoor version of the previous technique — for when actual darkness is unavailable. Close your eyes and find the natural blackness that is there. Then open your eyes and PROJECT that inner darkness outward — see blackness even with eyes open. When inner and outer darkness merge into totality, all “faults” (disturbances, impurities, egoic patterns) disappear — because in total darkness there is no ego, no identity, no separation between self and other.

How to Practice

  1. Find a quiet space: Any room, any time. Dim the lights if possible, but this technique works even in a lit room because you are working with inner darkness projected outward.
  2. Close your eyes: Look at the blackness that appears. Don’t look for colours, patterns, or images — focus on the BLACK itself. The darkness behind your eyelids. Let it deepen, thicken, become velvety.
  3. Stay with the blackness for 5–10 minutes: Let it become rich, deep, total. If colours or images appear, gently return attention to the black background behind them. The darkness is always there behind everything else.
