  4. Open your eyes: Now, keeping that quality of inner darkness alive, open your eyes. See the outer world but MAINTAIN the sense of darkness. Project the inner blackness outward. Let it overlay what you see. The room may appear darker, edges may soften, the visual field may seem less solid.
  5. Merge inner and outer: The inner darkness (eyes closed) and the outer seeing (eyes open) are not two different things. Let them merge. The darkness is everywhere — behind appearances, between objects, within objects. Everything floats in darkness.
  6. Alternate: Close eyes (find blackness) → open eyes (maintain blackness) → close eyes (deepen) → open eyes (maintain). Each cycle, the darkness becomes more stable and pervasive even with eyes open.
  7. “Faults disappear”: Osho explains that “faults” here means disturbances, impurities, mental agitations. In total darkness there is no ego — because ego needs light (reflection, visibility, comparison) to exist. When you carry total darkness even with eyes open, the ego has no mirror. Without a mirror, it cannot exist. Without ego, its faults (anger, greed, jealousy) have no home.
  8. Practice duration: 20–30 minutes per session. Can be done daily without special conditions.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
20–30 min
Posture
Seated
Eyes
Alternating open and closed
Best for
Those who cannot access outdoor darkness; city-dwellers; people who want to dissolve habitual patterns; those working with shadow material

Signs It’s Working

  • The inner blackness becomes genuinely deep — not grey but truly black, velvety, rich
  • When eyes open, you can maintain a quality of darkness pervading the visual field
  • A sense of peace and egolessness during the practice
  • Over weeks: habitual reactive patterns (anger, jealousy, comparison) lose their grip
  • A general sense of purification — old patterns falling away without effort

Common Pitfalls

  • Getting distracted by colours and patterns behind closed eyes — these are not the blackness; look past them
  • Giving up because you “can’t see blackness with eyes open” — it’s a quality, not a visual override; be subtle
  • Expecting dramatic experiences — this is a quiet, purifying technique; the results show in daily life, not in the session
  • Interpreting “faults disappear” as instant transformation — it is gradual dissolution over weeks

Questions From The Book

What is the difference between this darkness and depression?

Chapter 48, "Finding Blackness"

Osho distinguishes clearly: depression is darkness you are FIGHTING — you don’t want it, it oppresses you, you feel trapped. This meditative darkness is darkness you ENTER willingly with love and openness. One is resistance; the other is surrender. When you resist darkness, it becomes depression. When you embrace it, it becomes liberation. The darkness is the same — your relationship to it makes all the difference.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This technique uses the after-image principle in reverse. Normally, staring at a bright object creates a dark after-image. Here, you cultivate the “dark after-image” of inner darkness and project it onto the visual field. The result is a paradoxical state of seeing-yet-resting — the visual system is open but not in its usual acquisitive mode. This mirrors the “open awareness” state in contemplative practice.

Shadow work parallel: The “faults disappearing” maps to Jung’s concept of integrating the shadow. What you cannot see (because it’s in the dark, unconscious) controls you. When you voluntarily enter darkness — when you make friends with the unseen — the shadow loses its power over you. The faults don’t disappear by being fixed but by being seen, accepted, and integrated through darkness.

One-line essenceFind the darkness within; project it without. In total blackness, the ego finds no mirror — and without a mirror, its faults have nowhere to live.
Sutra 78 of 112
Category: Light & Gazing · Tags: attention · presence · immediacy · this-point · awareness
Wherever your attention alights, at this very point, experience.

In Plain English

This is perhaps the simplest and most profound technique in the entire light category — and it is disguised as nothing special. Wherever your attention goes — to a sound, a colour, a face, a sensation, a thought — at THAT very point, be completely present. Don’t move on. Don’t interpret. Don’t compare. Just EXPERIENCE the raw aliveness of whatever attention touches. The point of attention becomes luminous when you are fully there. The “light” in this technique is the light of awareness itself.

How to Practice

  1. No special conditions needed: This can be done anywhere, anytime, in any posture, with eyes open or closed. It requires nothing except total presence.
  2. Notice where your attention goes: Right now, something is attracting your attention — these words, a background sound, a body sensation, a thought. Whatever it is, DON’T interfere with it. Let attention go where it naturally goes.
  3. At THAT point, be totally present: The key word is “at this very point.” Don’t move to the next thing. Don’t label it. Don’t think about it. Just BE with it. Experience it raw, naked, without the layer of interpretation.
  4. The point becomes luminous: When you are fully present with anything — a sound, a colour, a texture, even a thought — it begins to glow with aliveness. This is not hallucination; it is what happens when attention is undivided. Normally attention is scattered; when collected at one point, it creates a quality of brilliance at that point.
  5. Don’t hold or grasp: When attention naturally moves to something else, let it. But at the NEW point, be fully there again. The practice is not concentration (forcing attention to stay) but presence (being total wherever attention happens to be).
  6. Formal practice (20 min): Sit quietly. Let attention wander freely. But wherever it alights — on a breath, a sound, an itch, a memory — be completely there for as long as it stays. When it moves, follow it with the same totality. Every point of attention becomes a meditation object.
  7. Informal practice (all day): Throughout the day, periodically notice: where is my attention right now? At that point, be fully present for a moment. Then continue with your activity. This takes zero time out of your day but transforms the quality of every moment.
  8. The insight: You will discover that what we call “light” or “consciousness” or “awareness” is simply what happens when attention is total. There is no separate mystical light to seek — the light IS attention itself, fully present. Every moment is already luminous; you just haven’t been fully there to notice.
  9. Don’t make it complicated: This technique is so simple that the mind refuses to believe it can work. The mind wants something complex, esoteric, special. But the sutra is clear: wherever attention alights, at THIS VERY POINT, experience. That’s all. Nothing more is needed.
  10. Deepening: Over time, the gaps between moments of presence shrink. You spend more and more of the day actually HERE rather than lost in commentary about here. This is the gradual illumination of life itself — not through special states but through ordinary attention made total.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner
Duration
All day (or 20 min formal sitting)
Posture
Any
Eyes
Open or closed
Best for
Everyone; especially those who find other techniques too complex; minimalists; those who want meditation integrated into daily life

Signs It’s Working

  • Moments of surprising vividness in ordinary experience
  • Colours seem brighter, sounds clearer, textures richer — not because they changed but because you are actually there
  • A sense of presence that is effortless and natural
  • Less time lost in mental commentary; more time in raw experience
  • Others notice you are more attentive, more “here” in conversations
  • The realisation that awareness itself is luminous — you don’t need to add light; you need to remove distraction

Common Pitfalls

  • Making it into concentration — forcing attention to stay on one thing. This is about being present WHEREVER attention goes, not about controlling where it goes
  • Dismissing it as too simple — “this can’t be it.” It is.
  • Adding interpretation: “I hear a bird” is interpretation. The raw sound BEFORE you know it’s a bird — that is experience
  • Trying to be present to EVERYTHING simultaneously — the sutra says “wherever attention alights” (singular). Be with one point at a time.

Questions From The Book

How can something so simple be called a “light technique”?

Chapter 49, "The Point of Attention"

Osho explains: light is not only what eyes see. The deepest light is awareness itself — the luminosity of knowing. When you are fully present with anything, that thing becomes lit up FROM WITHIN by your attention. A flower you truly see glows. A sound you truly hear shimmers. This is not metaphor — it is direct experience. Attention IS light. When you are absent-minded, the world is dim. When you are present, the world is radiant. This technique reveals that you ARE the source of light — not the sun, not a candle, but awareness itself.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This is open monitoring meditation in its purest form — non-judgemental awareness of whatever arises, without preference or control. Research shows this mode activates the salience network and insular cortex, increasing interoceptive and exteroceptive sensitivity. The world literally becomes more vivid because you are processing more of its richness rather than filtering it through concepts.

Phenomenological parallel: Husserl’s “return to the things themselves” is exactly this — bracketing all theory to encounter raw phenomena. When you experience the sound before labelling it “bird,” you encounter what the phenomenologists call the “given” — reality before interpretation. That raw given is always luminous. Interpretation dims it.

One-line essenceYou are the light. Wherever attention lands with total presence, that point glows. No special technique needed — just be completely here.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 53 · Sutras 79–81 · 3 techniques

From Death to Deathlessness

Osho's framing

All enlightened ones, Osho says, agree on one thing only: the ego is the single barrier between you and reality. Trying to drop it is unnecessary - it is a shadow of inattention; bring light to it and it disappears like darkness in a lit room. He gives a usable test: when you are truly silent inside, you exist but no "I" is felt. The instant you fall into anger or desire, the I crystallises again. The ego is the past mind organising itself at the periphery of consciousness - memory pretending to be a self. That is why love and meditation, both ego-dissolving, are the same phenomenon.

If you know love, there is no need to know God - you have known him already.

— Osho
Sutra 79 of 112
Category: Death Contemplation · Tags: fire · burning · ashes · deathless
Focus on fire rising through your form from the toes up until the body burns to ashes but not you.

In Plain English

Visualise fire consuming your body from the toes upward. Feel the flesh burning, bones turning to ash. Continue until nothing physical remains. Then notice: YOU are still here. What survives the fire? What cannot burn? That deathless awareness which witnesses even its own cremation — THAT is what you truly are. This is rehearsal for physical death.

How to Practice

Note from the source: Osho's instruction for this technique is much simpler and shorter than the elaborate ten-step narration originally on this page. He gives two prerequisites and a simple practice. Both are restored below.

Prerequisite 1 — Watch a cremation. Buddha required disciples to spend three months at the burning ground before attempting this. Osho relaxes the rule: "if you want to do this technique, go to the burning place. Watch — not for three months — but at least watch one body being burned; observe it." Without this, the visualisation has no real referent.

Prerequisite 2 — Pre-meditation of deep exhalation. For about 15 minutes, breathe in normally but pour all attention into the exhalation. Inhalation is associated with life; exhalation is associated with death. Osho says, "if you can pay all attention to exhalation… you will have a taste of [death]." This primes the body for the burning.

  1. Lie down in corpse pose on your back. Eyes closed. Body completely still. Conceive of yourself as already dead, laid out for cremation.
  2. Start the fire at the toes. Why toes? Osho is specific: because they are the most far away point from the ego. The ego sits in the head; cremating from the head down would meet too much resistance. Start where you are least defended.
  3. Watch the fire rise upward. Each part turns to ash. Don't narrate elaborately — just watch and let it burn. Take whatever time you need; don't hurry.
  4. Burn through to the head. When the head is gone, every cell is ash, every memory dissolved.
  5. "But not you." Stand on the hill. Now — this is the entire technique — look at the burning body from a distance, as a watcher on a hill looks at a corpse below. The fire could not reach you because you were never the body. You were always the one watching.
  6. Osho's diagnostic for whether this worked: "if the memory is still there, then the body remains and you have been playing tricks. If really you go deep in the feeling that the body is dead, burning, and the fire has completely destroyed it, you will not have any memory in that moment." No memory = it worked.
  7. End there. Do not "reform the body" or "put on the garment" — Osho's instruction does not include reconstitution. Simply rest as the watcher on the hill, then open your eyes when the sitting ends.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
25–30 min
Posture
Lying flat (corpse pose / shavasana)
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Visual meditators; those with fear of death; anyone wanting to discover the deathless experientially; people comfortable with intense visualisation

Signs It’s Working

Osho gives one criterion for this technique. Restored below.

  • Memory is gone in the moment of completion. Osho's exact criterion: if memory of who-you-are is still operating after the body has burned, the burning was play-acting. Genuine entry into the technique produces a moment with no remembered identity.
  • The "watcher on the hill" perspective stabilises — you can see the burned body from outside it
  • Fear of physical death noticeably lessens between sessions

Common Pitfalls

  • Skipping the cremation-observation prerequisite — without a real referent the imagery floats free of the body
  • Skipping the 15-minute exhalation pre-meditation — this is what primes the body for the burning
  • Starting from the head (the ego will refuse) — always start from the toes, as Osho specifies
  • Rushing — the fire's pace must be slow enough that each part actually feels gone before the next is reached
  • Reconstituting the body at the end — this is an addition not in Osho; simply end as the watcher
  • Treating it as relaxation — this is a death rehearsal; bring real gravity

Questions From The Book

What is the “you” that survives the burning?

Chapter 55, "The Fire of Dissolution"

Osho explains: it is not a soul in the religious sense — not a little person hiding inside. It is pure awareness, consciousness without content. It has no form, no name, no history, no preferences. It simply IS — the witnessing presence that was always here but was obscured by identification with the body-mind. The fire burns away the identification. What remains is not something new; it is what was always here, now revealed.

Is this technique dangerous for people with death anxiety?

Chapter 55

Osho says: it is precisely FOR people with death anxiety. The anxiety exists because death is avoided, never faced. This technique faces it deliberately and discovers: the thing feared (annihilation) does not actually happen. What you discover is that you CANNOT be annihilated — only the body can. Once this is experienced directly (not believed philosophically), death anxiety dissolves permanently. The cure for fear of death is dying consciously — in meditation — and discovering you survive.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This is a systematic disidentification practice using the body’s most primal fear (being consumed by fire) as the vehicle. By imaginatively destroying the body while maintaining awareness, you experientially discover that awareness is not dependent on the body. This is the same insight that near-death experience researchers report survivors describing: “I was aware even though my body was clinically dead.”

Terror Management Theory: Psychology shows that most human anxiety is ultimately rooted in death-terror (TMT research). This technique addresses that root directly. Rather than building elaborate defences against death-awareness (religion, legacy, distraction), it faces death head-on and discovers that awareness survives. This is the most radical form of anxiety treatment: dissolving the root rather than managing the symptoms.

One-line essenceLet fire consume every cell of your body until only ashes remain — then notice: you are still here. What the fire cannot touch is what you truly are. The deathless was always here.
Sutra 80 of 112
Category: Death Contemplation · Tags: meditate · make · believe · world
Meditate on the make-believe world as burning to ashes and become being above human.

In Plain English

This is the companion to sutra 79 (the body burning). Now widen the fire: the entire world — cities, mountains, oceans, people — reduces to ashes in your imagination. Osho calls the world “make-believe” because it stands only because you go on believing in it. Watch it burn, and the moment the imagined burning becomes real to you, you have stepped beyond the ordinary human view.

How to Practice

  1. This technique is best after you have practised the body-burning sutra (#79). If burning the body in imagination has become possible, the world is only the body extended — you can do it. If body-burning is too close, Osho explicitly says: start with the world instead, then come closer to yourself.
  2. Before sitting, train your imagination on something small. Osho gives a test: clasp both hands together, close your eyes, and for ten minutes vividly believe the hands cannot be opened. Then try to open them. About four people in ten will find their hands locked. If yours lock, you know your imagination is strong enough to take what follows seriously. If not, repeat for a few days — imagination is trainable.
  3. Sit comfortably, eyes closed.
  4. Begin far away. Picture the city you live in, then the country, then the continent, then the whole earth, the moon, the sun, the stars. Hold the whole world in your imagination as a single field.
  5. Now light it on fire. Not violently — let the flames begin somewhere and spread. The earth begins to burn. Buildings collapse into ash. Forests turn black. Oceans steam and dry. The mountains turn to powder. Continue until the whole world is one sheet of fire.
  6. Watch quietly. Don’t add fear, don’t add grief. The make-believe world is being shown to be make-believe — the fire only reveals what was already true.
  7. Stay with the burning long enough that the picture becomes vivid. Osho says the first attempts will feel “just imagination,” because the world will seem to still be there afterwards. Don’t worry about that. Keep the visualisation steady.
  8. As the fire works through, let people you know enter the field too — not as targets of cruelty, but as part of the make-believe. They too dissolve. Possessions, money, ambitions, fears all turn to ash.
  9. When everything has burned, sit in the silence of the after-fire. Only ash, and you watching the ash. Osho calls the one who can see this clearly “being above human” — literally, super-human, no longer caught in the world’s spell.
  10. Notice what cannot burn. The flames take everything that was made of belief. What is still there afterwards is what was never made of belief. That is what Shiva is pointing to.
  11. Don’t do this once and check it off. Repeat over weeks. Each session the burning becomes more vivid and the after-silence deeper.
  12. When you open your eyes, the world will of course still be there. Don’t treat that as failure. The technique’s test is not whether the world burned, but whether your relationship to it is lighter, less gripped. After enough sessions you walk through the world knowing it as ashes already.

Osho’s Key Insight

Commentary from 53. From Death to Deathlessness in The Book of Secrets.

Source: 53. From Death to Deathlessness, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 81 of 112
Category: Imagination · Tags: subjectively · letters · flow · words
As subjectively, letters flow into words and words into sentences, and as, objectively, circles flow into worlds and worlds into principles, find at last these converging in your being.

In Plain English

Inside, letters become words become sentences. Outside, atoms become worlds become great principles. Both rivers are flowing in the same direction — toward you. Open the doors of the body and let the universe pour through, until everything that is happening is happening through you. The ego, which lives by keeping the world out, cannot survive this kind of openness.

How to Practice

  1. This is a technique of vulnerability. Begin by accepting Osho’s starting point: life is insecure by its nature, and any attempt to seal yourself off from it kills the life as well as the danger. The ego is the citadel of security; this technique is meant to make a few cracks in the wall.
  2. Sit under a tree where there is some breeze. Close your eyes. Listen to the rustling of the leaves.
  3. Feel the wind move toward the tree, through the tree, around your body. Now make a small inner shift: instead of letting the wind pass by you, let it pass through you. Become like the tree — porous. The wind enters from one side and leaves from the other.
  4. This is not pure imagination. Osho is precise: every cell of the body breathes; if all the pores were sealed and only the nose left open, you would die in three hours. So the wind really is passing through — you have just forgotten. Re-feel it.
  5. Next, sit in early-morning sunlight. Don’t simply feel the rays touching the skin — let them enter you and pass through. The sunlight goes in through every pore, lights up the inside, and leaves on the other side.
  6. Try the same with sound. In a temple, beside a bell, beside a waterfall — listen with the whole body, not the ears. Every cell drinks the sound. The sound enters from one side, passes through, leaves from the other. Osho says round temple shapes were built so that bell-sound converges on the listener from all directions, exactly to support this practice.
  7. Now let language do the same. Read or hear words you trust. Don’t process them through the head; let them flow in — letters into words, words into sentences, sentences arriving in your being — and pass through. The first half of the sutra (the subjective river) is being lived.
  8. Now widen to the world. Stand outside under the open sky. Let the air, the light, the sound, the smells, the warmth, the cold, all converge on you. From every direction, the universe is moving inward. Don’t resist any of it. The second half of the sutra (the objective river — circles into worlds, worlds into principles) is converging on this point you call yourself.
  9. The instant resistance drops, the “I” cannot continue. Osho: “In that openness where the whole existence is converging in you, you cannot exist as an I. You will exist as an open space, but not as a crystallised I.”
  10. Don’t fight the natural fear that arises. Some part of you will protest because the wall is being asked to fall. Notice the protest, don’t obey it.
  11. Practise saying inner Yes. Osho calls a real theist someone who has said yes to the whole of existence and has no inner “no.” Each yes opens one more pore. The technique cannot proceed where there is even one held no.
  12. Sit daily under sky, tree, or sun. The training of openness is gradual. Each session, the universe converges a little more freely. The day you discover there is no longer anyone here for it to converge upon — that is the realisation the sutra promises.

Osho’s Key Insight

Commentary from 53. From Death to Deathlessness in The Book of Secrets.

Source: 53. From Death to Deathlessness, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 55 · Sutras 82–83 · 2 techniques

Only the Unreal Dissolves

Osho's framing

Osho opens with an anecdote: a small-town mayor finds out that the priest, the gas-station attendant and the barber all think he is a bum - yet the mayor himself believes he holds the office for its honour. That, he says, is exactly the ego's situation. Only you take your ego seriously; for everyone else it is irrelevant, and reality itself cannot support it because reality is one and the ego pretends to be separate. So we avoid reality, build a glasshouse of dreams, and live inside it. Deep sleep, where the I disappears, is why mornings feel fresh; dream-filled nights leave you tired because the I persists.

You are just a wave. The wave comes and goes; the ocean is not worried about it.

— Osho
Sutra 82 of 112
Category: Emptiness & Void · Tags: identification · organs · felt-sense · body · ownership
Feel: My thought, I-ness, internal organs — me.

In Plain English

Feel into your internal organs — heart beating, lungs breathing, belly digesting. Then ask: am I these? Feel into thoughts arising. Am I these? Feel into the sense of “I.” Am I even this? Each layer is investigated and found to be an OBJECT of awareness, not awareness itself. The void opens behind all identifications.

How to Practice

  1. Find a quiet space: Sit comfortably with eyes closed. Settle the body with 5–7 deep breaths. Let the surface mind quiet. Don’t rush into the technique — give yourself 3–5 minutes of simple breathing first.
  2. Feel the internal organs: Bring awareness to your heart — feel it beating. Don’t think about it, FEEL the pulse from inside. Move to your lungs — feel them expanding, contracting. Feel the belly working, digesting. Feel the blood moving through vessels. Spend 5 minutes simply FEELING your internal machinery at work.
  3. Ask the first question: “Am I these organs?” Not intellectually — FEEL the question. The heart beats — but you can observe it beating. If you can observe it, you cannot BE it. The observer is separate from the observed. FEEL this separation. The organs are objects in your awareness.
  4. Move to thoughts: Now notice thoughts arising. Watch them come and go like clouds. Ask: “Am I these thoughts?” Again, FEEL it — if you can watch a thought appear and disappear, you are not the thought. You are the space in which thoughts move. Let this sink in deeply — not as philosophy but as felt experience.
  5. Move to the “I” feeling: This is the subtlest layer. Feel the sense of “I am” — the raw feeling of existing, of being someone. Now ask: “Am I even this?” Can you observe the I-feeling itself? If yes, then even the I-sense is an OBJECT — something witnessed, not the witness.
  6. Rest in what remains: When you have peeled away organs, thoughts, and I-ness — what remains? Don’t answer with words. Don’t conceptualise. Just REST in whatever is left. It will feel like nothing — and that nothing is the void. It is spacious, peaceful, without centre or boundary.
  7. Stay in the void: Remain in this emptiness for as long as it holds naturally. Don’t try to maintain it — if thoughts return, simply notice them as objects again, and the void reopens. Each time you notice an object AS an object, the void behind it becomes visible.
  8. The neti-neti process: This is essentially the ancient Upanishadic method — “not this, not this.” You are not rejecting anything; you are simply seeing clearly that each layer is YOURS but not YOU. The body is yours — not you. Thoughts are yours — not you. Even I-ness is yours — not you.
  9. Return gently: When ready, allow the I-sense to reconstitute naturally. Feel the body again. Open eyes slowly. The insight remains: everything you thought was “you” is actually something you HAVE. What you ARE is the void itself — the empty awareness that has all these things.
  10. Daily integration: Throughout the day, periodically ask yourself: “What am I right now?” and instead of answering, simply feel the question dissolve into spaciousness. Each inquiry is a micro-taste of the void.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
25 min
Posture
Seated, eyes closed
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Intellectual types; those who enjoy inquiry; meditators comfortable with subtle investigation

Signs It’s Working

  • A clear felt sense that you can observe your own organs “from outside”
  • Thoughts begin to feel like weather — passing through, not personal
  • The I-sense becomes lighter, less solid, more transparent
  • Moments of spacious emptiness that feel profoundly restful
  • A background sense of “I am not this body” that persists into daily life
  • Reduced reactivity — emotions arise but are seen as objects, not as “me”

Common Pitfalls

  • Making it intellectual rather than felt — philosophising about identity instead of FEELING into it
  • Creating a new identity as “the void” — the void is not another thing to become; it is what remains when becoming stops
  • Dissociating from the body (numbing out) instead of clearly seeing it as an object — you should feel MORE, not less
  • Rushing through the layers without really settling into each one
  • Expecting a dramatic experience — the void is often experienced as profound ordinariness

Questions From The Book

How is this different from dissociation or depersonalisation?

Chapter 58, "Investigating Identity"

Osho clarifies: in dissociation, awareness CONTRACTS away from experience — you feel numb, disconnected, frightened. In this technique, awareness EXPANDS to include experience — you feel everything more clearly but recognise it as object rather than self. The test is simple: are you feeling MORE or LESS? If less, you are dissociating. If more, you are awakening. True void is full of feeling — it simply doesn’t identify with what it feels.

What does “I-ness” mean here?

Chapter 58

Osho explains: “I-ness” (ahamkara in Sanskrit) is not any particular thought but the background FEELING of being a separate entity. It is the feeling “I exist as someone distinct.” Even when all particular thoughts stop, this subtle feeling of separate existence usually continues. This sutra asks you to investigate even THAT — to ask whether even the sense of being “someone” is just another object that arises in the void of awareness.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This is the core method of Advaita Vedanta — self-inquiry. Everything that can be observed cannot be the observer. By systematically recognising each layer of experience as observed rather than observer, you arrive at the observer itself — which turns out to be empty of all qualities, yet aware. Modern neuroscience calls this “minimal phenomenal experience” — awareness stripped of all content.

Research parallel: Studies on meditation-induced ego dissolution show that experienced practitioners can reliably enter states where the sense of self-boundary disappears while awareness remains. fMRI shows reduced default mode network activity (the “self-network”) during these states. This technique provides a systematic path to that dissolution.

One-line essenceYou are not your thoughts, not your organs, not even your I-ness. Peel everything away and what remains — the void that witnesses all — is what you truly are.
Sutra 83 of 112
Category: Emptiness & Void · Tags: before-desire · before-knowing · prior-self · dissolution · beauty
Before desire and before knowing, how can I say I am? Consider. Dissolve in the beauty.

In Plain English

Before any desire arose in you, before you knew anything — WHAT WERE YOU? Not your name (that came later), not your body-image (that developed), not your desires (they weren’t born yet). Go back before all of it. What remains? A vast, beautiful emptiness — the void from which you arose. Dissolve into that beauty. This is your original face before your parents were born.

How to Practice

  1. Create sacred space: Sit in a quiet, dimly lit room. Light a candle if it helps establish reverence. This technique requires deep inward attention — eliminate all possible distractions. Allow 30 minutes minimum.
  2. Settle deeply: Close your eyes. Breathe slowly and deeply for 5 minutes. Let the body become very still. Let the mind slow down. You need to reach a contemplative depth before this inquiry will work — surface mind cannot access these layers.
  3. Remember your earliest desire: Go back in memory. What is the earliest desire you can remember? Wanting a toy? Wanting mother’s attention? Feel that desire. Now ask: BEFORE that desire existed, what were you? You existed before the desire arose — something was there that COULD desire. What was it?
  4. Go before knowledge: What is the earliest thing you “knew”? Your name? Language? The feel of warmth? Go before ALL of that. Before you knew anything at all — before language, before concepts, before any framework of understanding existed in you — WHAT WERE YOU?
  5. Feel, don’t think: The mind will try to answer: “I was a baby,” “I was consciousness,” “I was nothing.” Reject ALL answers. This is not a question that CAN be answered by the mind. It is a question designed to short-circuit the mind. Let the question dissolve all answers.
  6. The dissolving: As you feel into this impossible question — what was I before all desire and all knowing? — something begins to dissolve. The constructed self (which is MADE of desires and knowledge) begins to melt. What’s left is not nothing — it’s a vast, beautiful, empty awareness. The sutra says: “dissolve in the beauty of THAT.”
  7. Stay with the beauty: This void is not cold or frightening. The sutra specifically calls it BEAUTIFUL. When you touch it, there is a quality of pristine, innocent beauty — like fresh snow, like a clear dawn, like a newborn’s eyes. Let that beauty hold you. Dissolve INTO it rather than observing it from outside.
  8. The original face: What you are touching is what Zen calls your “original face” — what you were before conditioning, before desire, before knowledge, before identity was constructed. It is not a thing — it is the void that holds all things. And it is YOURS. It is what you have always been, underneath everything else.
  9. Return slowly: When the meditation ends naturally (or after 30 minutes), let desires and knowledge return gradually. Notice them arriving like guests returning to an empty house. The house is not the guests — you are the house. Empty, spacious, welcoming, but not defined by what it contains.
  10. Carry the question: Throughout the day, occasionally ask: “Before this desire, what am I?” Each time, the void flashes for an instant. These micro-glimpses accumulate until the void becomes your constant background.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Advanced
Duration
30 min
Posture
Seated, still, eyes closed
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Philosophical minds; those comfortable with existential inquiry; people ready for deep identity work

Signs It’s Working

  • The question creates a momentary “gap” in the mind — a blank, alive silence
  • A feeling of vertigo or groundlessness that is simultaneously beautiful
  • Desires feel less personal, less urgent — more like weather passing through
  • A sense of vast, innocent beauty that has no cause or object
  • The constructed self feels transparent — real but not solid
  • Moments of childlike wonder return to daily life

Common Pitfalls

  • Answering the question intellectually (“I was pure consciousness”) — every answer is wrong; the question must remain unanswered
  • Making it a memory exercise — you are not trying to remember being a baby; you are investigating the PRIOR state
  • Fear of the void — the ego may panic when its foundations dissolve; breathe through it, the void itself is safe
  • Treating it casually — this technique requires deep contemplative absorption; surface attention won’t reach it
  • Confusing philosophical understanding with experiential dissolution — THINKING about emptiness is not dissolving into it

Questions From The Book

Why does the sutra say “dissolve in the beauty” rather than just “dissolve”?

Chapter 59, "Before Desire"

Osho explains: the word “beauty” is essential because it prevents the common misunderstanding that the void is ugly, frightening, or nihilistic. When you actually experience the state before desire and knowledge, it is not cold emptiness — it is the most beautiful thing you have ever encountered. It is pristine, innocent, fresh, alive. The sutra uses “beauty” to reassure the seeker: what you will find is not death but the most alive, most beautiful state possible. It is the beauty of absolute purity.

What is the connection to the Zen koan “original face”?

Chapter 59

Osho notes this sutra is essentially the same teaching as the famous Zen koan: “What was your original face before your parents were born?” Both point to the same reality — the unconditioned awareness prior to all identity. But where Zen delivers this as a paradox to break the mind, the tantra delivers it gently: “Consider. Dissolve in the beauty.” Same destination, different approaches — Zen is the lightning method, tantra is the river method.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Identity is constructed from desires (motivation) and knowledge (cognition). If you remove both, the constructed self dissolves. What remains is what developmental psychologists call the “core self” — the pre-verbal, pre-conceptual sense of being that exists before language and socialization shape it into a personality. This technique is a direct route to that primordial ground.

Developmental parallel: Research on infant consciousness suggests that before the “narrative self” develops (around age 2-3), infants experience what some researchers describe as “pure presence” — awareness without story, identity, or temporal continuity. This technique asks the adult to touch that same quality of raw, storyless presence.

One-line essenceBefore your first desire, before your first knowing — you were already here. That pristine emptiness before the story began is still here, underneath everything. Dissolve into its beauty.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 57 · Sutras 84–85 · 2 techniques

You Are Everywhere

Osho's framing

Osho opens with a folk story: an old doctor, told of a patient choking on a billiard ball, says "tickle him with a feather" - the patient laughs, the ball flies out. The doctor's rule of life is "when you don't know what to do, do something." That rule, Osho says, is fatal for meditation. The mind is the most delicate mechanism in existence; mixing techniques or modifying them is dangerous, and a wrong move is hard to undo. These methods are revolutionary - shortcuts that compress millions of evolutionary years into a single jump. The first sutra targets body-attachment. You've been embodied for so many lives that you only really know yourself as a body; anything else is borrowed reading.

Non-doing will be more beneficial to you than any doing.

— Osho
Sutra 84 of 112
Category: Movement · Tags: bodyless · everywhere · joy · disidentification · expansion
Toss attachment for body aside, realizing I am everywhere. One who is everywhere is joyous.

In Plain English

Deliberately relax the grip on “I am this body.” Feel: I am not contained in this body. I am the awareness that USES this body but is not limited to it. Feel yourself as the space the body moves in, not as the body itself. One who is everywhere has nothing to gain, nothing to lose, nothing to protect. That is why joy arises — joy is the natural state when there is no fear, and fear only exists for something that can be harmed. Awareness cannot be harmed.

How to Practice

  1. Find a quiet, private place: This is an eyes-closed practice. Sit or lie comfortably. You need 25 minutes of undisturbed time. The body should be relaxed, comfortable, and forgotten as much as possible — no tight clothing, no uncomfortable posture.
  2. Scan and release the body: Spend 5 minutes doing a body scan from head to toes. With each area, deliberately RELEASE your identification with it. Not “I am relaxing my arm” but “this arm is not me.” Feel each body part becoming “not-self” — present, but not who you are.
  3. Feel the space around the body: Now shift attention from the body to the SPACE the body occupies. Feel the air touching your skin. Feel the room around you. Let your sense of “I” move from being inside the skin to being the space surrounding the skin.
  4. Expand the sense of “I”: Slowly, feel yourself as bigger than the body. First, expand to fill the room. Then the building. Then the neighbourhood. Don’t THINK it — FEEL it. Feel yourself as vast, spacious, unbounded. The body becomes a tiny point within you — you are no longer within the body.
  5. “Toss attachment aside”: The sutra says “toss” — not gently release but actively throw away. There is a decisiveness here. It’s like removing a costume: just take it off and toss it. The body is a costume awareness is wearing. Toss the attachment to it. Not the body itself — the ATTACHMENT.
  6. Realise: I am everywhere: When the body-identification loosens sufficiently, a realisation dawns: if I am not this body, then where am I? Everywhere. Awareness has no location. It is the space in which everything appears. YOU are that space. Feel it: I am everywhere. Not metaphorically — literally. Awareness is here, there, everywhere simultaneously.
  7. Notice the joy: As the sense of being everywhere stabilises, notice: joy arises by itself. Not happiness about something — causeless joy. This is because fear has dissolved. Fear requires a thing that can be harmed. If you are everywhere, you cannot be harmed — you are too vast, too all-pervading. Without fear, the natural state is joy.
  8. Rest in the joy: Don’t try to intensify the joy or hold onto it. Just notice it and let it be. It is your natural state — what you feel when the artificial contraction of body-identification relaxes. Stay here for 5–10 minutes.
  9. Return with awareness: Slowly, bring attention back to the body. Feel the breath. Feel the weight. Open your eyes. But carry the knowing: the body is IN you — you are not in the body. This knowing can remain even as you return to normal activity.
  10. Daily integration: Throughout the day, whenever you feel fear or contraction, ask: “Am I really just this body?” The question alone loosens the grip. Even a moment of remembering “I am everywhere” restores spaciousness and dissolves the contraction of fear.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
25 min
Posture
Seated or lying down
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Those over-identified with the body; people with body-image issues; anyone experiencing fear or anxiety; those who feel small or limited

Signs It’s Working

  • A felt sense of being larger than the body — spacious, vast, unconstrained
  • Causeless joy that arises when body-identification relaxes
  • Fear diminishes significantly — you feel unassailable
  • The body feels lighter, more transparent, less “solid”
  • A sense of freedom that does not depend on circumstances
  • Over time: less concern with aging, appearance, physical limitations — not from denial but from knowing you are more than the body

Common Pitfalls

  • Dissociating from the body (numbness, disconnection) rather than expanding beyond it — expansion feels spacious and joyful; dissociation feels flat and empty
  • Neglecting the body as a result of this practice — the point is not to reject the body but to stop being IMPRISONED by it
  • Making it intellectual: “I understand I am not the body” is not the same as FEELING yourself as everywhere
  • Forcing the expansion — it happens naturally when attachment is released; forcing creates tension, not spaciousness
  • Confusing temporary bliss experiences with stable realisation — keep practising even after beautiful experiences

Questions From The Book

Why does Osho connect bodylessness with joy?

Chapter 68, "Everywhere is Joyous"

Osho explains: all suffering requires a centre that can be hurt. All fear requires something that can be destroyed. The body provides that centre and that something. When you realise you are NOT the body — when you experience yourself as the awareness in which the body appears — what can be hurt? What can die? Nothing. And when nothing can be lost, what remains? Joy. Joy is not something you achieve — it is what remains when fear is removed. It is your natural state, obscured only by the false identification with a vulnerable body.

Is this not escapism from the body?

Chapter 68

Osho is clear: this is not escape. Escape would be denying the body, hating it, ignoring its needs. This is INCLUSION — you include the body within a larger identity. The body remains, is cared for, is used — but it is no longer a prison. It becomes a vehicle, an instrument, a temporary home. The difference is like the difference between being a prisoner in a house and being the owner who can leave whenever they choose. The house remains the same — the relationship changes utterly.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Modern psychology recognises that over-identification with the body is a source of immense suffering — body dysmorphia, aging anxiety, health anxiety, vanity, physical insecurity. This technique addresses the ROOT cause: not the body’s imperfections but the misidentification of “I” with the body. When the “I” expands beyond the body, all body-related suffering loses its foundation.

Research parallel: Studies on out-of-body experiences and the rubber hand illusion demonstrate that body ownership is a construct of the brain, not a fixed reality. The brain’s body schema can be expanded, contracted, or dissolved entirely under certain conditions. This technique deliberately triggers that expansion, revealing that “I am this body” is a neural construct, not an absolute truth.

One-line essenceToss the body-costume aside. Discover you are the space it moves in. And in that spaciousness, find the joy that fear had been hiding.
Sutra 85 of 112
Category: Self-Inquiry · Tags: nothing · void · emptiness · negation · dissolution
Thinking no thing will limited-self unlimit.

In Plain English

This is a paradoxical instruction — closer to a Zen koan than a meditation. How can you “think” no thing? The very effort breaks the mind. Try to think no-thing and thinking exhausts itself trying. When thought has no object, when nothing remains for thought to hold, the “limited self” (which is built out of thoughts and the things they cling to) loses its boundaries and becomes unlimited. Osho stresses this is not blanking the mind by force, nor concentrating on the word “nothing.” It is using the impossibility of the instruction to dissolve the very act of thinking.

How to Practice

  1. Sit in total stillness: Find a position you can hold without moving for 30 minutes. Eyes closed. Spine erect. Let the body become so still that you forget it. Five minutes of settling.
  2. Notice that thoughts are things: A thought arises — it is a THING. It has shape, duration, content. Recognise it as a thing. Don’t fight it, don’t suppress it — simply see that it is a thing. Then let it go. It dissolves on its own when you stop feeding it attention.
  3. Notice that sensations are things: An itch, a pressure, warmth, tingling — these are all things. Recognise each one as a thing. You are not these things. Let them be without identifying with them.
  4. Notice that images are things: A mental picture, a memory, a fantasy — these are things. They arise in you but they are not you. Let each one pass like a cloud through empty sky.
  5. Notice that emotions are things: Boredom, restlessness, peace, bliss — even pleasant states are things. Don’t cling to pleasant things or push away unpleasant ones. They are all things. Let them all go.
  6. Notice that blankness is a thing: This is the crucial trap. The mind may produce a state of blankness and say “This is it! This is no-thingness!” But blankness has a quality, a texture, a dullness — it too is a thing. Let it go.
  7. Rest as the space: When all things have been released, what remains? Not nothing — YOU remain. But not “you” as a thing. You as the SPACE in which things appear. This space is alive, aware, present — but it has no form, no boundary, no content. It is no-thingness.
  8. Don’t grab the experience: The moment you think “I’ve got it!” that thought is a thing and you’ve lost it. No-thingness cannot be possessed because the possessor is also a thing. Simply rest. Don’t claim. Don’t name.
  9. FEEL rather than think: Osho emphasises — this technique fails completely if you stay intellectual. The mind cannot understand no-thingness because the mind IS things. You must feel your way into the space between thoughts, and then let even the feeler dissolve.
  10. Practice for 30 minutes daily: This technique may take months or years to bear fruit. That is normal for something this advanced. Even brief glimpses of no-thingness — even half a second — are transformative. Don’t measure progress.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Very Advanced
Duration
30 min
Posture
Seated (absolute stillness)
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Experienced meditators who have exhausted object-based techniques; those drawn to Zen, Advaita, or Dzogchen

Signs It’s Working

  • Moments — even brief — of gap between thoughts where you are present but there is no content
  • A sense of spaciousness that is alive and aware, not dull or blank
  • Thoughts arising and dissolving without any sense of “my” thought — they just happen in the space
  • Deep peace that has no cause — not peace because something happened, but causeless peace
  • The distinction between observer and observed becoming thinner and eventually collapsing
  • Over months: a fundamental shift in identity from “I am this person” to “I am the space in which this person arises”

Common Pitfalls

  • Making “nothing” into a thing and then trying to think about it — you cannot think your way to no-thingness
  • Confusing blankness or dullness with no-thingness — no-thingness is vibrantly alive
  • Suppressing thoughts through willpower — that creates tension, not space
  • Getting frustrated by the constant stream of things — frustration is also just a thing; let it go too
  • Expecting results on a timeline — this technique works outside of time; let go of all measurement

Questions From The Book

How is this different from sleep or unconsciousness?

Chapter 57 — You Are Everywhere

Osho explains: in sleep, awareness is absent. In the state this technique points to, awareness is fully present but there is no object for thought to hold. Sleep is unconscious; this technique is conscious. The difference is total. In sleep you disappear. Here, things disappear but YOU remain — and the “limited self,” which was constructed out of thoughts and the things they cling to, becomes unlimited. This is the highest state possible: pure awareness without any content.

If I let go of everything, won’t I go mad?

Chapter 64

Osho says: madness comes from too many things, not from no things. The mad person is drowning in objects — thoughts they cannot stop, emotions they cannot control. No-thingness is the opposite of madness — it is supreme sanity. When all clutter is removed, what remains is pristine clarity. You won’t go mad; you’ll become sane for the first time.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This is pure awareness meditation — what Tibetans call rigpa, what Zen calls shikantaza (just sitting), what Advaita calls the natural state. It is the recognition that you are not the contents of experience but the space in which experience arises. Every wisdom tradition converges on this recognition as the pinnacle of practice.

Cognitive science parallel: Research on “content-free awareness” (experienced meditators reporting awareness without any identifiable content) suggests this state is neurologically distinct from both waking cognition and sleep. It correlates with global decreases in cortical signal complexity while maintaining consciousness — a paradox that fascinates researchers.

One-line essenceLet go of every thing that arises until only the space remains. That space — alive, aware, borderless — is what you are before you became a thing.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 59 · Sutras 86–87 · 2 techniques

Watch from the Hill

Osho's framing

Osho describes man as Janus-faced: animal in his past, divine in his potential future, living as a tense step between the two. Whatever side you satisfy, the other side rebels. He illustrates with a joke - a Jaguar enthusiast finds heaven has beautiful highways but allows no cars, while hell has cars but no highways. That, says Osho, is our predicament exactly. Religions take sides - they fight the animal for the divine - and so create the very split they pretend to heal. Tantra refuses both sides and only watches. By staying in the present, neither shamed by the past nor reaching for the future, the duality dissolves into a third position.

Tantra is in the present. It is neither past nor future.

— Osho
Sutra 86 of 112
Category: Imagination · Tags: suppose · contemplate · something · beyond
Suppose you contemplate something beyond perception, beyond grasping, beyond not being. – you.

In Plain English

Try to imagine what cannot be imagined — something that cannot be perceived, cannot be grasped, and yet is not nothing. The mind cannot do this; that is the point. Every image it offers, you discard. When the mind has been emptied of all its images and the inner screen is bare, the seer suddenly happens to itself. Shiva ends the sutra with one word — “you.” That is the answer to the impossible contemplation.

How to Practice

  1. Sit comfortably, eyes closed.
  2. Begin to contemplate something that cannot be perceived. Try to imagine it. The mind will immediately offer pictures — light, space, a void, a blue, a sphere, a god, a face. Watch them rise.
  3. Discard each one. Osho gives the formula: neti, neti — not this, not this. Whatever the mind shows you can be perceived; therefore, that is not it. Drop it.
  4. The next image will appear immediately. Drop that too. And the next, and the next. Be ready: many will come. New combinations will be invented — symbols, colours, sensations. None of them are the thing the sutra is asking for. All can still be perceived. Drop, drop, drop.
  5. Don’t fight the mind. Just keep saying inwardly “not this” and let each image fall. Osho calls this the “throwing the furniture out” phase. The room has to be empty.
  6. Don’t expect to succeed at the contemplation. The contemplation is impossible by design. The point is not to find the unimaginable; the point is the cleaning that the trying produces.
  7. Persist. The mind’s supply of images is large but finite for one sitting. Eventually the screen begins to thin out. The gaps between images grow longer.
  8. One moment the screen is empty. There is no object, no symbol, no dream, no picture — only bare attention with nothing in front of it.
  9. In that moment, Osho says, “the whole attention changes.” With nothing left to perceive, awareness reflects back upon itself. You become aware of the perceiver. The sutra closes with: “… — you.”
  10. Don’t try to grab this. The instant you treat the seer as another object, it has slipped away. Just remain in the empty awareness; let the recognition happen by itself.
  11. Don’t mistake the empty screen for nothing. Osho warns specifically: the mind will say “this is nothing, this doesn’t exist.” Don’t believe it. The sutra calls this “beyond not-being” — emphatically existing, just not perceivable.
  12. Repeat daily. The technique is arduous because the habit of clinging to objects is deep. Osho compares it to Svetketu being sent to live with cows for years until verbal mind dropped. Don’t expect quick results; expect a long, patient subtraction.
  13. End each sitting without judgement. Whether the screen emptied today or not, the practice was the discarding. That itself is the work.

Osho’s Key Insight

Commentary from 59. Watch from the Hill in The Book of Secrets.

Source: 59. Watch from the Hill, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 87 of 112
Category: Self-Inquiry · Tags: existing · mine · beloved · even
I am existing. This is mine. This is this. Oh beloved, even in such know illimitably.

In Plain English

The most ordinary feelings — “I am,” “this is mine,” “this is this” — are usually thrown away as too obvious. Shiva says: even here, in this most basic feeling, the limitless can be known. Don’t repeat the words like a mantra. Sit and feel the sheer fact “I am” — not as a thought in the head, but as a felt presence in the whole body. The plainness is the door.

How to Practice

  1. Sit comfortably. Eyes closed.
  2. Don’t make a mantra of this. Osho is firm: do not chant “I am, I am, I am” to yourself. Repetition produces sleep, not awareness. Auto-hypnosis is restful but it is not the technique.
  3. Instead, drop the words and feel the fact. Right now, you are. The sheer is-ness of being here is the object of attention.
  4. Move the feeling out of the head and into the body. Notice the pressure of the seat, the touch of clothes, the temperature of the air, the slight rustle of breath, the hum of the heart. All of these say one thing: I am.
  5. Notice what Osho calls the body’s subtle trembling — the constant fine vibration of being alive. The body is never static; it is dynamism. Feel that vibration, and feel it as “I am.”
  6. Stay with the felt “I am.” Don’t answer the question “what am I?” — the technique is one step before that. Just the bare fact: am.
  7. Now move to the second phrase the sutra gives: “This is mine.” Look around inwardly. Notice anything that genuinely feels like “mine” — this body, this breath, this room, this moment. Don’t list possessions; just feel the “mine” quality.
  8. Then the third: “This is this.” Whatever is presenting itself right now — the pressure on the back, the sound outside, the dull mood, the bright energy — let it simply be what it is. No name, no story, just this, as it is.
  9. Now Shiva’s real instruction: “Even in such, know illimitably.” In this very ordinariness — not a different state — recognise that the one feeling all this is limitless. Don’t go searching for a separate cosmic experience; the limitless is the very awareness inside the “I am.”
  10. Sit with this longer than you think you should. The mind will say “nothing is happening.” Osho says that monk-on-the-hill sitting is exactly the technique: not waiting for anyone, not looking for anything, not even praying. Just being here. If you can do that, the technique is doing itself.
  11. If you find yourself slipping into chanting or thinking, just drop it and return to the felt presence.
  12. End the session without conclusion. Open the eyes slowly. Carry through the day a soft remembrance of the bare “I am” — while walking, eating, working, the same fact continues underneath everything. That continuity is what the sutra calls knowing illimitably.

Osho’s Key Insight

I will tell you one Zen anecdote. Three friends were walking along a road. Evening was just falling and the sun was setting when they become aware of a monk standing on a nearby hill. They started talking about the monk, wondering what he was doing there. One of them said, “He must be waiting for his friends. He must have gone for a walk from his hermitage and his friends are left behind, so he...

Source: 59. Watch from the Hill, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 61 · Sutras 88–89 · 2 techniques

Techniques to Become One with the Whole

Osho's framing

Osho opens with Lord Mancroft cutting a speech short because his house is literally on fire. Then the punchline: your house too is on fire, but you don't seem the least bit flustered. Death is approaching every moment, life is shrinking, but the mind whispers there's plenty of time. He tells the devil's parable to drive the point home: in a hellish recession, the most cunning disciple proposed not to deny religion or hell but only to convince humans that "there is no hurry" - and from that day hell has had an overpopulation problem. Without crisis-level urgency, no technique can work. Understanding must become action; mere acquaintance is useless.

Your house is also on fire, but you don't even seem a little flustered.

— Osho
Sutra 88 of 112
Category: Witnessing · Tags: knower · known · unity · consciousness · non-duality
Each thing is perceived through knowing. The self shines in space through knowing. Perceive one being as knower and known.

In Plain English

Normally we divide experience into three: the knower (subject), the known (object), and the knowing (process). This sutra says: dissolve that trinity into one. The tree you see — is it separate from your seeing? Can you find where “seeing” ends and “tree” begins? Can you find where “you” end and “seeing” begins? In meditation: discover that knower, knowing, and known are one undivided event. The self shines not behind knowing but AS knowing. There is no separate knower — only knowing happening.

How to Practice

  1. Start with an object: Place a flower, a candle, or any simple object before you. Look at it. Normally you experience: “I” (the knower) am looking at “the flower” (the known) through “seeing” (the knowing). Three things. This technique dissolves them into one.
  2. Search for the boundary: Look at the flower. Now ask: where exactly does “seeing” end and “flower” begin? You experience colour, shape, beauty — but is the colour in the flower or in your seeing? Can you separate the two? Try. You will find: you cannot. The flower-as-experienced is inseparable from the seeing of it.
  3. Search for the knower: Now turn attention inward. Where is the “I” that sees? Can you find a separate entity behind the seeing? Look for the seer. All you find is: more seeing, more knowing. There is no little person BEHIND the experience watching it. There is just experiencing, seamlessly unfolding.
  4. The trinity collapses: When you cannot find a boundary between seer and seen, and you cannot find a separate seer behind the seeing, the three collapse into one: knowing. Just knowing. Not “someone knowing something” but knowing happening. One event, not three. This is non-duality.
  5. Apply to sound: Close your eyes. Listen to any sound. Ask: is the sound “out there” or “in here”? Where does hearing happen? In the ear? In the brain? In the sound itself? You cannot locate hearing in any single place because hearing is not a “thing” — it is an undivided event that includes sound, ear, brain, and awareness as one.
  6. Apply to thought: Watch a thought arise. Now ask: who is watching? Is the watcher different from the thought? Or is “watching the thought” itself another thought? And who watches THAT? Every attempt to find a separate knower leads to infinite regress. At some point you must give up the search and recognise: there is only knowing — luminous, self-aware knowing with no separate knower behind it.
  7. The self shines AS knowing: The sutra says “the self shines in space through knowing.” This means: your true self is not hiding behind experience, watching from a distance. Your self IS the knowing itself — the luminous awareness that constitutes every experience. You don’t HAVE awareness; you ARE awareness. Awareness doesn’t belong to you; you belong to it.
  8. Walking practice: Walk with open eyes. See the world. Feel: there is one seamless knowing happening — the visual field, the body sensations, the sounds — all one knowing, not divided into “me” and “world.” One flow of experiencing. Rest in this flow without trying to locate yourself within it.
  9. Formal sitting (20 min): Sit with eyes closed. Let experience flow: thoughts, sensations, sounds, silence. Notice: all of it is KNOWN. There is a knowing quality pervading everything. Now rest AS that knowing quality rather than as the one who “has” it. You are not experiencing knowing; you ARE knowing experiencing itself.
  10. The paradox: This technique leads to a beautiful paradox: the one you were searching for (the knower) was looking through your eyes the whole time. You cannot find it as an object because it is the subject of all seeking. You ARE what you seek. Recognise this, and the search ends in laughter.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Very Advanced
Duration
20 min (formal sitting)
Posture
Seated (stable base for deep inquiry)
Eyes
Open or closed (try both)
Best for
Intellectual seekers; those drawn to Advaita Vedanta or Zen; philosophers; meditators who have exhausted object-focused techniques; anyone who asks “Who am I?”

Signs It’s Working

  • Moments where the subject-object boundary dissolves — there is just seamless experiencing
  • The search for a separate “self” behind experience exhausts itself and collapses into laughter or peace
  • A recognition: “I am not IN awareness; I AM awareness”
  • Experiences become more vivid because the filter of “me experiencing this” drops away
  • The sense of distance between you and life dissolves — intimacy with everything replaces separation from everything
  • The question “Who am I?” stops feeling like a puzzle and starts feeling like a joke — because the asker and the answer are the same

Common Pitfalls

  • Intellectualising non-duality: “I understand that subject and object are one” — understanding is not enough; you must SEE it directly in experience
  • Creating a “super-witness” that watches everything — this is just another object, another identity. True non-duality has no position, no standpoint, no “one watching”
  • Getting lost in philosophical complexity — the technique is simple: look for the boundary between knower and known. You won’t find it. Rest in that not-finding.
  • Frustration that “I can’t find the knower” — that’s the POINT. The knower cannot be found because it IS the finding. The search failing IS the discovery.
  • Expecting a dramatic experience of “oneness” — non-duality is not a special state. It is the ordinary nature of every experience, recognised for the first time. It is subtle, not spectacular.

Questions From The Book

If knower and known are one, why does it seem like there are two?

Chapter 42, "Knower and Known"

Osho explains: the division into subject and object is the mind’s most fundamental operation — more basic than any other distinction. The mind cannot function without it. It needs a reference point (“I”) to orient all experience around. But this is a FUNCTIONAL division, not a real one — like the equator divides the globe into hemispheres without any actual line on the ground. The technique asks you to look for the line and discover it isn’t there. When the line is seen as imaginary, the two halves recognise themselves as one globe. Nothing changes except the illusion of division drops.

How does this relate to Ramana Maharshi’s “Who am I?”

Chapter 42

Osho notes that Ramana Maharshi’s self-inquiry is essentially the same technique approached from a different angle. Ramana asks: “Who am I?” — not expecting an answer but using the question to point attention back to its own source. This sutra does the same: “perceive one being as knower and known” — point attention to the knower and discover that knower and known are not two. Both techniques end in the same recognition: the self is not an object to be found but the knowing in which all objects appear.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This is the core insight of Advaita Vedanta, Dzogchen, and Zen — the recognition that awareness is not a “thing” possessed by a self but is itself the ground of all experience. In neurophenomenology, this is called “pre-reflective self-awareness” — awareness is always already self-knowing before any secondary reflection on it occurs. The “hard problem of consciousness” dissolves when you stop trying to find consciousness AS an object and recognise you ARE looking FROM it.

First-person research: This technique is essentially what Francisco Varela called “first-person methodology” — using attention to investigate the structure of experience itself. Modern contemplative science confirms: when trained practitioners investigate the boundary between observer and observed, they consistently report its dissolution — leading to states described as “nondual awareness,” characterised by reduced self-referential processing and increased reported clarity, peace, and connectedness.

One-line essenceYou cannot find the knower because you ARE the knower. Stop searching and rest as what you always already are — the luminous knowing in which all things shine.
Sutra 89 of 112
Category: Mind & Thought · Tags: beloved · moment · mind · knowing
Beloved, at this moment let, mind, knowing, breath, form, be included.

In Plain English

Most meditation says “I am not the body, not the breath, not the mind” and excludes everything to find the witness. This sutra goes the opposite way: include. Sitting still, refuse to divide yourself. Body, breath, thought, knowing — let everything in. Don’t set up a centre apart from them. The centre disappears, the ego cannot survive on inclusiveness, and what is left is consciousness like an open sky covering everything.

How to Practice

  1. Sit comfortably with eyes closed.
  2. Notice that your habit is to divide. The mind wants to say “I am the awareness, not the body,” or “I am the witness, not the thoughts.” For this technique, suspend the habit. Don’t exclude anything.
  3. Let the body in. Whatever sensations are there — pressure of the seat, warmth, slight aches, the soft weight of the limbs — include them in “I am.”
  4. Let the breath in. The in-breath is you. The out-breath is you. Don’t stand back and watch breath; be the breath as it comes and goes.
  5. Let thought in. Whatever thoughts are passing — planning, remembering, drifting — include them. Don’t fight them, don’t try to be the witness behind them. Just let thinking be one of the things that is happening as you.
  6. Let knowing in — the awareness itself. Even that is not a separate centre apart from breath and thought; include the knower along with the known.
  7. Don’t centre yourself anywhere. Osho is specific: “Don’t get yourself centred anywhere — be uncentred.” You are not in the head, not in the heart, not behind the eyes. Drop all locations.
  8. Notice the body’s form changing. Sometimes you will feel the body as enormous, filling the room; sometimes very small, atomic. As you include more, it grows; as you would-exclude, it shrinks. Don’t fix the size. Let it expand.
  9. Now extend the inclusion outward. Keeping the eyes closed, take in the room around you; let the room be inside you, not you inside the room. Then take in the building, the street, the sky. Whatever you can sense, take it inside.
  10. Try the daylight version too. Sit under a tree, look at it, then close your eyes and feel the tree is within you. Look at the rising sun, then close the eyes and feel the sun is rising within you. Osho says this is not pure imagination — the tree and you grow from the same earth, and the inclusion is recovering an actual fact.
  11. Include the difficult things last. A friend, an annoying neighbour, an enemy. Jesus’s instruction to love the enemy as yourself, Osho says, is exactly this technique — not for ethics but for expansion. If even the enemy is included, nothing is left outside; ego cannot survive that.
  12. Stay until you feel the centre dissolve. Don’t worry if it doesn’t happen on day one. The training of inclusiveness is gradual. End by gently opening the eyes and carrying a small inclusive flavour into the next activity — nothing is shut out.

Osho’s Key Insight

Commentary from 61. Techniques to Become One with the Whole in The Book of Secrets.

Source: 61. Techniques to Become One with the Whole, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 63 · Sutras 90–91 · 2 techniques

Start Creating Yourself

Osho's framing

Osho opens with a joke - a stranger at the back of a dull church is mistaken for a board member; he replies he must be the most bored man there. From the joke he turns serious: boredom is uniquely human. Animals feel pain but not anguish; for them suffering is momentary, an accident on the periphery, never the centre. Only humans carry pain forward into a constant wound, and only humans commit suicide. Aristotle called man the rational animal, but rationality is only a matter of degree - boredom is the cleaner definition. Hope is what stops most people from suicide; he tells the prime-minister-and-flying-horse story to show how a thin "maybe" keeps people dragging on. These techniques aim past hope, at real celebration.

Animals can be in pain, but they are not in suffering.

— Osho
Sutra 90 of 112
Category: Senses · Tags: eyes · touch · feather · subtle · heart-opening
Touching eyeballs as a feather, lightness between them opens into heart and there permeates the cosmos.

In Plain English

Close your eyes. Place your palms very gently over your closed eyelids — as light as feathers. Don’t press at all. Just the softest possible contact. Feel the eyeballs receiving this gentleness. The eyes hold tremendous tension (from constant looking, focusing, judging). When touched with absolute gentleness, the eyes relax — and with them the entire nervous system softens. The heart opens because the eyes and heart are connected through the vagus nerve.

How to Practice

  1. Prepare your hands: Rub your palms together vigorously for 10–15 seconds until they feel warm and alive with energy. The friction generates both heat and a subtle electromagnetic charge. Your palms become healing instruments.
  2. Close your eyes: Sit comfortably or lie down. Close your eyes and let them settle for a moment. Don’t force them shut — let the eyelids drop naturally, like curtains falling.
  3. Place palms over eyes: Cup your warm palms gently over your closed eyes. The heels of the palms rest on the cheekbones. The fingers rest on the forehead. The cups of the palms hover over the eyeballs. Almost no pressure — just the lightest possible contact.
  4. The key instruction — FEATHER touch: Osho’s specific word is “feathers.” Imagine your palms ARE feathers. The lightest substance. Barely there. If you are pressing even slightly, you are pressing too much. Less and less pressure until it is almost nothing — just the warmth and the presence of the hands.
  5. Feel the eyes receiving: Notice what happens to your eyeballs under this gentleness. They begin to relax. The muscles behind the eyes (which are constantly tense from focusing) begin to soften. The eyes may feel like they are floating, sinking backward into the skull, becoming liquid.
  6. Notice the subtlety within: As the eyes relax, subtler perceptions arise. You may see colours (violet, gold, blue). You may feel a tingling between the eyebrows. You may feel the eyes “opening inward” rather than outward. This is the “subtlety within” the sutra mentions.
  7. The heart connection: After 3–5 minutes of palming, bring your attention to the heart. You will notice: the heart has softened too. There may be warmth in the chest, a sense of openness or tenderness. This is not imagination — the vagus nerve physically connects the eyes and heart. When the eyes relax, the signal travels directly to the heart.
  8. Stay for 5–10 minutes: Keep the palms over the eyes for the full duration. The relaxation deepens progressively. What begins as eye-relaxation becomes whole-body softening. The nervous system shifts from sympathetic (fight-flight) to parasympathetic (rest-digest-love).
  9. Remove hands slowly: When ready, remove your palms very slowly. Keep eyes closed for another minute. Notice how different the inner space feels — softer, darker, more spacious. When you finally open your eyes, the world may look clearer, more vivid, as if a film has been cleaned away.
  10. Practice before sleep: This technique is particularly powerful at bedtime. The eye-relaxation cascades into whole-body relaxation, and sleep comes quickly and deeply. Many practitioners report this as the single most effective technique for insomnia.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Very Easy
Duration
5–10 min
Posture
Seated or lying
Eyes
Closed (palms covering)
Best for
Everyone; especially those with eye strain; computer workers; anxious people; insomniacs; those with heart-closure or difficulty being vulnerable

Signs It’s Working

  • Eyes feel like they are “floating” or “melting” under the gentle touch
  • Colours appear in the visual field (deep purple, gold, or blue) — signs of optic nerve relaxation
  • The heart area warms and opens — tenderness arises without a specific cause
  • The whole body relaxes progressively after the eyes let go
  • Vision is clearer and brighter when you open your eyes afterward
  • Sleep comes easily if practised at bedtime

Common Pitfalls

  • Pressing too hard — the key is FEATHER-light; any pressure activates the oculocardiac reflex (which slows the heart too much)
  • Letting the mind stay active while the eyes rest — let the mental activity wind down with the eyes
  • Doing it for only 30 seconds and expecting results — give it 5 full minutes minimum
  • Having cold hands — always warm the palms first; cold touch creates tension rather than relaxation
  • Treating it as mechanical rather than tender — approach your own eyes with love, as you would touch a child’s face

Questions From The Book

Why does Osho say the eyes are the key to the heart?

Chapter 56, "Eyes as Feathers"

Osho explains: the eyes are the most tense part of the body because they carry the burden of constant judgment, comparison, and desire. We look at things with hunger, with fear, with analysis — the eyes never rest. All this tension is transmitted through neural pathways directly to the heart (via the vagus nerve). A person with tense, hard eyes inevitably has a tense, closed heart. Relax the eyes deeply, and the heart has no choice but to open — the tension that was keeping it closed dissolves at its source.

Can this technique help with emotional numbness?

Chapter 56

Osho says yes — emotional numbness is often a defence that originates in the eyes. We learn to “not see” painful things by hardening the eyes. But hardened eyes also cannot see beauty, love, or tenderness. When the eye-tension melts through this gentle palming technique, emotions that were frozen begin to thaw. Tears may come — let them. They are the ice melting. Behind the numbness, the heart was always feeling — it was just locked away.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This is a vagal toning technique disguised as a meditation.
