The vagus nerve runs from the brainstem through the face (including the muscles around the eyes) to the heart, lungs, and gut. Gentle pressure on the eyeballs stimulates the oculocardiac reflex, which activates parasympathetic activity — slowing the heart, deepening the breath, and producing feelings of safety and openness.

Polyvagal Theory connection: Stephen Porges’s polyvagal theory explains that the ventral vagal complex (face-heart connection) mediates feelings of social safety and love. By relaxing the eye muscles (part of the “social engagement system”), this technique directly activates the neurological substrate of love and connection. The ancient sutra knew what modern neuroscience only recently confirmed.

One-line essenceYour eyes hold the tension of a lifetime of looking. Touch them as feathers, and the heart that was locked behind them swings open.
Sutra 91 of 112
Category: Imagination · Tags: kind · devi · etheric · presence
Kind Devi, enter etheric presence pervading far above and below your form.

In Plain English

Around the physical body there is a second, finer body — the etheric or electric body — visible as a bluish light, soft and soothing. Sit until the physical body feels weightless; then become aware of the bluish presence pervading above the form, below the form, all around it. Stay with it. The etheric body is the easiest of the inner bodies to enter; it is also the reservoir behind your “mood,” your aliveness, your healing energy.

How to Practice

  1. This technique is the second of a pair (the first is sutra #90, “touching eyeballs as a feather”). Osho says it can be done alone, but it is much easier after the first has produced a feeling of weightlessness. So begin by sitting until the body feels light — either by quiet sitting, or by slow deep breath, or by the eyeball-feather technique.
  2. Sit in a relaxed, upright posture. Eyes closed. The room should be quiet. For the visual confirmation step later, the room can also be made quite dark.
  3. Wait for the weightless quality. With closed eyes, you may begin to feel the body has no weight, that gravity has loosened its hold — even that you have risen slightly. Don’t worry whether you have actually risen. Osho is clear: feeling it with closed eyes is enough; the physical body may still be on the ground.
  4. Once the weightlessness is there, with eyes still closed feel the form of your body in detail — the toes, the legs, the trunk, the arms, the head. Visualise the outline as it is, sitting just as you are sitting.
  5. Now look just outside the outline. Around the form there is a bluish light. Don’t strain to see it; relax and let it appear. At first it will be hazy, an impression more than an image.
  6. The sutra is precise about its extent: “far above and below your form.” The bluish presence is not skin-tight; it pervades a distance above your head and below your feet, and on every side. Feel it as a loose robe of soft blue light surrounding you.
  7. Don’t analyse. Just look at it inwardly. Osho’s instruction is simply to look without doing — “not doing anything, just looking at the bluish form around you.” The look itself is the practice.
  8. Notice that thinking stops on its own. The etheric body needs no thought. Blue light is naturally calming — the sky, the deep sea, the body’s own subtler form. Let the calm spread.
  9. As you continue, the bluish presence expands. Don’t force it — just looking is what makes it grow, because when you are not doing, energy flows back into the etheric instead of leaking outward into action.
  10. For the optional visual step: do this practice at night in a dark room, no light at all. After some sittings of closed-eye practice, open the eyes briefly. Many people see the bluish light around the body directly, with open eyes, in the dark. Don’t make this the goal; it is only a confirmation.
  11. Don’t aim for levitation, healing powers, aura-reading, or anything else. Osho is direct: those are not the point of this technique and chasing them spoils it. The point is entering the etheric presence; everything else is a side phenomenon.
  12. End the sitting gently. Stay still for a minute with eyes open. The etheric reservoir you have been filling will be felt for hours afterwards as a quiet, refreshed quality — as if you have been “feasted” from inside.

Osho’s Key Insight

Whenever this happens – that you feel light, levitating, as if you can fly – suddenly you will become aware that around your body form there is a bluish light. But that you can see only when you feel that you can levitate, that your body can fly, that it has become light, completely free of any burden, completely free of any gravitation towards the earth.

Source: 63. Start Creating Yourself, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 65 · Sutras 92–93 · 2 techniques

Destroy the Limits

Osho's framing

Osho draws a sharp distinction: life is not a problem, it is a mystery. Science treats life as a problem and applies the mind to it; religion treats it as a mystery and asks for your whole self. A problem can be solved; a mystery can only be lived. That is why a brilliant scientist can be a fool in his own life - the mind sharpens within his speciality but cannot meet the wider mystery, which requires the whole. Religion has no answers, only doorways. These two short sutras - placing mind-stuff in fineness above, below and in the heart, and feeling some part of your form as limitlessly spacious - open one such doorway.

Life is not a problem but a mystery.

— Osho
Sutra 92 of 112
Category: Imagination · Tags: mind · stuff · inexpressible · fineness
Put mind-stuff in such inexpressible fineness above, below and in your heart.

In Plain English

The Sanskrit word here is chitta — not the busy thinking mind, but the very background on which thoughts float, like the sky on which clouds move. Refine the chitta until it has no thoughts in it at all — until it is so subtle, so empty of content, that nothing can be said about it. Carry that finest, thoughtless awareness above the heart, below the heart, into the heart itself. When pure consciousness reaches the heart, the centre of you shifts back to where it was in childhood, and the world looks completely different.

How to Practice

  1. Begin by understanding the difference between thoughts and mind-stuff. Thoughts are the clouds. Mind-stuff is the sky on which they pass. Today you live in the head, where the sky is full of clouds. The technique is to clear the sky.
  2. Sit upright, eyes closed. Let the body settle.
  3. Don’t fight thoughts. Just stop feeding them. Whatever appears, let it pass without engaging. The cloud moves on if you don’t hold it.
  4. Aim for the precise balance Osho describes. Two states are easy to recognise: ordinary thinking, and dreamless deep sleep. Both miss this technique. You want a third: the thoughtlessness of deep sleep plus the alertness of waking. Patanjali’s samadhi, says Osho, is “like sushupti (deep sleep) with one difference — you are not asleep.”
  5. If the mind goes blank but you fall asleep, you have lost alertness. If you stay alert but thoughts crowd in, you have lost the thoughtlessness. Both must coexist. This is delicate; expect to drift one way or the other for a long time before the balance comes.
  6. When the balance arrives, awareness becomes “inexpressibly fine” — nothing about it can be said, because there is no thing in it. You are aware, but there is no content of awareness.
  7. Now place this fine awareness above the head. Don’t use words; just feel pure consciousness extending upward beyond the crown.
  8. Then below — below the feet, into the ground, into the depths beneath the body. Pure consciousness, no thoughts, extending downward.
  9. Then in the heart. Bring the fine awareness fully into the chest, into the heart-centre. Stay there. The heart cannot hold thoughts; if you arrived with thoughts, you would still be in the head. Only thoughtless consciousness can settle in the heart.
  10. The moment thoughtless awareness rests in the heart, the “centre” of you shifts. Don’t do anything to make this happen — it happens by itself. Osho: “You are thrown back. You are thrown to the heart.”
  11. Don’t fight your ego or try to dissolve it directly. This sutra never mentions ego. Osho is explicit: by simply moving from head to heart, the ego (which is a head-projection) disappears on its own. Fighting it gives it food.
  12. When the sitting ends, return to the head as needed for daily life — but you now know the passage. From this day, you can drop back to the heart any time you need. Each return makes the passage clearer.

Osho’s Key Insight

Commentary from 65. Destroy the Limits in The Book of Secrets.

Source: 65. Destroy the Limits, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 93 of 112
Category: Imagination · Tags: consider · area · present · form
Consider any area of your present form as limitlessly spacious.

In Plain English

Pick any region of the body — the head, the chest, the belly, a hand — and imagine it as having no boundary at all. Let it expand into infinite space. The mind cannot coexist with the boundless; the moment one part of the body becomes truly limitless in your imagination, thoughts collapse and you fall through the body into pure space.

How to Practice

  1. Sit comfortably with eyes closed.
  2. Pick one region of the body to start with. Osho suggests the head, but it can be any area — chest, belly, mouth, even a single hand. The choice does not matter; pick what feels alive today.
  3. First, feel the chosen region clearly — its actual shape, weight, the boundary of skin, the inside of it. Get a vivid sense of where it ends.
  4. Now begin to dissolve the boundary. The skin softens, fades, opens. The region expands beyond its usual edge — an inch, a foot, a metre. Don’t stop there. Keep expanding.
  5. Push past the room. Past the building. Past the city. The chosen area continues outward — through trees, sky, planets, stars — with no boundary appearing anywhere to stop it.
  6. Hold this. The instruction is “limitlessly spacious” — the moment you settle for a finite size, the mind has won. Keep refusing every limit. Imagination wants to give you a sphere or a horizon — gently dissolve each one.
  7. Watch what happens to thinking. The mind cannot match the unbounded. As the chosen area becomes truly infinite in your inner sense, thoughts wobble, slow, and stop. Osho is explicit: “The mind cannot coexist with the unlimited. So if you can try something limitless, the mind will disappear.”
  8. Don’t panic at the disappearance. The mind’s alarms (“you’ll go mad,” “come back”) are reflexes. They pass if you don’t obey them.
  9. Once one region is limitless, you may let other regions of the body do the same, one by one, until the whole body is infinite space. Or stay with the single region — both work.
  10. Don’t fix on a particular experience. Some sit with limitless head, some with limitless chest, some with limitless belly — the result is the same: thought stops, the bordered self disappears, what remains is space.
  11. When you sense the sitting ending, don’t suddenly snap the boundaries back. Let the limitless slowly recollect itself into the body, like the tide returning. Open the eyes only when the body feels “here” again.
  12. Repeat daily. Each session, the area can be felt as limitless faster, and the disappearance of mind becomes more reliable. With time, you carry a faint sense of the spaciousness even with eyes open.

Osho’s Key Insight

Life is not a problem but a mystery. For science life is a problem, but for religion it is a mystery. A problem can be solved, a mystery cannot be solved – it can be lived but it cannot be solved. Religion offers no solutions, no answers. Science offers answers; religion has none.

Source: 65. Destroy the Limits, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 67 · Sutras 94–95 · 2 techniques

Go Beyond Mind and Matter

Osho's framing

Osho frames these techniques with a long-running philosophical fight: is mind or matter the basic stuff of the universe? Materialists like Charvak, Epicurus and Marx pick matter; idealists like the Vedantins pick mind. Modern physics has dissolved both - Eddington said the universe looks more like a thought than a thing. Tantra has always taken a third position: matter and mind are two forms of an unnamed X. The practical payoff is that you can enter reality through either door - Hatha Yoga works through the body, Raja Yoga through the mind, and Tantra uses both. This is why Shiva's techniques sometimes ask you to do something physical and sometimes only to imagine. For Tantra, even imagination is real.

Even imagination is a mode of reality because the mind is a manifestation of reality.

— Osho
Sutra 94 of 112
Category: Sleep & Dream · Tags: dissolution · cosmic · sleep · letting-go
Editor’s note — sutra not verbatim in Osho. A line-by-line audit of The Book of Secrets could not locate this sutra in Osho’s commentary on the 112 techniques. The phrasing “made of cosmic essences and leave it to be consumed” appears to be a synthesis. The five-element dissolution practice below is a defensible Tibetan/yogic body-scan but should not be treated as Osho’s instruction for a specific Vigyan Bhairav sutra. The closest authentic Osho sutra in this register is “Suppose your passive form to be an empty room with walls of skin — empty” (Chapter 79, The Philosophy of Emptiness).
Feel your substance, bones, flesh, blood, saturated with the cosmic essence.

In Plain English

As you fall asleep, feel the body dissolving back into its elements — earth, water, fire, air, space. Let it be consumed by the cosmos. In that dissolving, consciousness remains while the body “dies” into sleep. The body returns to the universe each night; only awareness persists. This is a nightly rehearsal for the ultimate dissolution — death — done gently, naturally, beautifully.

How to Practice

  1. Lie in bed, ready for sleep: This is an in-bed, eyes-closed, falling-asleep technique. All preparation is done. Lights off. Body in corpse pose or your natural sleep position. This is the last thing you do before sleep takes you.
  2. Feel the body as elements: Begin by recognising what the body is made of. The EARTH element: bones, muscles, flesh — everything solid. The WATER element: blood, saliva, lymph — everything fluid. The FIRE element: body heat, digestion, metabolism — everything warm. The AIR element: breath, gases, the lightness in the lungs. The SPACE element: the hollowness within — the spaces inside chest, belly, skull.
  3. Begin with earth dissolving: Feel the solid parts of the body growing heavy, sinking into the mattress, merging with the earth below. The bones dissolve downward. The muscles melt. The flesh returns to earth. Feel the solidity leaving your body — you become less solid, more transparent. The earth element is being consumed by the cosmos.
  4. Water dissolving: Now feel the fluid element evaporating, being absorbed. The blood becomes thinner, lighter. Saliva dries. The body’s water returns to the cosmic ocean. You feel drier, lighter, less embodied. Another layer of physicality dissolves.
  5. Fire dissolving: The warmth begins to leave the body. Not cold — but the fire-element returns to the cosmic fire, the sun. The body’s heat radiates outward, returning to the universe. Digestion stops. Metabolism quiets. The inner flame dims to an ember, then returns to its source.
  6. Air dissolving: The breath becomes thinner, lighter, barely perceptible. The air in the lungs returns to the atmosphere. Breathing becomes so subtle it almost stops. The air element leaves — you are no longer breathing; the cosmos breathes through what remains of you.
  7. Space dissolving: Finally, even the space within the body dissolves into the infinite space of the cosmos. The boundary between inner space and outer space disappears. There is no longer a “body” with space inside it — there is just space. Infinite, unbounded, cosmic space. And awareness floating in it.
  8. What remains: When all five elements have been consumed — when the body has been completely returned to the cosmos — what remains? Only consciousness. Pure awareness without a body. This is what persists through sleep. This is what persists through death. This is what you ARE. Let this bodiless awareness enter sleep. Let it rest in the cosmic space it has always been part of.
  9. Fall asleep in dissolution: Most people will fall asleep during this process — often during the earth or water phase. This is perfect. The dissolution continues unconsciously, and the quality of sleep is profoundly different — deeper, more restful, more cosmic. You wake feeling like you dissolved and were reassembled fresh.
  10. Morning reassembly: Upon waking, notice: the elements reassemble. Space condenses into body-space. Air fills the lungs with a deep breath. Fire returns as warmth floods the body. Water returns as you feel fluids moving. Earth returns as solidity and weight. You are reconstructed from the cosmos each morning. Every day is a rebirth.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner-friendly
Duration
15–30 min (or until sleep comes)
Posture
Lying in bed, natural sleep position
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Everyone; especially those who fear death; nature lovers; people who enjoy visualisation; those who want a beautiful, poetic sleep practice

Signs It’s Working

  • The body genuinely feels like it is dissolving — not imagining but FEELING the boundaries soften
  • A profound sense of letting go and returning — like a river reaching the ocean
  • Sleep comes naturally and deeply during the practice — often before completing all five elements
  • Waking feels like being rebuilt from scratch — fresh, new, lighter
  • A growing comfort with impermanence and death — if the body dissolves nightly, death is just the final dissolution
  • Dreams become more spacious, cosmic, less personal

Common Pitfalls

  • Making it purely intellectual/visual rather than FELT — you must feel the dissolution in the body, not just picture it
  • Anxiety about the dissolution (especially the breath phase) — if fear arises, slow down, stay with earth longer, let comfort build
  • Rushing through the elements to “complete” the practice — falling asleep during it is the GOAL, not a failure
  • Expecting to remain conscious through the entire night — even brief moments of conscious dissolution before sleep are transformative
  • Treating it as a rigid sequence rather than a natural letting-go — the elements can dissolve in any order; follow what feels true

Questions From The Book

Why does the sutra say “leave it to be consumed”?

Chapter 68, "Cosmic Essences"

Osho explains: “leave it” means you are NOT the body. You can leave it because it is not you — it is something you HAVE, like clothing. And “consumed” means returned to source — the elements don’t disappear; they return home. Earth to earth, water to water, fire to fire, air to air, space to space. Nothing is destroyed — everything simply returns to where it came from. The only thing that has no source to return to is consciousness itself — because it never came FROM anywhere. It simply IS. That is the discovery.

Is this a death meditation?

Chapter 68

Osho says: yes and no. It is a rehearsal for death — done every night, gently, safely, reversibly. By dissolving the body into elements each night and discovering that awareness persists, you prove to yourself experientially that death is not the end. The body dies (elements return to source), but YOU do not die because you are not the body. Every night becomes a small death, and every morning a small birth. When the final death comes, it will feel familiar — just another dissolution, just another night.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This technique uses the “body-scan” principle but takes it further — rather than just relaxing each area, you dissolve it entirely. Modern yoga nidra traditions use a similar approach (the “61 points” practice followed by dissolution). Neurologically, the progressive withdrawal of attention from body regions produces a state indistinguishable from the early stages of sleep onset, with one crucial difference: metacognitive awareness is maintained because the mind has a JOB (dissolving the next element) that keeps it alert without keeping it stimulated.

End-of-life research: Tibetan Buddhist dissolution meditations (derived from the same source tradition) are used in hospice care to help dying people face death with equanimity. Research shows that practitioners who have rehearsed the elemental dissolution report significantly less death anxiety and more peaceful dying experiences. Practising every night for years means the final dissolution is simply “one more night.”

One-line essenceEach night, let the body return to the cosmos — earth to earth, water to water, fire to fire, air to air, space to space. What remains when the body is consumed? You. Awareness. The immortal witness.
Sutra 95 of 112
Category: Tantric Union · Tags: feel · fine · qualities · creativity
Feel the fine qualities of creativity permeating your breasts’ and assuming delicate configurations.

In Plain English

This sutra is given by Shiva to Devi and is meant specifically for women. The breasts, says Tantra, are the positive pole of feminine creativity — the place where her energy is most concentrated. Sit, drop your awareness into both breasts at once, and let your whole being be there. The body grows light, sweetness pervades, and over time visions, scents, sounds and finally an inner emptiness arise. (Men using this technique will not feel sweetness; they should use the corresponding sutra at the root of the penis instead.)

How to Practice

  1. This is a women’s technique. Osho is direct: a man trying to concentrate on his breasts will feel ill within five minutes, because in the male body the breasts are the negative pole. Men have a corresponding technique at the root of the penis (the muladhara point Osho mentions in the same chapter); women use this one.
  2. Sit comfortably, spine upright but relaxed. Eyes closed. Loosen any tight clothing across the chest.
  3. Bring your full attention into both breasts at once. Osho is precise: both, simultaneously. Concentrating on only one can disturb the body — he warns this seriously, even mentioning that imbalance can be created. Always both, always together.
  4. Don’t hold the breasts as objects of inspection. Relax into them. Let the rest of the body fade into the background; let the breasts become foreground, then the whole of you.
  5. Don’t be tense. Tension creates a division between “you” and “the breasts,” and the technique requires there to be no division. Soften, melt, merge.
  6. Notice the body becoming lighter. Osho describes a sweet, deep sweetness that begins to envelop you — pulsating in, around, above and below. This is the first sign that the technique is taking. Don’t grasp at it; just let it spread.
  7. Stay. The merging deepens with time. Eventually the breasts feel less like a part of the body and more like the centre of being itself.
  8. Now allow whatever creativity wants to come. Osho says different women experience different sequences. Most commonly: a sense of presence or human figures (if pregnant or about to be, often the form of the child); subtle perfumes you have not smelled before; harmonious inner sounds; subtle visions of beauty. Don’t manufacture these; just receive what comes.
  9. Don’t cling to the visions. They are signs that the breast-centre has become creative; they are not the goal. Watch them as a witness, with the same equanimity you bring to thoughts.
  10. Continue daily. As the visions exhaust themselves, they begin to fade. Don’t fight the fading; that too is part of the sequence.
  11. One day the visions stop completely and a deep emptiness opens where the breasts used to be the centre of attention. Osho calls this shunyata: just space, the breasts themselves have disappeared from the inner field. This emptiness is the highest stage of the technique. Stay in it.
  12. Carry the quality into the day. Osho says this practice naturally brings out a more motherly tone in the woman who does it — not necessarily towards children alone, but a quality of compassion and warmth in every relationship. That quality is the technique living in you.

Osho’s Key Insight

Philosophers all over the world have been arguing for centuries about what basic stuff the universe is made of, what the substance is. There have been propositions, systems, that say that matter is the basic reality and mind is just an outgrowth, that matter is basic and mind is just a by-product, that mind is also material, only subtle. Charuvak proposed this in India, Epicurus in Greece, and ...

Source: 67. Go Beyond Mind and Matter, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 69 · Sutras 96–97 · 2 techniques

You Are Unknown to Yourself

Osho's framing

Osho opens with a stark statement: man is born alone and dies alone, and aloneness is therefore his basic reality - society is only accidental. Yet between birth and death we live so completely in others that the self we know is just labels they have given us: a name, a reputation, a moral image. No one is really known to themselves. That, he says, is the root of human anxiety, and no theory about "soul eternal" can cure it because that knowledge too is borrowed. He turns to Mahavira, who lived twelve years silent and naked in the forest - the real nakedness, Osho says, was the stripping of every social label. These two sutras open the same door.

Aloneness is his basic reality; society is just accidental.

— Osho
Sutra 96 of 112
Category: Imagination · Tags: vast-space · openness · no-landmarks · mind-pressure-ends
Abide in some place infinitely spacious, clear of trees, of hills, of habitations. Thence comes the end of mind pressures.

In Plain English

Go to (or imagine) a vast, empty landscape — desert, ocean, salt flat, open sky. No landmarks, no boundaries, no reference points. When the mind has nothing to land on, its pressure dissolves. Without objects to categorise, the mind simply stops.

How to Practice

  1. Find or imagine the space: If possible, physically go to a vast open space — a desert, a beach at low tide, an open plain, a hilltop with panoramic views, a frozen lake. If you cannot go physically, sit with eyes closed and IMAGINE such a space with full sensory detail. The key is: no landmarks, no boundaries, no objects for the mind to grasp.
  2. Open to the vastness: If physically present, stand or sit and look in all directions. Let the enormity of the space sink in. There is nothing to focus on, nothing to name, nothing to categorise. Just space extending in every direction without end. If imagining, build this vastness in your mind’s eye until it feels immersive and real.
  3. Feel the mind searching: Notice what the mind does in vast emptiness: it SEARCHES for objects. It wants something to land on — a tree, a building, a rock, a person. Without landmarks, the mind becomes restless. It scans, seeking something to label. FEEL this search happening. The mind is a categorising machine and here there is nothing to categorise.
  4. Let the search exhaust itself: Don’t fight the restless searching. Let the mind look. Let it scan. Let it try to find something to grasp. But there IS nothing. Gradually — like a bird that cannot find a perch — the mind exhausts its searching and simply STOPS. When there is truly nothing to land on, the bird of mind must rest in mid-air. That resting is meditation.
  5. Feel the pressure dissolve: “Mind pressures” — the sutra’s phrase — refers to the constant mental activity of categorising, labelling, comparing, judging. In the absence of objects, these pressures have nothing to press against. They dissolve. Feel this dissolution physically: the forehead relaxes, the jaw unclenches, the eyes soften, the shoulders drop. Mind pressure is also body pressure.
  6. Become the space: As the mind quiets, your sense of self begins to merge with the space. The boundary between “you” (small, located, bounded) and the space (vast, everywhere, boundless) thins and may disappear entirely. You don’t feel like a person IN space — you feel like space itself, aware. This is the end of mind pressures because there is no longer a compressed “someone” to be pressured.
  7. No reference points: The key principle is: the mind needs REFERENCE POINTS to maintain its activity. Without reference points (no trees, no hills, no habitations), the mind cannot orient, cannot measure, cannot compare. And a mind that cannot compare STOPS. A mind without comparison is a mind at peace. Spaciousness is not just visual — it is the absence of the mental activity that objects provoke.
  8. Stay as long as possible: Whether physically in a vast space or imagining one, stay for at least 20 minutes. The longer you remain in objectless space, the deeper the dissolution goes. Some practitioners find that after 40–60 minutes, the mind reaches a profound silence that can persist for hours afterward.
  9. Indoor variation: If going to vast spaces is impractical, create a minimal environment. Face a white wall. Remove all objects from your visual field. Or: close your eyes and imagine infinite white space in all directions — no floor, no ceiling, no horizon, no objects. Just white openness. The mind will respond the same way: search, fail, and stop.
  10. Daily micro-practice: Even in a cluttered city, you can briefly imagine infinite space around you. In a stressful meeting, in traffic, in an argument — close your eyes for 3 seconds and feel infinite space extending in all directions. The mind pressures immediately reduce. The space is always available in imagination, even when not in reality.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner-friendly
Duration
20–40 min
Posture
Standing, sitting, or lying in open space (or seated with eyes closed for imagination)
Eyes
Open (if in nature) or closed (if imagining)
Best for
Stressed, pressured minds; city dwellers craving relief; anyone who feels mentally compressed; those who respond well to nature and open spaces

Signs It’s Working

  • A physical sensation of pressure releasing — forehead, jaw, temples, and shoulders all relax
  • The mind’s scanning activity visibly slowing and then stopping
  • A feeling of expansion — the sense of self growing beyond the body’s boundaries
  • Thoughts becoming fewer, quieter, less insistent
  • A natural, effortless peace arising without trying to be peaceful
  • Returning to daily life with a sense of spaciousness that persists for hours

Common Pitfalls

  • Filling the imagined space with objects — the mind will try to add trees, buildings, clouds; keep the space EMPTY
  • Feeling uncomfortable with boundlessness and retreating to a smaller mental space — the discomfort is the ego resisting dissolution; stay with it
  • Making it a visual exercise only, without FEELING the spaciousness in the body — the technique works through felt sense, not just visual imagination
  • Choosing a space that isn’t empty enough — even a few landmarks give the mind something to cling to; make it truly bare
  • Rushing through the phase where the mind searches restlessly — that restlessness is part of the technique; let it happen and exhaust itself

Questions From The Book

Why does the sutra specifically say “clear of trees, of hills, of habitations”?

Chapter 74, "Spaciousness and Mind"

Osho explains: each item represents a category of mental object. Trees represent natural objects (the mind says “ah, a tree”). Hills represent large-scale landmarks (the mind orients itself relative to them). Habitations represent human structures (the mind thinks of people, social roles, purpose). By removing ALL three categories — natural objects, landmarks, and human structures — the sutra eliminates every possible thing the mind could grasp. Without ANY category of object, the mind is truly stranded in emptiness. And that is precisely where liberation lives.

Can imagination of space be as effective as physically being there?

Chapter 74

Osho says: yes, if the imagination is TOTAL. The nervous system responds to vividly imagined experience almost identically to actual experience. If you can truly FEEL infinite space — if your body responds with relaxation, expansion, and openness — then the imagination is as effective as reality. The key is totality of involvement. A half-hearted imagination of space will not work. But a vivid, whole-body, whole-mind immersion in imagined vastness produces the same dissolution of mind pressure as standing in the Sahara.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: The mind’s constant activity is largely REACTIVE — it responds to stimuli. In the absence of stimuli (an empty, featureless environment), the mind has nothing to react to and gradually falls silent. This is the same principle behind sensory deprivation tanks (float tanks) — remove all input and the mind’s processing slows to a profound stillness. This technique achieves a similar result through natural or imagined spaciousness.

Environmental psychology: Research confirms that open, natural spaces reduce cortisol levels, lower blood pressure, and improve mood. The Japanese practice of “shinrin-yoku” (forest bathing) and open-space therapy both exploit this. This sutra takes it further — not just nature but EMPTY nature, not just reduced stimulation but ZERO reference points. Maximum space equals maximum peace.

One-line essenceWhere there is nothing for the mind to land on, it must finally rest. In vast, empty space, all mental pressure dissolves. You become the spaciousness itself.
Sutra 97 of 112
Category: Imagination · Tags: consider · plenum · body · bliss
Consider the plenum to be your own body of bliss.

In Plain English

Plenum means the whole open space — the endless sky. Sitting in some vast natural place, feel that the whole sky is your own innermost “bliss body” (the deepest, second-to-last layer Tantra speaks of, just around the soul). Don’t start with bliss directly — you don’t know it well enough. Begin with silence, which you can feel. As the sky becomes silence, the silence deepens into bliss, and the bliss becomes the size of the sky. Your innermost layer and the outermost space are revealed as one.

How to Practice

  1. This technique pairs with sutra #96 (“Abide in some place endlessly spacious, clear of trees, hills, habitations”). Use the same setting: a hilltop, an open beach, a desert, a roof under open sky, anywhere with endless space and no human beings around.
  2. Sit alone, eyes open at first. The aloneness is essential. Osho explains why: the moment you see a human being you start reacting in old patterns; alone, you can forget for a while that you are even “a human being.”
  3. Don’t start with bliss. Osho is firm on this: if you have not tasted something, you cannot imagine it, and the practice becomes false. So begin with what you actually can feel.
  4. Begin with silence. Let the eyes rest on the open sky. Feel the silence around you — not as the absence of sound, but as a quality. The wind, the bird, the rustle of leaves, all of these can be silence too if they are harmonious; in nature even noises are silent.
  5. Let the silence spread. The whole sky is silence. The whole plenum is silence. Feel that the open space around you has become silence itself, deepening, deepening.
  6. As the silence grows you will notice a quality begin to rise inside you — relaxed, at-home, content. This is the first glimpse of bliss. Don’t name it too soon and don’t grab it; just let it appear.
  7. Once that glimpse is there, the second half of the technique becomes possible. Now imagine that this very quality — the bliss you have just tasted — is filling the whole sky. Not filling it from outside, but the sky is bliss.
  8. Then take Shiva’s exact step: the bliss body is yours. The whole plenum is your own body of bliss. The sky becomes the inside of you, the largest layer of yourself. You are not under the sky; the sky is your innermost robe.
  9. Stay there. Don’t move, don’t analyse. The body underneath, sitting on the hilltop, becomes a small visible point inside the much larger bliss-body that is the sky.
  10. Don’t fight thoughts. They will arise less when this magnitude is held. If they do come, return to silence first, then to bliss; never skip the order.
  11. If silence and bliss feel too abstract, follow Buddha’s suggestion that Osho recommends in this exact passage: substitute compassion. Everyone has tasted love at least a little. Feel that the whole sky is filled with your compassion. Then bliss will rise from compassion.
  12. End the sitting slowly. Bring the bliss-body back to the size of your physical body. Sit a few minutes before standing. The flavour of the practice tends to last for hours afterwards as a mild quiet sweetness.

Osho’s Key Insight

Man is born alone and dies alone, but between these two points he lives in society, he lives with others.

Source: 69. You Are Unknown to Yourself, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 71 · Sutras 98–99 · 2 techniques

Forget the Periphery

Osho's framing

Osho describes the surface of life as a cyclone - but the cyclone isn't life itself; deep down there is a silent centre. The trouble is identification with the surface, which keeps us mad. He tells the Sufi story: a fakir clings all night to a branch over what he believes is a bottomless abyss; when his frozen hands finally fail and he drops, he lands on solid ground just below. The whole ordeal was unnecessary. That, Osho says, is our condition exactly - we cling to the periphery, terrified of an inner abyss that is in fact the ground of our being. Tantra is letting go of the surface and falling backwards into oneself.

Meditation is the deepest sleep. It is total relaxation plus something more.

— Osho
Sutra 98 of 112
Category: Self-Inquiry · Tags: heart-space · armpits · peace · pervading · centering
In an easy position, gradually pervade an area between the armpits into great peace.

In Plain English

Sit easily. Bring attention to the space between the armpits — the chest cavity, the heart-space. This is not the physical heart on the left side; it is the centre of the chest, the energetic heart. Feel that space. Let awareness gradually FILL that space like warm light filling a room. As you pervade it, peace arises naturally. The heart-space is the seat of peace — not the head (which is the seat of thought). Rest attention here and peace emerges on its own.

How to Practice

  1. Find an easy position: The sutra emphasises “easy.” Don’t strain. Sit in a chair, on cushions, or recline slightly — whatever allows your body to be completely comfortable. The body should be so relaxed that you forget it within minutes.
  2. Locate the heart-space: Bring your attention to the centre of the chest — the area between the armpits. This is a physical space: the chest cavity, the ribcage, the area behind the breastbone. Feel it as a space, a cavity, a room inside your body.
  3. Feel the space without visualising: Osho is specific: don’t visualise anything. Don’t imagine light or colour. Simply FEEL the space. Feel its dimensions. Feel the air in your lungs. Feel the aliveness in the chest. Pure feeling, no imagery.
  4. Gradually pervade it: Let your awareness expand gently to fill the entire chest cavity. Like warmth spreading through a room on a cold day. Gradual, natural, effortless. You are not pushing awareness in — you are letting it spread on its own. “Gradually” is key — don’t force.
  5. Notice peace arising: As awareness fills the heart-space, peace will arise spontaneously. You don’t need to create peace — it is the natural quality of the heart-space when attention is there. The head produces thoughts; the heart produces peace. Simply by being there, peace comes.
  6. Don’t chase the peace: When peace arises, don’t grab it or try to intensify it. That moves you to the head (the grabber). Simply continue pervading the space. Let peace deepen on its own without interference.
  7. If thoughts arise, return to the space: The mind will pull you into thinking. Each time, gently return to FEELING the chest space. Don’t fight thoughts — just relocate attention from head to heart, from thinking to feeling.
  8. Let the peace expand beyond the chest: After 10–15 minutes, if the peace is strong, let it radiate outward from the chest into the arms, the belly, the head, the whole body. The heart-space becomes the source of peace that fills everything.
  9. Great peace: The sutra says “great peace” — not small peace, not relaxation. Great peace is peace without opposite, peace that cannot be disturbed, peace that remains even when you return to activity. This is what becomes available through sustained practice.
  10. 20 minutes daily: This is a gentle technique. Practice for 20 minutes morning and/or evening. It can also be done briefly (2–3 minutes) throughout the day whenever you need to return to centre. Place attention in the chest and peace returns immediately.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Beginner
Duration
20 min
Posture
Any comfortable position
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Complete beginners; anxious minds; those who overthink; anyone who needs immediate peace without complex technique

Signs It’s Working

  • A warm, gentle feeling in the centre of the chest within the first few minutes
  • Breathing naturally deepening and slowing without any effort
  • The mind becoming quieter without trying to silence it — peace in the heart naturally calms the head
  • A feeling of being “home” — of having found a place of rest that was always there
  • Ability to access the feeling quickly in daily life — just placing attention on the chest brings instant relief
  • Over weeks: a general state of peacefulness that persists throughout the day, not just during practice

Common Pitfalls

  • Trying too hard — the sutra says “easy position” and “gradually”; effort kills this practice
  • Visualising instead of feeling — imagining golden light or a lotus is not the same as feeling the space
  • Confusing the physical heart with the heart-space — it’s the centre of the chest, not the left side
  • Getting frustrated when thoughts pull you away — returning gently IS the practice
  • Dismissing it as too simple — simplicity is its power; complexity is what the mind uses to avoid actually practising

Questions From The Book

Why is the heart-space the seat of peace?

Chapter 65, "The Heart-Space"

Osho explains: you live in the head — that is where modern humanity resides. The head is the seat of thinking, analysing, worrying. The heart is a completely different dimension — it is the seat of feeling, of being, of rest. When attention drops from head to heart, thinking naturally subsides because you are no longer in thinking’s home territory. Peace is not something created in the heart; it is what the heart IS when attention arrives there. The head covers it with noise.

Can such a simple technique really produce “great peace”?

Chapter 65

Osho says: the greatest truths are always the simplest. The mind distrusts simplicity because the mind thrives on complexity. But peace is simple. Love is simple. Being is simple. Only the mind is complicated. This technique bypasses the mind entirely — it goes directly to the heart. And in the heart, great peace is always already waiting. You don’t create it; you uncover it.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Interoceptive attention to the cardiac region activates the parasympathetic nervous system (vagal tone), reduces cortisol, and produces measurable calming effects. This is not mysticism — it is neurophysiology. The heart-space is rich with nerve endings that communicate directly with the brain’s emotional centres.

HeartMath research: The HeartMath Institute has documented that focused attention on the heart area combined with positive feeling states creates cardiac coherence — a measurable state of physiological harmony associated with reduced anxiety, improved cognitive function, and emotional resilience. This technique predates that research by 5,000 years.

One-line essenceDrop from head to heart. Feel the chest-space. Peace was always there — you were simply looking in the wrong place.
Sutra 99 of 112
Category: Self-Inquiry · Tags: pervading · directions · expansion · limitless · everywhere
Feel yourself as pervading all directions, far, near.

In Plain English

Close your eyes and feel yourself expanding in all directions simultaneously — north, south, east, west, up, down. Not the body expanding but AWARENESS expanding. You pervade far and near at the same time. There is no distance that is “away” from you because you ARE all directions. Continue expanding until there is no centre and no periphery — just infinite pervading awareness with no location and no limit.

How to Practice

  1. Sit comfortably with eyes closed: Take 5–7 deep breaths to settle. Let the body become completely still. Bring awareness to the centre of your being — wherever that feels like it is. Start from a point.
  2. Feel awareness expanding forward: From the centre, feel awareness moving forward, through the space in front of you, through the wall, into the distance. Not thinking about distance — FEELING expansion in that direction.
  3. Add backward expansion: Simultaneously feel awareness expanding behind you. Forward and backward at the same time. You are not going somewhere — you already ARE in both directions.
  4. Add left and right: Now feel yourself pervading to the left and to the right simultaneously. Four directions at once. The sense of being located in one spot begins to dissolve.
  5. Add up and down: Feel awareness expanding upward through the ceiling, into the sky, into space. Simultaneously downward through the floor, into the earth, into the depths. Six directions at once.
  6. Let the directions merge: Now stop holding them as separate directions. Let the expansion become spherical — ALL directions at once, simultaneously. You are not expanding in six directions; you are expanding in infinite directions. The sphere of your awareness grows without limit.
  7. Far and near become the same: The sutra says “far, near.” Feel that far and near are equally within you. The wall across the room is as much within your awareness as the sensation of your breath. Both are equally HERE because you pervade all distances.
  8. Dissolve the centre: As expansion continues, the centre from which you started also dissolves. There is no longer a “here” from which you expand — there is only everywhere. No centre, no periphery. Just awareness pervading all directions without location.
  9. Rest as the infinite: In this state of limitless pervading, rest. Don’t try to hold it with effort. Simply allow the natural state of awareness — which has no boundary — to be as it is. You are not creating this; you are releasing the contraction that prevented you from knowing it.
  10. Return gently: After 20–25 minutes, allow attention to softly contract back toward the body. Feel the breath. Feel the body’s weight. Open eyes slowly. But carry the flavour: you are not limited to this point. You pervade. The contraction is voluntary, not necessary.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
25 min
Posture
Seated (comfortable, stable)
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Those who feel contracted, small, or trapped; people who respond to spatial practices; those who enjoyed Technique 92 and want a more structured approach to expansion

Signs It’s Working

  • A palpable sense of the body boundary dissolving — you can no longer clearly feel where “you” end
  • Deep relaxation as the contraction of being “located” releases
  • A shift from feeling like a point in space to feeling like the space itself
  • Time may seem to slow or stop — expansion in space often dissolves the sense of time too
  • A feeling of freedom, vastness, and total safety — nothing can threaten you because there is nothing outside you
  • Over weeks: the default sense of self expands from body-sized to unbounded

Common Pitfalls

  • Trying to visualise expansion instead of feeling it — this is not a mental movie; it is a felt sense of pervading
  • Expanding only mentally while the body remains contracted — feel the expansion in the BODY, not just the mind
  • Losing the sense of “near” while chasing “far” — the sutra says both; pervade near distances as fully as far ones
  • Getting dizzy or ungrounded — if this happens, bring attention to the lower body and earth below before ending
  • Making it a goal or achievement — expansion is not something you accomplish; it is something you allow

Questions From The Book

How can awareness have directions? Isn’t it non-spatial?

Chapter 66, "Pervading All Directions"

Osho explains: the directions are used as a device, not as a description of reality. Awareness is indeed non-spatial — but you don’t know that yet. You FEEL spatial, located, bounded. So the technique uses your current experience (spatial) and stretches it beyond all limits until the spatial sense itself collapses. You expand in all directions until direction becomes meaningless — and then you are in the truth: awareness has no location.

What is the difference between this and Technique 92 (omniscient, omnipotent, pervading)?

Chapter 66

Osho says: Technique 92 works through belief — you START with the conclusion (I am infinite) and let the feeling follow. This technique works through graduated practice — you start from a centre and expand step by step until you arrive at the same conclusion experientially. One is top-down; the other is bottom-up. Some minds need to start from the top; others need to build from the bottom. The destination is identical.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This is spatial decentering — the dissolution of the default sense of being located at a single point (usually behind the eyes). Advanced meditators consistently report loss of spatial self-location as one of the key shifts in their practice. This technique provides a direct, systematic path to that experience.

VR research parallel: Virtual reality studies on body ownership show that the sense of being “located here” is surprisingly flexible and malleable. Full-body illusions can shift self-location to entirely different spatial positions. This suggests our default sense of location is a construction — and this technique deconstructs it from the inside.

One-line essenceExpand from centre to circumference until both dissolve. What remains is awareness without location — infinite, free, all-pervading.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 73 · Sutras 100–101 · 2 techniques

Fear of Transformation Goes Deep

Osho's framing

Osho confronts a hard fact: many are interested in meditation, very few are transformed by it. The reason, he says, is fear - the fear of transformation, which is essentially the fear of death. The old self must go before the new can come, and we don't really want that. So the mind cunningly manufactures a superficial interest, lots of reading and discussing, while the real intensity is missing. The trick: even a wrong method works if you are deeply involved, and the right method fails if you are only deceiving yourself. He tells the Mulla story - the schoolhouse is on fire and Mulla is praying for rain, calling that "doing something." Prayer too can become a hideout.

You want to be transformed, but simultaneously you want to remain the old.

— Osho
Sutra 100 of 112
Category: Mind & Thought · Tags: subject · witness · attention · intermediate
The appreciation of objects and subjects is the same for an enlightened as for an unenlightened person. The former has one greatness: he remains in the subjective mood, not lost in things.

In Plain English

An awakened person sees the same world you do. Trees, people, food, problems — all the same. The only difference: they never lose track of the one who is seeing. They stay home — in the subject — while looking at objects. You leave home every time an object pulls you. The technique: stay with the seer, not with the seen. Don't change what you look at; change where you look from.

How to Practice

  1. Sit with eyes open. Look at anything — a wall, a tree, your hands.
  2. Now shift attention: instead of focusing on the object, focus on the fact that you are seeing.
  3. The object remains in your visual field. But your priority has shifted to the seer.
  4. Notice: the seer never changes. Objects change constantly. The seer is steady.
  5. Walk through your day with this background awareness: "I am the one who is seeing, hearing, feeling — not the things being seen, heard, felt."
  6. Whenever you "get lost" in a thing — a thought, an argument, a craving — gently return to the seer.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
Start with 10 min seated, then extend into daily life
Best time
Morning (clear mind helps), but can be done anytime
Posture
Seated initially, then walking and daily activity
Eyes
Open
Best for
People who feel constantly pulled outward; those overwhelmed by stimulation
Avoid if
You are solipsistic by temperament — ensure you don't use this to dismiss others' reality

Signs It's Working

  • A subtle sense of being "behind your eyes" rather than "in the world"
  • External situations become less urgent — not because they don't matter but because you are no longer lost in them
  • A quiet confidence that doesn't depend on circumstances
  • Less reactivity: you can hear criticism without immediately flinching

Common Pitfalls

  • Creating a false "watcher" identity — "I am the witness" can become a new ego. The witness has no name.
  • Dissociating from life: the technique is not about withdrawal, it's about stable presence WHILE engaging
  • Straining to "find" the subject — you can't see the seer as an object. You can only be it.

Questions From The Book

In which way can the practice of self-remembering transform the human mind?

Chapter 36, "From Illusion to Reality"

Osho's answer, in essence: Gurdjieff called this technique "self-remembering" — remember yourself while looking at anything. Normally you forget yourself; you become the thing you are watching. A movie absorbs you. An argument absorbs you. Self-remembering means: keep a thread of awareness on yourself while engaging with anything. It transforms the mind because energy that was constantly leaking outward now has a home to return to. You become a centre rather than a scattered field.

If I am always the subject, never lost in objects, won't I become cold and removed from life?

Chapter 73, "Fear of Transformation Goes Deep"

Osho's answer, in essence: The opposite happens. When you are not lost in things, you can actually enjoy them for the first time. A person drowning in water cannot enjoy swimming. But someone standing on solid ground who chooses to enter the water can play in it. The subjective mood is the ground. From there, engagement is a choice, not a compulsion. Love becomes purer when you are not lost in it, because there is no fear of losing yourself.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Attention is always flowing outward — toward screens, problems, people, desires. This creates what psychology calls "continuous partial attention" — you are everywhere and therefore nowhere. The sutra reverses the direction: instead of attention flowing outward toward objects, let attention rest in its source. The objects don't disappear; you just stop being consumed by them.

A modern analogy: Like the difference between a flashlight and a lighthouse. The flashlight moves from object to object, always chasing. The lighthouse stays home and illuminates whatever passes through its beam. Be the lighthouse.

Why neuroscience cares: The distinction between "subject" and "object" maps onto what researchers call metacognition — awareness of awareness. Metacognitive capacity is trainable and correlates with emotional regulation, decision quality, and reduced rumination. This sutra is a metacognition drill.

EssenceYou see the world and the world sees you. One of you never changes. Stay with that one.

Sutra 101 of 112
Category: Self-Inquiry · Tags: identity · omniscience · expansion · belief · divine
Believe omniscient, omnipotent, pervading.

In Plain English

Osho is emphatic: this is NOT positive thinking. It is not affirmation. It is not “fake it till you make it.” It is the stripping away of false limitations. You ARE already omniscient — you just don’t know it because layers of conditioning have convinced you that you are small, limited, separate. This technique asks you to FEEL the truth rather than believe a lie. You are consciousness itself — and consciousness has no boundaries.

How to Practice

  1. Sit in a comfortable position: Spine erect, eyes closed. Take 5 slow, deep breaths. Let the body settle completely. Don’t begin until the surface agitation has calmed.
  2. Begin with the body boundary: Feel the edges of your body — where skin meets air. Now gently question: “Am I really limited to this body?” Don’t answer intellectually. FEEL the question. Let the boundary soften.
  3. Expand to the room: Feel yourself pervading the room you are in. Not imagining — FEELING. You are the space that contains the furniture, the walls, the air. The body is still there but you are no longer confined to it. You pervade the room like warmth pervades a heated space.
  4. Expand to the building, the street: Let the feeling of pervading grow outward. You include the building, the street, the neighbourhood. Don’t think it — feel the expansion as a loosening, a relief, a dropping of tension.
  5. Expand to the city: You pervade the entire city. Millions of people, thousands of buildings — all within you, not outside you. Feel the vastness. Notice: the bigger you become, the more peaceful you feel.
  6. Expand to the earth: You pervade the planet. Oceans, mountains, forests, deserts — all arising within your awareness. The earth is not beneath you; it is within you.
  7. Expand to the cosmos: Stars, galaxies, infinite space — all within you. You are omnipresent. There is nowhere you are not. Feel the absolute freedom of having no boundary. This is not imagination; this is removing the imagined boundary that never actually existed.
  8. Rest as the pervading: Don’t hold the expansion with effort. Simply rest as what you are when you stop pretending to be small. Omniscient means all-knowing — in this expanded state, you don’t need to seek knowledge because you ARE the space in which all knowledge arises.
  9. Feel omnipotence as relaxation: Omnipotent does not mean forcing things. It means there is nothing opposing you because there is nothing outside you. Feel the absence of opposition. That is true power — the power of wholeness.
  10. Return slowly: After 20–30 minutes, gently bring attention back to the body. But carry the flavour of expansion. Walk through your day knowing the boundary is optional, not mandatory. Practise daily for 21 days.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Advanced
Duration
30 min
Posture
Seated (comfortable, spine erect)
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Those who feel small, limited, or stuck in self-doubt; those ready to question the boundaries of identity

Signs It’s Working

  • A palpable sense of expansion — the body boundary becomes less definite, less solid
  • Deep peace arising as you expand — smallness creates tension, vastness creates relaxation
  • Moments where the sense of “I am here” dissolves into “I am everywhere”
  • Spontaneous laughter at the absurdity of having believed you were limited to one body
  • Over weeks: less defensiveness, less fear, greater ease in all situations

Common Pitfalls

  • Turning this into positive thinking or affirmation — this is not “I am great” but “I am not limited”
  • Staying in the head — thinking “I pervade the cosmos” instead of FEELING the expansion in the body
  • Using it to inflate the ego — the ego cannot be omniscient; only consciousness can
  • Giving up after a few days because “nothing is happening” — 21 days minimum
  • Trying too hard — expansion happens by letting go of contraction, not by forcing

Questions From The Book

Isn’t this just self-delusion? How can I be omniscient?

Chapter 63, "Start With Believing"

Osho explains: you already ARE omniscient — consciousness is infinite by nature. The delusion is not in believing you are vast; the delusion is in believing you are small. Your smallness is the learned behaviour, the conditioning, the false belief. This technique simply removes the false. It does not add anything new. A wave that believes it is separate from the ocean is deluded; a wave that knows itself as ocean is simply seeing the truth.

What is the difference between this and megalomania?

Chapter 63

Osho is clear: the megalomaniac says “I am the greatest” — that is the ego inflating itself. This technique says “there is no separate I” — the ego dissolves into the whole. The megalomaniac becomes bigger; in this technique, the “me” disappears into omnipresence. One is ego; the other is the death of ego. You can tell the difference by the feeling: ego-inflation creates tension and defensiveness; true expansion creates peace and humility.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This technique is remarkably similar to what non-dual teachers call “recognising your true nature.” The instruction is not to become something new but to stop excluding. Awareness already includes everything — it is the mind that draws artificial boundaries.

Neuroscience parallel: The Default Mode Network (DMN) creates the sense of a bounded self. Meditation practices that dissolve self-referential processing (as measured by decreased DMN activity) are correlated with experiences of boundlessness, unity, and expanded identity — exactly what this technique describes.

One-line essenceYou are not becoming omniscient — you are dropping the pretence of being limited. What remains when all false boundaries are removed is what you have always been.

↑ Back to top

Chapter 75 · Sutras 102–105 · 4 techniques

Seek the Rhythm of Opposites

Osho's framing

Osho opens with a Walt Whitman line: "I contradict myself because I am big, because I contain all the opposites." The same, he says, can be said of Shiva and of Tantra. Tantra is the search for the rhythm of opposites: male and female, day and night, positive and negative, life and death. The river of life flows between two banks that look contradictory but together hold one current. That is why these one hundred and twelve techniques are so different and sometimes even diametrically opposed - they are not wrong; they are different banks for different temperaments. These four sutras work that way, weaving inner and outer, desire and silence, particular and universal into one rhythm.

Tantra is the search for the rhythm of opposites.

— Osho
Sutra 102 of 112
Category: Death Contemplation · Tags: spirit · within-without · universe · spiritualization
Imagine spirit simultaneously within and around you until the entire universe spiritualizes.

In Plain English

Feel consciousness both INSIDE the body and AROUND it simultaneously. Then expand: consciousness is inside and around everything — people, trees, buildings, the sky. The entire universe becomes spiritualized (recognised as consciousness). Death of the physical becomes irrelevant when you ARE the whole.

How to Practice

  1. Begin with awareness inside: Sit comfortably with eyes closed. Feel consciousness WITHIN your body — in the chest, the head, behind the eyes. Feel the aliveness, the inner glow, the knowing presence that inhabits this form. This is spirit as you normally experience it — located inside, looking out through the body’s windows.
  2. Expand awareness to the body’s surface: Now let awareness fill not just the centre but the ENTIRE body — fingertips, toes, skin surface. Feel consciousness extending to the very edge of the body, filling every cell equally. Not concentrated in the head but evenly distributed through the entire physical form.
  3. Feel awareness AROUND the body: This is the key shift. Let awareness expand BEYOND the skin. Feel it extending outward like an aura — 6 inches, then a foot, then several feet around you. Consciousness is no longer contained within the body; it surrounds the body as well. The body is INSIDE awareness, not the other way around.
  4. Hold both simultaneously: The sutra says “simultaneously within AND around.” Feel awareness permeating the interior of the body AND the space surrounding it AT THE SAME TIME. There is no boundary between inside and outside awareness — it is one continuous field, and the body is simply a denser area within that field.
  5. Expand to the room: Let the field of awareness expand to fill the room. The walls, the furniture, the air — all within your awareness. Not “seen” from inside the body but FELT as part of your own field. The room is inside you as much as you are inside the room. Spirit permeates everything equally.
  6. Expand to the surroundings: Keep expanding. Beyond the room — the building, the street, the neighbourhood. Trees, people, animals, sky — all within the same field of awareness. You are simultaneously within your body AND spread out through everything around you. The sense of being located in one place begins to dissolve.
  7. The universe spiritualizes: Continue expanding until awareness has no boundary. The entire world — the entire universe — is WITHIN consciousness. Everything is spirit. Matter is not separate from awareness; it IS awareness in dense form. The universe is not a dead machine but a living field of consciousness. Everything is alive with the same spirit that animates your body.
  8. Death becomes irrelevant: When you experientially FEEL yourself as the entire field — not just the body — then what can death do? It can dissolve the body (one small point in the infinite field) but it cannot touch the field itself. You are the ocean, not the wave. Waves die; the ocean remains. Feel the absolute safety of being the whole.
  9. Rest in universal spirit: Stay in this expanded state as long as it holds. There is no effort needed to maintain it — expansion is your natural state; contraction (into the body) is what requires effort. You are simply relaxing into what you actually are: boundless awareness in which bodies, minds, and worlds arise and dissolve.
  10. Return gently: When ready, allow attention to contract naturally back toward the body. But carry the knowledge: the expansion did not CREATE something new; it revealed what was always true. You are always this field. The body is always within you, not you within the body. Death is the dissolution of a point, not the end of the field.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Advanced
Duration
30 min
Posture
Seated, relaxed, eyes closed
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Spacious meditators; those who feel confined by the body; people ready for expanded states; experienced practitioners wanting to dissolve the death-fear at its root

Signs It’s Working

  • A palpable sense of awareness extending beyond the body’s boundary
  • The body feeling small, transparent, or like just one point in a vast field
  • A sense of “owning” or including the surrounding space — the room is INSIDE you
  • Reduction in claustrophobia (psychological or physical) as spaciousness becomes natural
  • The fear of death diminishing as you identify less with the body and more with the field
  • A feeling of kinship with everything — trees, people, sky all felt as part of your own being

Common Pitfalls

  • Imagining expansion intellectually rather than FEELING it — the body must participate; expansion is felt, not thought
  • Losing the “within” while expanding to “around” — the sutra says SIMULTANEOUSLY; maintain both
  • Creating a grandiose ego (“I am the universe!”) rather than recognising that the “I” dissolves in the expansion
  • Forcing expansion beyond what feels natural — let it unfold gradually over many sessions
  • Confusing dissociation (feeling nothing in the body) with expansion (feeling EVERYTHING including the body)

Questions From The Book

How does this relate to death?

Chapter 56, "Universal Spirit"

Osho explains: death is only frightening if you believe you ARE the body. If your identity is located exclusively in the physical form, then when that form dissolves, YOU dissolve — annihilation. But if you have experientially discovered that you are the FIELD in which the body appears (consciousness itself), then the body’s death is merely one event within you — like a wave dissolving back into the ocean. The ocean doesn’t die when a wave ends. This technique expands identity from wave to ocean, making death irrelevant.

What does “spiritualizes” mean here?

Chapter 56

Osho clarifies: “spiritualizes” does not mean the universe becomes ghostly or immaterial. It means you recognise that what you thought was “dead matter” is actually alive with the same consciousness that animates you. The universe was always spirit; you simply didn’t see it because you were confined to seeing from inside the body. When awareness expands to include everything, everything is recognised as consciousness — as YOUR consciousness. Nothing is dead; everything is spirit wearing different forms.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This is a boundary-dissolution technique. The sense of self normally has a clear edge (the skin boundary). By deliberately extending awareness beyond this edge and feeling into surrounding space, you weaken the brain’s “body schema” — its map of where “you” end and “world” begins. Weakening this boundary produces oceanic states similar to those reported in mystical experiences and psilocybin research.

Panpsychism parallel: The philosophical position that consciousness is fundamental to the universe (not an emergent property of brains alone) is gaining traction in academic philosophy of mind. This technique provides an experiential taste of what panpsychism describes theoretically: consciousness not confined to skulls but permeating all of reality. Whether or not this is metaphysically “true,” the experience dissolves death-fear by dissolving body-identification.

One-line essenceYou are not consciousness trapped inside a body. You are consciousness in which a body appears. When you feel this — spirit everywhere, within and around — death loses its power. You are the whole.
Sutra 103 of 112
Category: Mind & Thought · Tags: entire · consciousness · start · desire
With your entire consciousness in the very start of desire, of knowing, know.

In Plain English

The instant a desire begins to arise, look at it — not partially, but with the whole of you. Don’t fight it; don’t indulge in it; don’t analyse it. Just bring your entire consciousness to the very first sprouting moment. In the heat of that total look, the seed burns, the desire dissolves without struggle, and the energy that would have gone into the desire returns to you. Don’t wait until the desire has grown into a tree — once it has, it must run its course.

How to Practice

  1. This is a daily-life technique, but it requires a baseline of awareness you build on the cushion. Sit some time each day until your attention is steady enough to catch a thought before it has unfolded.
  2. Through the day, watch for the very first moment a desire arises — the wish for food, for praise, for sex, for distraction, for an angry retort. Catch the sprout, not the full tree.
  3. Do not fight the desire. Osho is emphatic: “Tantra doesn’t say to fight it. That is futile. No one can fight a desire.” Fighting splits you in two.
  4. Do not indulge it either. Tantra is not licence; the technique works precisely because you neither fight nor fall in.
  5. Instead, the moment the desire is sensed, bring your entire consciousness to it. Not a corner of attention, not a part of you commenting from the side. Total presence. Be the look.
  6. Don’t look with hostility. The look is not antagonistic; it is not aimed at destroying the desire. Just a pure, total seeing — without enmity, without intent.
  7. Notice what happens in that intensity. Osho describes it precisely: “In the intensity of that total look, the seed is burnt… the arising desire disappears, just like smoke in the sky.” No struggle, no after-effect.
  8. Notice the energy left over. When a desire dissolves without fight, its energy is not spent — it is yours. You feel powerful, awake, glad. Compare that to the dragged-out feeling that comes from either suppression or indulgence; both leak energy.
  9. If you miss the start — if the desire is already a sprout, a stem, a small tree — let it run its course. Trying to apply this technique mid-stream creates only frustration. Just commit to catching the next desire earlier.
  10. Practise on small desires first: the urge to scroll, the urge to interrupt, the urge for sweets. Build the muscle of catching the start. Big desires (sex, anger, ambition) come more rarely and need finer attention.
  11. Don’t look for a particular “state” afterwards. The byproduct is gentle: a steady, ungrudging non-reactivity. The graceful quality Osho attributes to a Buddha is exactly this — nothing has been suppressed, so nothing is leaking.
  12. Over weeks, notice that this seeing becomes available without the special “hot” moment of catching. A relaxed, total awareness begins to be present at the start of every arising — and the technique has become how you live.

Osho’s Key Insight

Commentary from 75. Seek the Rhythm of Opposites in The Book of Secrets.

Source: 75. Seek the Rhythm of Opposites, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 104 of 112
Category: Mind & Thought · Tags: shakti · each · particular · perception
O Shakti, each particular perception is limited, disappearing in omnipotence.

In Plain English

Whatever you perceive looks limited — this tree, this room, this body. The limitation is not in the thing; it is in your eye. Look at any limited form and keep looking past its boundary. Don’t stop at any edge. The tree melts into the sun, the sun into the galaxy, the galaxy into space. Mind cannot ride the unlimited and quietly drops away. What remains is the consciousness for which there is no boundary — what Shiva calls omnipotence.

How to Practice

  1. Sit somewhere with at least one open thing to look at — the sky, a tree, a far view, a flame. The technique is done with eyes open.
  2. Choose one object — for example, a tree in the garden. Look at it as it is.
  3. Now begin to look past its visible edge. Don’t stop at the leaves. The tree depends on sunlight. Where is the tree without the sun? Travel inwardly along that thread — tree to sunlight, sunlight back to the sun (a hundred and eighty-six thousand miles a second, ten minutes from there to here, says Osho). The tree is melting into the sun.
  4. Don’t stop at the sun. The sun is one star among billions. Travel further — out beyond the solar system, out beyond the galaxy.
  5. Apply the same to any other object. The room melts into the building, the building into the city, the city into the country, the country into the planet, the planet into space. There is no edge anywhere; every edge is your eye, not the world.
  6. Don’t make it intellectual. This is a meditation, not a thought experiment. Feel the boundary dissolving in each step, not just argue it.
  7. Apply it to your own form. Where do you end? At the skin? But the air enters and leaves; the food becomes you, the breath becomes you, the warmth of the sun is in you. Try to find the edge of yourself; you cannot. You are melting in every direction.
  8. Keep going beyond, and beyond, and beyond. Osho’s exact instruction: “Don’t stop anywhere. Go on and on and on, until you lose your mind, until you lose all your limited patterns.”
  9. The mind will get tired. It cannot match the unlimited — it can only relate to forms with edges. After some time it will say “enough,” “stop,” “this is boring.” Don’t obey. Keep going past every horizon.
  10. At some point the mind drops, exhausted. What continues moving is not mind but consciousness — consciousness that has no problem with the boundless. Suddenly you see the universe as one melting, not separated.
  11. Stay in this seeing as long as it lasts. There is nothing to do. The omnipotence the sutra speaks of is not a power; it is the everything-flowing-into-everything that you are now seeing without obstruction.
  12. Sit for an hour at a time when possible. Brief sessions don’t reach the point where mind tires; this technique requires duration. Repeat regularly.

Osho’s Key Insight

One of the greatest poets, Walt Whitman, is reported to have said, “I contradict myself because I am big, I contradict myself because I contain all the opposites, because I am all.” The same thing can be said about Shiva, about Tantra.

Source: 75. Seek the Rhythm of Opposites, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 105 of 112
Category: Mind & Thought · Tags: truth · forms · inseparate · omnipresent
In truth forms are inseparate. Inseparate are omnipresent being and your own form. Realize each as made of this consciousness.

In Plain English

Where the previous sutra dissolved boundaries by gazing past them, this one goes straight to the conclusion: forms only appear separate. Beneath every form is a single fabric. Realise that you are made of consciousness — and that everything else is also made of consciousness. The first half guards against losing the realisation in things; the second half guards against trapping it in your own ego.

How to Practice

  1. Sit comfortably. The technique is contemplative; eyes can be open or closed.
  2. Try Osho’s thought-experiment first: imagine you are alone on the earth. Everyone else is gone. Who are you now? Try to fix on a single attribute of yourself.
  3. You will see that nothing sticks. Are you good or bad? Both are relative to others. Beautiful or ugly? Same. Wise or foolish? Same. Even “man” or “woman” loses meaning — these qualities exist only in relation. Form after form falls away.
  4. Notice the conclusion: you are not an isolated form; you exist by being woven with other forms. Without the rest, you would be nothing. The sutra calls this inseparateness.
  5. Now extend the recognition outward. Look at each form around you and feel the same weave. The chair is what it is only with floor, with light, with air. The tree only with sun, soil, water, sky. Every form is interlocked; pull one thread and the rest unravel.
  6. Now realise the second part: realise that you are made of consciousness. This is the easier half; it tends to delight the ego. Don’t stop here. The sutra is precise: “Realise each as made of this consciousness.”
  7. Look at every form — the wall, the cup, the dog, the stranger — and recognise: this too is made of the same consciousness as I am. Apply it everywhere. The looker and the looked-at are the same fabric.
  8. Osho is specific about why both halves are needed. If you only realise that you are consciousness, the ego inflates. If you only realise that the world is consciousness, you lose yourself in things. Both together produce humbleness — you are divine, but so is everything else.
