  9. When Jesus said, “I and my father in heaven are one,” Osho says he was teaching this technique — and was misunderstood as making a unique claim. Every “I,” says the technique, is one with the source. Don’t make this technique your special private revelation; it is universal.
  10. Don’t try to feel a cosmic emotion. The realisation is calm: just an inner certainty that the boundary you used to draw between “me” and “not-me” was painted on something seamless.
  11. Carry it into encounters. The next person you speak with: the same consciousness inside both faces. The next ant on the path: the same consciousness in two forms. Practise this seeing through the day.
  12. Sit again the next morning and the recognition deepens. Over weeks, the inseparateness stops being a thought and becomes the natural perspective from which you see the world.

Osho’s Key Insight

One of the greatest poets, Walt Whitman, is reported to have said, “I contradict myself because I am big, I contradict myself because I contain all the opposites, because I am all.” The same thing can be said about Shiva, about Tantra.

Source: 75. Seek the Rhythm of Opposites, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 77 · Sutras 106–108 · 3 techniques

Become Each Being

Osho's framing

Osho roots these techniques in a single insight: existence is one, but self-consciousness gives each of us the false feeling of being a separate centre, and that false separation is the source of every anxiety, every fear of death. In deep sleep we lose this separation and merge back into the whole - that is why mornings feel reborn. Samadhi, he says, is just sleep done consciously: Buddha goes nightly to the same source you visit in dreamless sleep, only he goes awake. The ego, like an eggshell, has its protective use early on, but eventually it must dissolve to let the inner being sprout. These three sutras - feeling each consciousness as your own, becoming each being - work directly to crack that shell.

Hindus have always believed that samadhi is conscious sleep.

— Osho
Sutra 106 of 112
Category: Movement · Tags: empathy · becoming-others · consciousness · compassion · dissolving-self
Feel the consciousness of each person as your own consciousness. So, leaving aside concern for self, become each being.

In Plain English

Look at any person and FEEL their consciousness as your own. Not “what are they thinking” (that’s projection) but feel: the same awareness that looks through my eyes looks through theirs. The same “I am” lives in them. When you leave aside concern for YOUR self entirely and become each being, the isolated self dissolves into the universal consciousness that was always shared.

How to Practice

  1. Choose your setting: Sit in a café, a park, a train station — anywhere people are present and you can observe without being disturbed. Alternatively, begin with a single person: a friend, a family member, even a stranger on a bench. You need at least 20 minutes of uninterrupted observation.
  2. Settle into your own awareness first: Before attempting to feel another’s consciousness, become firmly aware of your OWN consciousness. Close your eyes briefly. Feel: I am aware. I exist. There is a “looking” happening from behind these eyes. This is your starting point — know what consciousness feels like from the inside.
  3. Look at a person: Open your eyes and look at one specific person. Not their clothes, not their face, not their story. Look at the ALIVENESS behind their eyes. The fact that they, too, are looking out from a centre. They, too, feel “I am.”
  4. Feel, don’t think: Do NOT think “they have consciousness too.” That is a philosophy, not an experience. Instead, FEEL: the same awareness that is here, looking through my eyes, is THERE, looking through theirs. It is literally the same awareness wearing a different mask. Feel it as you feel warmth or hunger — directly, not as a concept.
  5. Leave aside concern for self: This is the key instruction. As long as you are concerned with YOUR self (your appearance, your comfort, your thoughts about them), you cannot truly feel another’s consciousness. Drop all self-referencing. Forget yourself entirely. BECOME the other person’s awareness.
  6. Expand to multiple people: Once you can feel one person’s consciousness as your own, expand. Feel two people simultaneously. Then five. Then the entire café. Each one of them: the same awareness, the same “I am,” looking out from different locations. You are all of them.
  7. Include “enemies” and strangers: The real test is with people you dislike, fear, or feel superior/inferior to. Can you feel THEIR consciousness as yours? Can you feel the same awareness looking through the eyes of someone you find repulsive? If yes, the technique is working deeply.
  8. Include non-human beings: Extend the practice to animals — a dog, a bird, an insect. Feel: consciousness is there too, looking out, experiencing. Even plants: feel the aliveness. This is not anthropomorphism — it is the recognition that awareness pervades all living forms.
  9. The dissolving: At some point, the sense of “my” consciousness versus “their” consciousness collapses. There is just one consciousness — everywhere, in everything. The isolated self was a fiction. This dissolving is the goal of the technique.
  10. Carry it into daily life: Once practised formally for 21 days, begin carrying this perception throughout daily interactions. When talking to someone, FEEL their awareness as your own. Conflict dissolves. Judgment dissolves. Compassion arises naturally — not as a moral duty but as the inevitable result of experiencing unity.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Intermediate
Duration
20 min or throughout the day
Posture
Any — seated, walking, in conversation
Eyes
Open
Best for
Those who feel isolated or disconnected; people wanting to develop genuine compassion; anyone struggling with judgment of others

Signs It’s Working

  • A sudden feeling of intimacy with strangers — as if you know them from the inside
  • Judgment of others spontaneously drops away
  • Compassion arises not as effort but as natural response to feeling shared awareness
  • The sense of “me vs. them” softens or disappears entirely
  • Moments of overwhelming love for random people — a waitress, a bus driver, a child
  • The loneliness that comes from isolation dissolves; you feel accompanied by all beings

Common Pitfalls

  • Projecting YOUR thoughts onto others and calling it “feeling their consciousness” — projection is not the same as recognition of shared awareness
  • Intellectualising: thinking ABOUT consciousness rather than FEELING it — keep it experiential
  • Skipping the self-awareness step — you must know what consciousness feels like in yourself before recognising it in others
  • Using this as people-reading or empathic manipulation — the point is dissolution of self, not gaining power over others
  • Becoming overwhelmed in crowds — start with one person, build gradually

Questions From The Book

How is this different from ordinary empathy or projection?

Chapter 67, "Become Each Being"

Osho is emphatic: ordinary empathy projects YOUR feelings onto others (“I imagine how THEY feel”). This technique does the opposite: you recognise that the SAME awareness lives in both. You are not imagining their experience — you are recognising that the consciousness looking through their eyes is the very same consciousness that looks through yours. Not similar — identical. One ocean with many waves. This is not empathy but identity — at the level of awareness, not personality.

Why does Osho say “leaving aside concern for self” is essential?

Chapter 67

As long as you maintain concern for your separate self — how you look, what they think of you, what you might gain — you remain isolated in your own bubble. The bubble of self-concern prevents you from truly FEELING another’s consciousness. It’s like trying to listen while talking: impossible. You must stop the inner monologue of self-concern to actually FEEL the awareness that lives in another.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: Neuroscience has discovered mirror neurons — brain cells that fire both when you perform an action and when you watch someone else perform it. This technique leverages that hardware but goes far beyond it: not mirroring actions but recognising shared awareness itself. It’s the difference between feeling what someone does and recognising that the same “knower” exists in both of you.

Practical parallel: In psychotherapy, Carl Rogers described “unconditional positive regard” as the foundation of healing relationship. This technique produces that regard naturally — when you experience someone’s consciousness as your own, judgment becomes impossible. You cannot condemn yourself. This is compassion that arises from reality, not morality.

One-line essenceThe same “I am” looks through every pair of eyes. Recognise it, and the prison of isolation dissolves forever.
Sutra 107 of 112
Category: Movement · Tags: one-consciousness · nothing-else · monism · unity · seeing
This consciousness exists as each being, and nothing else exists.

In Plain English

This is the culmination of the previous technique. See that consciousness is the ONLY thing that exists — appearing as different forms (person, tree, rock, sky) but always the same awareness. Nothing else exists except this one consciousness playing all roles. The multiplicity is appearance; the unity is reality. When walking in a crowd, see one consciousness wearing different masks. When in nature, see one consciousness being trees, birds, river, sky.

How to Practice

  1. Master Technique 98 first: This technique builds on the previous one. You must already be able to feel another person’s consciousness as your own. If you cannot, return to Technique 98 and practise for at least 21 days before attempting this one.
  2. Sit in a rich environment: A park, a busy street, a natural landscape — anywhere where MANY forms are present: people, animals, plants, buildings, sky. You need multiplicity around you to practise seeing through it to the unity beneath.
  3. Begin with people: Look at the crowd and feel: one consciousness looking through all these eyes. Not many consciousnesses — ONE. The same awareness playing many characters. A theater with one actor in countless costumes.
  4. Extend to animals: See a bird. Feel: the same consciousness that looks through my eyes looks through those tiny black eyes. The same “I am” flies in that body. Not a different consciousness — the same one. Consciousness does not have boundaries — it fills whatever form it inhabits completely.
  5. Extend to plants: See a tree. Feel: consciousness is there too, but slower, quieter, more patient. The same awareness that rushes through the human mind stands still and tall in the tree for a hundred years. Same consciousness, different tempo.
  6. Extend to “inanimate” matter: This is the radical step. See a rock. Feel: even here, consciousness rests. In deep sleep, as it were. Not dead matter but consciousness so deeply at rest it appears inert. Modern physics confirms: matter is energy, energy is vibration, and vibration requires... a field in which to vibrate. That field is consciousness.
  7. The key insight — “nothing else exists”: Now see it all at once. Everything you look at — EVERYTHING — is consciousness in different costumes. There is literally nothing else. Not consciousness PLUS matter, not consciousness INSIDE matter, but consciousness AS everything. The chair, the sky, your hand, the sound of traffic — all consciousness appearing in different forms.
  8. Let multiplicity dissolve: Hold this perception steadily. The apparent diversity of the world — so many separate things! — begins to shimmer and reveal its underlying unity. Like looking at a movie screen: at first you see characters, plot, scenery. Then you step back and see: it’s all just light on a screen. One light, infinite images.
  9. Rest in the seeing: When the perception stabilises even for a moment, rest in it. Don’t grasp. Don’t try to hold it. Just rest. One consciousness, everywhere, being everything. Nothing else exists. You are That. Everything is That.
  10. Return gently: When ready, allow the ordinary multiplicity to return. But notice: it never quite looks the same again. Even when you see separate things, you remember what you saw. The unity becomes a background knowing that informs all perception.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Advanced
Duration
20 min
Posture
Seated or walking slowly
Eyes
Open (soft, receptive gaze)
Best for
Those who have mastered Technique 98; philosophically inclined minds; people seeking non-dual awakening; those tired of separation

Signs It’s Working

  • Moments where the world “flickers” — you see the unity beneath the diversity
  • A sense of overwhelming awe: everything is alive, everything is one
  • Boundaries between “self” and “world” become transparent
  • Fear diminishes dramatically — what is there to fear when you ARE everything?
  • Spontaneous love for all forms — not sentimental but the natural response to seeing oneself everywhere
  • A deep, unshakeable peace that does not depend on circumstances

Common Pitfalls

  • Making it a philosophical belief rather than a lived perception — BELIEVING “all is one” is not the same as SEEING it
  • Attempting this without the foundation of Technique 98 — it will remain intellectual
  • Using this as spiritual bypassing: “nothing matters because all is one” — true non-dual perception INCREASES compassion, not dismissiveness
  • Getting frustrated when the perception slips — it comes in flashes at first; stability takes months or years
  • Confusing the perception with a mood — “I feel connected” is not the same as SEEING one consciousness everywhere

Questions From The Book

What does Osho mean by “nothing else exists”?

Chapter 67, "Nothing Else Exists"

Osho explains: this is the most radical statement in tantra. Not that consciousness exists alongside matter (dualism), not that consciousness is one of many things (pluralism), but that consciousness is the ONLY thing that exists (monism). What we call matter, energy, space, time — all are forms of consciousness. When you see a stone, you are seeing consciousness in its most condensed, sleeping form. When you see a human being, you are seeing consciousness in its most awake form. But the substance is always the same. One ocean, many waves — but the waves ARE the ocean.

How does this relate to modern physics?

Chapter 67

Osho notes that physics has already dissolved matter into energy (E=mc²), and energy into probability waves that only “collapse” into definite states when observed. The observer — consciousness — turns out to be inseparable from the observed. The tantra arrived at this conclusion 5,000 years ago through inner observation: everything is consciousness appearing as form. Physics approaches the same truth from the outside.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This is the quintessential non-dual (advaita) teaching: there is only one “thing” — awareness — and it appears as the entire manifest world. The technique asks you to shift from the default mode of seeing many separate things to seeing one consciousness expressing itself in infinite forms. This is not a metaphysical claim to adopt but a perceptual shift to undergo.

Phenomenological parallel: When you dream at night, the entire dream world — characters, landscape, events — is made of one substance: your dreaming mind. This technique suggests that waking reality has the same structure: one consciousness dreaming the world into being. The forms are real (as appearances), but the substance is always and only consciousness.

One-line essenceOne consciousness, wearing all masks, playing all roles, being all things — and nothing else has ever existed.
Sutra 108 of 112
Category: Movement & Body · Tags: consciousness · spirit · guidance · each
This consciousness is the spirit of guidance of each one. Be this one.

In Plain English

The same consciousness that runs the body without consulting the mind — that beats the heart, balances the inner ear, knows when you are about to fall — is also a guide for living. Osho calls it the inner guide. It does not argue, it does not explain; it simply rises from below the navel as a clear pull when the head has been quieted. The technique is to stop using the head where the inner guide is the right tool, and to let it move you.

How to Practice

  1. This is a daily-life technique, not only a sit. But begin with a sit, because the head must be still enough for the inner guide to be heard. Sit until thinking has become a soft hum rather than a roar.
  2. Learn the felt difference between mind and inner guide. Osho describes it precisely: when something comes from the inner guide, it rises upward — you feel a flow, a warmth, beginning around the navel and moving up. Mind, by contrast, sits at the surface of the head and pushes downward. Sit and watch your decisions for a few days; you will start to feel which one is which.
  3. Choose a small situation to test it. Not the wedding, not the job change — small things first. Where to sit in a restaurant. Which book to pick up. Whether to call a friend now or later. Don’t reason; pause, feel, and act on the upward rise.
  4. Don’t expect explanations. Reason wants proofs; the inner guide does not provide them. Osho: it “simply bubbles up.” Don’t demand evidence and don’t back-rationalise.
  5. Trust the small successes. When the inner guide turns out right, notice. The mind will dismiss it as luck; don’t let it. Each confirmation builds the courage to use it the next time.
  6. Use it in genuinely puzzled moments — the moments when no map applies. Osho gives the example of being lost in a forest with no path and nobody to ask. Sit under a tree. Let the chatter subside. When a moment of non-thinking arrives, stand up and start walking. Let the body move where it moves; you stay a witness. Don’t interfere. Practise this kind of surrender on small “forests”: a strange neighbourhood, a stuck creative project, a tangled relationship.
  7. Don’t skip the use of reason where reason is the right tool. Madame Curie’s insight came in a dream — after she had exhausted the head with weeks of work. The inner guide does not replace clear thinking; it supplements it where thinking has run out. Use both, in order.
  8. Practise in fast, no-time situations. Driving on a sudden hazard. A child about to fall. Words needed in a moment of conflict. There is no time to think; whoever does not have the inner guide available is too late. The samurai sword image Osho uses is precise: when there is no time, only the inner guide can act.
  9. Don’t outsource. The outer teacher, says Osho, can only point you to your inner guide; once found, the outer teacher is no longer needed. Don’t replace your inner guide with someone else’s.
  10. Be ready for the inner guide to lead you into difficulty. It is not a comfort-machine. If it pulls you towards a hard decision, follow. Osho is direct: even if it leads into danger, go — that danger is your path of growth.
  11. Notice the second half of the sutra: “Be this one.” The aim is not just to consult the inner guide occasionally, but to be the consciousness that guides every being. Don’t identify only with the head and ask the heart for advice; gradually move your sense of “I” downward, to where the guidance is.
  12. Over months, the inner guide stops being a separate voice and becomes simply how you live — an unhurried, accurate, quiet movement that does not require the chatter that used to precede every action.

Osho’s Key Insight

Commentary from 77. Become Each Being in The Book of Secrets.

Source: 77. Become Each Being, The Book of Secrets

↑ Back to top

Chapter 79 · Sutras 109–112 · 4 techniques

The Philosophy of Emptiness

Osho's framing

Osho closes the book with the most delicate techniques of all - those of emptiness. He explains that Buddha used these very four sutras for his bhikkhus, and was so badly misunderstood that Buddhism was uprooted from Indian soil. Buddha denied God, denied soul, denied any substantial goal - not because he was an atheist, but to clear the ground for total emptiness. Earlier masters had told seekers to "be desireless" while still dangling moksha as the prize, which Osho calls a basic contradiction. Buddha removed even the prize so desirelessness could finally be real. The result is not nothing - it is a vacant room in which existence can speak.

You can enter the truth only when you are totally empty.

— Osho
Sutra 109 of 112
Category: Emptiness & Void · Tags: suppose · passive · form · empty
Suppose your passive form to be an empty room with walls of skin – empty.

In Plain English

Sit completely still — not as a doer, but as a body that has nothing inside. The skin is a wall; the room within those walls is empty. Thoughts may keep coming, like clouds passing through an empty sky — they do not belong to anyone. Don’t try to be empty by an act; be empty by suspending all action. As Buddha put it, do not meditate — be in meditation.

How to Practice

  1. Sit in a passive, locked posture. Osho recommends siddhasana — cross-legged with one foot tucked, hands folded one on top of the other in the lap, eyes closed, spine straight. Any closed cross-legged posture works. The body should be hung on the spine, not held by muscle.
  2. Spend a few minutes settling. Don’t set a goal. Don’t intend to achieve anything. The whole technique depends on passivity — the moment you start doing, a doer comes into being and the technique is lost.
  3. Now imagine the body as a room with walls of skin. The skin is the only boundary; behind it, where you usually feel a self, is empty space.
  4. Don’t imagine a person in the room imagining the empty room. There is no one inside. The room is just a room.
  5. Thoughts will keep arising. They will. Osho says this is just “old habit, old momentum.” Don’t fight them and don’t identify. Treat each thought as a cloud crossing an empty sky — rootless, belonging to no-one. Watch them pass without owning them.
  6. Resist the mind’s standard moves: catching a thought, expanding it, fighting it, judging it. Each of these requires a doer; bring no doer. Just “there is no one to do anything with this thought.”
  7. Stay long enough that the thoughts begin to slow on their own. The clouds thin out. Gaps begin to appear — a thought, an interval, a thought, an interval. In those gaps, for the first time, you taste the emptiness.
  8. Don’t name the gap. The moment you say “I am empty” the gap has refilled with you. Just rest in the cloudless space, without commentary.
  9. Notice that disturbances need a self to be disturbing. Traffic, voices, a child playing — these only become disturbance because there is someone inside the room reacting. With the room empty, sound passes through and leaves nothing wounded. Test this with whatever sounds your room contains.
  10. Don’t hurry to end. The longer you can stay, the more reliably the gaps appear. With time the proportion shifts: more empty room, fewer clouds.
  11. If you find yourself trying to “be empty,” you have started doing again. Drop the trying. Simply continue not interfering with anything.
  12. End the sitting slowly. Open the eyes. Notice that for some moments after, the world too looks “through” you rather than at you — the empty room has not yet refilled. Carry this flavour as long as it lasts.

Osho’s Key Insight

These techniques are concerned with emptiness – they are the most delicate, the most subtle. Even to conceive of emptiness seems impossible. Buddha used all these four techniques for his disciples and bhikkhus, and because of these four techniques he was totally misunderstood. Buddhism got completely uprooted from Indian soil just because of these four techniques.

Source: 79. The Philosophy of Emptiness, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 110 of 112
Category: Emptiness & Void · Tags: gracious · play · universe · empty
Gracious one, play. The universe is an empty shell wherein your mind frolics infinitely.

In Plain English

You can sit empty for an hour, but the rest of the day is action. So Tantra adds a companion technique: take the same empty universe into activity — by playing rather than working. Play has no goal; the doing is its own meaning. Drop the result, drop the seriousness, and the same emptiness that you found in passive sitting becomes the medium of your day.

How to Practice

  1. This technique pairs with sutra #109 (the empty room). One is for passive sitting; this one is for the rest of life. Use them together.
  2. Start with the recognition. Look at children playing. There is no profit, no destination, no calculation. The football match could be decided by tossing a coin and the result would be the same — but they would not get to play. The play is the point. Hold this image as the standard.
  3. Pick one daily task and treat it as play for one whole day. Cooking, walking, working at a desk, talking with a colleague. Do it with the quality of a child throwing pebbles into a pond — engaged, absorbed, but with no eye on the result.
  4. Notice the difference: when the same activity becomes work, you carry the future as a weight; when it becomes play, the present is the whole event. Future may still come, but you are no longer dragged forward by it.
  5. Drop seriousness, not engagement. Osho is precise: the player is intensely engaged — the children are throwing themselves fully — but they are not serious. Be intense without being serious.
  6. Watch the mind do its thing as you play. It will frolic with thoughts: profit fantasies, worries, judgements. The sutra calls these mind-frolics infinite. Don’t crush them; just don’t take them as commands. Let them play too.
  7. Don’t feel guilty about “bad” thoughts and don’t feel proud of “great” ones. Both are mind-froth, frolicking. Osho says even the noble thought of saving humanity is an overflow, not a fact about you.
  8. Apply this to relationships too. The spouse is not a project; the friend is not a strategy. Be playful with them — present, light, engaged, without calculation.
  9. Apply it to ambitions you cannot drop. You may still need to earn money, finish a thesis, complete a deal — do them. But change the quality from work to play. Krishna’s instruction to Arjuna in the Gita, says Osho, is exactly this: act fully, leave the result in the divine’s hands.
  10. Watch what happens to fatigue. Work tires; play does not, even when it lasts longer. The same act, done with the empty-shell quality, refreshes instead of draining.
  11. Pair this technique with the previous one. Sit some part of the day in the empty-room sutra; spend the rest of the day in this empty-shell, playful sutra. The two are sides of one coin: emptiness in stillness, emptiness in movement.
  12. Don’t turn this into a new seriousness. The very moment you say “I am playing my way through life as a discipline,” play has died. Stay loose. The technique is light by nature; carry it lightly.

Osho’s Key Insight

These techniques are concerned with emptiness – they are the most delicate, the most subtle. Even to conceive of emptiness seems impossible. Buddha used all these four techniques for his disciples and bhikkhus, and because of these four techniques he was totally misunderstood. Buddhism got completely uprooted from Indian soil just because of these four techniques.

Source: 79. The Philosophy of Emptiness, The Book of Secrets

Sutra 111 of 112
Category: Mind & Thought · Tags: emptiness · transcendence · knowing · advanced
Sweet hearted one, meditate on knowing and not-knowing, existing and not-existing. Then leave both aside that you may be.

In Plain English

The mind can only think in pairs: knowing/not-knowing, existing/not-existing, yes/no, something/nothing. This sutra says: hold both opposites in awareness simultaneously. Let them cancel. Then drop both. What remains cannot be named — but it IS. The sutra calls it "you" — "that you may be." This is not a philosophical exercise; it is a direct pointing to what you are when all categories are abandoned.

How to Practice

  1. Sit in deep stillness. 10 minutes of silent settling first.
  2. Contemplate: "I know things." Feel the state of knowing — the confidence, the structure it gives.
  3. Now contemplate: "I know nothing." Feel the state of not-knowing — the openness, the uncertainty.
  4. Hold both simultaneously. Don't choose one over the other. Let them coexist.
  5. Now do the same with existence: "I exist" — feel that. "I do not exist" — feel that too.
  6. Hold both. Neither is false; neither is complete.
  7. Now: leave both aside. Don't land on either pole. Simply be — without any statement about what you are.
  8. Rest there. 20–40 minutes.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Advanced (one of the final techniques)
Duration
30–45 min
Best time
Deep night or pre-dawn — when the world is quiet and mind is least defended
Posture
Seated, fully erect, utterly still
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Experienced meditators who have already worked through earlier techniques and are ready for the final leap
Avoid if
You are new to meditation, or if existential questions trigger anxiety rather than curiosity

Signs It's Working

  • The mind stops grasping — a feeling like releasing a clenched fist you didn't know was clenched
  • A strange peace that isn't "positive" or "negative" — it just IS
  • The sense that categories like "self/other," "here/there," "then/now" are optional overlays, not reality
  • Moments where you genuinely cannot say whether you exist or not — and it doesn't bother you

Common Pitfalls

  • Intellectualizing: "I get it, it's neither!" — understanding the idea is not doing the technique
  • Choosing one pole: "I'll go with not-knowing, it sounds more spiritual" — the technique requires both, then neither
  • Trying this as your first technique — without prior grounding, it can feel like vertigo rather than freedom
  • Expecting a blinding insight — the result is subtler: a gentle absence of all conclusion

Questions From The Book

You call the inner space emptiness, but also call it being. How can something be both empty and full?

Chapter 80, "All and Nothing Mean the Same"

Osho's answer, in essence: "All" and "nothing" are contradictory only in the dictionary, not in existence. If you love all, it is the same as loving no one in particular. If you are everything, you are also nothing specific. These are two ways of pointing at the absolute. One is via negation (empty, void, nothingness), the other via affirmation (fullness, totality, God). Neither is more correct. They meet at the same point — the point where mind's categories break down. The technique takes you there by holding both and then dropping both.

Why did Buddhism get uprooted from India because of emptiness teachings?

Chapter 79, "The Philosophy of Emptiness"

Osho's answer, in essence: India's temperament is devotional, theistic. The idea of total emptiness — no God, no soul, no permanence — felt like nihilism to the Indian heart. People could not understand that Buddha was using emptiness as a method, not a philosophy. He denied God not because God doesn't exist, but because any concept of God becomes a hiding place for the ego. He denied the soul so that no subtle "self" could survive. The technique requires total emptiness as the door — but what you find inside is the fullest fullness. People heard only the door and missed what lay behind it.

Were techniques like "become universal consciousness" meant only for very advanced persons?

Chapter 78, "The Inner Guide"

Osho's answer, in essence: They were meant for very innocent persons, not very advanced ones. An innocent person can hear "you are already that" and simply accept it — and in that acceptance, it becomes actual. We need elaborate techniques only because we cannot trust. We cannot simply hear and be. The simpler you are, the fewer steps you need. The more complex your mind, the more technique is required to exhaust it.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: The mind operates in binary code: 1 or 0, yes or no, exists or doesn't exist. This sutra says: what if reality is neither 1 nor 0? What if it is the screen on which both 1 and 0 appear? You cannot think your way to what is beyond thought — but you can exhaust thought by giving it two opposites to hold simultaneously. The mind, unable to resolve the contradiction, eventually surrenders. In that surrender, something else becomes apparent.

A modern analogy: Like a computer encountering an instruction that says "this statement is false." The logical processor halts. In that halt, you become aware of the processor itself — not its output, but its existence. That awareness of the processor (not any calculation it produces) is what the sutra calls "you."

If this feels too advanced — the simpler version: Start with Technique 3 (wherever the mind wanders, this) for a month. Then move to Technique 6 (remain in the subject). Only after those feel natural should you attempt this final dissolution. It is the last door, not the first.

Why this technique is placed near the end of the 112: It requires that you have already become comfortable with paradox, with not-knowing, with the absence of ground. Without that preparation, it feels terrifying. With it, it feels like coming home.

EssenceWhen knowing and not-knowing are both released, what breathes? That.

Sutra 112 of 112
Category: Movement · Tags: space · support · eternal · stillness · absolute
Enter space, supportless, eternal, still.

In Plain English

This is the final movement technique — movement into absolute stillness. Enter SPACE — not physical space but the space of awareness itself. It is supportless (nothing holds it up — it holds everything). It is eternal (it was here before birth and will be here after death). It is still (thoughts move within it but it itself never moves). Just enter it. Don’t look for support, don’t look for ground. Be groundless, supportless, floating in the eternal still space that you are.

How to Practice

  1. Understand what is meant by “space”: Not the physical space between objects. Not outer space. But the inner space of awareness — the infinite field in which all experience appears. Thoughts appear in it. Sensations appear in it. Emotions appear in it. But IT is always still, always present, always unchanged. This is what you are entering.
  2. Sit in absolute stillness: This technique requires physical stillness as a gateway to inner stillness. Sit in a comfortable position and resolve: I will not move for 30 minutes. Not a finger. Not an eyelid if possible. Let the body become a statue. Physical stillness creates the conditions for discovering the stillness that is always present within.
  3. Release all support: Normally, awareness clings to supports: the body (“I am here”), thoughts (“I am thinking this”), identity (“I am so-and-so”), time (“it is Tuesday”). One by one, release these supports. Stop relying on the body as your anchor. Stop relying on thoughts as your identity. Stop relying on time to orient you. Let go of EVERY support.
  4. The terror of supportlessness: When supports are released, fear may arise — a vertigo, as if falling into infinite space with nothing to hold. This is normal. It is the ego’s panic at losing all its reference points. Breathe through it. The fear is temporary; what lies beyond it is eternal peace. You are not falling — you are discovering that you never needed support in the first place.
  5. Discover: you ARE the support: The fear passes when you realise: awareness does not NEED support because it IS the support. Everything else rests in awareness — the body, thoughts, the world. Awareness rests in nothing. It is self-existing, self-luminous, self-sufficient. It is the ground of all grounds. When you stop looking for support OUTSIDE awareness, you discover that awareness is supportless — and that this is freedom, not terror.
  6. Feel the eternity: This space — this awareness — has no beginning and no end. It was not born with the body and will not die with the body. Feel this: not as a belief but as a direct recognition. This awareness that is present right now has ALWAYS been present. It never changes. Bodies change, thoughts change, worlds change — but this awareness is eternal, unchanging, still.
  7. Enter the stillness: Awareness itself never moves. Thoughts move within it (like fish in an ocean) but the ocean itself is still. Sensations arise and pass (like waves on a surface) but the depths are motionless. Enter those depths. Sink below the waves of thought and sensation into the absolute stillness of pure awareness. It is always here — you have only to stop being distracted by the surface activity.
  8. Rest without reference: In this space there is no time, no location, no identity. You cannot say “I am here” because there is no “here.” You cannot say “it has been 10 minutes” because there is no time. You cannot say “I am [name]” because there is no personal identity. Just pure being. Pure awareness. Pure space. Rest here.
  9. Duration: Stay as long as the stillness holds — 5 minutes, 30 minutes, an hour. When the surface mind begins to stir again (and it will), gently let it. You have tasted the eternal. It doesn’t go anywhere. You can return anytime.
  10. Return: When ready to return to ordinary activity, do so slowly. Feel the body. Feel time returning. Feel identity reassembling. But now you know: beneath all of that, the supportless, eternal, still space remains — always. You can rest in it at any moment by simply releasing your grip on supports.

Quick Facts

Difficulty
★★★★★  Very Advanced
Duration
30 min
Posture
Seated in absolute physical stillness
Eyes
Closed
Best for
Experienced meditators seeking the absolute; those with a solid meditation foundation; practitioners ready to face the groundlessness of being

Signs It’s Working

  • A sense of vast, still space — infinite in all directions
  • The disappearance of time: 30 minutes feel like 2 minutes (or like no time at all)
  • Complete cessation of the inner monologue — not forced silence but natural absence of thought
  • A profound, unshakeable peace that nothing can disturb
  • The feeling of being “self-existing” — needing nothing, depending on nothing
  • After the session: a quality of depth, gravity, and stillness that others may notice in your presence

Common Pitfalls

  • Looking for the space as an object — space is not something you find; it is what you ARE when you stop being something else
  • Panicking at the groundlessness and grasping for a reference point — the panic itself is a reference point; let it pass
  • Confusing dullness or blankness with the eternal space — the space is ALIVE, luminous, aware; if it feels dull, you have gone into torpor, not stillness
  • Attempting this without adequate preparation — build a foundation with the earlier techniques in this category first
  • Trying to MAINTAIN the state through effort — the space is effortless; if you’re straining, you’re still in the mind

Questions From The Book

What does Osho mean by “supportless”?

Chapter 68, "Enter Space"

Osho explains: everything in the world needs support. The body needs food and air. Thoughts need objects. Emotions need triggers. The mind needs something to think about. But awareness itself needs NOTHING. It is self-existing. It doesn’t depend on the body (it was here in the womb before the body was fully formed). It doesn’t depend on thoughts (it exists in the gap between thoughts). It doesn’t depend on time (time is a concept that appears WITHIN it). This is what “supportless” means: awareness is the ultimate ground, needing no ground beneath it.

Why does the sutra say “enter” — where is this space?

Chapter 68

Osho clarifies: “enter” is slightly misleading because you are already IN it — you ARE it. But the language uses “enter” because from the perspective of the mind, it feels like entering something new. In reality, it is more like a fish “entering” the ocean — it was always in the ocean but didn’t know it. You “enter” space by dropping the illusion that you are separate from it. The space is not somewhere else to go to; it is what remains when you stop being distracted by content.

Modern Commentary

AI-distilled, not from Osho

What this is really pointing at: This is the culmination of all movement techniques — the ultimate “movement” is into complete stillness. It describes the nature of awareness itself: uncaused, unsupported, eternal, and still. In Zen, this is called “the gateless gate” — you cannot enter it through effort because you are already it. You can only recognise what has always been the case.

Phenomenological parallel: Husserl’s phenomenology sought the “transcendental ego” — the pure awareness that precedes all content of experience. This technique is the experiential equivalent: strip away all content (body, thought, time, identity) and what remains is pure awareness itself — the space in which all phenomena appear. It is “supportless” because it is the ultimate support; “eternal” because time appears within it; “still” because all movement happens within its unmoving expanse.

One-line essenceEnter the space you have always been. It needs no ground, knows no time, moves nowhere — and it is what you are when everything else is released.

↑ Back to top

Appendix — Find Your Path

Self-Discovery: Which Door Suits You?

Section 1 of 6
How You Move Through the World
0 of 56 answered · 0% complete

Ten questions about your foundational orientation — do you live primarily through the body, the mind, the heart, or pure awareness? Answer with your honest first instinct, not what sounds spiritually impressive.

Question 1 of 10single

When you have an unscheduled Sunday afternoon and no obligations, what do you actually want to do?

Not what you should do. Not what your friends do. What does your body genuinely pull toward?
Question 2 of 10single

When you read a book that genuinely changes you, what kind of book is it usually?

Question 3 of 10single

When you’re under intense stress, what does your body actually do?

Stress reveals dominant nature; calm hides it. What is your factory-default response?
Question 4 of 10single

Where do you feel most yourself?

Question 5 of 10single

How do you typically learn something new and difficult?

Question 6 of 10single

What is the hardest thing for you to do, daily?

Question 7 of 10single

What is your relationship with your body?

Question 8 of 10single

What pace of life feels truly natural to you?

Question 9 of 10single

If you could only choose one for the rest of your life, which would it be?

Question 10 of 10single

When you imagine your most authentic self, the version of you nobody has seen yet, what is that person mostly doing?

Eight questions about your past. The clues you came with. If memory feels patchy, that’s fine — answer with whatever surfaces. Even fuzzy memories carry signal.

Question 1 of 8multi-select · pick up to 3

As a child between roughly 5 and 12, what activity did you genuinely love most?

Children naturally express their nature before culture trains it out. The activities that lit you up are honest signal. Pick the top one to three that feel most you.
Question 2 of 8single

Looking back at your school years, which subject genuinely lit you up?

Not the one you got the best grades in. The one that made you forget the clock.
Question 3 of 8single

When you were 7-10, what did you tell people you wanted to be when you grew up?

Not what your parents told you to say. The thing YOU answered when no one was managing you.
Question 4 of 8single

Even if you don’t remember details well, which of these emotional themes recurs in your memories?

Trust the texture, not the facts. What FEELING comes up when you reach back?
Question 5 of 8multi-select · pick up to 2

What did your childhood home FEEL like — regardless of how it looked?

This question quietly reveals attachment patterns. Pick what felt true at the time, not what you tell others now.
Question 6 of 8open text · saved

What is the earliest moment you remember feeling fully alive?

A single moment. A specific place, a specific feeling. It doesn’t have to be impressive. The everyday moments are the most honest. Skip if nothing comes — come back later.
Question 7 of 8single

When you were 14–17, what kind of person did you secretly want to be?

The hero you imitated — the one whose life you envied. Not who you became. Who you wanted to become.
Question 8 of 8open text · saved

What activity from your past have you given up that you sometimes miss?

Not necessarily a regret — just something you used to do that you don’t anymore. Sometimes the path forward is also the path back.
Recap · Section 1

What you’ve told me so far — foundational orientation

A quick mirror, so you can pause and feel what is being said about you. Edit anything by going back.
Answer the questions in Section 1 to see your recap.

Ten questions about your daily life now — what energises you, what drains you, your rhythms, your reading, your music, your phone. The texture of your real life is more honest than your story about your life.

Question 1 of 10multi-select · pick all that energise

Which of these consistently put energy INTO you?

Not what should energise you. What actually does. Pick everything that’s true.
Question 2 of 10multi-select · pick all that drain

Which of these consistently take energy OUT of you?

Same list, opposite question. Don’t worry about contradictions — you can drain and energise differently than the average person.
Question 3 of 10single

What time of day are you genuinely most alive?

Question 4 of 10single

How do you sleep, typically?

This question quietly reveals your dosha — the body’s baseline temperament.
Question 5 of 10single

When you have a full free day with no obligations, you most want to…

Question 6 of 10single

When you procrastinate — what do you actually DO instead?

The avoidance pattern is honest. It tells you which channel your unconscious uses to escape.
Question 7 of 10multi-select · pick 5-7 you’ve genuinely loved

Which kinds of books have you genuinely loved? Pick five to seven.

Honest answer: the books you couldn’t put down, not the ones you wanted to be seen reading. This question reveals you more than any other.

Which films or shows have moved you most? Pick three to five.

3 · Your Guna Balance

Sattva · Rajas · Tamas

The three qualities in classical Indian thought: sattva (clarity, lightness), rajas (activity, fire), tamas (heaviness, inertia). All three are present in everyone; the ratio is what matters.

—

—

From Ayurveda. Vata (air, wind), Pitta (fire), Kapha (earth, water). The body’s baseline temperament. Affects what kind of meditation will heal vs. harm.

8 · Save / Print / Download

Your answers and results are stored locally in your browser. To keep them somewhere safer:

If memory is hard for you, download. Print. Tape it to a wall. The portrait is most useful when you can return to it for weeks afterward.

The Four Yogic Paths — in depth

The Indian traditions identified four primary roads up the mountain: Karma (the path of action), Bhakti (the path of devotion), Jnana (the path of knowledge), and Raja (the path of the witness). They are not better or worse than each other. They are paths for different temperaments — and almost everyone is a blend, with one dominant.

Below, each path is described in full: the inner shape, the kind of person it fits, the modern manifestations (because few of us are mountain monks), and the Vigyan Bhairav technique categories most native to that path. Read all four. The one you can’t stop nodding along to is probably yours.

Karma Yoga — The Path of Action

karma yoga · the doer’s liberation

The Karma yogi liberates through doing. Their meditation is not in the pause; it is in the work itself. They pour themselves into action so completely that the small self dissolves into what they’re doing — and what arises in its place is presence, vitality, and a strange clarity that has nothing to do with stillness.

The classical instruction is “act without attachment to the fruit”. The Karma yogi is not lazy; they work harder than anyone. But they work without the constant calculation of reward, recognition, outcome. They do because doing is who they are. The doing becomes the prayer.

The danger is workaholism dressed as practice — using busyness to outrun feeling. The medicine is to bring presence INTO the action, not to escape into it. To wash dishes as meditation. To run as meditation. To raise children as meditation. Every act is a meditation if the doer is awake inside it.

You may be a Karma yogi if…
  • You can’t sit still for long; movement is your home.
  • You learn by doing, not reading.
  • Service energises you; sitting in silence drains you.
  • You distrust spiritual people who don’t do anything practical.
  • Your best meditation has happened on long runs, in your kitchen, in your work.
  • You’d rather build something than understand something.
  • The phrase “just be” secretly annoys you.
  • You feel most yourself with your hands busy.
Modern manifestations

The entrepreneur who treats the company like a spiritual practice. The chef who cooks for thousands as devotion. The nurse whose presence at a bedside is meditation. The mother who turns three hours of laundry into kindness toward her family. The athlete who has burned through ego in the long training. The activist whose work for justice is the prayer. The carpenter who builds with reverence. The therapist who serves their clients as though serving the divine. None of these need a cushion to awaken.

Best technique categories Movement Sensory Tantric Union Breath Centering

Bhakti Yoga — The Path of Devotion

bhakti yoga · the lover’s liberation

The Bhakti yogi liberates through love. The whole journey, for them, is the ripening of the heart until it becomes so full it overflows the small self. They are moved by beauty. They cry easily. They love specific people, specific songs, specific moments. They cannot fake feeling; they cannot meditate by pretending not to feel.

For the Bhakta, devotion to anything — a person, a teacher, a deity, a piece of music, a baby, a tree — is the doorway. The form doesn’t matter. The fervour does. They sing themselves into the silence. They weep themselves into clarity. The heart, fully open, takes them where the mind cannot follow.

The danger is sentimentality, dependency, or losing themselves in a teacher or partner who doesn’t deserve that depth. The medicine is to keep the love wide and undefended — not narrowed to one object, not turned into a transaction, not collapsed into longing. Love that has nowhere to go but everywhere.

You may be a Bhakti yogi if…
  • You cry at music, at sunsets, at strangers being kind.
  • Logic alone leaves you cold; you need to FEEL it.
  • You love specific people fiercely; love is your highest value.
  • You’ve had at least one relationship that felt sacred.
  • Cynicism feels like a small death to you.
  • You’re moved by saints, mystics, lovers; biographies of them undo you.
  • Spiritual sincerity matters more to you than philosophical sophistication.
  • You’d rather be foolish in love than clever in detachment.
Modern manifestations

The therapist whose clients feel utterly held. The musician who plays as prayer, even if the album is secular. The poet whose work is a long love letter. The parent whose love is unobstructed. The kirtan singer in a crowded room of strangers. The hospice worker whose presence at deathbeds is sacred. The friend who is undefended, who lets you be whatever you are. The fan whose love for an artist is itself a kind of contemplation. The grandmother whose kitchen is a temple. None of these would call themselves spiritual; all of them are.

Best technique categories Heart & Devotion Sound & Mantra Tantric Union Imagination Sensory

Jnana Yoga — The Path of Knowledge

jnana yoga · the inquirer’s liberation

The Jnana yogi liberates through inquiry. Their meditation is the sharp, repeated question: who is having this experience? what is real? what is “I”? They cannot believe their way to truth; they have to see it for themselves. Faith feels like dishonesty to them. They will burn down every concept until only the unprovable remains, and then they will examine that.

Ramana Maharshi’s self-inquiry is the classic Jnana practice: ask “Who am I?” and refuse every answer that the mind hands back, because every answer is itself a thought, not the one having the thought. Eventually the thinker is exposed as another thought, and silence opens.

The danger is intellectual sterility — getting addicted to the inquiry, the cleverness, the seeing-through, while the heart starves. The medicine is to let the mind reach its limit and surrender. The Jnani who completes the path doesn’t end up cold; they end up paradoxically full of love, because the small one who feared loving has been seen through.

You may be a Jnana yogi if…
  • You suspect everything you’re told, including by yourself.
  • The question “who am I really?” has been with you since childhood.
  • You’d rather understand than feel; understanding IS your feeling.
  • Spiritual platitudes irritate you intensely.
  • You’re drawn to non-dual teachers, advaita, Krishnamurti, Buddhist abhidharma.
  • You’ve already noticed that the “I” that woke up isn’t quite the same as the “I” that went to sleep.
  • You can’t devotionally sing, but you can sit in the question.
  • Your idea of love is to relentlessly tell someone the truth.
Modern manifestations

The scientist whose research is itself contemplation. The philosopher who treats every concept as a knot to untie. The investigative journalist whose only loyalty is to what is actually the case. The therapist using IFS or psychoanalysis to inquire into the parts of self. The skeptic who, having dismantled their own belief, finds something deeper than belief on the other side. The programmer who debugs their own consciousness with the same care as their code. The professor whose lectures are inquiries. The recovered-religious person who couldn’t believe but never stopped seeking.

Best technique categories Self-Inquiry & Negation Witnessing Mind & Thought Emptiness & Void Death Contemplation

Raja Yoga — The Royal Path of the Witness

raja yoga · the witness’s liberation

The Raja yogi liberates through silent observation. Where the Jnani inquires actively, the Raja yogi simply watches. They notice thought; they don’t engage it. They notice feeling; they don’t fix it. They notice the body; they don’t correct it. Slowly the witness stabilises until, eventually, only awareness remains.

This path was systematised by Patanjali in the Yoga Sutras: ethical foundations, posture, breath, sense-withdrawal, concentration, meditation, absorption. Eight limbs, an architecture for stilling the mind’s movements. But the heart of it is what some traditions call turiya — the fourth state, the awake-while-asleep, the witness behind waking, dreaming, and deep sleep.

The danger is dissociation or spiritual bypass — using witnessing to avoid embodiment, relationship, life. The medicine is to bring the witness INTO daily life, not as a refuge from it. To watch with full participation. The mature Raja yogi is not detached; they are intensely present without identification.

You may be a Raja yogi if…
  • Stillness is your home; you crave it physiologically.
  • You’ve had moments where you were “just watching” your own life.
  • Solitude restores you; people, even loved ones, eventually drain you.
  • You sense subtle energies and atmospheres easily.
  • You’re drawn to silence, slow walks, long sits.
  • Pre-dawn or late night feels mystical to you in a way the rest of the day doesn’t.
  • You’ve been called “old soul” or “deep” since childhood.
  • Your inner life is more vivid than your outer one.
Modern manifestations

The contemplative whose long sits are not advertised. The somatic therapist whose presence does the work. The retreat-keeper, the librarian, the ranger in a remote forest. The introvert who has refined a life around quiet aliveness. The astrophysicist whose hours alone with data feel mystical. The minimalist whose reduction is an act of contemplation. The hospice nurse who can sit with anything. The artist whose long quiet hours produce the work. The aging person who has finally stopped running. The teenager who knew, even when no one understood why, that they needed to be alone.

Best technique categories Witnessing Light & Gazing Centering Emptiness & Void Sleep & Waking
You are usually a blend. Pure types are rare. Most people lead with one path and have a strong supporting second. The Karma yogi who is also a Bhakta; the Jnani with a quiet Raja undertow; the Bhakta who turns out to be a fierce Karma yogi when the cause is right. Your portrait above shows your blend. The first 21 days, however, should serve the dominant path — one path, one technique, one practice. Variety later. Singularity first.
